text: We carried The Boss to the cave and gave his wound, which was not very serious, the best care we could. In this service we had the help of Merlin, though we did not know it. He was disguised as a woman, and appeared to be a simple old peasant goodwife. In this disguise, with brown-stained face and smooth shaven, he had appeared a few days after The Boss was hurt and offered to cook for us, saying her people had gone off to join certain new camps which the enemy were forming, and that she was starving. The Boss had been getting along very well, and had amused himself with finishing up his record.

We carried The Boss to the cave and tended his wound as well as we could, though it was not at all serious. In this task Merlin helped us, though we did not realize it at the time. He had disguised himself as a woman and looked like nothing more than an ordinary old country wife. In that disguise, with his face stained brown and his beard clean-shaven, he had shown up a few days after The Boss was injured and offered to do our cooking, saying that her own people had gone off to join certain new camps the enemy was setting up, and that she was starving. The Boss had been coming along very well and had entertained himself by putting the finishing touches on his record.

We were brought up together; there was not quite a year difference in our ages. I need not say that we were strangers to any species of disunion or dispute. Harmony was the soul of our companionship, and the diversity and contrast that subsisted in our characters drew us nearer together. Elizabeth was of a calmer and more concentrated disposition; but, with all my ardour, I was capable of a more intense application and was more deeply smitten with the thirst for knowledge. She busied herself with following the aerial creations of the poets; and in the majestic and wondrous scenes which surrounded our Swiss home —the sublime shapes of the mountains, the changes of the seasons, tempest and calm, the silence of winter, and the life and turbulence of our Alpine summers—she found ample scope for admiration and delight. While my companion contemplated with a serious and satisfied spirit the magnificent appearances of things, I delighted in investigating their causes. The world was to me a secret which I desired to divine. Curiosity, earnest research to learn the hidden laws of nature, gladness akin to rapture, as they were unfolded to me, are among the earliest sensations I can remember.

We grew up together, with less than a year between our ages. I need hardly say that we knew nothing of estrangement or quarrels. Harmony was the very heart of our friendship, and the differences in our natures only drew us closer. Elizabeth was calmer and more self-possessed; for all my eagerness, I was capable of a more sustained application and was more keenly driven by a hunger for knowledge. She occupied herself with pursuing the airy visions of poets, and in the grand and marvelous scenery that encircled our Swiss home—the sublime forms of the mountains, the shifting seasons, storm and stillness, the hush of winter, and the vitality and turmoil of our Alpine summers—she found abundant cause for wonder and pleasure. While my companion regarded the splendid spectacle of the world with a grave and contented mind, I took delight in probing its causes. The world was, to me, a mystery I longed to unravel. Curiosity, an earnest desire to discover nature’s hidden laws, and a joy bordering on ecstasy as they were revealed to me, are among the earliest feelings I can recall.

They fished with a spade, at first. Presently the honoured parent appeared to be adjusting some instrument like a great corkscrew. Whatever tools they worked with, they worked hard, until the awful striking of the church clock so terrified Young Jerry, that he made off, with his hair as stiff as his father’s.

They began by fishing with a spade. After a while, the respected father seemed to be setting some contrivance in order, a thing like a monstrous corkscrew. Whatever tools they used, they used them laboriously, and kept at it with all their might, until the dreadful clang of the church clock so alarmed Little Ned that he fled away, his hair standing as bristly as his father’s.

"It's a bad phrase," said Mr. Britling. "I'll withdraw it. Let me try and state exactly what I have in mind. I mean something that is coming up in America and here and the Scandinavian countries and Russia, a new culture, an escape from the Levantine religion and the Catholic culture that came to us from the Mediterranean. Let me drop Neo-European; let me say Northern. We are Northerners. The key, the heart, the nucleus and essence of every culture is its conception of the relations of men and women; and this new culture tends to diminish the specialisation of women as women, to let them out from the cell of the home into common citizenship with men. It's a new culture, still in process of development, which will make men more social and co-operative and women bolder, swifter, more responsible and less cloistered. It minimises instead of exaggerating the importance of sex....

“That’s a clumsy turn of phrase,” said Mr. Halewright. “I’ll retract it. Let me lay out precisely what I’m getting at. I’m talking about a shift taking root across the U.S., right here in Britain, throughout the Nordic nations, and in Russia—an emerging cultural framework, a break from the Levantine faiths and the Catholic cultural lineage that flowed to us from the Mediterranean basin. Let’s set aside the term ‘Neo-European’; I’ll call it Northern instead. We are Northern peoples, after all. The beating heart, the unshakable core of any cultural order is how it frames the relationship between men and women; and this rising new order works to erode the rigid specialization of women purely on the basis of their gender, unlocking them from the confined sphere of the domestic home to stand as equal citizens alongside men. It’s a still-evolving cultural project, one that will shape men to be more communal and collaborative, and women to be more daring, quick-thinking, accountable, and far less confined to sheltered, separate lives. It downplays, rather than amplifies, the outsized emphasis placed on sex as a defining category....

The young man glanced at the clock again and frowned darkly. When his gaze strayed from the glowing horologue of time it rested intently upon a four-story red brick house in the row of dwellings opposite to where he sat. The shades were drawn, and the lights in many rooms shone dimly through them.

The young man looked at the clock once more, his brow furrowing with a deep scowl. As his eyes drifted from the luminous timepiece, they fixed themselves keenly on a four-storey red-brick house among the row of residences across from where he sat. The blinds were lowered, and through them the lights in several rooms glowed faintly.

The two sat down very quietly to tea in Bathsheba’s parlour in the evening of the same day, for it had been arranged that Farmer Oak should go there to live, since he had as yet neither money, house, nor furniture worthy of the name, though he was on a sure way towards them, whilst Bathsheba was, comparatively, in a plethora of all three.

The pair shared a hushed evening tea in Elara’s sitting-room later that same day, for it was settled that Silas should take up residence there. He was, as yet, without capital, a dwelling, or any effects of significant merit—notwithstanding his steady progress toward their acquisition—whereas Elara possessed a comparative surfeit of all three.

“You don’t know the social mill, dad. She’s part of the stream that turns it. Every hour and minute of her time is arranged for days in advance. I must have that girl, dad, or this town is a blackjack swamp forevermore. And I can’t write it—I can’t do that.”

“You don’t understand the social machine, father. She belongs to the current that keeps it moving. Every hour and every minute of her life is booked days ahead. I’ve got to have that girl, father, or this town will be nothing but a blackjack swamp from now on. And I can’t write it—I can’t do that.”

The despatch from General French put him into a kindlier frame of mind. He wrote instead briefly but affectionately. As a gentleman should. "How could you doubt our fleet or our army?" was the gist of his letter. He ignored completely every suggestion of a visit to Pyecrafts that her letter had conveyed. He pretended that it had contained nothing of the sort.... And with that she passed out of his mind again under the stress of more commanding interests....

The message from Commander Ashford softened his temper. He replied with brevity yet warmth. As a man of honour would. “How could you question our navy or our troops?” formed the core of his note. He overlooked entirely every hint of a journey to the Whitmores that her correspondence had implied. He feigned that it had held no such suggestion at all.... And with that, she slipped from his thoughts once more beneath the weight of more pressing concerns....

His thoughts grew firmer and clearer; they went deeper and wider. His first superficial judgments were endorsed and deepened or replaced by others. He thought along the lonely lanes at night; he thought at his desk; he thought in bed; he thought in his bath; he tried over his thoughts in essays and leading articles and reviewed them and corrected them. Now and then came relaxation and lassitude, but never release. The war towered over him like a vigilant teacher, day after day, week after week, regardless of fatigue and impatience, holding a rod in its hand.

His thoughts became steadier and more lucid; they sank deeper and spread farther. His early, merely superficial judgments were confirmed and enriched, or else supplanted by others. He thought as he walked the lonely lanes at night; he thought at his desk; he thought in bed; he thought in his bath; he rehearsed his ideas in essays and leading articles, then revised and corrected them. At moments there was ease and weariness, but never any real escape. The war loomed above him like a watchful schoolmaster, day after day, week after week, unmoved by fatigue or impatience, a rod in its hand.

Joe Larrabee came out of the post-oak flats of the Middle West pulsing with a genius for pictorial art. At six he drew a picture of the town pump with a prominent citizen passing it hastily. This effort was framed and hung in the drug store window by the side of the ear of corn with an uneven number of rows. At twenty he left for New York with a flowing necktie and a capital tied up somewhat closer.

Joe Larrabee hailed from the post-oak flatlands of the Midwest, bursting with innate genius for visual art from the moment he left those rural stretches. At six years old, he drew a sketch of the town pump, with one of the community’s most prominent residents hurrying past it in a rush. The piece was framed and hung in the local drugstore window, right alongside the ear of corn that had an odd, uneven number of rows. When he turned twenty, he set off for New York, wearing a loose, flowing necktie and carrying a small pool of savings he’d stowed away far more tightly than that casual neckwear was knotted.

Poor child! he slumped to the floor, twisted his silken limbs in one great straining contortion of pain, and was dead. Out of the old king was wrung an involuntary “O-h!” of compassion. The look he got, made him cut it suddenly short and not put any more hyphens in it. Sir Uwaine, at a sign from his mother, went to the anteroom and called some servants, and meanwhile madame went rippling sweetly along with her talk.

The miserable youth collapsed upon the stones, his slender frame knotting in a single, violent paroxysm of agony before he drew his final breath. A sharp, unbidden cry of pity broke from the elderly monarch’s lips, but a freezing glare from his consort caused him to stifle the sound instantly, severing his grief before it could find full voice. At a subtle gesture from his mother, Prince Gaheris withdrew to the vestibule to fetch the menials; meanwhile, the lady resumed her melodic conversation, her words flowing with a terrifying, untroubled grace.

Mr. Bumble was fairly taken by surprise, and fairly beaten. He had a decided propensity for bullying: derived no inconsiderable pleasure from the exercise of petty cruelty; and, consequently, was (it is needless to say) a coward. This is by no means a disparagement to his character; for many official personages, who are held in high respect and admiration, are the victims of similar infirmities. The remark is made, indeed, rather in his favour than otherwise, and with a view of impressing the reader with a just sense of his qualifications for office.

Mr. Bumble found himself utterly startled and thoroughly outmatched. He possessed a marked inclination for tyranny, taking no small delight in the practice of casual cruelty, and was therefore—needless to state—a craven at heart. This is far from a slight upon his nature, for many esteemed and admired public figures suffer from comparable frailties. The observation is offered, in truth, more to his credit than not, aiming to furnish the reader with a proper appreciation of his fitness for authority.

The book was closed on his forefinger, and before he turned he was thrilling to the first new impression, which was not of the girl, but of her brother’s words. Under that muscled body of his he was a mass of quivering sensibilities. At the slightest impact of the outside world upon his consciousness, his thoughts, sympathies, and emotions leapt and played like lambent flame. He was extraordinarily receptive and responsive, while his imagination, pitched high, was ever at work establishing relations of likeness and difference. “Mr. Eden,” was what he had thrilled to—he who had been called “Eden,” or “Martin Eden,” or just “Martin,” all his life. And “_Mister_!” It was certainly going some, was his internal comment. His mind seemed to turn, on the instant, into a vast camera obscura, and he saw arrayed around his consciousness endless pictures from his life, of stoke-holes and forecastles, camps and beaches, jails and boozing-kens, fever-hospitals and slum streets, wherein the thread of association was the fashion in which he had been addressed in those various situations.

The book rested closed upon his forefinger, and before he turned he felt a tremor from a fresh impression—not of the girl herself, but of her brother’s phrasing. Beneath that muscular frame he was a knot of trembling sensitivity. At the lightest touch of the outer world upon his awareness, his thoughts, sympathies, and feelings would leap and flicker like dancing flame. He was exceptionally open and reactive, and his high-strung imagination constantly wove connections of similarity and contrast. “Mr. Eden”—that was what had stirred him, he who had been called “Eden,” or “Martin Eden,” or simply “Martin,” his entire life. And “_Mister_!” That’s really something, he remarked inwardly. His mind seemed to shift, in a flash, into a vast dark chamber, and around his consciousness he glimpsed endless scenes from his past: stokeholds and forecastles, camps and shorelines, jails and drinking dens, fever wards and slum alleys, all threaded together by the way he had been addressed in each of those places.

"I don't like his going," he said. "I hope it will be all right with him.... Teddy's so grave nowadays. It's a mean thing, I know, it has none of the Roman touch, but I am glad that this can't happen with Hugh--" He computed. "Not for a year and three months, even if they march him into it upon his very birthday....

“I dislike his going,” he said. “I trust everything will turn out well for him.... Teddy has grown so solemn lately. I know it is a shabby sentiment; it has nothing of the Roman spirit in it, but I am grateful that this cannot befall Hugh—” He made a calculation. “Not for a year and three months yet, even if they push him into it on his very birthday....”

“They are,” Mr. Lorry whispered the words, glancing fearfully round at the locked room, “murdering the prisoners. If you are sure of what you say; if you really have the power you think you have--as I believe you have--make yourself known to these devils, and get taken to La Force. It may be too late, I don’t know, but let it not be a minute later!”

“They are,” Mr. Lorry breathed, casting a terrified look about the sealed chamber, “slaughtering the captives. If you are certain of your claim; if you truly possess the influence you imagine—and I trust you do—reveal yourself to these fiends, and arrange to be carried to La Force. It might already be too late, I cannot tell, but do not let another moment slip!”

The passage to the Conciergerie was short and dark; the night in its vermin-haunted cells was long and cold. Next day, fifteen prisoners were put to the bar before Charles Darnay’s name was called. All the fifteen were condemned, and the trials of the whole occupied an hour and a half.

The corridor leading to the Conciergerie was short and swallowed up in total darkness; the night spent in its vermin-crawling holding cells dragged on endlessly, sharp with unforgiving cold. The following day, fifteen prisoners were brought before the tribunal before Charles Darnay’s name was finally called. Every single one of the fifteen was condemned, and the full batch of trials wrapped up in only an hour and a half total.

And here was the man Spencer, organizing all knowledge for him, reducing everything to unity, elaborating ultimate realities, and presenting to his startled gaze a universe so concrete of realization that it was like the model of a ship such as sailors make and put into glass bottles. There was no caprice, no chance. All was law. It was in obedience to law that the bird flew, and it was in obedience to the same law that fermenting slime had writhed and squirmed and put out legs and wings and become a bird.

Spencer stood as the architect of all understanding, weaving every fragment of knowledge into a singular, cohesive system. He defined the fundamental nature of reality, revealing a cosmos so tangible and structured that it resembled those intricate model ships that sailors carefully assemble inside glass bottles. There was no room for randomness or caprice; everything was governed by law. It was these immutable rules that allowed a bird to take flight, and the very same principles that had once driven primordial ooze to churn and evolve, eventually sprouting the limbs and feathers required to transform into a creature of the air.

The captain was a fishy-eyed Norwegian who somehow had fallen into possession of a complete Shakespeare, which he never read, and Martin had washed his clothes for him and in return been permitted access to the precious volumes. For a time, so steeped was he in the plays and in the many favorite passages that impressed themselves almost without effort on his brain, that all the world seemed to shape itself into forms of Elizabethan tragedy or comedy and his very thoughts were in blank verse. It trained his ear and gave him a fine appreciation for noble English; withal it introduced into his mind much that was archaic and obsolete.

The skipper, a Norwegian with dull, glassy eyes, had somehow acquired a full collection of Shakespeare that he ignored entirely. Martin had taken to laundering the man’s garments as payment for the privilege of poring over the treasured books. Before long, he became so saturated with the dramas and the numerous lines that lodged themselves naturally in his memory that the entire world appeared to take the shape of an Elizabethan play. He even found himself thinking in the steady rhythm of blank verse. While this immersion refined his sense of rhythm and instilled a deep respect for elegant English, it simultaneously filled his head with a great deal of antiquated and useless language.

Meanwhile, Oliver gradually throve and prospered under the united care of Mrs. Maylie, Rose, and the kind-hearted Mr. Losberne. If fervent prayers, gushing from hearts overcharged with gratitude, be heard in heaven—and if they be not, what prayers are!—the blessings which the orphan child called down upon them, sunk into their souls, diffusing peace and happiness.

Oliver continued to flourish and grow strong, nurtured by the collective kindness of Mrs. Maylie, Rose, and the generous Mr. Losberne. If sincere prayers born of profound gratitude find their way to heaven—and truly, what else could?—the blessings the boy invoked for his protectors took root in their hearts, spreading a quiet sense of serenity and contentment.

And also, he wanted to see the old country from which his maternal grandmother had sprung. Wasn't there even now in his bedroom in New York a water-colour of Market Saffron church, where the dear old lady had been confirmed? And generally he wanted to see Europe. As an interesting side show to the excursion he hoped, in his capacity of the rather underworked and rather over-salaried secretary of the Massachusetts Society for the Study of Contemporary Thought, to discuss certain agreeable possibilities with Mr. Britling, who lived at Matching's Easy.

On top of everything else, he’d long been curious to visit the old country his maternal grandmother had hailed from. Didn’t he still keep a watercolor of Market Saffron church propped on a shelf in his New York bedroom, the very church where that dear old woman had been confirmed as a young girl? More broadly, he was just eager to explore Europe at large, full stop. As a low-stakes, enjoyable side piece of the excursion, he hoped to connect with Mr. Britling, who lived in Matching’s Easy, to talk through a few promising potential opportunities in his capacity as secretary of the Massachusetts Society for the Study of Contemporary Thought— a position that left him drastically underworked and noticeably overpaid, if anyone bothered to ask.

Fagin laid his hand upon the bundle, and locking it in the cupboard, sat down again without speaking. But he did not take his eyes off the robber, for an instant, during this action; and now that they sat over against each other, face to face, he looked fixedly at him, with his lips quivering so violently, and his face so altered by the emotions which had mastered him, that the housebreaker involuntarily drew back his chair, and surveyed him with a look of real affright.

Fagin rested his hand on the wrapped bundle, stowed it in the cupboard and turned the key in the lock, then settled back into his seat without uttering a sound. Not once during this whole sequence did his gaze stray from the robber’s face, and now that they sat directly across from each other, face to face, he pinned the other man with a fixed stare. His lips quivered so violently, and his features were so warped by the overwhelming emotions that had seized him, that the housebreaker pulled his chair back without thinking, eyeing him with a look of unfeigned, sharp fright.

The tranquillity which I now enjoyed did not endure. Memory brought madness with it, and when I thought of what had passed, a real insanity possessed me; sometimes I was furious and burnt with rage, sometimes low and despondent. I neither spoke nor looked at anyone, but sat motionless, bewildered by the multitude of miseries that overcame me.

The brief peace I had found soon withered away. My memories ushered in a state of frenzy, and whenever I reflected on what had transpired, a true delirium seized my mind. I swung between fits of searing rage and depths of hollow despair. I became silent and unseeing, sitting in a frozen stupor, paralyzed by the overwhelming weight of the tragedies that had befallen me.

The red-dye peddler appeared optimistic; following her remark, his third effort felt as though it might actually succeed. "Since we’ve finally started to speak more candidly, miss," he said, "I’ll tell you what I’m prepared to suggest. As you know, I’m on the road quite often now that I’ve taken up this trade."

The door of the Doctor’s room opened, and he came out with Charles Darnay. He was so deadly pale--which had not been the case when they went in together--that no vestige of colour was to be seen in his face. But, in the composure of his manner he was unaltered, except that to the shrewd glance of Mr. Lorry it disclosed some shadowy indication that the old air of avoidance and dread had lately passed over him, like a cold wind.

The door to the doctor’s chambers swung open, and he stepped out alongside Charles Darnay. His face was ghastly pale, drained of every last trace of color—a stark change from how he’d looked when the two of them had gone inside together only moments earlier. His steady, unruffled manner was otherwise entirely unchanged, save for a faint, fleeting tell that only Mr. Lorry’s shrewd, practiced eye caught: the old, familiar air of avoidance and dread had just passed over him, sharp and quick as a gust of icy wind.

"She won't.... Some years ago I used to believe in the inevitable European war," Mr. Britling explained to Mr. Direck, "but it's been threatened so long that at last I've lost all belief in it. The Powers wrangle and threaten. They're far too cautious and civilised to let the guns go off. If there was going to be a war it would have happened two years ago when the Balkan League fell upon Turkey. Or when Bulgaria attacked Serbia...."

She won't.... Years ago, I was convinced a European war was unavoidable," Mr. Britling told Mr. Direck, "but it's been looming for so long that I've finally stopped believing in it. The Great Powers bicker and bluster. They're much too careful and refined to actually start shooting. If war were truly coming, it would have broken out two years ago when the Balkan League descended on Turkey. Or when Bulgaria struck at Serbia....

It was the custom on Egdon to begin the preface to a story before absolutely entering the house, so as to be well in for the body of the narrative by the time visitor and visited stood face to face. Christian had been saying to them while the door was leaving its latch, “To think that I, who go from home but once in a while, and hardly then, should have been there this morning!”

On Egdon it was customary to begin the preface to a story before one had actually stepped inside the house, so that by the time host and visitor came face to face, one might already be well launched into the main part of the tale. Christian had been saying to them, as the door was still opening on its latch, “To think that I, who leave home only now and then, and scarcely even then, should have been there this morning!”

“I expected this reception,” said the dæmon. “All men hate the wretched; how, then, must I be hated, who am miserable beyond all living things! Yet you, my creator, detest and spurn me, thy creature, to whom thou art bound by ties only dissoluble by the annihilation of one of us. You purpose to kill me. How dare you sport thus with life? Do your duty towards me, and I will do mine towards you and the rest of mankind. If you will comply with my conditions, I will leave them and you at peace; but if you refuse, I will glut the maw of death, until it be satiated with the blood of your remaining friends.”

“I anticipated this kind of welcome,” the creature said. “Society has always detested the miserable; imagine, then, the scorn I must face, being the most wretched of all living things. Even you, my maker, abhor and reject your own creation, though we are bound by a link that can only be broken by the death of one of us. You intend to take my life, but how can you treat the gift of existence so lightly? Fulfill your responsibilities to me, and I will honor my obligations to you and the rest of humanity. If you agree to my terms, I will leave you and everyone else in peace; however, if you refuse, I will feed the hunger of death until it is gorged on the blood of every friend you have left.”

He danced like a faun; he brought in elegance and style and atmosphere; the words danced lightly on his tongue, and—he waltzed twice in a row with the paper-box girl that Dempsey Donovan had brought.

The strange luminous semi-opacities of fine autumn afternoons and eves intensified into Rembrandt effects the few yellow sunbeams which came through holes and divisions in the canvas, and spirted like jets of gold-dust across the dusky blue atmosphere of haze pervading the tent, until they alighted on inner surfaces of cloth opposite, and shone like little lamps suspended there.

The inside of the tent was dim, steeped in a strange, distinctive soft shadow. The odd, glowing half-translucence of crisp autumn late afternoons and early evenings turned the handful of pale gold sunbeams slipping through tears and gaps in the canvas into dramatic Rembrandt-style light effects: they streaked like sprays of fine gold dust across the hazy, dusky blue air that filled the entire space, before landing on the opposite inner canvas lining, where they glowed like tiny, suspended lamps.

“I’m not afraid to die, Citizen Evrémonde, but I’ve done nothing wrong. I don’t even mind losing my life, if the Republic that’s supposed to bring so much good to us poor folks actually benefits from my death; but I have no idea how that could possibly be the case, Citizen Evrémonde. I’m just such a poor, weak little creature, after all.”

A place of business in London like Tellson’s place of business in Paris, would soon have driven the House out of its mind and into the Gazette. For, what would staid British responsibility and respectability have said to orange-trees in boxes in a Bank courtyard, and even to a Cupid over the counter? Yet such things were. Tellson’s had whitewashed the Cupid, but he was still to be seen on the ceiling, in the coolest linen, aiming (as he very often does) at money from morning to night. Bankruptcy must inevitably have come of this young Pagan, in Lombard-street, London, and also of a curtained alcove in the rear of the immortal boy, and also of a looking-glass let into the wall, and also of clerks not at all old, who danced in public on the slightest provocation. Yet, a French Tellson’s could get on with these things exceedingly well, and, as long as the times held together, no man had taken fright at them, and drawn out his money.

A London banking house that mirrored Tellson’s Paris branch would swiftly have lost its wits and found itself in the Gazette. What would sober British prudence and decorum have made of potted orange-trees adorning a bank courtyard, or a painted Cupid poised above the clerks? Yet there they stood. Tellson’s had coated the little god in whitewash, but he still hovered on the ceiling, clad in the airiest drapery, aiming (as is his eternal habit) at lucre from dawn till dusk. Ruin would surely have followed this heathen boy in Lombard-street, along with the curtained recess tucked behind him, and the mirror sunk into the wall, and the far-from-elderly clerks who broke into dances at the merest excuse. Still, a French Tellson’s managed these flourishes perfectly well, and so long as the age kept its seams intact, no one took alarm or withdrew a single sovereign.

As for Thomasin, I never expected much from her; and she has not disappointed me. But I forgave her long ago; and I forgive him now. I’ll go.”

“Well, since matters have reached this pass,” Mrs. Yeobright said mournfully, “I will confess to you, reddleman, that I had considered going. I would be far easier in my heart if we made peace. The marriage cannot be undone, my days may be few, and I long to die in charity. He is my only son; and seeing what sons are made of, I am not grieved that I have no other. As for Thomasin, I never looked for much from her, and she has not failed my expectation. But I pardoned her long ago, and I pardon him now. I shall go.”

Dogs are not generally apt to revenge injuries inflicted upon them by their masters; but Mr. Sikes’s dog, having faults of temper in common with his owner, and labouring, perhaps, at this moment, under a powerful sense of injury, made no more ado but at once fixed his teeth in one of the half-boots. Having given in a hearty shake, he retired, growling, under a form; just escaping the pewter measure which Mr. Sikes levelled at his head.

Dogs are not often disposed to avenge the wrongs done them by their masters; yet Mr. Vane’s hound, inheriting, as it were, the ill-humour of his keeper, and perhaps at that instant oppressed by a very lively consciousness of being ill-used, wasted no time in resentment, but at once clamped his teeth upon one of the worn half-boots. After administering to it a vigorous shake, he withdrew beneath a settle, still growling, and so narrowly escaped the pewter measure which Mr. Vane flung at his head.

From a pedigreed yellow pup I grew up to be an anonymous yellow cur looking like a cross between an Angora cat and a box of lemons. But my mistress never tumbled. She thought that the two primeval pups that Noah chased into the ark were but a collateral branch of my ancestors. It took two policemen to keep her from entering me at the Madison Square Garden for the Siberian bloodhound prize.

I began life as a yellow puppy of distinguished lineage, only to mature into a nameless yellow mongrel resembling something between an Angora cat and a crate of lemons. Yet my mistress never caught on. She believed those two primordial hounds that Noah herded onto the ark were merely a distant offshoot of my family tree. It required two officers to restrain her from entering me in the Madison Square Garden show for the Siberian bloodhound award.

And yet, through her very terror ran the fibres of fascination that had drawn and that still drew her to him—that had compelled her to lean towards him, and, in that mad, culminating moment, lay her hands upon his neck. She was hurt and outraged by what had taken place, and yet she lay in his arms and quivered while he went on muttering, “The beasts! The beasts!” And she still lay there when he said: “I’ll not bother your table again, dear. They do not like me, and it is wrong of me to thrust my objectionable presence upon them. Besides, they are just as objectionable to me. Faugh! They are sickening. And to think of it, I dreamed in my innocence that the persons who sat in the high places, who lived in fine houses and had educations and bank accounts, were worth while!”

A new surge of his intricate rage flared up, and Ruth felt a cold terror take hold of her. She had never seen him so consumed by fury, a state that remained entirely baffling and irrational to her understanding. Even so, through her fear ran the same magnetic threads of fascination that had always pulled her toward him—the same impulse that had forced her to lean in and, in a frantic, climactic moment, clasp her hands behind his neck. Though she felt wounded and insulted by what had transpired, she stayed trembling in his arms as he continued to mutter, “The animals! The wretched animals!” She remained there as he added, “I won’t trouble your dinner table again, my dear. They have no liking for me, and it is a mistake for me to impose my unwelcome presence on them. In truth, they are just as loathsome to me. It’s revolting. To think that in my ignorance I actually believed those in high society, with their grand estates, educations, and fortunes, were people of any real value!”

Soldiers with lanterns, at the guard-house there. “Your papers, travellers!” “See here then, Monsieur the Officer,” said Defarge, getting down, and taking him gravely apart, “these are the papers of monsieur inside, with the white head. They were consigned to me, with him, at the--” He dropped his voice, there was a flutter among the military lanterns, and one of them being handed into the coach by an arm in uniform, the eyes connected with the arm looked, not an every day or an every night look, at monsieur with the white head. “It is well. Forward!” from the uniform. “Adieu!” from Defarge. And so, under a short grove of feebler and feebler over-swinging lamps, out under the great grove of stars.

Beneath the swaying streetlamps—glowing ever more brilliant in the finer quarters, and ever more feeble in the poorer—past bright shopwindows, lively throngs, lit-up cafés, and theatre entrances, they rolled on toward one of the city gates. At the guardhouse, soldiers stood holding lanterns. “Your documents, travellers!” “Here, if you please, Monsieur the Officer,” said Defarge, climbing down and drawing him soberly aside. “These belong to the gentleman inside, the one with the white hair. He was entrusted to me, along with the gentleman himself, at the—” He lowered his voice; the military lanterns bobbed and flickered, and one was passed into the carriage by a uniformed arm. The eyes above that arm fixed on the white-haired gentleman with a look that was no ordinary glance, by day or night. “All is in order. Proceed!” came from the uniform. “Farewell!” from Defarge. And so, beneath a brief avenue of ever-weakening, swaying lamps, they emerged into the vast avenue of stars.

Intellectual “work” is misnamed; it is a pleasure, a dissipation, and is its own highest reward. The poorest paid architect, engineer, general, author, sculptor, painter, lecturer, advocate, legislator, actor, preacher, singer is constructively in heaven when he is at work; and as for the musician with the fiddle-bow in his hand who sits in the midst of a great orchestra with the ebbing and flowing tides of divine sound washing over him--why, certainly, he is at work, if you wish to call it that, but lord, it's a sarcasm just the same. The law of work does seem utterly unfair--but there it is, and nothing can change it: the higher the pay in enjoyment the worker gets out of it, the higher shall be his pay in cash, also. And it's also the very law of those transparent swindles, transmissible nobility and kingship.

The label "intellectual labor" is a misnomer; it is a delight, an indulgence, and carries its own supreme compensation. The most underpaid architect, engineer, general, writer, sculptor, painter, speaker, lawyer, lawmaker, performer, minister, vocalist is essentially in paradise while engaged in his craft; and as for the musician, bow in hand, seated within a vast orchestra while surging currents of sublime sound cascade over him—well, yes, he is working, if you insist on calling it that, but heavens, the term is a mockery all the same. The principle of work appears profoundly unjust—yet there it stands, immutable: the greater the pleasure a worker derives from his toil, the greater his monetary reward will be as well. And this is precisely the same rule governing those blatant frauds, hereditary aristocracy and monarchy.

Almost for the first time in his life, Troy, as he stood by this dismantled grave, wished himself another man. It is seldom that a person with much animal spirit does not feel that the fact of his life being his own is the one qualification which singles it out as a more hopeful life than that of others who may actually resemble him in every particular. Troy had felt, in his transient way, hundreds of times, that he could not envy other people their condition, because the possession of that condition would have necessitated a different personality, when he desired no other than his own. He had not minded the peculiarities of his birth, the vicissitudes of his life, the meteor-like uncertainty of all that related to him, because these appertained to the hero of his story, without whom there would have been no story at all for him; and it seemed to be only in the nature of things that matters would right themselves at some proper date and wind up well. This very morning the illusion completed its disappearance, and, as it were, all of a sudden, Troy hated himself. The suddenness was probably more apparent than real. A coral reef which just comes short of the ocean surface is no more to the horizon than if it had never been begun, and the mere finishing stroke is what often appears to create an event which has long been potentially an accomplished thing.

Perhaps for the first time since his youth, Julian, as he lingered beside the disturbed earth of the grave, felt a profound longing to be any man but himself. It is a rare occurrence for one possessed of such vital energy not to feel that the simple fact of his own identity is the very quality that renders his life more promising than the lives of those who might otherwise mirror him. Julian had realized, in his shallow fashion, countless times before, that he could never truly envy the station of another; to possess their world would require a different spirit, and he had never wished for any spirit but his own. He had been indifferent to the scandals of his birth, the turbulence of his career, and the flickering instability of his future, for these were merely the attributes of the central figure in a drama that would be nothing without him; it seemed only natural that the plot would eventually resolve itself into a graceful end. On this particular morning, however, the deception vanished, and Julian was seized by a sudden, sharp hatred for his own being. The immediacy of this change was more illusion than fact. A bank of coral that stops just short of the tide is no more visible to the sailor than if it did not exist, and the final, crowning growth is often mistaken for the creation of a thing that has, in truth, been building toward completion for a long time.

We elected to walk upright, for convenience's sake; we argued that if discerned, we should be taken for friends rather than enemies, and in any case we should be out of reach of swords, and these gentry did not seem to have any spears along. Well, it was a curious trip. Everywhere dead men were lying outside the second fence--not plainly visible, but still visible; and we counted fifteen of those pathetic statues--dead knights standing with their hands on the upper wire.

We resolved to venture along the path between the inner barriers. We chose to move erect, for ease of motion; we reasoned that if spotted, we would be mistaken for allies rather than foes, and besides, we would remain beyond the sweep of blades, and these fellows appeared to carry no lances with them. It was, I must say, a strange journey. On every side fallen men lay scattered beyond the second paling—not starkly exposed, yet still discernible; and we tallied fifteen of those sorrowful figures—slain soldiers upright with their fingers clutching the topmost strand.

The mazes of the dance were ecstatic. Soft whispering came into her ear from under the radiant helmet, and she felt like a woman in Paradise. Suddenly these two wheeled out from the mass of dancers, dived into one of the pools of the heath, and came out somewhere into an iridescent hollow, arched with rainbows. “It must be here,” said the voice by her side, and blushingly looking up she saw him removing his casque to kiss her. At that moment there was a cracking noise, and his figure fell into fragments like a pack of cards.

Out of the shifting pageant, a quieter vision slowly took shape: the heath emerged in shadowy outline behind the brilliant foreground. She was dancing to unearthly music, partnered by the silver-armored man who had moved beside her through all the earlier fantastic transformations, his helmet’s visor shut. The dance’s winding steps were rapturous. A soft whisper reached her ear from beneath the gleaming helmet, and she felt herself a woman in Paradise. Suddenly the two of them spun away from the crowd of dancers, plunged into one of the heath’s dark pools, and surfaced in a shimmering hollow vaulted with rainbows. “It must be here,” said the voice at her side, and when she looked up, blushing, she saw him lifting his helmet to kiss her. His face was described as bilaterally asymmetrical, and various other signs of degeneration were described. Especially notable were his thuglike hands and the fiery gleams in his blood-shot eyes.

The text went on to map the supposed irregularities of his skull, citing them as evidence of a primitive character. His fingers were described as those of a common ruffian, and much was made of the predatory, blood-rimmed glare that supposedly defined his expression.

An oil painting caught and held him. A heavy surf thundered and burst over an outjutting rock; lowering storm-clouds covered the sky; and, outside the line of surf, a pilot-schooner, close-hauled, heeled over till every detail of her deck was visible, was surging along against a stormy sunset sky. There was beauty, and it drew him irresistibly. He forgot his awkward walk and came closer to the painting, very close. The beauty faded out of the canvas. His face expressed his bepuzzlement. He stared at what seemed a careless daub of paint, then stepped away. Immediately all the beauty flashed back into the canvas. “A trick picture,” was his thought, as he dismissed it, though in the midst of the multitudinous impressions he was receiving he found time to feel a prod of indignation that so much beauty should be sacrificed to make a trick. He did not know painting. He had been brought up on chromos and lithographs that were always definite and sharp, near or far. He had seen oil paintings, it was true, in the show windows of shops, but the glass of the windows had prevented his eager eyes from approaching too near.

An oil painting seized his attention. A massive surf roared and shattered against a jutting rock; dark storm clouds smothered the sky; and beyond the breaking waves, a pilot schooner, sails hauled tight, leaned so steeply that every line of her deck lay exposed as she drove forward against a turbulent sunset. There was beauty in it, and it pulled him with irresistible force. He forgot his clumsy gait and moved nearer to the painting, very near. The beauty dissolved from the canvas. His face clouded with bewilderment. He peered at what now looked like a careless smear of paint, then stepped back. Instantly all the beauty flared back into the canvas. “A trick picture,” he thought, dismissing it, though amid the countless impressions flooding in on him he still found room for a stab of resentment that so much beauty should be wasted on a trick. He knew nothing of painting. He had been raised on chromolithographs that stayed crisp and distinct, whether viewed up close or from a distance. He had seen oil paintings, it was true, in shop windows, but the glass had always kept his hungry eyes from pressing too near.

We pass rapidly on into the month of March, to a breezy day without sunshine, frost, or dew. On Yalbury Hill, about midway between Weatherbury and Casterbridge, where the turnpike road passes over the crest, a numerous concourse of people had gathered, the eyes of the greater number being frequently stretched afar in a northerly direction. The groups consisted of a throng of idlers, a party of javelin-men, and two trumpeters, and in the midst were carriages, one of which contained the high sheriff. With the idlers, many of whom had mounted to the top of a cutting formed for the road, were several Weatherbury men and boys—among others Poorgrass, Coggan, and Cain Ball.

Time moved forward quickly into the month of March, to a blustery day that lacked both the warmth of the sun and the crispness of frost or dew. At the summit of Yalbury Hill, halfway between Weatherbury and Casterbridge where the main road crosses the ridge, a large crowd had assembled, their gazes frequently fixed on the northern horizon. This gathering consisted of a local throng, a contingent of javelin-men, and two trumpeters; in their center stood several carriages, one of which held the high sheriff. Among the spectators, many of whom had climbed the steep embankments flanking the road, were several men and boys from Weatherbury—including Poorgrass, Coggan, and Cain Ball.

The young tenant in the chair allowed these thoughts to file, soft-shod, through his mind, while there drifted into the room furnished sounds and furnished scents. He heard in one room a tittering and incontinent, slack laughter; in others the monologue of a scold, the rattling of dice, a lullaby, and one crying dully; above him a banjo tinkled with spirit. Doors banged somewhere; the elevated trains roared intermittently; a cat yowled miserably upon a back fence. And he breathed the breath of the house—a dank savour rather than a smell—a cold, musty effluvium as from underground vaults mingled with the reeking exhalations of linoleum and mildewed and rotten woodwork.

The young man seated in the chair let these reflections pass through his mind with silent, padded steps, as the room filled with borrowed noises and borrowed odors. From one chamber came a giggling, loose and unrestrained laughter; from others, a scold’s endless harangue, the clatter of dice, a cradle song, and a muffled weeping; overhead, a banjo twanged with energy. Doors slammed somewhere in the distance; elevated trains thundered at intervals; a cat wailed wretchedly on a back fence. And he inhaled the house’s exhalation—a damp essence more than a smell—a chill, stale vapor as from subterranean crypts blended with the pungent emissions of linoleum and decaying, moldy timber.

But the most radical change of all, and the one that pleased her most, was the change in his speech. Not only did he speak more correctly, but he spoke more easily, and there were many new words in his vocabulary. When he grew excited or enthusiastic, however, he dropped back into the old slurring and the dropping of final consonants. Also, there was an awkward hesitancy, at times, as he essayed the new words he had learned. On the other hand, along with his ease of expression, he displayed a lightness and facetiousness of thought that delighted her. It was his old spirit of humor and badinage that had made him a favorite in his own class, but which he had hitherto been unable to use in her presence through lack of words and training. He was just beginning to orientate himself and to feel that he was not wholly an intruder. But he was very tentative, fastidiously so, letting Ruth set the pace of sprightliness and fancy, keeping up with her but never daring to go beyond her.

But the deepest alteration of all, and the one that gave her the keenest pleasure, was the change in his speech. He not only spoke more correctly, but with greater ease, and his vocabulary had grown rich with many unfamiliar words. Yet when excitement or ardor took hold of him, he slipped back into the old drawl and the soft swallowing of final sounds. There was, too, at times an uneasy hesitation as he tried out the new words he had acquired. On the other hand, with this new fluency there came a quickness and playful wit in his thinking that delighted her. It was the same old turn for humor and banter that had once made him a favorite among his own companions, though until now he had been unable to bring it forth in her presence for want of words and training. He was only just beginning to find his bearings and to feel that he was not altogether a stranger. But he proceeded with great caution, almost with punctilious care, letting Clara determine the tempo of gayety and invention, matching her pace, yet never venturing to pass beyond it.

“You have given me a glimpse of fairyland,” Martin said on the ferry-boat. “It makes life worth while to meet people like that. My mind is all worked up. I never appreciated idealism before. Yet I can’t accept it. I know that I shall always be a realist. I am so made, I guess. But I’d like to have made a reply to Kreis and Hamilton, and I think I’d have had a word or two for Norton. I didn’t see that Spencer was damaged any. I’m as excited as a child on its first visit to the circus. I see I must read up some more. I’m going to get hold of Saleeby. I still think Spencer is unassailable, and next time I’m going to take a hand myself.”

“You’ve let me peer into an enchanted realm,” Martin said as the ferry churned across the water. “Meeting people like that makes existence feel worthwhile. My thoughts are spinning. I’d never truly grasped idealism before. Yet I can’t embrace it. I know I’ll always be a realist—it’s simply how I’m built, I suppose. Still, I wish I’d answered Kreis and Hamilton, and I believe I’d have had a few things to say to Norton as well. I didn’t see Spencer suffer any real blow. I’m as thrilled as a child stepping into a circus for the first time. Clearly, I need to read more. I’m going to find Saleeby. I remain convinced Spencer is beyond reproach, and next time I intend to join the fray myself.”

Restless, shifting, fugacious as time itself is a certain vast bulk of the population of the red brick district of the lower West Side. Homeless, they have a hundred homes. They flit from furnished room to furnished room, transients forever—transients in abode, transients in heart and mind. They sing “Home, Sweet Home” in ragtime; they carry their _lares et penates_ in a bandbox; their vine is entwined about a picture hat; a rubber plant is their fig tree.

Unquiet, mutable, and as evanescent as time itself is a certain great multitude of the inhabitants of the red-brick quarter of the lower West Side. Homeless, yet lodged in a hundred dwellings, they pass from furnished room to furnished room, eternal wanderers—wanderers in their roofs, wanderers in their hearts and thoughts. They croon “Home, Sweet Home” to a syncopated tune; they bear their _lares et penates_ in a hatbox; their vine winds itself about a feathered hat; a rubber plant serves them for a fig tree.

And yet, somehow, he had a feeling that Brissenden had been right, after all. “The Shame of the Sun” had been the cause of his success more than the stuff he had written. That stuff had been merely incidental. It had been rejected right and left by the magazines. The publication of “The Shame of the Sun” had started a controversy and precipitated the landslide in his favor. Had there been no “Shame of the Sun” there would have been no landslide, and had there been no miracle in the go of “The Shame of the Sun” there would have been no landslide. Singletree, Darnley & Co. attested that miracle. They had brought out a first edition of fifteen hundred copies and been dubious of selling it. They were experienced publishers and no one had been more astounded than they at the success which had followed. To them it had been in truth a miracle. They never got over it, and every letter they wrote him reflected their reverent awe of that first mysterious happening. They did not attempt to explain it. There was no explaining it. It had happened. In the face of all experience to the contrary, it had happened.

Still, he couldn’t shake the sense that Brissenden had been correct all along. The triumph belonged less to his own writing than to “The Shame of the Sun.” His work had merely tagged along. Magazine after magazine had turned it down. It was the publication of “The Shame of the Sun” that ignited a firestorm and set off the avalanche in his favor. Without that piece, there would have been no avalanche; without the inexplicable magic of its release, no avalanche either. Singletree, Darnley & Co. could vouch for that magic. They had printed a cautious first run of fifteen hundred copies, doubtful they’d ever sell them. Seasoned publishers, they were more stunned than anyone by the ensuing success. To them it was a genuine marvel. They never fully recovered from the shock, and every letter they sent him quivered with reverent wonder at that first uncanny event. They offered no explanation. There was none to give. It had simply occurred. Against every known precedent, it had occurred.

He had been a lad of whom something was expected. Beyond this all had been chaos. That he would be successful in an original way, or that he would go to the dogs in an original way, seemed equally probable. The only absolute certainty about him was that he would not stand still in the circumstances amid which he was born.

He was a young man from whom much was anticipated, yet outside of that singular promise, everything remained a muddle. It appeared just as likely that he would achieve success through some unconventional path as it was that he would ruin himself in an equally singular way. The one undeniable truth was that he could never stay stagnant within the station of his birth.

Monseigneur could swallow a great many things with ease, and was by some few sullen minds supposed to be rather rapidly swallowing France; but, his morning’s chocolate could not so much as get into the throat of Monseigneur, without the aid of four strong men besides the Cook.

He remained sequestered in his private suite, a sanctum sanctorum to the fawning masses gathered in the adjoining rooms. Monseigneur was about to consume his chocolate. While he was a man who could swallow much with little effort—and was even rumored by a few bitter critics to be busily devouring the wealth of France—his morning beverage could not reach his throat without the assistance of four robust attendants working alongside the Chef.

Springing to her accustomed perpendicular like a bowed sapling, and satisfying herself that nobody was in sight, she seated herself in the manner demanded by the saddle, though hardly expected of the woman, and trotted off in the direction of Tewnell Mill.

The rider appeared perfectly at ease on any part of her horse’s back. Once the plantation was behind her and there was no longer a need for her strange posture, she transitioned into a much more sensible stance. She lacked a side-saddle, and it was obvious that she could never maintain a secure grip on the polished leather by sitting sideways. Straightening up like a resilient young tree and ensuring she was unobserved, she took her seat astride the animal as the saddle required, despite the social impropriety, and trotted away toward Tewnell Mill.

If he had had as many conveniences as he lacked, we should have been plenty comfortable enough; even as it was, we made out very well, in his queer old castle, by the help of comforts and luxuries from the ship.

The medical experts advised that for the girl to regain her vigor, we had to get her away from home. Sea air was considered essential. Thus, we commissioned a warship and a staff of two hundred and sixty, spending a fortnight at sea before landing on the French shore. The doctors recommended we remain there for a while. The local ruler offered us his guest quarters, an invitation we accepted. If his home had been half as well-equipped as it was deficient, we would have been perfectly content; as it happened, we fared well enough in his strange, ancient castle by relying on the supplies and luxuries we brought from our vessel.

Thus has a week passed away, while I have listened to the strangest tale that ever imagination formed. My thoughts and every feeling of my soul have been drunk up by the interest for my guest which this tale and his own elevated and gentle manners have created. I wish to soothe him, yet can I counsel one so infinitely miserable, so destitute of every hope of consolation, to live? Oh, no! The only joy that he can now know will be when he composes his shattered spirit to peace and death. Yet he enjoys one comfort, the offspring of solitude and delirium; he believes that when in dreams he holds converse with his friends and derives from that communion consolation for his miseries or excitements to his vengeance, that they are not the creations of his fancy, but the beings themselves who visit him from the regions of a remote world. This faith gives a solemnity to his reveries that render them to me almost as imposing and interesting as truth.

A full week has slipped by as I have absorbed the most extraordinary story ever spun by mortal fancy. My mind and every pulse of my soul have been consumed by the fascination for my visitor which his narrative and his own lofty, tender bearing have awakened. I long to comfort him, yet dare I urge a being so fathomlessly wretched, so stripped of any hope of solace, to endure? Ah, no! The sole happiness left to him now will arrive when he lays his fractured spirit to rest in stillness and oblivion. Still, he possesses one strange consolation, born of solitude and fevered vision; he trusts that when in sleep he converses with his companions and draws from that fellowship either relief for his sorrows or fuel for his revenge, they are no phantoms of his own invention, but the very souls who visit him from the borders of some distant sphere. This conviction lends his trances a gravity that makes them, to my perception, nearly as majestic and compelling as reality.

About noon next day, when the Dodger and Master Bates had gone out to pursue their customary avocations, Mr. Fagin took the opportunity of reading Oliver a long lecture on the crying sin of ingratitude; of which he clearly demonstrated he had been guilty, to no ordinary extent, in wilfully absenting himself from the society of his anxious friends; and, still more, in endeavouring to escape from them after so much trouble and expense had been incurred in his recovery. Mr. Fagin laid great stress on the fact of his having taken Oliver in, and cherished him, when, without his timely aid, he might have perished with hunger; and he related the dismal and affecting history of a young lad whom, in his philanthropy, he had succoured under parallel circumstances, but who, proving unworthy of his confidence and evincing a desire to communicate with the police, had unfortunately come to be hanged at the Old Bailey one morning. Mr. Fagin did not seek to conceal his share in the catastrophe, but lamented with tears in his eyes that the wrong-headed and treacherous behaviour of the young person in question, had rendered it necessary that he should become the victim of certain evidence for the crown: which, if it were not precisely true, was indispensably necessary for the safety of him (Mr. Fagin) and a few select friends. Mr. Fagin concluded by drawing a rather disagreeable picture of the discomforts of hanging; and, with great friendliness and politeness of manner, expressed his anxious hopes that he might never be obliged to submit Oliver Twist to that unpleasant operation.

Around noon the following day, once the Dodger and Master Bates had departed to attend to their usual trades, Mr. Fagin seized the chance to deliver Oliver a lengthy sermon on the grievous vice of ingratitude. He made it abundantly plain that the boy had been guilty of no small measure of it—first by deliberately withdrawing from the company of his solicitous friends, and worse, by attempting to flee them after they had gone to such pains and expense to reclaim him. Fagin placed particular emphasis on the fact that he had taken Oliver in and cared for him when, without that timely intervention, the child might well have starved; and he recounted the pitiful, moving tale of another young lad whom, out of pure benevolence, he had aided in nearly identical straits, but who—proving unworthy of his trust and showing a regrettable inclination to parley with the police—had unhappily ended up hanged at the Old Bailey one morning. Fagin made no effort to disguise his own part in that catastrophe, yet lamented, with tears standing in his eyes, that the perverse and treacherous conduct of the youth in question had forced the necessity of his becoming the subject of certain Crown evidence—which, if not strictly accurate, was absolutely essential to the security of himself (Mr. Fagin) and a small circle of select companions. He closed by painting a rather disagreeable portrait of the unpleasantness of hanging, and with great cordiality and courteousness of manner expressed his fervent hope that he might never be obliged to subject Oliver Twist to that disagreeable procedure.

He recalled her conduct towards him throughout the last few weeks, when they had often been working together in the garden, just as they had formerly done when they were boy and girl under his mother’s eye. What if her interest in him were not so entirely that of a relative as it had formerly been? To Yeobright any possibility of this sort was a serious matter; and he almost felt troubled at the thought of it. Every pulse of loverlike feeling which had not been stilled during Eustacia’s lifetime had gone into the grave with her. His passion for her had occurred too far on in his manhood to leave fuel enough on hand for another fire of that sort, as may happen with more boyish loves. Even supposing him capable of loving again, that love would be a plant of slow and laboured growth, and in the end only small and sickly, like an autumn-hatched bird.

He thought back to her manner with him over the past several weeks, when they had often laboured side by side in the garden, just as they used to do as boy and girl watched over by his mother. What if her care for him were no longer merely that of a kinswoman, as it had once been? To Yeobright any hint of such a shift was a grave affair, and the notion almost unsettled him. Every pulse of romantic feeling that had not been extinguished while Eustacia lived had been buried with her. His love for her had struck too late in his manhood to leave behind enough tinder for another blaze of that kind, as can occur with younger, more boyish affections. Even if he were capable of loving again, that love would be a slow and toilsome growth, stunted and sickly in the end, like a bird hatched in autumn.

Mr. Britling, from his praise of the enterprise that had at last brought a car he could drive within his reach, went on to that favourite topic of all intelligent Englishmen, the adverse criticism of things British. He pointed out that the central position of the brake and gear levers in his automobile made it extremely easy for the American manufacturer to turn it out either as a left-handed or a right-handed car, and so adapt it either to the Continental or to the British rule of the road. No English cars were so adaptable. We British suffered much from our insular rule of the road, just as we suffered much from our insular weights and measures. But we took a perverse pride in such disadvantages. The irruption of American cars into England was a recent phenomenon, it was another triumph for the tremendous organising ability of the American mind. They were doing with the automobile what they had done with clocks and watches and rifles, they had standardised and machined wholesale, while the British were still making the things one by one. It was an extraordinary thing that England, which was the originator of the industrial system and the original developer of the division of labour, should have so fallen away from systematic manufacturing. He believed this was largely due to the influence of Oxford and the Established Church....

Having finished his eulogy for the mechanical foresight that had finally put a motor within his grasp, Mr. Sterling drifted toward that most cherished preoccupation of the educated Briton: the systematic condemnation of his own country. He observed that the central arrangement of the levers allowed the Yankee firm to assemble the machine for either side of the highway with effortless indifference, catering with equal ease to the habits of the Continent or the eccentricities of the Isles. No home-grown carriage offered such flexibility. The English public, he argued, were victims of their own parochial traffic laws and antiquated measurements, yet they clung to these burdens with a stubborn, almost masochistic, satisfaction. 

The sudden flood of American imports was a modern marvel, another victory for the vast, coordinating intellect of the States. They were applying to the engine the same principles of uniformity they had once imposed upon the clock and the musket, achieving a wholesale precision while the British artisan still labored over individual parts. It remained a profound paradox that the very nation which had birthed the steam age and the specialization of toil should have retreated so far from the path of industrial order. This decline, he maintained, was the inevitable consequence of the classical air of the universities and the stagnant weight of the Anglican pulpit.

Other memories of Heinrich flitted across Mr. Britling's reverie. Heinrich at hockey, running with extreme swiftness and little skill, tricked and baffled by Letty, dodged by Hugh, going headlong forward and headlong back, and then with a cry flinging himself flat on the ground exhausted.... Or again Heinrich very grave and very pink, peering through his glasses at his cards at Skat.... Or Heinrich in the boats upon the great pond, or Heinrich swimming, or Heinrich hiding very, very artfully from the boys about the garden on a theory of his own, or Heinrich in strange postures, stalking the deer in Claverings Park. For a time he had had a great ambition to creep quite close to a deer and _touch_ it.... Or Heinrich indexing. He had a passion for listing and indexing books, music, any loose classifiable thing. His favourite amusement was devising schemes for the indentation of dictionary leaves, so that one could turn instantly to the needed word. He had bought and cut the edges of three dictionaries; each in succession improved upon the other; he had had great hopes of patents and wealth arising therefrom.... And his room had been a source of strange sounds; his search for music upon the violin. He had hoped when he came to Matching's Easy to join "some string quartette." But Matching's Easy produced no string quartette. He had to fall back upon the pianola, and try to play duets with that. Only the pianola did all the duet itself, and in the hands of a small Britling was apt to betray a facetious moodiness; sudden alternations between extreme haste and extreme lassitude....

Other glimpses of Heinrich drifted through Mr. Britling's daydream. Heinrich on the hockey field, darting with tremendous speed and scant control, bewildered and outmaneuvered by Letty, eluded by Hugh, plunging recklessly forward and scrambling backward, then collapsing flat on the turf with a groan of exhaustion.... Or Heinrich, solemn and flushed, squinting through his spectacles at his Skat hand.... Or Heinrich rowing across the wide lake, or Heinrich swimming, or Heinrich concealing himself with elaborate cunning among the garden shrubs from the other boys, following some private theory of invisibility, or Heinrich in contorted poses, creeping after the deer in Claverings Park. For a season he had cherished the ambition to steal near enough to a deer to _lay a hand upon it_.... Or Heinrich compiling. He had a mania for cataloguing and indexing books, scores, any stray items that could be sorted. His chief delight lay in designing systems of thumb-notches for dictionary pages, so that one could flip directly to the required entry. He had acquired and trimmed the margins of three dictionaries, each one an improvement on its predecessor; he had nursed grand visions of patents and fortunes springing from the idea.... And his chamber had been a wellspring of peculiar noises; his quest for melody upon the violin. He had imagined that upon coming to Matching's Easy he would join "some string quartette." But Matching's Easy yielded no string quartette. He was forced to resort to the pianola, and attempt to perform duets with it. Only the pianola insisted on playing the entire duet alone, and under the fingers of a young Britling was prone to exhibit a whimsical capriciousness; abrupt shifts between furious velocity and profound lethargy....

But keep it beneath your hat, Thomas! beneath your hat, I say! A queer instinct, friends, this urge to hide, and no credit in it. And yet there stands the Beatitudes with a roll of the favoured at the start, and certain unassuming souls may find their place there.”

Mr. Van der Pant had been in charge of the dynamos at the Antwerp Power Station, he had been keeping the electrified wires in the entanglements "alive," and he had stuck to his post until the German high explosives had shattered his wires and rendered his dynamos useless. He gave vivid little pictures of the noises of the bombardment, of the dead lying casually in the open spaces, of the failure of the German guns to hit the bridge of boats across which the bulk of the defenders and refugees escaped. He produced a little tourist's map of the city of Antwerp, and dotted at it with a pencil-case. "The--what do you call?--_obus_, ah, shells! fell, so and so and so." Across here he had fled on his _bécane_, and along here and here. He had carried off his rifle, and hid it with the rifles of various other Belgians between floor and ceiling of a house in Zeebrugge. He had found the pirate steamer in the harbour, its captain resolved to extract the uttermost fare out of every refugee he took to London. When they were all aboard and started they found there was no food except the hard ration biscuits of some Belgian soldiers. They had portioned this out like shipwrecked people on a raft.... The _mer_ had been _calme_; thank Heaven! All night they had been pumping. He had helped with the pumps. But Mr. Van der Pant hoped still to get a reckoning with the captain of that ship.

Mr. Van der Pant had overseen the dynamos at the Antwerp Power Station, where he kept the electrified barbed wire entanglements charged right up until German high-explosive shells shredded his wiring and rendered the dynamos completely useless. He shared sharp, vivid little anecdotes about the roar of the bombardment, the dead left splayed uncollected in open public spaces, and the way German gunners repeatedly missed the floating boat bridge that the vast majority of defending troops and fleeing civilians used to escape the city. He pulled out a tiny tourist map of Antwerp and dotted spots across it with a pencil he fished from his carrying case. “The—what do you call it?—*obus*, ah, shells! They fell right here, and here, and all along this stretch.” He’d fled across this section on his *bécane*, he explained, cutting along these side streets and then down this long road. He’d brought his rifle with him, and hid it alongside weapons belonging to a dozen other Belgian men in the gap between the floorboards and ceiling joists of a house in Zeebrugge. He’d stumbled on a shady unregulated steamer in the harbor, whose captain was dead set on squeezing every last possible penny out of every refugee he ferried to London. Once everyone was onboard and the ship had pulled away from the docks, they realized the only food aboard was the hard ration biscuits carried by a handful of Belgian soldiers. They split the small stock evenly between everyone, rationing as carefully as castaways stranded on a life raft. The *mer* had been *calme*, he said, thank Heaven. They’d spent the entire night bailing water out of the leaking hull, and he’d pitched in with the pumping the whole time. Even now, Mr. Van der Pant still hoped he’d get a chance to settle the score with that ship’s captain one day.

Eustacia remained within the bank looking at the fire, intending to go indoors, yet lingering still. Had she not by her situation been inclined to hold in indifference all things honoured of the gods and of men she would probably have come away. But her state was so hopeless that she could play with it. To have lost is less disturbing than to wonder if we may possibly have won; and Eustacia could now, like other people at such a stage, take a standing-point outside herself, observe herself as a disinterested spectator, and think what a sport for Heaven this woman Eustacia was.

Eustacia stayed inside the bank, gazing at the fire and meaning to go back indoors, yet still lingering there. Had her circumstances not already disposed her to regard with indifference everything revered by gods and men, she would likely have left at once. But her condition was so utterly desperate that she could almost toy with it. Loss is less troubling than the question of whether we may perhaps have won after all; and now Eustacia, like others in such a state, could stand apart from herself, watch herself as though she were a detached observer, and wonder what a jest she made for Heaven, this woman Eustacia.

Wildeve kept his eyes fixed on the earth near Venn’s boots until he let his gaze climb slowly up Diggory’s figure, bathed in the candle’s glow, to rest upon his features. “Ha! Our conversations are not always confined to his own history and misfortunes. On every point of general literature he displays unbounded knowledge and a quick and piercing apprehension. His eloquence is forcible and touching; nor can I hear him, when he relates a pathetic incident or endeavours to move the passions of pity or love, without tears. What a glorious creature must he have been in the days of his prosperity, when he is thus noble and godlike in ruin! He seems to feel his own worth and the greatness of his fall.

We don’t always limit our conversations to his personal history and the misfortunes he’s endured. On every subject within general literature, he showcases a staggering breadth of knowledge and a sharp, perceptive grasp of nuance. He speaks with such compelling, heartfelt eloquence that I never manage to hold back tears when he recounts a devastating story, or tries to rouse feelings of pity or tenderness in those listening. If he carries himself with this much dignity and almost divine grace even in ruin, I can only fathom how extraordinary he must have been in the height of his success. He seems fully conscious of both his own inherent worth, and the sheer magnitude of how far he has fallen.
