His wilful hands and feet began to beat and churn about, spasmodically and feebly. But he had fooled them and the will to live that made them beat and churn. He was too deep down. They could never bring him to the surface. He seemed floating languidly in a sea of dreamy vision. Colors and radiances surrounded him and bathed him and pervaded him. What was that? It seemed a lighthouse; but it was inside his brain—a flashing, bright white light. It flashed swifter and swifter. There was a long rumble of sound, and it seemed to him that he was falling down a vast and interminable stairway. And somewhere at the bottom he fell into darkness. That much he knew. He had fallen into darkness. And at the instant he knew, he ceased to know.

His rebellious limbs thrashed weakly, twitching in erratic spasms. Yet he had outwitted them, and the stubborn life force that drove their flailing. He had sunk too far. They could never haul him back up. He drifted as if suspended in a drowsy sea of hallucinatory light. Hues and luminous glows wrapped around him, soaked through him, saturated his being. What was that? It looked like a lighthouse, but it pulsed inside his skull—a flickering, brilliant white beam. It blinked faster and faster. A long, rolling thunder of noise swelled, and he felt himself tumbling down an immense, never-ending staircase. And somewhere at its base he plunged into blackness. That much he grasped. He had plunged into blackness. And the moment he grasped it, he grasped nothing at all.

“Well, somebody has—and look here, neighbours,” Gabriel, though one of the quietest and most gentle men on earth, rose to the occasion, with martial promptness and vigour. “That’s my fist.” Here he placed his fist, rather smaller in size than a common loaf, in the mathematical centre of the maltster’s little table, and with it gave a bump or two thereon, as if to ensure that their eyes all thoroughly took in the idea of fistiness before he went further. “Now—the first man in the parish that I hear prophesying bad of our mistress, why” (here the fist was raised and let fall as Thor might have done with his hammer in assaying it)—“he’ll smell and taste that—or I’m a Dutchman.”

“Well, someone definitely did—and listen up, neighbors,” Gabriel, who was normally one of the quietest, gentlest men you’d ever meet, rose to the occasion with all the brisk, unyielding fire of a soldier jumping into action. “This right here is my fist.” He set his knuckles down, only slightly smaller than an average loaf of bread, exactly dead center of the maltster’s small table, rapping it twice against the worn wood as if to make sure every pair of eyes in the room got the full picture of exactly what that fist could do before he kept speaking. “Now—the first man in this parish I hear badmouthing our mistress, well” —he lifted the fist and slammed it back down the way Thor might have tested the weight of his hammer—“he’s gonna taste and feel every bit of it, and that’s a promise. If I don’t follow through, you can call me a liar.”

The very next day Yeobright went to Humphrey’s cottage, and borrowed of him leggings, gloves, a whetstone, and a hook, to use till he should be able to purchase some for himself. Then he sallied forth with his new fellow-labourer and old acquaintance, and selecting a spot where the furze grew thickest he struck the first blow in his adopted calling. His sight, like the wings in Rasselas, though useless to him for his grand purpose, sufficed for this strait, and he found that when a little practice should have hardened his palms against blistering he would be able to work with ease.

The morning after, Yeobright made his way to Humphrey’s cottage and asked to borrow leggings, gloves, a whetstone, and a hook until he could buy his own. Thus equipped, he set out with his new workmate—who was also an old friend—and chose a patch where the furze stood thickest, driving the first stroke of his new trade. His vision, like the wings in Rasselas, though worthless for the great design he had once cherished, proved sufficient for this narrow path, and he sensed that once a little practice had toughened his palms against blisters, the labour would come easily.

Most typical picture of all would be Mr. Britling writing in a little circle of orange lamplight, with the blinds of his room open for the sake of the moonlight, but the window shut to keep out the moths that beat against it. Outside would be the moon and the high summer sky and the old church tower dim above the black trees half a mile away, with its clock--which Mr. Britling heard at night but never noted by day--beating its way round the slow semicircle of the nocturnal hours. He had always hated conflict and destruction, and felt that war between civilised states was the quintessential expression of human failure, it was a stupidity that stopped progress and all the free variation of humanity, a thousand times he had declared it impossible, but even now with his country fighting he was still far from realising that this was a thing that could possibly touch him more than intellectually. He did not really believe with his eyes and finger-tips and backbone that murder, destruction, and agony on a scale monstrous beyond precedent was going on in the same world as that which slumbered outside the black ivy and silver shining window-sill that framed his peaceful view.

The most iconic image of all was of Mr. Hale bent over his pages in the small warm circle of amber lamp glow, the room’s blinds drawn up to let in the silver of the moon, but the casement latched tight to bar the moths that battered softly at its panes. Beyond the glass stretched the moon-swept expanse of high summer sky, the weathered stone church tower half a mile off blurring faint above the crowns of shadowed oaks, its clock—whose chimes Mr. Hale registered automatically in the small hours but never so much as glanced at in daylight—ticking its steady path through the long, slow arc of the nocturnal hours. He had always loathed strife and ruin, convinced that war between modern, civilized nations was the purest distillation of human folly: a senseless blight that choked progress and every unfettered, idiosyncratic expression of human life, and he had insisted a thousand times over that such a catastrophe was impossible. Even now, with his own nation locked in combat, he could barely bring himself to accept that this crisis could ever reach him beyond the realm of abstract thought. He did not truly believe, not in the marrow of his bones, not in the press of his fingertips against the page or the hush of the room around him, that murder, ruin, and suffering of a scale so monstrous it had no parallel was unfolding in the very same world that lay sleeping outside, framed by the glossy dark ivy and the moon-glazed window ledge that bordered his quiet, unshaken view.

London!—that great place!—nobody—not even Mr. Bumble—could ever find him there! He had often heard the old men in the workhouse, too, say that no lad of spirit need want in London; and that there were ways of living in that vast city, which those who had been bred up in country parts had no idea of. It was the very place for a homeless boy, who must die in the streets unless some one helped him. As these things passed through his thoughts, he jumped upon his feet, and again walked forward.

London! That sprawling, endless city! No one could ever track him down there, not even Mr. Bumble himself. He had often heard the elderly men in the workhouse say, too, that no plucky lad need ever go hungry in London, that the vast metropolis held all sorts of ways to get by that people raised in the countryside could never even fathom. It was exactly the place for a homeless boy, one who would surely die out on the roads if no one stopped to help him. As these thoughts ran through his mind, he leaped to his feet and kept moving forward.

He came closer and said, “I must leave you now.”

At length, when nine o’clock had come, and they began to think they were to hear no more that night, Mr. Losberne and Mr. Grimwig entered the room, followed by Mr. Brownlow and a man whom Oliver almost shrieked with surprise to see; for they told him it was his brother, and it was the same man he had met at the market-town, and seen looking in with Fagin at the window of his little room. Monks cast a look of hate, which, even then, he could not dissemble, at the astonished boy, and sat down near the door. Mr. Brownlow, who had papers in his hand, walked to a table near which Rose and Oliver were seated.

Finally, when nine o’clock arrived and they had begun to assume the night's business was over, Mr. Losberne and Mr. Grimwig entered the room. They were followed by Mr. Brownlow and a man whose appearance made Oliver nearly cry out in shock. They informed him that this was his brother—the same individual he had encountered in the market town and later seen peering through his bedroom window with Fagin. Monks directed a look of pure, undisguised malice at the stunned boy before taking a seat near the door. Mr. Brownlow, clutching a handful of documents, walked over to a table where Rose and Oliver were sitting.

If any one had told him he was going to be stunned for five or six minutes, cut about the brow and face and have a bone in his wrist put out, and that as a consequence he would find himself pleased and exhilarated, he would have treated the prophecy with ridicule; but here he was lying stiffly on his back with his wrist bandaged to his side and smiling into the darkness even more brightly than he had smiled at the Essex landscape two days before. The fact is pain hurts or irritates, but in itself it does not make a healthily constituted man miserable. The expectation of pain, the certainty of injury may make one hopeless enough, the reality rouses our resistance. Nobody wants a broken bone or a delicate wrist, but very few people are very much depressed by getting one. People can be much more depressed by smoking a hundred cigarettes in three days or losing one per cent. of their capital.

Had someone prophesied that he would shortly be dazed for several minutes, slashed across the forehead and cheeks, suffer a dislocated wrist-bone, and emerge from the ordeal oddly content and buoyant, he would have scoffed at the forecast. Yet there he was, stretched rigidly upon his back, his injured wrist strapped tightly against his ribs, beaming into the blackness with a radiance keener than the smile he had bestowed upon the Essex countryside two mornings earlier. Pain, in truth, stings or vexes, but by itself it does not render a soundly built man wretched. The dread of agony, the certitude of harm may breed despair; the actual event summons our defiance. No one seeks a fractured bone or a fragile joint, yet scarcely anyone sinks into gloom upon acquiring one. A man can be far more dejected by smoking a hundred cigarettes over three days or by forfeiting one per cent of his fortune.

They exchanged very few words along the way. Oliver was caught in a flurry of restlessness and unresolvable doubt that left him unable to collect his thoughts, barely even able to speak at all, and that same frayed unease seemed to weigh just as heavily on his two female traveling companions, if not more. Mr. Brownlow had already carefully walked all three of them through the full substance of the confessions Monks had been pressured into making, and while they understood their present journey was meant to see through the final steps of the work that had already been so successfully launched, the whole affair was still shrouded in enough ambiguity and secrecy to leave them caught in a state of nearly unbearable, taut suspense.

The birds sang in more cheerful notes, and the leaves began to bud forth on the trees. Happy, happy earth! Fit habitation for gods, which, so short a time before, was bleak, damp, and unwholesome. My spirits were elevated by the enchanting appearance of nature; the past was blotted from my memory, the present was tranquil, and the future gilded by bright rays of hope and anticipations of joy.”

The gentle rains and tender warmth of spring transformed the face of the land. Figures who had earlier seemed buried in shadowy hollows now emerged and scattered across the fields, devoting themselves to the various tasks of cultivation. The birds lifted their voices in lighter, gladder strains, and fresh leaves began to unfurl upon the branches. Blessed, blessed earth! A dwelling worthy of gods, where only a brief span before all had been raw, sodden, and sickly. My heart rose with the enchanting beauty of the world around me; the past was erased from my mind, the present lay serene, and the future gleamed with bright shafts of hope and the promise of joy.

Troy was full of activity, but his activities were less of a locomotive than a vegetative nature; and, never being based upon any original choice of foundation or direction, they were exercised on whatever object chance might place in their way. Hence, whilst he sometimes reached the brilliant in speech because that was spontaneous, he fell below the commonplace in action, from inability to guide incipient effort. He had a quick comprehension and considerable force of character; but, being without the power to combine them, the comprehension became engaged with trivialities whilst waiting for the will to direct it, and the force wasted itself in useless grooves through unheeding the comprehension.

Troy was always on the move, but his constant activity was less purposeful than a steam engine and more like the unplanned spread of a climbing plant; he never chose a clear core aim or direction for his efforts, so he simply poured energy into whatever random thing chance dropped in front of him. That’s why, while he could occasionally turn out brilliantly sharp, off-the-cuff lines when speaking, his actions were far worse than unremarkable—he simply couldn’t guide the first flickers of effort into anything coherent. He had a quick, perceptive mind and no shortage of inherent force of character, but he lacked the ability to make those two strengths work in tandem. His sharp perception would fixate on meaningless small details while it waited for his will to give it a clear goal, and all that inherent grit burned away on pointless, repetitive dead ends because he never let his good judgment steer it.

He caught a Telegraph Avenue car that was going to Berkeley. It was crowded with youths and young men who were singing songs and ever and again barking out college yells. He studied them curiously. They were university boys. They went to the same university that she did, were in her class socially, could know her, could see her every day if they wanted to. He wondered that they did not want to, that they had been out having a good time instead of being with her that evening, talking with her, sitting around her in a worshipful and adoring circle. His thoughts wandered on. He noticed one with narrow-slitted eyes and a loose-lipped mouth. That fellow was vicious, he decided. On shipboard he would be a sneak, a whiner, a tattler. He, Martin Eden, was a better man than that fellow. The thought cheered him. It seemed to draw him nearer to Her. He began comparing himself with the students. He grew conscious of the muscled mechanism of his body and felt confident that he was physically their master. But their heads were filled with knowledge that enabled them to talk her talk,—the thought depressed him. But what was a brain for? he demanded passionately. What they had done, he could do. They had been studying about life from the books while he had been busy living life. His brain was just as full of knowledge as theirs, though it was a different kind of knowledge. How many of them could tie a lanyard knot, or take a wheel or a lookout? His life spread out before him in a series of pictures of danger and daring, hardship and toil. He remembered his failures and scrapes in the process of learning. He was that much to the good, anyway. Later on they would have to begin living life and going through the mill as he had gone. Very well. While they were busy with that, he could be learning the other side of life from the books.

He boarded a Shattuck Avenue tram bound for the Berkeley hills. The car brimmed with young men and boys, all of them chanting college anthems and now and then loosing sharp fraternity cries. He watched them with a detached, searching gaze. They were students of the university—her university. They moved in her social sphere, shared her classrooms, might speak with her, might glimpse her any ordinary day if the whim took them. It puzzled him that the whim did not take them, that they had chosen an evening’s carousal instead of her company, her conversation, the chance to gather round her in reverent, devoted attention. His mind drifted. He marked one with thin-slitted eyes and a mouth too slack at the corners. That one, he judged, was mean-hearted. On a ship he would prove a skulker, a complainer, a tale-bearer. He, Martin Eden, stood above that breed. The thought braced him. It seemed to lessen the distance between himself and Her. He fell to measuring his own worth against the students. He became aware of his body’s corded machinery and felt a sure pride that he could master any of them in physical things. Yet their heads held the learning that let them speak her language—the reflection weighed him down. But what, after all, was a brain meant for? he asked with sudden heat. Whatever they had done, he could do. They had been pulling life from printed pages while he had been wrestling life itself. His mind stored just as much knowledge as theirs, only of another grain. How many of them could whip a lanyard knot, or stand a wheel, or keep a lookout watch? His past unrolled before him like a moving panorama of risk and mettle, privation and labor. He recalled his stumbles and narrow escapes while he learned. That much, at least, he had gained. In time they would have to start living life and grinding through the mill as he had ground. So be it. While they were taken up with that, he could be mastering the other side of life from books.

By degrees, he grew more calm, and besought, in a low and broken voice, that he might be rescued from his present dangers; and that if any aid were to be raised up for a poor outcast boy who had never known the love of friends or kindred, it might come to him now, when, desolate and deserted, he stood alone in the midst of wickedness and guilt.

Overwhelmed by a sudden surge of panic, the boy snapped the book shut and shoved it aside. Falling to his knees, he begged the heavens to keep him from such atrocities, pleading for a quick death rather than a future destined for such grisly and horrific acts. Slowly, his agitation subsided, and in a hushed, trembling voice, he prayed for deliverance from his current peril. He implored that if any mercy remained for a lonely orphan who had never known the love of kin or companions, it might reach him now, as he stood forsaken and isolated in the heart of such pervasive evil and guilt.

Again she looked. All the centuries of woman since sex began were eloquent in her eyes. And he measured her in a careless way, and knew, bold now, that she would begin to retreat, coyly and delicately, as he pursued, ever ready to reverse the game should he turn fainthearted. And, too, he was human, and could feel the draw of her, while his ego could not but appreciate the flattery of her kindness. Oh, he knew it all, and knew them well, from A to Z. Good, as goodness might be measured in their particular class, hard-working for meagre wages and scorning the sale of self for easier ways, nervously desirous for some small pinch of happiness in the desert of existence, and facing a future that was a gamble between the ugliness of unending toil and the black pit of more terrible wretchedness, the way whereto being briefer though better paid.

Again she looked. In her eyes were all the centuries of womanhood since sex first began to stir. And he measured her with a kind of careless confidence, knowing now, boldly, that as he advanced she would start to retreat, shyly and delicately, always ready to turn the game about again should he grow weak. Yet he was human too, and could not help feeling her attraction, while his vanity could not but relish the tribute of her kindness. Oh, he understood it all, knew them thoroughly from A to Z. Decent, as decency went among people of their sort; laboring hard for small wages and scorning to sell themselves for easier gain; anxiously hungry for some little scrap of happiness in the desert of life, and staring toward a future that was a gamble between the ugliness of endless drudgery and the black abyss of a more dreadful misery, the road to which was shorter, though better paid.

Too busily occupied with practical realities to follow the thought of the age through all its divagations and into all its recesses, these Massachusetts business men had had to consider methods of access more quintessential and nuclear. And they had decided not to hunt out the best thought in its merely germinating stages, but to wait until it had emerged and flowered to some trustworthy recognition, and then, rather than toil through recondite and possibly already reconsidered books and writings generally, to offer an impressive fee to the emerged new thinker, and to invite him to come to them and to lecture to them and to have a conference with them, and to tell them simply, competently and completely at first hand just all that he was about. To come, in fact, and be himself--in a highly concentrated form. In this way a number of interesting Europeans had been given very pleasant excursions to America, and the society had been able to form very definite opinions upon their teaching. And Mr. Britling was one of the representative thinkers upon which this society had decided to inform itself. It was to broach this invitation and to offer him the impressive honorarium by which the society honoured not only its guests but itself, that Mr. Direck had now come to Matching's Easy. He had already sent Mr. Britling a letter of introduction, not indeed intimating his precise purpose, but mentioning merely a desire to know him, and the letter had been so happily phrased and its writer had left such a memory of pleasant hospitality on Mr. Britling's mind during Mr. Britling's former visit to New York, that it had immediately produced for Mr. Direck an invitation not merely to come and see him but to come and stay over the week-end.

Too wrapped up in the pressing practical realities of their work to track every meandering trend and obscure niche of the era’s intellectual thought, these Massachusetts businessmen had settled on a far more streamlined, direct approach. Rather than seek out promising ideas in their half-formed, germinal stages, they waited until a thinker had risen to reliable, widespread acclaim for their fully realized work. Then, instead of slogging through dense, often recondite texts that might already have been revised or walked back by their author, they offered a generous speaking fee to the established thinker, inviting him to visit, deliver lectures, hold informal discussions, and share the full scope of his work plainly, expertly, and unfiltered straight from the source. The request was, in essence, for him to bring his full self and ideas in a tight, concentrated form. This arrangement had already sponsored pleasant, all-expenses-paid trips to America for a number of notable European thinkers, while allowing the society to form clear, first-hand judgments of each of their teachings. Mr. Britling was one of the prominent thinkers the society had decided they wanted to learn more about. It was to extend this invitation, along with the substantial honorarium the society offered both as a courtesy to its guests and a mark of its own prestige, that Mr. Direck had now traveled to Matching’s Easy. He had already sent Mr. Britling a letter of introduction, which did not lay out his exact errand, only noting that he hoped to make his acquaintance. The note had been so warmly phrased, and Mr. Britling still held such fond memories of Mr. Direck’s gracious hospitality during his own prior trip to New York, that Mr. Direck received not just an invitation to call, but an offer to stay for the entire weekend immediately upon the letter’s arrival.

A little emblem of true love—a little ring that symbolised unending and unmercenary affection—was the cause of our Richard finding his happiness. He dropped it in the street, and got out to recover it. And before they could continue the blockade occurred. He spoke to his love and won her there while the cab was hemmed in. Money is dross compared with true love, Anthony.”

And oh, brother Elias, never again boast of how all-conquering money is. A tiny token of genuine devotion—a thin little ring that symbolized endless, unmercenary affection—was the very reason our Jude found his lasting happiness. He dropped it onto the cobblestones mid-ride, and climbed out of the cab to retrieve it. Before they could nudge back into the street’s flow, the gridlock settled in. Not that, all this time, his mind was, for an instant, free from one oppressive overwhelming sense of the grave that opened at his feet; it was ever present to him, but in a vague and general way, and he could not fix his thoughts upon it. Thus, even while he trembled, and turned burning hot at the idea of speedy death, he fell to counting the iron spikes before him, and wondering how the head of one had been broken off, and whether they would mend it, or leave it as it was. Then, he thought of all the horrors of the gallows and the scaffold—and stopped to watch a man sprinkling the floor to cool it—and then went on to think again.

It wasn’t as if, the entire time, his mind ever stopped being hounded by the heavy, suffocating awareness of an open grave waiting right at his feet. That dread hung over him constantly, but it felt fuzzy and unfocused, like he couldn’t pin his mind down to really grapple with it. So even as he shook all over, his skin prickling with feverish heat at the thought of how soon he would die, he found himself counting the iron spikes set before him, puzzling over how the tip of one had snapped clean off, and if someone would eventually fix it or just leave it broken like that. Next, his mind drifted to every grisly detail of the gallows and the execution scaffold—until he paused to stare at a man sprinkling water across the floor to bring down the temperature—before his wandering thoughts pulled him right back to his grim ruminations.

“As the night advanced, a fierce wind arose from the woods and quickly dispersed the clouds that had loitered in the heavens; the blast tore along like a mighty avalanche and produced a kind of insanity in my spirits that burst all bounds of reason and reflection. I lighted the dry branch of a tree and danced with fury around the devoted cottage, my eyes still fixed on the western horizon, the edge of which the moon nearly touched. A part of its orb was at length hid, and I waved my brand; it sank, and with a loud scream I fired the straw, and heath, and bushes, which I had collected. The wind fanned the fire, and the cottage was quickly enveloped by the flames, which clung to it and licked it with their forked and destroying tongues.

As the night wore on, a brutal, howling wind gusted up from the deep woods, scattering the slow-loitering clouds that had hung heavy across the sky in seconds. The gale tore through the air like a crashing avalanche, tipping my mind into a rabid frenzy that shattered every last boundary of reason and careful thought. I lit a dry, gnarled tree branch and danced in a wild rage around the cottage I’d marked for destruction, my eyes locked tight to the western horizon where the moon hovered just a hair’s breadth above the edge. When the first sliver of its disc dipped out of view, I swung my burning torch high; the moment it sank fully out of sight, I let out a deafening, ragged scream and touched the flame to the pile of straw, heath, and dry brush I’d stashed beforehand. The wind fanned the fire to a roar, and the cottage was quickly wrapped in roiling flames, which coiled tight to its walls and lapped at every inch of it with their sharp, forked, ravenous tongues.

Through my father’s efforts, a portion of Elizabeth’s inheritance had been reclaimed for her by the Austrian government. A modest estate on the shores of Como belonged to her. That declaration, however, was scarcely justified by what followed. Once more the instruments brought the air to its close; once more they struck up again with all the same ardor and emotion, as though it were only their opening phrase. It was now that sort of tune with no discernible first act, middle, or finish, which, among all the dances that crowd a gifted fiddler’s imagination, perhaps most nearly suggests the endless—the much-vaunted “Old Nick’s Reverie.” The wildness of bodily motion awakened by the wildness of the music could be faintly divined by those observers in the moonlight from the rare thuds of toe and heel against the door, whenever the circling had grown faster than usual.

That Oliver Twist was moved to resignation by the example of these good people, I cannot, although I am his biographer, undertake to affirm with any degree of confidence; but I can most distinctly say, that for many months he continued meekly to submit to the domination and ill-treatment of Noah Claypole: who used him far worse than before, now that his jealousy was roused by seeing the new boy promoted to the black stick and hat-band, while he, the old one, remained stationary in the muffin-cap and leathers. Charlotte treated him ill, because Noah did; and Mrs. Sowerberry was his decided enemy, because Mr. Sowerberry was disposed to be his friend; so, between these three on one side, and a glut of funerals on the other, Oliver was not altogether as comfortable as the hungry pig was, when he was shut up, by mistake, in the grain department of a brewery.

As Oliver Twist’s biographer, I can’t claim with any real confidence that the example of these virtuous people prompted Oliver to resign himself to his circumstances; but I can state unequivocally that for months on end he meekly endured the domination and abuse of Noah Claypole. Noah treated him far worse than he had before, his jealousy sparked when he saw the new boy promoted to the black walking stick and official hatband, while he, the longer-serving staff member, was stuck wearing the same old muffin cap and leather work gear he’d had for ages. Charlotte picked on him too, just because Noah did, and Mrs. Sowerberry made no secret of being his sworn enemy, all because Mr. Sowerberry was inclined to be kind to him. Caught between these three ganging up on him on one side, and a constant glut of funerals keeping him overworked on the other, Oliver was quite a bit less comfortable than that hungry pig that once got accidentally locked in a brewery’s grain storage room.

He drew up the other chair. The tea brightened the girl’s eyes and brought back some of her colour. She began to eat with a sort of dainty ferocity like some starved wild animal. She seemed to regard the young man’s presence and the aid he had rendered her as a natural thing—not as though she undervalued the conventions; but as one whose great stress gave her the right to put aside the artificial for the human. But gradually, with the return of strength and comfort, came also a sense of the little conventions that belong; and she began to tell him her little story. It was one of a thousand such as the city yawns at every day—the shop girl’s story of insufficient wages, further reduced by “fines” that go to swell the store’s profits; of time lost through illness; and then of lost positions, lost hope, and—the knock of the adventurer upon the green door.

He pulled the second empty chair across the scuffed linoleum and settled into it. The steaming chamomile tea gilded the young woman’s irises and painted faint rose back into her sallow, hollowed cheeks. She picked at the bread and jam first, then tore into it with a delicate, ravenous hunger, sharp as a starved wren that’s stumbled across a forgotten windowsill feeder. She treated his presence and the kindness he’d extended as something unremarkable, expected even—not that she dismissed ordinary social niceties; but when one has been stretched thin past breaking point, the unspoken rules of politeness fall away to make room for raw, unvarnished need. But as warmth seeped back into her frozen fingertips and the tight knot of hunger in her stomach unclenched, the weight of those small, unwritten social rules crept back in, and she began to haltingly share what had led her there. It was the kind of tale the great gray city swallowed by the dozen each day, no more notable than a raindrop hitting the sidewalk: a sales clerk’s account of wages too meager to cover rent, whittled even thinner by arbitrary “infraction fees” that padded the department store’s annual profits; weeks missed from work after a bout of winter flu that left her too weak to stand; then a pink termination slip, no savings left, no prospects, and finally, the desperate rap of someone with nowhere else to turn on his forest-green apartment door.

Martin had enjoyed the fight, with a recrudescence of the old fighting thrills. But they quickly died away, and he was oppressed by a great sadness. He felt very old—centuries older than those careless, care-free young companions of his others days. He had travelled far, too far to go back. Their mode of life, which had once been his, was now distasteful to him. He was disappointed in it all. He had developed into an alien. As the steam beer had tasted raw, so their companionship seemed raw to him. He was too far removed. Too many thousands of opened books yawned between them and him. He had exiled himself. He had travelled in the vast realm of intellect until he could no longer return home. On the other hand, he was human, and his gregarious need for companionship remained unsatisfied. He had found no new home. As the gang could not understand him, as his own family could not understand him, as the bourgeoisie could not understand him, so this girl beside him, whom he honored high, could not understand him nor the honor he paid her. His sadness was not untouched with bitterness as he thought it over.

Martin relished the conflict, feeling a brief revival of his former combative spirit. However, the sensation faded rapidly, leaving him burdened by a profound melancholy. He felt incredibly old—eons older than the reckless, happy-go-lucky friends of his youth. He had moved too far along his path to ever retreat. Their lifestyle, which he once embraced, now seemed repulsive to him. He felt let down by everything; he had become a total outsider. Just as the local beer tasted harsh, their presence felt unrefined. The distance between them was immense, an abyss created by the countless books he had read. He was an exile of his own making, having ventured so far into the intellectual wilderness that his old home was unreachable. Yet, being human, his natural desire for community remained starving. He hadn't found a new sanctuary. His old friends, his family, and the social elite all failed to understand him, and even the girl at his side—the one he placed on a pedestal—could not grasp his true self or the depth of his respect for her. A touch of bitterness colored his sorrow as he dwelled on his isolation.

When the town clock struck two, she was already leaning against its weathered slats, and when it chimed four, she turned for home with a quiet, resigned slump to her shoulders. On days when the rain stayed light and the wind did not bite hard enough to risk her young daughter Mia falling ill, the girl came with her, kicking at loose gravel along the path as they went; on harsher days she made the trip alone, but she never once missed a single visit.

We’re also issued exactly three pairs of socks each. We work ourselves to the bone day in and day out, and none of us know the first thing about darning. Once the heel of a sock wears through, blisters pop up right away. Bad enough blisters can put a man completely out of action. There are millions of surplus women referenced earlier (see above), yet the government lacks even the basic sense to assign any of them to mend our socks. So a steady portion of us end up lame, unable to work. And so it goes. And so it always goes.

Each new insight only sharpened the truth that I was a miserable exile. The fellows were not bricklayers, but, as in the old days, they attended all Sunday picnics for the dancing, and the fighting, and the fun. Martin drank with them, and began to feel really human once more. He was a fool to have ever left them, he thought; and he was very certain that his sum of happiness would have been greater had he remained with them and let alone the books and the people who sat in the high places. Yet the beer seemed not so good as of yore. It didn’t taste as it used to taste. Brissenden had spoiled him for steam beer, he concluded, and wondered if, after all, the books had spoiled him for companionship with these friends of his youth. He resolved that he would not be so spoiled, and he went on to the dancing pavilion. Jimmy, the plumber, he met there, in the company of a tall, blond girl who promptly forsook him for Martin.

He found himself surrounded by the familiar old crew—faces he’d known for half his life, dotted with empty spots where old companions used to be, and the occasional unfamiliar newcomer. None of them were stonemasons, but just like they’d done back in their younger years, they showed up to every Sunday riverside picnic for the foot-stomping dancing, the scrappy backlot fights, and the rowdy, unplanned joy of it all. Leo drank round after round with them, and for the first time in months, he felt like a real, flesh-and-blood person instead of a ghost haunting library stacks. He’d been an idiot to ever walk away, he thought; he was certain his total share of happiness would have been far greater if he’d stayed right here, never touching the stuffy leather-bound books or simpering at the pretentious wealthy folks who spent their nights in gilded, high-up drawing rooms. Still, the cheap draft beer didn’t hit the way it used to. It was flat, strangely bitter, nothing like the crisp cold pints he remembered from years back. Elias had ruined him for low-grade bar beer, he concluded, and a cold little niggling thought wondered if all those books had ruined him for the company of the friends he’d grown up with. He shook the thought off, determined not to let that kind of distance take root, and wandered over to the open-air dance pavilion. He ran into Mickey the pipefitter there, accompanied by a lanky, ash-blonde girl who immediately left Mickey’s side to make her way straight over to Leo.

While he was closing the little horn door a figure rose from behind a neighbouring bush and came forward into the lantern light. It was the reddleman approaching.

Christian dragged his boots over his feet, and with labored gasps that carried far across the stillness, hauled his frame upright, staggered upright, and stumbled away until the darkness swallowed him. Wildeve began fastening the lantern, intending to go back indoors, for he judged the hour too advanced to set out for Mistover and meet his wife, who was expected to return in the captain’s carriage. As he latched the small horn panel, a shape lifted itself from the shadow of a nearby thicket and stepped into the circle of light. The reddleman was drawing near.

Were they relying as we were relying on Dreadnoughts, or had they their secrets and surprises for us? To-night, perhaps, the great ships were steaming to conflict....

All manner of rumors had reached Mr. Britling concerning the fleets of England, France, and Germany; there had been open quarrels among authorities, and a great deal of hushed conjecture behind closed doors. Britain possessed the weightier hulls and the larger ordnance, yet it was far from certain that she led in science and ingenuity. Were they staking everything on Dreadnoughts as we were, or did they hold hidden stratagems and startling innovations in reserve? He left his two letters with a trusty porter, to be delivered half an hour before midnight, and no sooner; took horse for Dover; and began his journey. “For the love of Heaven, of justice, of generosity, of the honour of your noble name!” was the poor prisoner’s cry with which he strengthened his sinking heart, as he left all that was dear on earth behind him, and floated away for the Loadstone Rock.

A hidden power now pulled him swiftly toward its core, every current and gust aligning with fierce purpose in that single direction. He entrusted his two letters to a reliable porter, with strict orders they be handed over no earlier than half an hour before midnight; then he mounted his horse at Dover and set out. “For the love of Heaven, for justice, for mercy, for the honour of your noble name!”—that desperate prisoner’s plea was what he used to steady his faltering spirit as he abandoned everything precious in this world and drifted toward the Loadstone Rock.

He occupied rooms in the Bank, in his fidelity to the House of which he had grown to be a part, like strong root-ivy. It chanced that they derived a kind of security from the patriotic occupation of the main building, but the true-hearted old gentleman never calculated about that. All such circumstances were indifferent to him, so that he did his duty. On the opposite side of the courtyard, under a colonnade, was extensive standing--for carriages--where, indeed, some carriages of Monseigneur yet stood. Against two of the pillars were fastened two great flaring flambeaux, and in the light of these, standing out in the open air, was a large grindstone: a roughly mounted thing which appeared to have hurriedly been brought there from some neighbouring smithy, or other workshop. Rising and looking out of window at these harmless objects, Mr. Lorry shivered, and retired to his seat by the fire. He had opened, not only the glass window, but the lattice blind outside it, and he had closed both again, and he shivered through his frame.

He lived in rooms at the Bank, clinging to the House that had absorbed him as ivy clings to a wall with deep, tenacious roots. It so happened that his presence in the main building offered a certain patriotic reassurance, but the honest old man never paused to reckon such things. So long as he did his duty, all else was immaterial to him. Across the courtyard, beneath a colonnade, stretched a broad standing for carriages—where, in fact, a few of Monseigneur’s carriages still remained. Two pillars held a pair of blazing flambeaux, and in their glare, exposed to the night air, stood a large grindstone: a rough, hastily mounted thing that looked as though it had been dragged there from some nearby smithy or workshop. Mr. Lorry rose, gazed out at these unremarkable objects, shivered, and withdrew to his seat beside the fire. He had opened not only the glazed window but the lattice blind beyond it, and after closing both again, a tremor ran through his whole frame.

I don’t care for close ways.”

“Even if they were feeling ashamed, I still don’t see why they couldn’t have held the whole thing right here,” said the broad, ample-framed woman, whose stiff stays creaked like well-worn shoes every time she stooped or turned. “It’s a fine thing to gather all the neighbors together and raise a good, lively ruckus now and again; a wedding’s just as fitting an occasion for it as any seasonal holiday, if you ask me. I’ve never had any patience for stuffy, close-mouthed ways of doing things.”

“Our friend was born in this village. This town then included the adjoining towns to the north and south. The region was then more sparsely settled, although many houses standing then have disappeared. While he was sleeping peacefully in the cradle, while he was opening on the world childhood's wide, wondering eyes, those great men whose names are our perpetual benediction were planning for freedom from a foreign yoke. While he was passing through the happy years of early-childhood, the fierce clash of arms resounded through the little strip of territory which then made up the United States. I can hardly realize that, as a child, he heard as a fresh, new, real story, of the deeds of Lexington, from the lips of men then young who had been in the fight, or listened as one of an eager group gathered about the fireside, or in the old, now deserted tavern on the turnpike, to the story of Bunker Hill.

He entered the world in this very village. In those days, the settlement stretched to include the neighboring communities both north and south. The countryside was more thinly populated back then, and many homes that once stood have since vanished. As he lay dreaming in his cradle, as his young eyes first opened wide with childhood’s wonder, the great figures whose names we still bless were already scheming to cast off foreign rule. Throughout the carefree seasons of his early youth, the clangor of battle echoed across the narrow stretch of land that then formed the United States. I can scarcely grasp that, as a boy, he heard the exploits of Lexington told as fresh, immediate news from the lips of men still young who had fought there, or sat among a breathless circle by the hearth—or in the old, now-abandoned roadside inn—listening to the tale of Bunker Hill.

Thus strangely are our souls constructed, and by such slight ligaments are we bound to prosperity or ruin. When I look back, it seems to me as if this almost miraculous change of inclination and will was the immediate suggestion of the guardian angel of my life—the last effort made by the spirit of preservation to avert the storm that was even then hanging in the stars and ready to envelop me. Her victory was announced by an unusual tranquillity and gladness of soul which followed the relinquishing of my ancient and latterly tormenting studies. It was thus that I was to be taught to associate evil with their prosecution, happiness with their disregard.

How strangely our souls are fashioned, and by what frail threads we are tethered to fortune or disaster. Looking back, I feel as though this nearly wondrous shift in desire and resolve came as a direct whisper from the guardian spirit of my existence—a final effort by the preserving force to turn aside the tempest that already loomed in the constellations, poised to swallow me. Its triumph was heralded by a rare stillness and lightness of heart that arrived the moment I abandoned my long-accursed studies. So I was schooled to link misery with their pursuit, and joy with their release.

He could hardly command the spring to flow before such a vast assembly; a throng of spectators was as fatal to a medieval wonder as a skeptic is to a modern séance. The smooth, spotless creature lifted itself free of its fleece—you would have to see the sight to fully grasp how perfectly it mirrored Aphrodite rising from the seafoam—looking startled and timid now that its covering was gone, the garment spread across the floor in a single, soft, unbroken cloud. Only the inner layer of the fleece was visible, a surface that had never been uncovered before, as white as fresh fallen snow, with not even the tiniest speck, scratch, or blemish to be found anywhere.

Bathsheba was far from dreaming that the dark and silent shape upon which she had so carelessly thrown a seed was a hotbed of tropic intensity. Had she known Boldwood’s moods, her blame would have been fearful, and the stain upon her heart ineradicable. Moreover, had she known her present power for good or evil over this man, she would have trembled at her responsibility. Luckily for her present, unluckily for her future tranquillity, her understanding had not yet told her what Boldwood was. Nobody knew entirely; for though it was possible to form guesses concerning his wild capabilities from old floodmarks faintly visible, he had never been seen at the high tides which caused them.

Bathsheba had no idea that the dark, silent figure into which she had so carelessly cast a seed contained a furnace of tropical intensity. Had she understood Boldwood’s moods, her self-reproach would have been dreadful, and the stain upon her conscience impossible to erase. And had she realized the power she now held, for good or for ill, over this man, she would have shuddered at the weight of such responsibility. Fortunately for her at that moment, though disastrously for her later peace, she had not yet grasped what Boldwood truly was. No one understood him completely; although one could infer something of his wild possibilities from the old floodmarks still faintly visible, he had never been seen in the high tides that had made them.

This was enough to re-establish him in his original opinion. He knew now that he was right, and that Troy was wrong. Every voice in nature was unanimous in bespeaking change. But two distinct translations attached to these dumb expressions. Apparently there was to be a thunder-storm, and afterwards a cold continuous rain. The creeping things seemed to know all about the later rain, but little of the interpolated thunder-storm; whilst the sheep knew all about the thunder-storm and nothing of the later rain.

That was all it took to snap him back to the convictions he’d held from the start. He knew now he was right, and Troy was wrong. Every small signal from the natural world aligned to confirm a shift was on its way. But these wordless, quiet cues carried two very separate interpretations. It was clear a thunderstorm would roll through first, followed by an unbroken stretch of cold, steady rain. The small crawling creatures seemed to sense every detail of the coming rain, but had no idea the thunderstorm would strike first; the sheep, by contrast, knew full well the thunderstorm was imminent, but had no notion of the lingering cold rain that would come after it.

Since he lived six times as many working-days as Sundays, Oak’s appearance in his old clothes was most peculiarly his own—the mental picture formed by his neighbours in imagining him being always dressed in that way. He wore a low-crowned felt hat, spread out at the base by tight jamming upon the head for security in high winds, and a coat like Dr. Johnson’s; his lower extremities being encased in ordinary leather leggings and boots emphatically large, affording to each foot a roomy apartment so constructed that any wearer might stand in a river all day long and know nothing of damp—their maker being a conscientious man who endeavoured to compensate for any weakness in his cut by unstinted dimension and solidity.

Because Oak spent six times as many days laboring as he did resting, his working attire became his truest signature—the image his neighbors conjured whenever they thought of him. He wore a low-crowned felt hat, its brim splayed wide from being jammed down firmly against the wind, and a coat reminiscent of Dr. Johnson’s. His legs were sheathed in ordinary leather gaiters, and his feet in boots of such emphatic size that each one formed a roomy chamber, so stoutly built that a man could stand in a river all day and never feel the damp—their maker being a scrupulous craftsman who made up for any shortcomings in his pattern with unsparing dimension and heft.

The trees beneath which she sat were singularly battered, rude, and wild, and for a few minutes Mrs. Yeobright dismissed thoughts of her own storm-broken and exhausted state to contemplate theirs. Not a bough in the nine trees which composed the group but was splintered, lopped, and distorted by the fierce weather that there held them at its mercy whenever it prevailed. Some were blasted and split as if by lightning, black stains as from fire marking their sides, while the ground at their feet was strewn with dead fir-needles and heaps of cones blown down in the gales of past years. The place was called the Devil’s Bellows, and it was only necessary to come there on a March or November night to discover the forcible reasons for that name. On the present heated afternoon, when no perceptible wind was blowing, the trees kept up a perpetual moan which one could hardly believe to be caused by the air.

Some were scorched and riven as though by lightning, with dark stains running down their trunks like fire-marks, while the earth below was littered with dead pine-needles and drifts of cones flung down by the gales of former years. The place was known as the Devil’s Bellowing, and one had only to visit it on a March or November night to understand the strength of the name. This dialogue had taken place at Mr. Lorry’s usual desk, with Monseigneur swarming within a yard or two of it, boastful of what he would do to avenge himself on the rascal-people before long. It was too much the way of Monseigneur under his reverses as a refugee, and it was much too much the way of native British orthodoxy, to talk of this terrible Revolution as if it were the only harvest ever known under the skies that had not been sown--as if nothing had ever been done, or omitted to be done, that had led to it--as if observers of the wretched millions in France, and of the misused and perverted resources that should have made them prosperous, had not seen it inevitably coming, years before, and had not in plain words recorded what they saw. Such vapouring, combined with the extravagant plots of Monseigneur for the restoration of a state of things that had utterly exhausted itself, and worn out Heaven and earth as well as itself, was hard to be endured without some remonstrance by any sane man who knew the truth. And it was such vapouring all about his ears, like a troublesome confusion of blood in his own head, added to a latent uneasiness in his mind, which had already made Charles Darnay restless, and which still kept him so.

This exchange unfolded at Mr. Lorry’s regular work desk, with a crowd of refugee aristocrats clustered barely three feet away, loudly bragging about how they would soon take brutal revenge on the lowborn rabble they blamed for their troubles. That kind of talk was par for the course for these deposed nobles navigating their exile, and just as common among rigid, conventional British thinkers of the time: they spoke of the horrific Revolution as if it were the only harvest in human history that had sprung up entirely unseeded. They acted as if no choice, no failure to act, had paved the way for the uprising; as if the people who’d watched France’s millions of destitute citizens suffer, who’d seen the resources that should have made them thrive hoarded and misused for the elite, hadn’t seen the collapse coming for years, and hadn’t documented their observations in clear, unambiguous writing. All this empty posturing, paired with the aristocrats’ absurd schemes to restore a regime that had already completely devoured itself, alienating every last person on earth and even defying all sense of divine justice in the process, was almost impossible for any sensible person who knew the full truth to tolerate without speaking up. And it was this endless stream of loud, foolish rhetoric swirling right around him, buzzing in his ears like the disorienting thud of blood rushing to his own skull, layered over the quiet, underlying anxiety he already held in his chest, that had first left Charles Darnay restless – and that still kept him that way.

It was a woman, approaching with her head tilted askance, as if she was carefully scanning every part of the area around her. When she veered a little further left and drew closer still, Bathsheba could make out the newcomer’s profile outlined against the bright, sunlit sky. She knew that soft, flowing curve stretching from forehead to chin, with no sharp angles or defined harsh lines anywhere along it, instantly: it was the familiar contour of Liddy Smallbury.

But at length, he began to mend by almost imperceptible degrees, now and then able to murmur a few wobbly, tear-thick words about how deeply he cherished the kindness of the two gentle women who’d taken him in, and how ardently he hoped that once he regained his full strength and health, he might find some small way to show his gratitude. It only needed to be some small act, something that would lay bare the overflowing devotion and sense of duty swelling in his breast; no matter how slight the effort, it would prove that their soft, unhesitating goodness had not been cast aside for nothing, that the ragged, destitute boy their charity had pulled from misery, and almost certain death, ached to devote every beat of his heart and every scrap of his strength to their care.

It was a long walk to North Oakland, but it was not until he went up the steps and entered his room that he knew he had walked it. He found himself sitting on the edge of the bed and staring about him like an awakened somnambulist. He noticed “Overdue” lying on the table and drew up his chair and reached for his pen. There was in his nature a logical compulsion toward completeness. Here was something undone. It had been deferred against the completion of something else. Now that something else had been finished, and he would apply himself to this task until it was finished. What he would do next he did not know. All that he did know was that a climacteric in his life had been attained. A period had been reached, and he was rounding it off in workman-like fashion. He was not curious about the future. He would soon enough find out what it held in store for him. Whatever it was, it did not matter. Nothing seemed to matter.

The road to North Berkeley had been a long one, but it was not until he climbed the stairs and let himself into his room that he was aware he had traversed it. He found himself seated on the edge of the bed, gazing about him with the blank wonder of a sleepwalker suddenly roused. On the table he saw “Past Due,” and he drew his chair close and took up his pen. There was in him a strict, almost mathematical need for wholeness. Something remained unfinished. It had been put aside until another matter should be brought to an end. Now that other thing was done, and he would give himself to this remaining labor until it too was done. What he should do afterward he did not know. Only this he knew: that a turning point in his life had been reached. A sentence had ended, and he was setting the final stop upon it with careful, workmanlike hands. He felt no curiosity concerning the future. In due time he would learn what awaited him there. Whatever it might be, it no longer seemed to signify. Nothing seemed to signify.

Then Edith had come into his life, Edith that honest and unconscious defaulter. And there again he should have stuck to his disappointment. He had stuck to it--nine days out of every ten. It is the tenth occasion, the singular, tempting minute, the flash of arrogant certainty—and your optimistic soul is back in the gutter.

The only signs of the terrible sorrow Boldwood had been combating through the night, and was combating now, were the want of colour in his well-defined face, the enlarged appearance of the veins in his forehead and temples, and the sharper lines about his mouth. The horse bore him away, and the very step of the animal seemed significant of dogged despair. Gabriel, for a minute, rose above his own grief in noticing Boldwood’s. He saw the square figure sitting erect upon the horse, the head turned to neither side, the elbows steady by the hips, the brim of the hat level and undisturbed in its onward glide, until the keen edges of Boldwood’s shape sank by degrees over the hill. To one who knew the man and his story there was something more striking in this immobility than in a collapse. The clash of discord between mood and matter here was forced painfully home to the heart; and, as in laughter there are more dreadful phases than in tears, so was there in the steadiness of this agonized man an expression deeper than a cry.

The only hints of the crippling grief Boldwood had fought all night, and was still fighting in that moment, showed in the drained, pale cast of his sharp-featured face, the bulging veins standing out across his forehead and temples, and the newly harsh edges of the lines framing his mouth. The horse carried him off, and even the creature’s plodding gait felt heavy with the same stubborn, unyielding despair clinging to its rider. For a few seconds, Gabriel set his own heartache aside to watch Boldwood’s distress unfold. He tracked the broad, square-shouldered form sitting rigidly upright in the saddle, head held straight without glancing left or right, elbows fixed firmly at his sides, the brim of his hat sitting perfectly even as he glided forward, until the sharp, distinct outline of Boldwood’s figure slowly dipped out of sight over the crest of the hill. For anyone who knew Boldwood and what he’d been through, this rigid stillness hit far harder than any public breakdown would have. The jarring mismatch between his outward composure and inner turmoil cut straight to the chest with a sharp, painful clarity; just as there are strains of laughter far more terrible than sobs, the unshakable calm of this man raw with agony held a weight of emotion far more profound than any screamed outburst.

He had struck a gold mine, and he knew he could return to the source for more whenever he wished. His plans involved upgrading his wardrobe, subscribing to numerous periodicals, and purchasing the dozens of reference volumes he currently had to visit the library to use. Even so, a significant portion of the four hundred and twenty dollars remained untouched. This surplus troubled him until he hit upon the idea of employing a domestic worker for Gertrude and getting a bicycle for Marian.

This was the first intimation Mr. Britling got that the want of foresight of the War Office only began with its unpreparedness for recruits. Men were talking very freely in the club; one of the temporary effects of the war in its earlier stages was to produce a partial thaw in the constitutional British shyness; and men who had glowered at Mr. Britling over their lunches and had been glowered at by Mr. Britling in silence for years now started conversations with him.

The crudely made weapon was meant for recruit drill exercises until proper firearms could be sourced, and each one cost only a shilling and sixpence to manufacture. That was the first inkling Mr. Thorne had that the War Office’s staggering lack of foresight extended far beyond its abject failure to prepare for the sudden flood of new volunteers. Conversation flowed uncommonly freely across the club’s common rooms; one curious transient effect of the war’s opening weeks had been to partially melt the ingrained, habitual reserve of British men. He accompanied her up the hill, explaining to her the details of his forthcoming tenure of the other farm. They spoke very little of their mutual feeling; pretty phrases and warm expressions being probably unnecessary between such tried friends. Theirs was that substantial affection which arises (if any arises at all) when the two who are thrown together begin first by knowing the rougher sides of each other’s character, and not the best till further on, the romance growing up in the interstices of a mass of hard prosaic reality. This good-fellowship—_camaraderie_—usually occurring through similarity of pursuits, is unfortunately seldom superadded to love between the sexes, because men and women associate, not in their labours, but in their pleasures merely. Where, however, happy circumstance permits its development, the compounded feeling proves itself to be the only love which is strong as death—that love which many waters cannot quench, nor the floods drown, beside which the passion usually called by the name is evanescent as steam.

Edith's home was more prosperous than Mary's; she brought her own money to it; the bringing up of her children was a far more efficient business than Mary's instinctive proceedings. Hugh had very nearly died in his first year of life; some summer infection had snatched at him; that had tied him to his father's heart by a knot of fear; but no infection had ever come near Edith's own nursery. And it was Hugh that Mr. Britling had seen, small and green-faced and pitiful under an anaesthetic for some necessary small operation to his adenoids. His younger children had never stabbed to Mr. Britling's heart with any such pitifulness; they were not so thin-skinned as their elder brother, not so assailable by the little animosities of dust and germ. And out of such things as this evolved a shapeless cloud of championship for Hugh. Jealousies and suspicions are latent in every human relationship. We go about the affairs of life pretending magnificently that they are not so, pretending to the generosities we desire. And in all step-relationships jealousy and suspicion are not merely latent, they stir.

It was Hugh whom Mr. Britling had once seen, small and green-faced and miserable under an anaesthetic for a necessary little operation on his adenoids. His younger children had never pierced Mr. Britling’s heart with any such pity; they were not as delicate as their elder brother, not so exposed to the petty hostilities of dust and germs. From things like this there grew a vague, formless cloud of championship for Hugh. Jealousies and suspicions lie hidden in every human relation. We move through life magnificently pretending they are not there, pretending to the generosity we would like to possess. And in all step-relationships jealousy and suspicion are not merely hidden; they are alive.

He went away vexed with himself, and ashamed of having for this one time in his life done anything which could be called underhand. But he had, after all, lighted upon one fact by way of repayment. It was a singularly fresh and fascinating fact, and though not without its sadness it was pertinent and real. In little more than six years from this time Bathsheba might certainly marry him. There was something definite in that hope, for admitting that there might have been no deep thought in her words to Liddy about marriage, they showed at least her creed on the matter.

He stormed off furious at his own choices, mortified that for the first time in his entire life he’d done something anyone could label sneaky or underhanded. Boldwood was never any good at subtle maneuvering, after all—he had as much tact as a sledgehammer. He squirmed under the conviction that he’d come off not just foolish, but far worse: petty and small. Still, he’d stumbled on one small truth as a sort of consolation prize. It was such a vivid, thrilling little detail, and even if it carried a twinge of melancholy, it was unshakably concrete and relevant. A little over six years from that day, Bathsheba could very well agree to marry him. That hope had a real, tangible weight to it; even if she hadn’t put much careful thought into what she’d told Liddy about her marriage plans, those offhand words still revealed her core beliefs on the subject.

It may have been observed that there is no regular path for getting out of love as there is for getting in. Some people look upon marriage as a short cut that way, but it has been known to fail. Separation, which was the means that chance offered to Gabriel Oak by Bathsheba’s disappearance, though effectual with people of certain humours, is apt to idealize the removed object with others—notably those whose affection, placid and regular as it may be, flows deep and long. Oak belonged to the even-tempered order of humanity, and felt the secret fusion of himself in Bathsheba to be burning with a finer flame now that she was gone—that was all.

It is often observed that while there is a clear road for falling into love, no such defined path exists for getting out of it. Some regard marriage as a shortcut to that destination, yet it is a cure known to fail. Separation, the remedy chance offered Gabriel Oak upon Bathsheba’s disappearance, might be effective for certain personalities, but it often serves to idealize the absent person for others—specifically those whose love, however quiet and steady, runs deep. Oak belonged to this even-tempered class of men, and he found the secret union of his soul with Bathsheba’s to be burning with a more refined intensity now that she was gone.

Anyone who had stood by now would have pitied her, not so much on account of her exposure to weather, and isolation from all of humanity except the mouldered remains inside the tumulus; but for that other form of misery which was denoted by the slightly rocking movement that her feelings imparted to her person. Extreme unhappiness weighed visibly upon her. Between the drippings of the rain from her umbrella to her mantle, from her mantle to the heather, from the heather to the earth, very similar sounds could be heard coming from her lips; and the tearfulness of the outer scene was repeated upon her face. The wings of her soul were broken by the cruel obstructiveness of all about her; and even had she seen herself in a promising way of getting to Budmouth, entering a steamer, and sailing to some opposite port, she would have been but little more buoyant, so fearfully malignant were other things. She uttered words aloud. When a woman in such a situation, neither old, deaf, crazed, nor whimsical, takes upon herself to sob and soliloquize aloud there is something grievous the matter.

Any onlooker lingering at the spot would have felt their heart twist for her, not first for the lash of the cold rain on her skin or her utter solitude, broken only by the decayed remains tucked inside the ancient barrow at her back; but for the slow, unsteady sway of her frame, a physical echo of the agony churning beneath her skin. A suffocating weight of despair hung off her, impossible to miss even through the fog of falling rain. Rain pattered off the edge of her oilskin umbrella to her thick wool cloak, dripped from her cloak to the gorse and heather at her feet, seeped from the heather down into the dark loam below; and between those soft, pattering beats came the faint, hitching sound of her sobs, the damp grief of the storm mirrored in the streaks of tears carving tracks through the rain on her cheeks. The very wings of her spirit had been snapped by the cold, unyielding cruelty of every turn her life had taken lately; even if she’d suddenly found herself on the clear road to Brineport, stepping onto a steam packet bound for some distant shore across the channel, she would not have felt a scrap lighter, so heavy and unforgiving was the weight of the wrongs that had been done to her. She spoke fragments of sentences out loud to the empty air. When a woman in such straits, who is neither elderly, hard of hearing, unwell in the mind, nor given to fanciful displays, weeps and murmurs to herself in the open wild, you know her suffering is beyond bearing.

He had a quite exaggerated idea of the amount of munitions that America was sending to the Allies, he was inclined to believe that they were entirely dependent upon their transatlantic supplies, and so he found himself persuaded that the victory of the Allies and the honour of America were incompatible things. And--in spite of his ethical aloofness--he loved the Allies. He wanted them to win, and he wanted America to abandon a course that he believed was vitally necessary to their victory. It was an intellectual dilemma. He hid this self-contradiction from Matching's Easy with much the same feelings that a curate might hide a poisoned dagger at a tea-party....

Yet this vision of America as a dispassionate judicial presence, vocal in protest but deliberately aloof from the fray, led him to a conclusion that left him deeply scandalized. If America will not, and indeed ought not, wield military force in pursuit of just ends, he argued, then it has no right to manufacture and export weapons of war. It cannot trade in the very violence it publicly disavows. He held a wildly inflated sense of how much munitions America was sending to the Allies, even leaning into the belief that the Allies were entirely dependent on those transatlantic supplies to keep fighting. As a result, he grew convinced that an Allied victory and America’s national honor were fundamentally incompatible. And for all his dedication to America’s stated ethical distance from the war, he loved the Allies deeply. He wanted desperately to see them win, even as he believed America ought to abandon the course of action he was certain was critical to that victory. It was a torturous intellectual dilemma. He hid this glaring self-contradiction from the community at Matching’s Easy with the same skittish, ashamed urgency a curate might bring to hiding a poisoned dagger in his coat at a polite afternoon tea party.

I was rooted straight to the spot.

And then he made a great many other remarks, no less illustrative of his humility, which were received with equal favour and applause, and were, withal, as original and as much to the purpose, as the remarks of great men commonly are.

At this news, the two maidservants raised their hands in wonder. Mr. Giles, smoothing his shirt-front, demurred with a "No, no," requesting that they inform him if he ever appeared too arrogant toward his social inferiors. He then offered several other comments intended to showcase his humility; these were received with great acclaim and were, in truth, about as insightful and relevant as the pronouncements of great men typically tend to be.

"Nobody planned the British estate system, nobody planned the British aristocratic system, nobody planned the confounded constitution, it came about, it was like layer after layer wrapping round an agate, but you see it came about so happily in a way, it so suited the climate and the temperament of our people and our island, it was on the whole so cosy, that our people settled down into it, you can't help settling down into it, they had already settled down by the days of Queen Anne, and Heaven knows if we shall ever really get away again. We're like that little shell the _Lingula_, that is found in the oldest rocks and lives to-day: it fitted its easy conditions, and it has never modified since. Why should it? It excretes all its disturbing forces. Our younger sons go away and found colonial empires. Our surplus cottage children emigrate to Australia and Canada or migrate into the towns. It doesn't alter _this_...."

The British system of estates and the aristocracy were never officially designed, nor was our convoluted constitution ever part of a master plan. These things simply emerged, forming gradually like the concentric layers of an agate. Yet, there was a certain harmony to this development; it aligned so perfectly with the temperament of our people and the climate of our island. It offered such a sense of security that we naturally settled into it—and once you have settled in, it is nearly impossible to leave. We were already firmly rooted by the reign of Queen Anne, and it is doubtful we will ever truly escape. We are like the *Lingula*, that ancient shellfish found in the earliest fossils that still survives today; it adapted to its easy environment and never felt the need to evolve. Why bother? It merely expels any destabilizing influences. Our younger sons head abroad to build empires, while the surplus children of the working class move to the cities or emigrate to Canada and Australia. But none of that ever touches the core of the system.
