Elias Thorne’s upland farm had been fully stocked by a regional supply merchant—who’d agreed to the arrangement solely on the weight of Thorne’s earnest bearing and solid local reputation—who collected a fixed percentage of the farm’s monthly earnings until the full sum of the initial advance was repaid. When Thorne tallied all his holdings, he discovered the total worth of livestock, crop stores, and tools that rightfully belonged to him was barely enough to settle all his outstanding debts, leaving him unburdened by obligations but owning nothing beyond the scuffed work clothes he had on his back.

Out of each week’s earnings Chandler set aside $1. At the end of each ten weeks with the extra capital thus accumulated, he purchased one gentleman’s evening from the bargain counter of stingy old Father Time. He arrayed himself in the regalia of millionaires and presidents; he took himself to the quarter where life is brightest and showiest, and there dined with taste and luxury. With ten dollars a man may, for a few hours, play the wealthy idler to perfection. The sum is ample for a well-considered meal, a bottle bearing a respectable label, commensurate tips, a smoke, cab fare and the ordinary etceteras.

Every week, Chandler tucked one dollar away from his earnings. Once every ten weeks, with that small stockpile of savings, he bought himself a proper gentleman’s evening from the discount shelf of thrifty old Father Time. He dressed himself in the kind of fine garments worn by millionaires and heads of state, then headed for the city’s most glittering, ostentatious neighborhood, where he ate a meal curated for elegance and indulgence. Ten dollars is more than enough for a man to pull off the role of a carefree, wealthy idler flawlessly for a few hours. The sum covers a thoughtfully chosen meal, a bottle of reputable wine, appropriately generous tips, a smoke, cab fare, and all the usual small incidental costs that come with such an excursion.

One thing was certain: the Morses had not cared to have him for himself or for his work. Therefore they could not want him now for himself or for his work, but for the fame that was his, because he was somebody amongst men, and—why not?—because he had a hundred thousand dollars or so. That was the way bourgeois society valued a man, and who was he to expect it otherwise? But he was proud. He disdained such valuation. He desired to be valued for himself, or for his work, which, after all, was an expression of himself. That was the way Lizzie valued him. The work, with her, did not even count. She valued him, himself. That was the way Jimmy, the plumber, and all the old gang valued him. That had been proved often enough in the days when he ran with them; it had been proved that Sunday at Shell Mound Park. His work could go hang. What they liked, and were willing to scrap for, was just Mart Eden, one of the bunch and a pretty good guy.

This much was beyond dispute: the Morses had never wanted him for his own sake or for his writing. So they could hardly want him now for his own sake or for his writing, but only for the renown that clung to him, because he counted among men, and—why pretend otherwise?—because he had a hundred thousand dollars or thereabouts. That was how the middle class weighed a man, and what right had he to imagine anything different? Yet he was proud. He scorned such reckoning. He longed to be prized for himself, or for his writing, which was, in the end, a part of himself. That was how Lizzie prized him. With her, the writing did not even enter. She prized him, the man. That was how Jimmy, the plumber, and all the old crew prized him. They had shown it often enough in the days when he ran beside them; they had shown it that Sunday at Shell Mound Park. His writing could be damned. What they loved, and would fight for, was simply Mart Eden, one of their own and a decent fellow.

“These were the reflections of my hours of despondency and solitude; but when I contemplated the virtues of the cottagers, their amiable and benevolent dispositions, I persuaded myself that when they should become acquainted with my admiration of their virtues they would compassionate me and overlook my personal deformity. Could they turn from their door one, however monstrous, who solicited their compassion and friendship? I resolved, at least, not to despair, but in every way to fit myself for an interview with them which would decide my fate. I postponed this attempt for some months longer, for the importance attached to its success inspired me with a dread lest I should fail. Besides, I found that my understanding improved so much with every day’s experience that I was unwilling to commence this undertaking until a few more months should have added to my sagacity.

Such were the musings of my bleak and lonely hours; yet when I dwelled upon the cottagers’ goodness—their gentle, generous natures—I convinced myself that once they perceived how deeply I revered their kindness, they would pity me and disregard my monstrous form. Could they really spurn a creature, however hideous, who came begging only for their sympathy and fellowship? I determined, at least, not to surrender to despair, but to prepare myself by every means for the meeting that would seal my destiny. I deferred the attempt some months longer, for the gravity of its outcome filled me with a terror of failing. Moreover, I sensed my understanding sharpening with each day’s experience, and I was loath to begin the venture until a few more months had seasoned my wisdom.

Had she not been his wife, Troy would have yielded at once; as things stood, he resolved he would deceive her no more.

Mr. Britling was probably much franker and more open-eyed with himself and the universe than a great number of intelligent people, and yet there were quite a number of aspects of his relations with his wife, with people about him, with his country and God and the nature of things, upon which he turned his back with an attentive persistence. But a back too resolutely turned may be as indicative as a pointing finger, and in this retrogressive way, and tacitly even so far as his formal thoughts, his unspoken comments, went, Mr. Britling knew that he loved his son because he had lavished the most hope and the most imagination upon him, because he was the one living continuation of that dear life with Mary, so lovingly stormy at the time, so fine now in memory, that had really possessed the whole heart of Mr. Britling. The boy had been the joy and marvel of the young parents; it was incredible to them that there had ever been a creature so delicate and sweet, and they brought considerable imagination and humour to the detailed study of his minute personality and to the forecasting of his future. Mr. Britling's mind blossomed with wonderful schemes for his education. All that mental growth no doubt contributed greatly to Mr. Britling's peculiar affection, and with it there interwove still tenderer and subtler elements, for the boy had a score of Mary's traits. But there were other things still more conspicuously ignored. One silent factor in the slow widening of the breach between Edith and Mr. Britling was her cool estimate of her stepson. She was steadfastly kind to this shock-headed, untidy little dreamer, he was extremely well cared for in her hands, she liked him and she was amused by him--it is difficult to imagine what more Mr. Britling could have expected--but it was as plain as daylight that she felt that this was not the child she would have cared to have borne. It was quite preposterous and perfectly natural that this should seem to Mr. Britling to be unfair to Hugh.

Mr. Britling was probably far more candid and clear-sighted with himself and with the world than many intelligent people, and yet there were a good many features of his relations with his wife, with the people around him, with his country, with God, and with the very nature of things, to which he persistently turned his back. But a back too determinedly turned may mean as much as a finger pointed, and in this backward fashion, and in a tacit way so far as even his formal thoughts and unspoken remarks were concerned, Mr. Britling knew that he loved his son because he had poured into him the greatest amount of hope and imagination, because he was the one living continuation of that dear life with Mary, so lovingly stormy then, so beautiful now in memory, which had truly taken possession of Mr. Britling’s whole heart. The boy had been the delight and wonder of the young parents; they could scarcely believe there had ever been a being so fragile and sweet, and they brought a great deal of imagination and humour to the close study of his tiny personality and to the anticipation of his future. Mr. Britling’s mind was full of splendid plans for his education. All that mental expansion no doubt helped greatly to shape Mr. Britling’s singular affection, and woven through it were even gentler and more delicate strands, for the boy had many of Mary’s qualities. But there were other matters that were ignored even more conspicuously. One quiet element in the slow widening of the rift between Edith and Mr. Britling was her cool opinion of her stepson. She was consistently kind to this tousle-haired, untidy little dreamer; he was very well looked after in her care, she liked him and was amused by him—it is hard to see what more Mr. Britling could have wanted—but it was as clear as day that she felt this was not a child she would have cared to bear herself. There was a slight noise from the direction of the dim corner where the ladder was. It was the king descending. I could see that he was bearing something in one arm, and assisting himself with the other. He came forward into the light; upon his breast lay a slender girl of fifteen. She was but half conscious; she was dying of smallpox. Here was heroism at its last and loftiest possibility, its utmost summit; this was challenging death in the open field unarmed, with all the odds against the challenger, no reward set upon the contest, and no admiring world in silks and cloth of gold to gaze and applaud; and yet the king's bearing was as serenely brave as it had always been in those cheaper contests where knight meets knight in equal fight and clothed in protecting steel. He was great now; sublimely great. The rude statues of his ancestors in his palace should have an addition--I would see to that; and it would not be a mailed king killing a giant or a dragon, like the rest, it would be a king in commoner's garb bearing death in his arms that a peasant mother might look her last upon her child and be comforted.

A faint rustle emanated from the gloom near the wooden rungs. It was the sovereign making his descent. I watched as he climbed down, one arm occupied by a heavy weight, the other guiding his passage. When he stepped into the pale light, I saw a girl of tender years resting against his chest, her consciousness fading as the pestilence claimed her. Here was the pinnacle of human courage; a confrontation with the reaper in the starkest of terms, where no prizes were offered and no banners waved. There were no throngs in velvet to cheer this lonely struggle, yet his resolve was as unshakable as when he faced an armed peer in the safety of his harness. In this hour, he reached a height of grandeur beyond any crown. I determined then that the hall of his forefathers needed a new monument—not another iron-clad warrior triumphing over a mythic foe, but a king in humble wool, carrying mortality itself so a mother’s heart might be stilled by one last glimpse of her child.

Then it was that the Madness of Manhattan, the frenzy of Fuss and Feathers, the Bacillus of Brag, the Provincial Plague of Pose seized upon Towers Chandler. He was on Broadway, surrounded by pomp and style, and there were eyes to look at him. On the stage of that comedy he had assumed to play the one-night part of a butterfly of fashion and an idler of means and taste. He was dressed for the part, and all his good angels had not the power to prevent him from acting it.

At that instant, the New York mania for status and the infectious drive to show off took hold of Towers Chandler. There on Broadway, amidst the glitter and the gaze of the crowd, he found himself on a stage of his own making. He had decided to spend one night portraying a wealthy man of fashion and taste. Clad in the perfect attire, he was beyond the reach of his better judgment; he was committed to the performance, and nothing could stop him from playing the role.

There would then be no way for him to return to Oakland, and he knew no one in San Francisco from whom to borrow another ten cents.

Two weeks had slipped by since Martin last encountered him, and he racked his mind in vain for any slight he might have given. The dime took Martin on the ferry to San Francisco, and as he strode up Market Street he considered what would become of him if he couldn’t obtain the money. He’d have no means to get back to Oakland, and he was acquainted with no one in San Francisco who could lend him another dime.

Hmm, what was my best move here? Rushing into anything was definitely out of the question. I needed to conjure up some small distraction, something to occupy my hands while I sorted through my options, and give these dazed lads time to shake off their stupor. Luka was perched on the rough wooden stool beside me, frozen mid-motion as he’d been trying to figure out his wind-up pellet shooter—locked stiff in the exact pose he’d struck the second my heavy iron post driver slammed into the earth, the little toy still clamped in his unresponding fingers. So I gently pried it from his grip and offered to walk him through how it worked. As if it was some great secret! It was nothing more than a cheap, clunky trinket, really—but it might as well have been magic, to folks from that corner of the world, in that far simpler time.

But I—I have lost everything and cannot begin life anew.”

“I concur with your sentiment,” the stranger answered. “We are incomplete beings, only partially realized, if someone more insightful, more virtuous, and more beloved than ourselves—the very definition of a true friend—does not offer their guidance to help refine our fragile and flawed characters. I once possessed such a friend, the most honorable of men, and so I feel qualified to speak on the nature of companionship. You are filled with hope and have your whole life ahead of you, with no reason to succumb to misery. But I—I have been stripped of everything and lack the strength to begin again.”

The ballad relates how a lovely young woman concealed herself in an oaken chest during a Christmas game of hide-and-seek, only to be discovered years later as a withered relic. The tale seized Herr Heinrich’s fancy with extraordinary force. “Come,” he said, “let us now inspect every trunk, every closet, every drawer. And mark each one as we proceed….”

Bathsheba knew no other way of showing kindness to persons departed than by giving them flowers. She knew not how long she remained engaged thus. She forgot time, life, where she was, what she was doing. A slamming together of the coach-house doors in the yard brought her to herself again. An instant after, the front door opened and closed, steps crossed the hall, and her husband appeared at the entrance to the room, looking in upon her.

Driven by a need for penance, she gathered blossoms from a vase near the window and started arranging them around the head of the deceased girl. Bathsheba could conceive of no other gesture of compassion for the dead save offering them flowers. She had no sense of how long she lingered at this task. Time, existence, her surroundings, her own actions—all slipped from her awareness. The sharp clap of the coach-house doors banging shut in the yard jolted her back to herself. A moment later, the front door swung open and then closed, footsteps crossed the hall, and her husband stood in the doorway, gazing in at her.

Never was ingenuity exercised so sorely as the traveller here exercised hers. Every conceivable aid, method, stratagem, mechanism, by which these last desperate eight hundred yards could be overpassed by a human being unperceived, was revolved in her busy brain, and dismissed as impracticable. She thought of sticks, wheels, crawling—she even thought of rolling. But the exertion demanded by either of these latter two was greater than to walk erect. The faculty of contrivance was worn out. Hopelessness had come at last.

No wanderer had ever strained their cleverness as thin as Elowen did that day. Every plausible trick, tool, ruse, or makeshift she could imagine for slipping across those final, desperate eight hundred yards without being spotted cycled through her racing mind, only to be discarded as unworkable. She considered propping herself on scavenged branches, rigging a low rolling frame, creeping on hands and knees—she even entertained the thought of tucking into a tight ball and rolling the whole stretch. But both crawling and rolling would demand far more physical exertion than simply striding upright, conspicuous as that would be. Every last spark of resourcefulness had burned down to ash. Hopelessness settled over her at last, cold and unyielding.

"I should have explained these young people. They're the sort of young people we are producing over here now in quite enormous quantity. They are the sort of equivalent of the Russian Intelligentsia, an irresponsible middle class with ideas. Teddy, you know, is my secretary. He's the son, I believe, of a Kilburn solicitor. He was recommended to me by Datcher of _The Times_. He came down here and lived in lodgings for a time. Then suddenly appeared the young lady."

I ought to have prefaced this with a word about these young people. They’re the type we churn out in enormous numbers over here these days, our homegrown counterpart to the old Russian intelligentsia: a feckless, idea-addled segment of the middle class. Leo, you’ll remember, is my secretary. I believe he’s the son of a Walthamstow solicitor, recommended to me by Hale of *The Guardian*. He moved out here and stayed in a set of lodgings for a while, and then, all at once, the young woman turned up.

“Very well,” said Wildeve, rising. Searching about with the lantern, he found a large flat stone, which he placed between himself and Christian, and sat down again. The lantern was opened to give more light, and its rays directed upon the stone. Christian put down a shilling, Wildeve another, and each threw. Christian won. They played for two, Christian won again.

"Fine," Wildeve replied, getting to his feet. Scouring the area by the lantern’s glow, he located a wide, level stone, positioned it between himself and Christian, and sat back down. He opened the lantern to brighten the area, focusing its beams onto the rock. Christian set down a shilling, Wildeve matched the bet, and each of them threw. Christian won. They played for two shillings, and Christian was victorious again.

His remarks had the effect of floating off from some busy mill of thought within him. Hitherto Mr. Direck had been inclined to think this silent observant youth, with his hands in his pockets and his shoulders a little humped, as probably shy and adolescently ineffective. But the head was manifestly quite busy....

His words seemed to drift out from some inner engine of contemplation, as though released from a quietly humming mental workshop. Until now, Mr. Direck had taken this quiet, watchful young man—hands buried in his pockets, shoulders faintly stooped—for someone shy and youthfully ineffectual. Yet it was plain that his mind was very much at work.

She came up and looked around—then on the other side of the hedge. Gabriel was about to advance and restore the missing article when an unexpected performance induced him to suspend the action for the present. The path, after passing the cowshed, bisected the plantation. It was not a bridle-path—merely a pedestrian’s track, and the boughs spread horizontally at a height not greater than seven feet above the ground, which made it impossible to ride erect beneath them. The girl, who wore no riding-habit, looked around for a moment, as if to assure herself that all humanity was out of view, then dexterously dropped backwards flat upon the pony’s back, her head over its tail, her feet against its shoulders, and her eyes to the sky. The rapidity of her glide into this position was that of a kingfisher—its noiselessness that of a hawk. Gabriel’s eyes had scarcely been able to follow her. The tall lank pony seemed used to such doings, and ambled along unconcerned. Thus she passed under the level boughs.

She stepped to the edge of the thicket, craning her neck first one way, then peering over to the far side of the hawthorn hedge. Elias was just preparing to step out and hand back the linen handkerchief she’d dropped moments earlier, when an unanticipated bit of whimsy made him suspend his movement for the time being. The dirt track, once it curved past the weathered stone cowshed, cut straight through the middle of the old pine plantation. It was no wide bridle road—only a narrow footpath worn soft by decades of walkers, and the pine boughs stretched out level barely seven feet above the packed earth, making it impossible for anyone on horseback to sit upright and pass beneath without getting swatted by needles. The young woman, who wore a simple cotton frock instead of a formal riding habit, glanced over her shoulders twice, as if confirming no other soul was nearby to see her, then flipped nimbly backward until she lay flat across the pony’s broad back, her head lolling near its tail, her boots propped against its shoulders, her gaze fixed straight up at the patchy blue sky between the boughs. She shifted into the position as fast as a kingfisher diving for minnows, as quiet as a hawk gliding on a thermal. Elias could barely track the movement, quick as it was. The tall, rangy pony seemed perfectly accustomed to such antics, and plodded along unconcerned without so much as a twitch of its ear. Thus she slipped unhindered beneath the low-hanging boughs.

Well, the king was out of the hole; and on terms satisfactory to the Church and the rest of the aristocracy, no doubt. Men write many fine and plausible arguments in support of monarchy, but the fact remains that where every man in a State has a vote, brutal laws are impossible. Arthur's people were of course poor material for a republic, because they had been debased so long by monarchy; and yet even they would have been intelligent enough to make short work of that law which the king had just been administering if it had been submitted to their full and free vote. There is a phrase which has grown so common in the world's mouth that it has come to seem to have sense and meaning--the sense and meaning implied when it is used; that is the phrase which refers to this or that or the other nation as possibly being “capable of self-government”; and the implied sense of it is, that there has been a nation somewhere, some time or other which _wasn't_ capable of it--wasn't as able to govern itself as some self-appointed specialists were or would be to govern it. The master minds of all nations, in all ages, have sprung in affluent multitude from the mass of the nation, and from the mass of the nation only--not from its privileged classes; and so, no matter what the nation's intellectual grade was; whether high or low, the bulk of its ability was in the long ranks of its nameless and its poor, and so it never saw the day that it had not the material in abundance whereby to govern itself. Which is to assert an always self-proven fact: that even the best governed and most free and most enlightened monarchy is still behind the best condition attainable by its people; and that the same is true of kindred governments of lower grades, all the way down to the lowest.

The king had finally escaped his predicament, surely under conditions that satisfied both the Church and the nobility. Many eloquent justifications are written in defense of kingship, but the truth is simple: wherever universal suffrage exists, cruel laws cannot endure. Arthur’s subjects were admittedly poor candidates for a republic, having been diminished by centuries of royal oppression; yet even they would have been wise enough to dismantle the harsh statutes the king had just upheld if the matter were put to a free vote. A particular phrase has become so common that it is often mistaken for logic—the idea that a nation might not be “capable of self-government.” This suggests that there has ever been a people less fit to govern themselves than the self-anointed specialists who would presume to do it for them. In every era, the finest minds have risen in great numbers from the common folk, not the privileged few. No matter the general state of a nation’s intellect, the vast majority of its capability lies with the nameless poor; consequently, no society has ever lacked the internal strength to manage its own destiny. It is an undeniable fact that even the most enlightened monarchy lags behind the potential of a self-governing people, a truth that applies to every form of autocracy, from the most refined to the most base.

Ikey went behind the prescription desk. There he crushed to a powder two soluble tablets, each containing a quarter of a grain of morphia. To them he added a little sugar of milk to increase the bulk, and folded the mixture neatly in a white paper. Taken by an adult this powder would insure several hours of heavy slumber without danger to the sleeper. This he handed to Chunk McGowan, telling him to administer it in a liquid if possible, and received the hearty thanks of the backyard Lochinvar.

Ikey stepped behind the prescription counter. Once there, he ground two dissolvable tablets—each holding a quarter grain of morphine—into a fine powder. He mixed in a small pinch of milk sugar to bulk the dose up slightly, then folded the combined mixture tidily into a slip of white paper. For a full-grown adult, this powder would guarantee several hours of deep, uninterrupted sleep with no risk of harm to the person taking it. He handed the folded packet to Chunk McGowan, advised him to mix it into a drink if at all possible when giving it out, and got an effusive, heartfelt thank-you from that scrappy backyard Lochinvar.

My life, as it passed thus, was indeed hateful to me, and it was during sleep alone that I could taste joy. O blessed sleep! Often, when most miserable, I sank to repose, and my dreams lulled me even to rapture. The spirits that guarded me had provided these moments, or rather hours, of happiness that I might retain strength to fulfil my pilgrimage. Deprived of this respite, I should have sunk under my hardships. During the day I was sustained and inspirited by the hope of night, for in sleep I saw my friends, my wife, and my beloved country; again I saw the benevolent countenance of my father, heard the silver tones of my Elizabeth’s voice, and beheld Clerval enjoying health and youth. Often, when wearied by a toilsome march, I persuaded myself that I was dreaming until night should come and that I should then enjoy reality in the arms of my dearest friends. What agonising fondness did I feel for them! How did I cling to their dear forms, as sometimes they haunted even my waking hours, and persuade myself that they still lived! At such moments vengeance, that burned within me, died in my heart, and I pursued my path towards the destruction of the dæmon more as a task enjoined by heaven, as the mechanical impulse of some power of which I was unconscious, than as the ardent desire of my soul.

The life I led during those days was utterly repugnant to me, and only in the sanctuary of sleep could I experience any sense of gladness. O, holy rest! Many times, when my despair was most profound, I would fall into a slumber where my dreams offered a comfort bordering on bliss. It seemed as if protective spirits had arranged these fleeting hours of peace to ensure I possessed the fortitude to finish my grim journey. Had I been denied such respite, I would have certainly collapsed beneath my burdens. During the daylight hours, I was buoyed by the promise of the dark, for in my dreams I walked again with my friends, my wife, and through the landscapes of my native land. I saw once more the gentle face of my father, heard the sweet, clear notes of Elizabeth’s voice, and beheld Clerval in all his youthful vitality. Often, when worn down by a grueling day's march, I told myself that the sunlit world was the illusion and that I would wake to the truth only when night restored me to the arms of those I held dear. What a piercing, desperate love I bore for them! I reached out to their beloved ghosts even when I was awake, convincing myself they had never truly left me. At such times, the vengeance that scorched my heart would grow cold, and I followed the trail of the monster more as a divinely appointed chore—a mechanical impulse of some hidden force—than as the fervent obsession of my own spirit.

“See!” said the Doctor of Beauvais, raising his hand towards the moon. “I have looked at her from my prison-window, when I could not bear her light. I have looked at her when it has been such torture to me to think of her shining upon what I had lost, that I have beaten my head against my prison-walls. I have looked at her, in a state so dull and lethargic, that I have thought of nothing but the number of horizontal lines I could draw across her at the full, and the number of perpendicular lines with which I could intersect them.” He added in his inward and pondering manner, as he looked at the moon, “It was twenty either way, I remember, and the twentieth was difficult to squeeze in.”

“See!” said the Doctor of Beauvais, lifting his hand toward the moon. “I have watched her from my prison window when I could not endure her light. I have watched her when it tortured me to think of her shining on what I had lost, so that I struck my head against the prison walls. I have watched her in such a dull, stupefied state that I have thought of nothing except the number of horizontal lines I could draw across her at the full, and the number of upright lines with which I might cross them.” He added, in his inward, meditative way, as he gazed at the moon, “It was twenty each way, I remember, and the twentieth was hard to fit in.”

“Cain Ball, you be no longer a babe and suckling,” said Joseph in the sepulchral tone the circumstances demanded, “and you know what taking an oath is. ’Tis a horrible testament mind ye, which you say and seal with your blood-stone, and the prophet Matthew tells us that on whomsoever it shall fall it will grind him to powder. Now, before all the work-folk here assembled, can you swear to your words as the shepherd asks ye?”

“Cain Ball, you are no mere child at the breast any longer,” Joseph intoned, his voice as grave and hollow as the moment required. “You understand what it means to swear an oath. It is a fearsome covenant, mark you—one you speak aloud and bind with your very life’s seal, and the prophet Matthew warns that on whomever it falls, it will crush him utterly to dust. Now, before all the laborers gathered here, can you swear to your testimony as the shepherd demands?”

I’ve got a couple of nice ones tucked in my pocket for him, if he happens to show up.

The cluck of their oars was the only sound of any distinctness upon the sea, and as they laboured amid the thickening shades the lamp-lights grew larger, each appearing to send a flaming sword deep down into the waves before it, until there arose, among other dim shapes of the kind, the form of the vessel for which they were bound.

Evening settled slowly over the vast, watery expanse ahead. Nearby, where the coast curved to trace a dark ribbon against the horizon, a row of amber sparks began to flicker into view, signaling the location of Budmouth as the lamps were lit along the promenade. The rhythmic thud of their oars was the only distinct sound on the water, and as they rowed through the deepening shadows, the lights grew brighter, each casting a fiery blade deep into the waves. Finally, among the other hazy outlines, the silhouette of the ship they were heading for loomed into sight.

Had only Yeobright’s own future been involved he would have proposed to Thomasin with a ready heart. He had nothing to lose by carrying out a dead mother’s hope. But he dreaded to contemplate Thomasin wedded to the mere corpse of a lover that he now felt himself to be. He had but three activities alive in him. One was his almost daily walk to the little graveyard wherein his mother lay; another, his just as frequent visits by night to the more distant enclosure which numbered his Eustacia among its dead; the third was self-preparation for a vocation which alone seemed likely to satisfy his cravings—that of an itinerant preacher of the eleventh commandment. It was difficult to believe that Thomasin would be cheered by a husband with such tendencies as these.

If Clym’s own destiny were the only thing at stake, he would have asked Thomasin to marry him without hesitation. He had nothing to lose by honoring his late mother’s final wish. Yet, he was troubled by the idea of Thomasin being tied to the emotional shell of a man he felt himself to be. Only three interests still flickered within him. One was his nearly daily pilgrimage to the small graveyard where his mother rested; another was his equally regular nighttime visits to the more distant burial ground where Eustacia lay; and the last was his preparation for the only calling that seemed capable of quieting his restless spirit—serving as a wandering preacher of the eleventh commandment. It was hard to imagine that Thomasin would find any comfort in a husband defined by such grim preoccupations.

“I never made contact with you,” Troy said in a calm voice. “It was only a trick of the hand. The blade went right past your back. You’re not frightened now, are you? For if you are, I won’t be able to go on. I promise I’ll neither harm you nor so much as brush against you.”

Meantime there was one thing which had got pushed into the background of my mind. That was the half-conviction that when the nature of my proposed calamity should be reported to those superstitious people, it would have such an effect that they would want to compromise. So, by and by when I heard footsteps coming, that thought was recalled to me, and I said to myself, “As sure as anything, it's the compromise. Well, if it is good, all right, I will accept; but if it isn't, I mean to stand my ground and play my hand for all it is worth.”

Meanwhile, a thought had been lurking in the back of my mind, half-formed: that once word of the disaster I intended reached those superstitious folk, they’d be shaken enough to seek a settlement. So when I caught the sound of approaching footsteps, that notion surfaced again, and I told myself, “This will be the compromise, I’m certain. If it’s fair, fine—I’ll take it. But if not, I’m resolved to hold firm and play my hand for everything it’s worth.”

But, though the Doctor tried hard, and never ceased trying, to get Charles Darnay set at liberty, or at least to get him brought to trial, the public current of the time set too strong and fast for him. The new era began; the king was tried, doomed, and beheaded; the Republic of Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, declared for victory or death against the world in arms; the black flag waved night and day from the great towers of Notre Dame; three hundred thousand men, summoned to rise against the tyrants of the earth, rose from all the varying soils of France, as if the dragon’s teeth had been sown broadcast, and had yielded fruit equally on hill and plain, on rock, in gravel, and alluvial mud, under the bright sky of the South and under the clouds of the North, in fell and forest, in the vineyards and the olive-grounds and among the cropped grass and the stubble of the corn, along the fruitful banks of the broad rivers, and in the sand of the sea-shore. What private solicitude could rear itself against the deluge of the Year One of Liberty--the deluge rising from below, not falling from above, and with the windows of Heaven shut, not opened!

Even as the Doctor poured every ounce of his effort into securing Charles Darnay’s release, or at the very least getting him a formal court hearing, and never once let up in that pursuit, the roaring tide of public sentiment in that era moved far too quickly and powerfully for him to push against. A new age had dawned: the king was put on trial, condemned to death, and publicly beheaded. The Republic, whose rallying cry was Liberty, Equality, Fraternity, or Death, had declared that it would accept nothing short of total victory or utter destruction in its fight against the armed monarchies of the rest of the world. A pitch-black flag flew around the clock from the towering spires of Notre Dame Cathedral. Three hundred thousand men, called to rise up against the tyrants ruling every corner of the globe, had sprung up across every inch of French soil, as if the mythic dragon’s teeth had been scattered far and wide, bearing fruit just as readily on craggy hilltops and flat lowlands, in bare rock, gravel beds, and soft alluvial silt; under the blazing southern sun and the mist-shrouded northern clouds; across windswept moors and dense old forests, through sun-warmed vineyards, gnarled olive groves, pastures of clipped grass and golden corn stubble, along the lush banks of wide, winding rivers, and in the fine sand of the coastline. What small, personal worry could possibly hold its ground against the overwhelming flood of the first year of Liberty – a flood that swelled upward from the ground below rather than pouring down from the sky, with the windows of Heaven sealed shut rather than thrown open to unleash the waters?

I turned with loathing from the woman who could utter so unfeeling a speech to a person just saved, on the very edge of death; but I felt languid and unable to reflect on all that had passed. The whole series of my life appeared to me as a dream; I sometimes doubted if indeed it were all true, for it never presented itself to my mind with the force of reality.

I turned away in revulsion from the woman who could utter such a cold, unfeeling remark to someone who’d just been pulled back from the very brink of death; but I was so weak and listless I could barely parse the full weight of all that had unfolded. The entire arc of my life felt like a hazy, unmoored dream; sometimes I doubted if any of it had actually been real, for none of it ever settled in my mind with the sharp, tangible force of lived truth.

We stood there awhile, in the thick darkness and stillness, looking toward the red blur in the distance, and trying to make out the meaning of a far-away murmur that rose and fell fitfully on the night. Sometimes it swelled up and for a moment seemed less remote; but when we were hopefully expecting it to betray its cause and nature, it dulled and sank again, carrying its mystery with it. We started down the hill in its direction, and the winding road plunged us at once into almost solid darkness--darkness that was packed and crammed in between two tall forest walls. We groped along down for half a mile, perhaps, that murmur growing more and more distinct all the time. The coming storm threatening more and more, with now and then a little shiver of wind, a faint show of lightning, and dull grumblings of distant thunder. I was in the lead. I ran against something--a soft heavy something which gave, slightly, to the impulse of my weight; at the same moment the lightning glared out, and within a foot of my face was the writhing face of a man who was hanging from the limb of a tree! That is, it seemed to be writhing, but it was not. It was a grewsome sight. Straightway there was an ear-splitting explosion of thunder, and the bottom of heaven fell out; the rain poured down in a deluge. No matter, we must try to cut this man down, on the chance that there might be life in him yet, mustn't we? The lightning came quick and sharp now, and the place was alternately noonday and midnight. One moment the man would be hanging before me in an intense light, and the next he was blotted out again in the darkness. I told the king we must cut him down. The king at once objected.

We lingered for a duration in the dense shadow and silence, watching a ruby smudge on the horizon and straining to interpret a distant hum that ebbed and flowed fitfully through the night. At times it swelled, seeming for a moment more proximate; yet, when we anticipated it might reveal its source, it grew dull and subsided, its secret still guarded. We set off down the slope toward it, the winding track casting us at once into a nearly palpable darkness—an obscurity packed tight between two high ramparts of timber. We groped forward for perhaps half a mile, the murmur growing increasingly audible. The impending storm became more threatening, bringing occasional tremors of wind, pale flickers of lightning, and the low growls of remote thunder. I was at the front. I collided with an object—a soft, heavy mass that gave way slightly under my momentum; in that same instant, the lightning blazed, and a foot from my face hung the distorted visage of a man dangling from a branch! It seemed to twitch, though it was actually still. It was a morbid sight. Immediately, an ear-splitting crash of thunder followed, and the very floor of heaven gave way; the rain fell in a deluge. Regardless, we had to attempt to cut him down, for there was a chance that a spark of life remained, was there not? The lightning became rapid and piercing, the woods alternating between the clarity of midday and the void of midnight. One heartbeat the man was suspended before me in a fierce light, and the next he was vanished into the gloom. I told the Sovereign we must lower him. The Sovereign at once demurred.

“Maria,” Martin announced that night, “I’m going to leave you. And you’re going to leave here yourself soon. Then you can rent the house and be a landlord yourself. You’ve a brother in San Leandro or Haywards, and he’s in the milk business. I want you to send all your washing back unwashed—understand?—unwashed, and to go out to San Leandro to-morrow, or Haywards, or wherever it is, and see that brother of yours. Tell him to come to see me. I’ll be stopping at the Metropole down in Oakland. He’ll know a good milk-ranch when he sees one.”

“Maria,” Martin declared that night, “I am moving on. And you will be leaving this place yourself before long. You’ll be the one renting the house out, a landlady in your own right. You have a brother in the dairy trade over in San Leandro or Haywards. I want you to return all this laundry just as it is—unwashed, do you understand?—and go see him tomorrow. Tell him to come find me at the Metropole in Oakland. He’ll be able to recognize a quality milk ranch when he sees one.”

Since then the appellation which I had thought to reserve to the horizons and landscapes of a merely realistic dream-country, has become more and more popular as a practical definition; and the dream-country has, by degrees, solidified into a utilitarian region which people can go to, take a house in, and write to the papers from. But I ask all good and gentle readers to be so kind as to forget this, and to refuse steadfastly to believe that there are any inhabitants of a Victorian Wessex outside the pages of this and the companion volumes in which they were first discovered.

Since that time, the name I once meant to keep for the distant views and countryside of a purely imagined realm has steadily gained currency as a handy label; and that dream-realm has gradually hardened into a practical district where folk can settle, lease a cottage, and send letters to the press. Yet I entreat every kind and courteous reader to put this out of mind, and to resist with firm conviction the notion that any denizens of a Victorian Wessex exist beyond the covers of this book and the companion volumes where they first came to light.

Once the wagon had rolled onward, Gabriel pulled back from his hidden vantage point, climbed down to the road, and followed the vehicle to the turnpike gate a short way past the hill’s foot, where the figure he’d been intently observing had now stopped to pay toll. He was still roughly twenty paces from the gate when the sound of a dispute reached his ears. The disagreement, over a mere twopence, was between the people accompanying the wagon and the man manning the toll bar.

The restaurant was not so showy or pretentious as the one further down Broadway, which he always preferred, but it was nearly so. The tables were well filled with prosperous-looking diners, there was a good orchestra, playing softly enough to make conversation a possible pleasure, and the cuisine and service were beyond criticism. His companion, even in her cheap hat and dress, held herself with an air that added distinction to the natural beauty of her face and figure. And it is certain that she looked at Chandler, with his animated but self-possessed manner and his kindling and frank blue eyes, with something not far from admiration in her own charming face.

The bistro wasn’t quite as ostentatious or overdone as the spot three blocks further down Michigan Avenue that Elias usually favored, but it came remarkably close. Every table was occupied by well-heeled patrons, a small chamber ensemble played low enough that easy, unstrained conversation never felt like a chore, and both the menu and the waitstaff’s attentiveness were unimpeachable. His dining companion, even in her thrifted wool hat and simple, unadorned cotton dress, carried herself with a quiet grace that lent an extra edge of elegance to her already striking features and willowy frame. And there was no mistaking the warm, near-admiring glint in her own soft, expressive face as she watched Elias, his manner lively but never unsteady, his bright, honest blue eyes sparkling as he spoke.

TO THE HONORABLE THE COMMANDER OF THE INSURGENT CHIVALRY OF ENGLAND: You fight in vain. We know your strength—if one may call it by that name. We know that at the utmost you cannot bring against us above five and twenty thousand knights. Therefore, you have no chance—none whatever. Reflect: we are well equipped, well fortified, we number 54. Fifty-four what? Men? No, _minds_—the capablest in the world; a force against which mere animal might may no more hope to prevail than may the idle waves of the sea hope to prevail against the granite barriers of England. Be advised. We offer you your lives; for the sake of your families, do not reject the gift. We offer you this chance, and it is the last: throw down your arms; surrender unconditionally to the Republic, and all will be forgiven.

To the honorable commander of England’s rebelling aristocracy: Your resistance is futile. We have gauged your strength—if it even deserves that name. We know that, at most, you can muster only twenty-five thousand knights. You have, therefore, no hope of victory—none at all. Consider: we are well-armed, heavily fortified, and we number fifty-four. Fifty-four what? Men? No—*minds*, the most formidable in the world. Against such a power, raw physical force can no more prevail than the aimless waves can hope to shatter the granite cliffs of England. Take heed. We offer you your lives; for the sake of your families, do not reject this mercy. This is your final opportunity: lay down your weapons, surrender unconditionally to the Republic, and you will be forgiven.

What then became of me? I know not; I lost sensation, and chains and darkness were the only objects that pressed upon me. Sometimes, indeed, I dreamt that I wandered in flowery meadows and pleasant vales with the friends of my youth, but I awoke and found myself in a dungeon. Melancholy followed, but by degrees I gained a clear conception of my miseries and situation and was then released from my prison. For they had called me mad, and during many months, as I understood, a solitary cell had been my habitation.

What then became of me? I cannot say; I lost all feeling, and nothing but chains and darkness seemed to bear upon me. At times, indeed, I dreamed that I roamed through flowery meadows and pleasant valleys with the friends of my youth, yet I awoke to find myself in a dungeon. A melancholy mood came over me, but little by little I recovered a clear sense of my sufferings and my condition, and was then released from my prison. They had, it seems, declared me mad, and for many months, as I afterward learned, a solitary cell had been my dwelling.

The mouth seemed formed less to speak than to quiver, less to quiver than to kiss. Some might have added, less to kiss than to curl. Viewed sideways, the closing-line of her lips formed, with almost geometric precision, the curve so well known in the arts of design as the cima-recta, or ogee. The sight of such a flexible bend as that on grim Egdon was quite an apparition. It was felt at once that the mouth did not come over from Sleswig with a band of Saxon pirates whose lips met like the two halves of a muffin. One had fancied that such lip-curves were mostly lurking underground in the South as fragments of forgotten marbles. So fine were the lines of her lips that, though full, each corner of her mouth was as clearly cut as the point of a spear. This keenness of corner was only blunted when she was given over to sudden fits of gloom, one of the phases of the night-side of sentiment which she knew too well for her years.

That mouth looked shaped not so much for speaking as for trembling, not so much for trembling as for kissing. Some would have added that it was made less for kissing than to curl softly at the corners. Seen in profile, the seam where her lips pressed together traced, with nearly mathematical exactness, the curve familiar to decorative artists as the cima-recta, or ogee. To catch sight of such a soft, supple curve on harsh, unforgiving Egdon Heath was little short of a ghostly apparition. You knew immediately that this mouth had not crossed the sea from Sleswig alongside a band of Saxon raiders, whose lips fit together as plain and unshapely as the two halves of a cut muffin. Most would have assumed such delicate lip curves only existed half-buried in southern soil, broken fragments of long-forgotten marble statues. The lines of her lips were so exquisitely shaped that, even at their fullest, each corner of her mouth was chiseled as sharply as the tip of a spear. That sharp definition at the corners softened only when she was seized by sudden bouts of melancholy, one facet of the darker, more brooding side of feeling that she knew far too intimately for someone so young.

As I said this I suddenly beheld the figure of a man, at some distance, advancing towards me with superhuman speed. He bounded over the crevices in the ice, among which I had walked with caution; his stature, also, as he approached, seemed to exceed that of man. I was troubled; a mist came over my eyes, and I felt a faintness seize me, but I was quickly restored by the cold gale of the mountains. I perceived, as the shape came nearer (sight tremendous and abhorred!) that it was the wretch whom I had created. I trembled with rage and horror, resolving to wait his approach and then close with him in mortal combat. He approached; his countenance bespoke bitter anguish, combined with disdain and malignity, while its unearthly ugliness rendered it almost too horrible for human eyes. But I scarcely observed this; rage and hatred had at first deprived me of utterance, and I recovered only to overwhelm him with words expressive of furious detestation and contempt.

Just as I spoke, I suddenly glimpsed a figure in the distance, rushing toward me with a speed that defied nature. He leaped effortlessly over the deep cracks in the ice where I had trodden so tentatively; as he neared, his towering height seemed to far surpass that of any mortal. I felt a surge of unease; my vision clouded and a sudden lightheadedness took hold of me, yet the freezing mountain air quickly revived my spirit. As the shape drew closer—a sight both magnificent and repulsive—I recognized the miserable creature I had fashioned. I shuddered with a combination of fury and dread, determined to wait for him and then grapple in a fight to the finish. He reached me; his expression revealed a profound, bitter suffering joined with scorn and ill-will, while his ghastly, inhuman features were almost too revolting for the human eye to endure. But I took little note of this; my intense hatred had at first stolen my breath, and I found my voice only to assault him with a torrent of words filled with violent loathing and disdain.

Two other passengers, besides the one, were plodding up the hill by the side of the mail. All three were wrapped to the cheekbones and over the ears, and wore jack-boots. Not one of the three could have said, from anything he saw, what either of the other two was like; and each was hidden under almost as many wrappers from the eyes of the mind, as from the eyes of the body, of his two companions. In those days, travellers were very shy of being confidential on a short notice, for anybody on the road might be a robber or in league with robbers. As to the latter, when every posting-house and ale-house could produce somebody in “the Captain’s” pay, ranging from the landlord to the lowest stable non-descript, it was the likeliest thing upon the cards. So the guard of the Dover mail thought to himself, that Friday night in November, one thousand seven hundred and seventy-five, lumbering up Shooter’s Hill, as he stood on his own particular perch behind the mail, beating his feet, and keeping an eye and a hand on the arm-chest before him, where a loaded blunderbuss lay at the top of six or eight loaded horse-pistols, deposited on a substratum of cutlass.

Beside the lone one, two more passengers were trudging up the hill alongside the mail-coach. All three were muffled to the cheekbones and over their ears, and all wore jack-boots. None of the three could have told, from anything visible, what either of the other two looked like; each, to the eyes of the mind as much as to the eyes of the body, was hidden from his companions by nearly as many wrappings. In those days, travellers were very cautious about becoming confidential at short notice, since anyone on the road might prove to be a robber, or in collusion with robbers. And as for the latter, when every posting-house and ale-house could furnish someone in “the Captain’s” pay, from the landlord down to the most insignificant stable-hand, that seemed the likeliest possibility of all. So the guard on the Dover mail reflected, on that Friday night in November of 1775, as he laboured up Shooter’s Hill, standing on his own special perch behind the coach, stamping his feet and keeping both eye and hand on the arm-chest in front of him, where a loaded blunderbuss lay atop six or eight loaded horse-pistols, all resting on a base of cutlass.

I and my boys were at work, straight off. The ruins of the old bath were there yet in the basement of the monastery, not a stone missing. They had been left just so, all these lifetimes, and avoided with a pious fear, as things accursed. In two days we had it all done and the water in--a spacious pool of clear pure water that a body could swim in. It was running water, too. It came in, and went out, through the ancient pipes. The old abbot kept his word, and was the first to try it. He went down black and shaky, leaving the whole black community above troubled and worried and full of bodings; but he came back white and joyful, and the game was made! another triumph scored.

Right away, my lads and I set to work. The old bath's remains still lay in the monastery cellar, not a single stone displaced. All these generations they had been left untouched, shunned with a kind of holy dread, as things under a curse. Within two days we had finished the job and filled it—a roomy basin of clean, limpid water fit for swimming. It was fresh running water, too, flowing in and out through the age-old pipes. The old abbot kept his promise and went down first to test it. He descended dark-skinned and trembling, while the whole black community above fretted and murmured, heavy with foreboding; but he came back up white and radiant, and the venture was won—another victory notched.

"And now you expect me to tell of Germans and the fight and shelling and all sorts of things. _I haven't seen a live German_; I haven't been within two hundred yards of a shell burst, there has been no attack and I haven't got the V.C. I have made myself muddy beyond describing; I've been working all the time, but I've not fired a shot or fought a ha'porth. We were busy all the time--just at work, repairing the parapet, which had to be done gingerly because of snipers, bringing our food in from the rear in big carriers, getting water, pushing our trench out from an angle slantingways forward. Getting meals, clearing up and so on takes a lot of time. We make tea in big kettles in the big dug-out, which two whole companies use for their cooking, and carry them with a pole through the handles to our platoons. We wash up and wash and shave. Dinner preparation (and consumption) takes two or three hours. Tea too uses up time. It's like camping out and picnicking in the park. This first time (and next too) we have been mixed with some Sussex men who have been here longer and know the business.... It works out that we do most of the fatigue. Afterwards we shall go up alone to a pitch of our own....

Doubtless you anticipate a chronicle of the Kaiser’s men, of barrages and the thunder of the front, but the reality is far more tedious. I have yet to lay eyes on a living foe; no shell has fallen within a hundred paces of my position, and I remain entirely unburdened by any medals for valor. My primary achievement has been to coat myself in a layer of muck so thick it defies vocabulary. Though I labor incessantly, I have neither pulled a trigger nor engaged in the slightest skirmish. Our days are consumed by the drudgery of maintenance—shoring up the breastworks with a cautious eye toward the sharpshooters, lugging heavy tins of rations from the support lines, and extending our saps at an oblique angle into the waste. The simple mechanics of existence—preparing food and tidying our quarters—devour the hours. We boil tea in massive cauldrons within a communal cellar shared by two companies, then hoist the vessels on staves to carry them back to our sections. We scrub our tins, we scrub ourselves, we scrape away the stubble. The ritual of the midday meal occupies half the afternoon. It is less like a crusade and more akin to a damp, extended holiday in the woods. For this rotation, we are interspersed with some veterans from the Kentish ranks who understand the local rhythms. Consequently, we find ourselves assigned the lion's share of the manual labor. Eventually, we shall be granted a sector of our own to hold.

Clarence was twenty-two now, and was my head executive, my right hand. He was a darling; he was equal to anything; there wasn't anything he couldn't turn his hand to. Of late I had been training him for journalism, for the time seemed about right for a start in the newspaper line; nothing big, but just a small weekly for experimental circulation in my civilization-nurseries. He took to it like a duck; there was an editor concealed in him, sure. Already he had doubled himself in one way; he talked sixth century and wrote nineteenth. His journalistic style was climbing, steadily; it was already up to the back settlement Alabama mark, and couldn't be told from the editorial output of that region either by matter or flavor.

Clarence was now twenty-two and served as my chief lieutenant, my indispensable right hand. He was a marvel, capable of rising to any occasion; there was simply no task he couldn't master. Lately, I had been mentoring him in the field of journalism, as the time felt right to launch a newspaper—nothing major, just a modest weekly for trial distribution in my various outposts of progress. He embraced the work instinctively; a true editor was hidden within him. He had already developed a dual nature: he spoke the local sixth-century dialect but wrote in the nineteenth-century style. His journalistic prose was steadily improving; it had already reached the standard of a rural Alabama paper, matching that region's editorial output perfectly in both content and tone.

The farmers were bound to throw in something, to sort of offset my liberality, whether I would or no; so I let them give me a flint and steel; and as soon as they had comfortably bestowed Sandy and me on our horse, I lit my pipe. When the first blast of smoke shot out through the bars of my helmet, all those people broke for the woods, and Sandy went over backwards and struck the ground with a dull thud. They thought I was one of those fire-belching dragons they had heard so much about from knights and other professional liars. I had infinite trouble to persuade those people to venture back within explaining distance. Then I told them that this was only a bit of enchantment which would work harm to none but my enemies. And I promised, with my hand on my heart, that if all who felt no enmity toward me would come forward and pass before me they should see that only those who remained behind would be struck dead. The procession moved with a good deal of promptness. There were no casualties to report, for nobody had curiosity enough to remain behind to see what would happen.

The farmers felt compelled to contribute something, as a sort of return for my generosity, whether I wanted it or not; so I allowed them to give me a flint and steel. And as soon as they had snugly seated Sandy and me on the horse, I struck a light for my pipe. When the first plume of smoke burst out through the bars of my helmet, the whole crowd bolted for the woods, and Sandy toppled backward and hit the ground with a heavy thump. They took me for one of those fire-belching dragons they had heard so much about from knights and other professional liars. I had endless difficulty persuading them to come back within speaking range. At last I told them this was merely a bit of enchantment that would harm no one except my enemies. Then I declared, hand over my heart, that if all who bore me no ill will would come forward and pass in front of me, they should see that only those who stayed behind would be struck dead. The procession got under way with surprising speed. There were no losses to report, for no one was curious enough to linger and see what would happen.

“We must try to track ’em, since we can’t hear ’em,” said Jan, fumbling in his pockets. He struck a light, and held the match to the ground. The rain had been heavier here, and all foot and horse tracks made previous to the storm had been abraded and blurred by the drops, and they were now so many little scoops of water, which reflected the flame of the match like eyes. One set of tracks was fresh and had no water in them; one pair of ruts was also empty, and not small canals, like the others. The footprints forming this recent impression were full of information as to pace; they were in equidistant pairs, three or four feet apart, the right and left foot of each pair being exactly opposite one another.

“We’ve got to follow their trail now that we can’t pick up any sound of them,” Jan said, fumbling through his pockets. He struck a match and held its wavering flame low to the earth. The rain had fallen harder in this spot, so every footprint and horse hoof print left before the storm had been eroded and blurred by the falling drops, reduced to tiny shallow pools of water that glinted back the match’s flame like watching eyes. Only one set of tracks was fresh, with no water pooled inside its indentations; a single pair of wagon ruts was also dry, rather than the narrow little water channels the older ruts had turned into. Those recent footprints held clear clues about the pace of the person who’d left them: they fell in evenly spaced pairs, three or four feet between each set, the right and left foot of each pair perfectly aligned side by side.

She was a plain, pragmatic, industrious woman, yet she carried an abundant gift of faith.

Towards nine o’clock he smoothed his ruffled aspect, and, presenting as respectable and business-like an exterior as he could overlay his natural self with, issued forth to the occupation of the day.

Jerry Cruncher, his eyes bloodshot and his face severe as though he'd spent the night at a gathering that had soured into something far from festive, merely picked at his breakfast instead of eating it, muttering over the food like some caged beast in a zoo. As nine o'clock drew near, he composed his disheveled demeanor, and, cloaking his true nature beneath the most decent and industrious front he could muster, stepped out to take up the day's work.

"The psychology of all this recent insubordination and violence is--curious. Exasperating too.... I don't quite grasp it.... It's the same thing whether you look at the suffrage business or the labour people or at this Irish muddle. People may be too safe. You see we live at the end of a series of secure generations in which none of the great things of life have changed materially. We've grown up with no sense of danger--that is to say, with no sense of responsibility. None of us, none of us--for though I talk my actions belie me--really believe that life can change very fundamentally any more forever. All this",--Mr. Britling waved his arm comprehensively--"looks as though it was bound to go on steadily forever. It seems incredible that the system could be smashed. It seems incredible that anything we can do will ever smash the system. Lady Homartyn, for example, is incapable of believing that she won't always be able to have week-end parties at Claverings, and that the letters and the tea won't come to her bedside in the morning. Or if her imagination goes to the point of supposing that some day _she_ won't be there to receive the tea, it means merely that she supposes somebody else will be. Her pleasant butler may fear to lose his 'situation,' but nothing on earth could make him imagine a time when there will not be a 'situation' for him to lose. Old Asquith thinks that we always have got along, and that we always shall get along by being quietly artful and saying, 'Wait and see.' And it's just because we are all convinced that we are so safe against a general breakdown that we are able to be so recklessly violent in our special cases. Why shouldn't women have the vote? they argue. What does it matter? And bang goes a bomb in Westminster Abbey. Why shouldn't Ulster create an impossible position? And off trots some demented Carsonite to Germany to play at treason on some half word of the German Emperor's and buy half a million rifles....

The mindset behind all this recent defiance and unrest is—peculiar. It’s irritating, too... I can’t quite fathom it. It is the same pattern whether you examine the suffrage movement, the labor strikes, or this disaster in Ireland. It’s possible people have become too secure. You see, we are the products of several stable generations during which the pillars of society haven't truly shifted. We have been raised without any real threat—which is to say, without any real accountability. Not one of us—and I admit my own behavior contradicts my words—genuinely believes that life can ever be fundamentally altered again. All of this"—Mr. Britling made a broad, sweeping gesture—"appears as though it is destined to continue forever. The notion that the system could be destroyed seems unthinkable. It feels impossible that anything we do could actually break it. Lady Homartyn, for instance, cannot conceive of a world where she won't host weekend parties at Claverings, or where her mail and morning tea aren't brought to her bedside. Even if she manages to imagine a day when she herself is gone, she simply assumes someone else will be there to receive that tea. Her charming butler might worry about losing his position, but he cannot imagine a reality where such a position no longer exists to be lost. Old Asquith assumes we have always managed, and will always manage, by being subtly clever and advising everyone to 'wait and see.' And it is precisely because we are so certain that a total societal collapse is impossible that we allow ourselves to be so dangerously reckless in our individual crusades. 'Why shouldn’t women vote?' they ask. 'What’s the harm?' And then a bomb explodes in Westminster Abbey. 'Why shouldn’t Ulster make things impossible?' And suddenly some fanatic Carsonite is rushing off to Germany to flirt with treason over a vague hint from the Kaiser, buying up half a million rifles..."

Three years ago Kraft, Bill Judkins (a poet), and I took our meals at Cypher’s, on Eighth Avenue. I say “took.” When we had money, Cypher got it “off of” us, as he expressed it. We had no credit; we went in, called for food and ate it. We paid or we did not pay. We had confidence in Cypher’s sullenness and smouldering ferocity. Deep down in his sunless soul he was either a prince, a fool or an artist. He sat at a worm-eaten desk, covered with files of waiters’ checks so old that I was sure the bottomest one was for clams that Hendrik Hudson had eaten and paid for. Cypher had the power, in common with Napoleon III. and the goggle-eyed perch, of throwing a film over his eyes, rendering opaque the windows of his soul. Once when we left him unpaid, with egregious excuses, I looked back and saw him shaking with inaudible laughter behind his film. Now and then we paid up back scores.

Three years back, Kraft, Bill Judkins—who wrote verse—and I used to eat at Cypher’s place on Eighth Avenue. I say “used to.” Whenever we had cash, Cypher, as he put it, “took it off of us.” We carried no tab; we simply walked in, ordered, and ate. Sometimes we settled up, sometimes we didn’t. We relied on Cypher’s brooding, pent-up rage. In the lightless depths of his spirit, he was either a nobleman, an idiot, or an artist. He sat behind a worm-gnawed desk buried under stacks of waiters’ checks so ancient I felt certain the bottommost one was for shellfish Hendrik Hudson himself had consumed and paid for. Like Napoleon III and the pop-eyed perch, Cypher could draw a veil across his eyes, clouding the windows of his soul. Once, when we’d walked out without paying, trailing flimsy excuses, I glanced back and saw him quaking with silent laughter behind that veil. Every so often, we cleared our old debts.

“Well, sir!” cried Mr. Lorry, thoroughly heated by his indifference, “business is a very good thing, and a very respectable thing. And, sir, if business imposes its restraints and its silences and impediments, Mr. Darnay as a young gentleman of generosity knows how to make allowance for that circumstance. Mr. Darnay, good night, God bless you, sir! I hope you have been this day preserved for a prosperous and happy life.--Chair there!”

“Now look here, sir!” exclaimed Mr. Hale, his cheeks flushed hot with frustration at the other man’s cool unconcern, “trade is an honorable calling, a thoroughly reputable one. And if, sir, trade demands its own measure of discretion, its unspoken reserve, its unavoidable hindrances, Mr. Thorne, as a young man of generous spirit, certainly understands how to make allowances for such a state of things. Mr. Thorne, a very good night to you, and may heaven smile on you, sir! I trust this day has found you well, and that a long, prosperous, joyful life lies ahead of you.--Bring the chair over here!”

A knock at the door aroused him. He was not asleep, and his mind immediately connected the knock with a telegram, or letter, or perhaps one of the servants bringing back clean clothes from the laundry. He was thinking about Joe and wondering where he was, as he said, “Come in.”

A tapping at the door pulled him back to the present. He hadn't been asleep, and his mind immediately jumped to the idea of a telegram, a letter, or perhaps a staff member returning with his fresh laundry. He was still preoccupied with Joe, wondering about his whereabouts, as he called out, “Come in.”

“Called up the Morses. Miss Morse answered the ’phone. And here I am.” He tugged at his coat pocket and flung a thin volume on the table. “There’s a book, by a poet. Read it and keep it.” And then, in reply to Martin’s protest: “What have I to do with books? I had another hemorrhage this morning. Got any whiskey? No, of course not. Wait a minute.”

“Rang the Halsteads earlier. Read through it, you can keep it.” Then, cutting off Elias’s fumbling protest: “What use would I have for books anyway? I had another bleeding spell this morning. You got any whiskey stashed somewhere? No, of course you don’t. Hang on a minute.”

“Sometimes,” she said, a little wearily, “Clementina tries me. I’m afraid she doesn’t practise enough, and I have to tell her the same things so often. And then she always dresses entirely in white, and that does get monotonous. But Gen. Pinkney is the dearest old man! I wish you could know him, Joe. He comes in sometimes when I am with Clementina at the piano—he is a widower, you know—and stands there pulling his white goatee. ‘And how are the semiquavers and the demisemiquavers progressing?’ he always asks.

“Every now and then,” she said, with a soft note of weariness lacing her tone, “Clementina really tries my patience. I’m afraid she never puts in nearly enough practice, so I end up going over the exact same corrections with her time and time again. What’s more, she wears nothing but head-to-toe white, which does get dreadfully monotonous after a while. But General Pinkney is the dearest old soul! I so wish you could know him, Joe. He stops by sometimes when I’m sitting with Clementina at the piano—he’s a widower, you know—and stands there plucking at his neat white goatee. ‘And how are the semiquavers and demisemiquavers coming along?’ he always asks.”

Wildeve had been brooding ever since they started on the mean estimation in which he was held by his wife’s friends; and it cut his heart severely. As the minutes passed he had gradually drifted into a revengeful intention without knowing the precise moment of forming it. This was to teach Mrs. Yeobright a lesson, as he considered it to be; in other words, to show her if he could that her niece’s husband was the proper guardian of her niece’s money.

Ever since their departure, Wildeve had been fixated on the low opinion his wife’s kin harbored toward him, a slight that galled him to his core. With each passing mile, he found himself sliding toward a retaliatory scheme, though he could not have said exactly when the impulse first took root. His aim was to provide Mrs. Yeobright with what he deemed a necessary lesson: to prove that, as her niece’s husband, he was the only rightful custodian of the woman’s fortune.

As the quiet wake it left upon the water faded, the prayer that had risen from his heart for a merciful judgment of all his poor blindnesses and mistakes ended with the words, “I am the resurrection and the life.”

As my sickness quitted me, I was absorbed by a gloomy and black melancholy that nothing could dissipate. The image of Clerval was for ever before me, ghastly and murdered. More than once the agitation into which these reflections threw me made my friends dread a dangerous relapse. Alas! Why did they preserve so miserable and detested a life? It was surely that I might fulfil my destiny, which is now drawing to a close. Soon, oh, very soon, will death extinguish these throbbings and relieve me from the mighty weight of anguish that bears me to the dust; and, in executing the award of justice, I shall also sink to rest. Then the appearance of death was distant, although the wish was ever present to my thoughts; and I often sat for hours motionless and speechless, wishing for some mighty revolution that might bury me and my destroyer in its ruins.

When my illness had at last departed, I became possessed by a heavy and funereal melancholy from which nothing could deliver me. The figure of Hartwell was ever before my eyes, pale, distorted, and slain. More than once the turmoil into which such meditations drove me caused my companions to fear a fatal relapse. Alas! Why had they spared so wretched and loathed a life? Surely only that I might complete the fate appointed me, which now seemed near its end. Soon, ah, very soon, death would extinguish these palpitations and release me from the vast burden of sorrow that pressed me to the earth; and, while the sentence of justice was fulfilled, I too should descend into repose. At that time death still appeared remote, though the desire for it was never absent from my mind; and I would often remain for hours without motion or speech, longing for some mighty convulsion that might bury both me and my persecutor beneath its ruins.
