Mrs. Sowerberry’s generosity toward Oliver had shown itself in showering upon him all the dirty scraps and leavings that no one else would eat; and so there was much meekness and self-sacrifice in her choosing to remain under Mr. Bumble’s harsh accusation, of which, to do her justice, she was entirely innocent in thought, word, and deed.

It was on a dreary night of November that I beheld the accomplishment of my toils. With an anxiety that almost amounted to agony, I collected the instruments of life around me, that I might infuse a spark of being into the lifeless thing that lay at my feet. It was already one in the morning; the rain pattered dismally against the panes, and my candle was nearly burnt out, when, by the glimmer of the half-extinguished light, I saw the dull yellow eye of the creature open; it breathed hard, and a convulsive motion agitated its limbs.

It was a grim, dreary November night when I finally stood witness to the fruit of all my grueling toil. I was twisted up with an anxiety so sharp it edged close to outright agony as I arranged my life-giving instruments around me, ready to breathe a spark of being into the lifeless form stretched out on the floor at my feet. It was already one in the morning; rain pattered miserably against the windowpanes, and my candle was guttering down to its final dregs, when in the faint, wavering glow of the half-extinguished light, I saw the creature’s murky yellow eye flutter open. It pulled in a harsh, ragged breath, and a sharp convulsive tremor shook through all of its limbs.

He walked at the other’s heels with a swing to his shoulders, and his legs spread unwittingly, as if the level floors were tilting up and sinking down to the heave and lunge of the sea. The wide rooms seemed too narrow for his rolling gait, and to himself he was in terror lest his broad shoulders should collide with the doorways or sweep the bric-a-brac from the low mantel. He recoiled from side to side between the various objects and multiplied the hazards that in reality lodged only in his mind. Between a grand piano and a centre-table piled high with books was space for a half a dozen to walk abreast, yet he essayed it with trepidation. His heavy arms hung loosely at his sides. He did not know what to do with those arms and hands, and when, to his excited vision, one arm seemed liable to brush against the books on the table, he lurched away like a frightened horse, barely missing the piano stool. He watched the easy walk of the other in front of him, and for the first time realized that his walk was different from that of other men. He experienced a momentary pang of shame that he should walk so uncouthly. The sweat burst through the skin of his forehead in tiny beads, and he paused and mopped his bronzed face with his handkerchief.

He tramped at the other man’s heels, shoulders swinging loosely, his legs unconsciously splayed as if the flat floorboards were rolling and dipping with the ocean’s heave and surge. The spacious rooms felt far too cramped for his rolling gait, and he was privately terrified his broad shoulders would bash against doorframes or send the bric-a-brac clattering off the low mantelpiece. He flinched left and right around assorted furnishings, inflating risks that existed entirely in his own head. The gap between a grand piano and a central table stacked high with books was wide enough for half a dozen people to walk side by side, yet he edged through it with skittish dread. His heavy arms hung limp at his sides; he had no idea what to do with those gangly limbs, and when his overwrought gaze spotted his arm brushing perilously close to the volumes stacked on the table, he lurched away like a spooked horse, barely clearing the piano stool. He watched the easy, unselfconscious stride of the man ahead, and for the first time it hit him that his walk was nothing like other men’s. A sharp, fleeting stab of shame pricked him at how uncouth his gait was. Tiny beads of sweat burst across his forehead, and he stopped short, dabbing at his bronzed face with his handkerchief.

With Bathsheba a hastened act was a rash act; but, as does not always happen, time gained was prudence insured. It must be added, however, that time was very seldom gained. At this period the single opinion in the parish on herself and her doings that she valued as sounder than her own was Gabriel Oak’s. And the outspoken honesty of his character was such that on any subject, even that of her love for, or marriage with, another man, the same disinterestedness of opinion might be calculated on, and be had for the asking. Thoroughly convinced of the impossibility of his own suit, a high resolve constrained him not to injure that of another. This is a lover’s most stoical virtue, as the lack of it is a lover’s most venial sin. Knowing he would reply truly she asked the question, painful as she must have known the subject would be. Such is the selfishness of some charming women. Perhaps it was some excuse for her thus torturing honesty to her own advantage, that she had absolutely no other sound judgment within easy reach.

For Bathsheba, any choice made in a rush was always a reckless one; and unusually, whenever she gave herself time to deliberate, she was guaranteed to act sensibly. The catch, though, was that she almost never carved out that extra time for herself. Right then, the only person in the whole parish whose take on her and her choices she trusted more than her own judgment was Gabriel Oak. His character was so unflinchingly, bluntly honest that she could count on him to give a fully unbiased take no matter the topic, even if it was about her having feelings for another man, or marrying someone else—all she had to do was ask. He was fully convinced there was no chance she’d ever return his own romantic feelings, so he held himself to a strict, noble code not to sabotage any other suitor’s shot with her. That’s the most stoic, unselfish virtue a person in love can have, just as failing to live up to that standard is the most forgivable fault a lover can commit. She knew he’d give her a truthful answer, so she asked him the question anyway, even though she had to have realized how much that topic would hurt him. That’s the kind of casual selfishness some utterly captivating women carry with them. To be fair, there was a small excuse for her leaning on his honesty for her own gain even when it pained him: she truly didn’t have anyone else whose good sense she could easily access to weigh in on the matter.

“His rooms we’re going to. One time professor—fired from university—usual story. A mind like a steel trap. Makes his living any old way. I know he’s been a street fakir when he was down. Unscrupulous. Rob a corpse of a shroud—anything. Difference between him and the bourgeoisie is that he robs without illusion. He’ll talk Nietzsche, or Schopenhauer, or Kant, or anything, but the only thing in this world, not excepting Mary, that he really cares for, is his monism. Haeckel is his little tin god. The only way to insult him is to take a slap at Haeckel.”

We’re heading to his place right now. He used to be a professor, got canned from the university, same old run-of-the-mill story. He’s got a mind as sharp as a steel trap, never lets a single detail slip past him. He scrapes by doing whatever odd work he can scare up, and I know for a fact he worked as a cheap street con artist when he hit rock bottom. The man’s completely unscrupulous—would steal the burial shroud right off a corpse if it suited him, no questions asked, no guilt whatsoever. The only real difference between him and your typical bourgeois type is that he doesn’t hide behind self-serving illusions when he takes what he wants. He’ll hold forth for hours about Nietzsche, Schopenhauer, Kant, any philosopher you bring up, but the only thing in the entire world he actually cares about—not even Mary is an exception—is his monist philosophy. Haeckel is his personal little tin god, the one figure he reveres without question. The only surefire way to insult him is to talk trash about Haeckel.

Hockey as it was played at the Dower House at Matching's Easy before the war, was a game combining danger, physical exercise and kindliness in a very high degree. Except for the infant in the perambulator and the outwardly calm but inwardly resentful aunt, who wheeled the child up and down in a position of maximum danger just behind the unnetted goal, every one was involved. Quite able-bodied people acquainted with the game played forward, the less well-informed played a defensive game behind the forward line, elderly, infirm, and bulky persons were used chiefly as obstacles in goal. Several players wore padded leg-guards, and all players were assumed to have them and expected to behave accordingly.

Field hockey as it was played at Ashcombe Manor in the days before the war was a pastime in which peril, bodily exertion, and benevolence were blended in very generous measure. With the exception of the baby in the pram and the apparently placid but inwardly indignant aunt who pushed it to and fro in a spot of maximum hazard just behind the goal without a net, everyone took part. Those in full possession of their limbs and some acquaintance with the rules went forward; the less informed played in a more retiring manner behind the front line; and the elderly, the frail, and the overweight were employed chiefly as impediments in front of goal. A number of the players wore padded shin-guards, and all the rest were taken to possess them and expected to conduct themselves on that understanding.

Instantly she understood. Already on four different occasions since Teddy's disappearance she had seen the good man coming towards her, always with a manifest decision, always with the same faltering doubt as now. Often in their happy days had she and Teddy discussed him and derided him and rejoiced over him. They had agreed he was as good as Jane Austen's Mr. Collins. He really was very like Mr. Collins, except that he was plumper. And now, it was as if he was transparent to her hard defensive scrutiny. She knew he was impelled by his tradition, by his sense of fitness, by his respect for his calling, to offer her his ministrations and consolations, to say his large flat amiabilities over her and pat her kindly with his hands. And she knew too that he dreaded her. She knew that the dear old humbug knew at the bottom of his heart quite certainly that he was a poor old humbug, and that she was in his secret. And at the bottom of his heart he found himself too honest to force his poor platitudes upon any who would not be glad of them. If she could have been glad of them he would have had no compunction. He was a man divided against himself; failing to carry through his rich pretences, dismayed.

She got it right away. In the stretch of time since Teddy vanished, she’d already watched this decent man approach her four separate times, every time visibly set on doing what he believed was his obligation, every time carrying the same unsteady, hesitant doubt he wore on his face right now. Back when she and Teddy were still happy and building their life together, they’d brought him up constantly, teasing him, mocking his stuffy manner, even laughing fondly at his harmless absurdities. They’d both agreed he was a perfect match for Jane Austen’s Mr. Collins, if Mr. Collins had put on a few extra pounds around the middle. Right now, under her sharp, defensive stare, he might as well have been made of glass. She knew he was pushed by the long-held customs he abided by, his rigid sense of what was appropriate, his devotion to his role, to offer her his official support and condolences, to recite his bland, overblown pleasantries for her benefit and pat her arm in that soft, well-meaning way of his. She also knew he was afraid of her. That sweet old fraud was fully aware deep in his bones that he was nothing but a sad, out-of-touch phony, and that she was completely wise to his whole act. Deep down, he was too honest at his core to foist his tired, empty platitudes on anyone who clearly had no interest in hearing them. If she’d seemed glad for his words, he would have delivered them with no guilt or hesitation at all. He was a man torn apart by his own conflicting impulses, too flustered to pull off his usual over-the-top performative kindness, left standing there uneasy and defeated.

“Miss Manette, it _was_ I. And you will see how truly I spoke of myself just now, in saying I had no feelings, and that all the relations I hold with my fellow-creatures are mere business relations, when you reflect that I have never seen you since. No; you have been the ward of Tellson’s House since, and I have been busy with the other business of Tellson’s House since. Feelings! I have no time for them, no chance of them. I pass my whole life, miss, in turning an immense pecuniary Mangle.”

“Miss Hale, that was indeed I. You will grasp how truthful I was only moments ago, when I said I carry no personal attachments, and that every bond I hold with my fellow men is purely transactional, the second you recall I have not so much as glimpsed you since that day. No; you have been the ward of Hargrove & Sons Merchant Bank all this while, and I have been swamped with the rest of Hargrove & Sons’ affairs ever since. Attachments? I have no time to spare for them, no opportunity to nurture them. I spend my entire existence, miss, turning the crank of a colossal, coin-clogged mangle.”

The impression Mr. Britling had made upon Mr. Direck was one of indefatigable happiness. But there were times when Mr. Britling was called upon to pay for his general cheerful activity in lump sums of bitter sorrow. There were nights--and especially after seasons of exceptional excitement and nervous activity--when the reckoning would be presented and Mr. Britling would welter prostrate and groaning under a stormy sky of unhappiness--active insatiable unhappiness--a beating with rods.

The image Mr. Direck formed of Mr. Britling was that of a man buoyed by unflagging contentment. Yet there were moments when fate exacted from Mr. Britling a heavy toll for his prevailing lightness of spirit, delivering it all at once in dark, concentrated grief. On certain nights—particularly after stretches of heightened fervor and mental strain—the bill would come due, and Mr. Britling would lie sprawled and moaning beneath a tempestuous heaven of misery, a restless, ravenous misery that lashed him like whips.

She left me, and I continued some time walking up and down the passages of the house and inspecting every corner that might afford a retreat to my adversary. But I discovered no trace of him and was beginning to conjecture that some fortunate chance had intervened to prevent the execution of his menaces when suddenly I heard a shrill and dreadful scream. It came from the room into which Elizabeth had retired. As I heard it, the whole truth rushed into my mind, my arms dropped, the motion of every muscle and fibre was suspended; I could feel the blood trickling in my veins and tingling in the extremities of my limbs. This state lasted but for an instant; the scream was repeated, and I rushed into the room.

After she went inside, I spent some time pacing the hallways of the house, searching every corner that might offer a hiding place for my enemy. Finding no sign of him, I began to hope that some stroke of luck had prevented him from carrying out his threats, when suddenly a sharp, terrifying shriek rang out. It came from the room where Elizabeth had gone to rest. The moment I heard it, the full horror of the situation crashed down on me; my arms fell limp, and every muscle in my body froze. I could feel the blood crawling through my veins, a cold tingling sensation reaching my very fingertips. This paralysis lasted only a second; the scream echoed again, and I bolted toward the room.

She passed with her candle into an inner room, where, among other utensils, were two large brown pans, containing together perhaps a hundredweight of liquid honey, the produce of the bees during the foregoing summer. On a shelf over the pans was a smooth and solid yellow mass of a hemispherical form, consisting of beeswax from the same take of honey. Susan took down the lump, and cutting off several thin slices, heaped them in an iron ladle, with which she returned to the living-room, and placed the vessel in the hot ashes of the fireplace. As soon as the wax had softened to the plasticity of dough she kneaded the pieces together. And now her face became more intent. She began moulding the wax; and it was evident from her manner of manipulation that she was endeavouring to give it some preconceived form. The form was human.

Candle in hand, she stepped into a back room where, amid other household items, stood two broad brown basins holding close to a hundred pounds of liquid honey, the bees’ yield from the summer just past. On a shelf above them rested a firm, yellow hemisphere of beeswax drawn from that same harvest. Susan lifted the block down, sliced off several thin pieces, and piled them into an iron ladle. Carrying it back to the living-room, she set the ladle among the hot embers of the hearth. Once the wax softened to the pliancy of dough, she worked the fragments together. Her expression now sharpened with concentration. She began to shape the wax, and the way her hands moved made it clear she was striving to model a form she already held in mind. The form was a human one.

He sat crouching over a cold hearth, wrapped in an old torn coverlet, with his face turned towards a wasting candle that stood upon a table by his side. His right hand was raised to his lips, and as, absorbed in thought, he bit his long black nails, he disclosed among his toothless gums a few such fangs as should have been a dog’s or rat’s.

He hunkered low over a dead, cold hearth, swaddled in a frayed, tattered old coverlet, his face turned toward a guttering candle propped on the small table at his elbow. His right hand was lifted to his mouth; lost entirely in his own thoughts as he gnawed at his overgrown, blackened nails, he revealed between his mostly toothless gums a handful of sharp, jagged fangs that belonged far more properly to a stray dog or a scavenging rat.

Mr. Chitling was older in years than the Dodger: having perhaps numbered eighteen winters; but there was a degree of deference in his deportment towards that young gentleman which seemed to indicate that he felt himself conscious of a slight inferiority in point of genius and professional aquirements. He had small twinkling eyes, and a pock-marked face; wore a fur cap, a dark corduroy jacket, greasy fustian trousers, and an apron. His wardrobe was, in truth, rather out of repair; but he excused himself to the company by stating that his “time” was only out an hour before; and that, in consequence of having worn the regimentals for six weeks past, he had not been able to bestow any attention on his private clothes. Mr. Chitling added, with strong marks of irritation, that the new way of fumigating clothes up yonder was infernal unconstitutional, for it burnt holes in them, and there was no remedy against the county. The same remark he considered to apply to the regulation mode of cutting the hair: which he held to be decidedly unlawful. Mr. Chitling wound up his observations by stating that he had not touched a drop of anything for forty-two moral long hard-working days; and that he “wished he might be busted if he warn’t as dry as a lime-basket.”

Mr. Chitling was older than the Dodger, perhaps by several years; he may have been about eighteen. Yet there was something in the way he carried himself toward that young gentleman that suggested he was aware of a slight inferiority in matters of wit and professional skill. He had small, restless eyes and a face pitted with marks; he wore a fur cap, a dark corduroy jacket, greasy fustian trousers, and an apron. His clothes, in truth, were in a poor state; but he made his excuse to the company by saying that his “time” had only run out an hour earlier, and that, after wearing the regimentals for the past six weeks, he had not had a chance to give any attention to his own wardrobe. Mr. Chitling went on, with evident annoyance, to declare that the new method of fumigating clothes up there was infernally unconstitutional, because it burned holes in them, and there was no redress against the county. He said the same complaint applied to the approved way of cutting the hair, which he considered flatly unlawful. He ended by saying that he had not taken a drop of anything for forty-two long, hard, moral days of labor, and that he “wished he might be busted if he warn’t as dry as a lime-basket.”

“My name, sir,” said the old gentleman, speaking _like_ a gentleman, “my name, sir, is Brownlow. Permit me to inquire the name of the magistrate who offers a gratuitous and unprovoked insult to a respectable person, under the protection of the bench.” Saying this, Mr. Brownlow looked around the office as if in search of some person who would afford him the required information.

“My name, sir,” said the old gentleman, speaking like a gentleman, “is Brownlow. Allow me to ask the name of the magistrate who is ready to offer an unprovoked and gratuitous insult to a respectable man, while sheltering himself behind the bench.” As he said this, Mr. Brownlow glanced about the office, as though looking for someone who could give him the information he wanted.

After a short return of the stupor in which he had been so long plunged, Oliver: urged by a creeping sickness at his heart, which seemed to warn him that if he lay there, he must surely die: got upon his feet, and essayed to walk. His head was dizzy, and he staggered to and fro like a drunken man. But he kept up, nevertheless, and, with his head drooping languidly on his breast, went stumbling onward, he knew not whither.

Oliver drifted back from the stupor that had held him so long, only to feel a slow, sickening dread coil around his heart—a dread that whispered he would surely die if he stayed motionless. He forced himself upright and tried to walk. Dizziness swam through his head, and he reeled and swayed like a drunkard. Yet he remained on his feet, and with his head lolling heavily against his chest, he staggered forward, blind to where his steps might lead.

Only a little while ago Cissie's imagination might have been captured by so romantic a dream. She was still but a year or so out of the stage of melodrama. But she was out of it. She was growing up now to a subtler wisdom. People, she was beginning to realise, do not do these simple things. They make vows of devotion and they are not real vows of devotion; they love--quite honestly--and qualify. There are no great revenges but only little mean ones; no life-long vindications except the unrelenting vengeance of the law. There is no real concentration of people's lives anywhere such as romance demands. There is change, there is forgetfulness. Everywhere there is dispersal. Even to the tragic story of Teddy would come the modifications of time. Even to the wickedness of the German princes would presently be added some conflicting aspects. Could Letty keep things for years in her mind, hard and terrible, as they were now? Surely they would soften; other things would overlay them....

Not long ago, such a poetic fantasy might have captivated Cissie’s imagination. She was only a year or two past her phase of theatrical melodrama, but she had definitely moved on. She was maturing now, gaining a more nuanced insight. People, she was starting to understand, rarely act with such simplicity. They swear oaths of loyalty that aren't truly binding; they love with genuine intent, yet always with reservations. There are no grand acts of vengeance, only small, spiteful ones; no lifelong crusades for justice, except for the cold, persistent machinery of the law. Life never possesses the intense focus that romantic stories demand. Instead, there is transition and a tendency to forget. Everything eventually dissolves. Even Teddy’s tragic history would be altered by the steady march of time. Even the cruelty of the German princes would eventually be viewed through more complicated lenses. Was it possible for Letty to maintain her current harsh and horrific perspective for years? Surely the intensity would fade; new layers of life would eventually cover it all.

A consciousness of a vast impassivity in all which lay around him took possession even of Yeobright in his wild walk towards Alderworth. He had once before felt in his own person this overpowering of the fervid by the inanimate; but then it had tended to enervate a passion far sweeter than that which at present pervaded him. It was once when he stood parting from Eustacia in the moist still levels beyond the hills.

A sense of the profound neutrality in the surrounding world gripped Julian during his frantic journey toward Blackwood. He had experienced this crushing of human heat by the lifeless material of the earth once before, though in that earlier instance, the sensation had merely dampened a sentiment far gentler than the one now consuming him. It had occurred as he stood taking leave of Elara within the damp, hushed lowlands that lay beyond the crest of the hills.

Henery Fray spoke, exhibiting miserable eyes at the same time: “I don’t see why a maid should take a husband when she’s bold enough to fight her own battles, and don’t want a home; for ’tis keeping another woman out. But let it be, for ’tis a pity he and she should trouble two houses.”

Tobin Mear spoke, his heavy, red-rimmed eyes glistening with quiet wretchedness as he mumbled: “I can’t fathom why a lass would take a husband when she’s stout enough to fight her own battles, and has no use for a ready-made home; all it does is shut some other poor woman out of a match. But let it be, for ’tis a pity he and she would go on to trouble two separate households.”

“And as such,” quoth Mr. Lorry, whom the counsel learned in the law had now shouldered back into the group, just as he had previously shouldered him out of it--“as such I will appeal to Doctor Manette, to break up this conference and order us all to our homes. Miss Lucie looks ill, Mr. Darnay has had a terrible day, we are worn out.”

“Therefore,” murmured Mr. Lorry, now nudged back into the circle by the very barrister who had earlier edged him aside—“therefore I shall petition Doctor Manette to dissolve this gathering and bid us each return to our dwellings. Miss Lucie appears unwell, Mr. Darnay has endured a harrowing day, and we are all spent.”

“Oh, it is true enough. I may as well be hung for a sheep as a lamb (an old country saying, not of much account, but it will do for a rough soldier), and so I will speak my mind, regardless of your pleasure, and without hoping or intending to get your pardon. Why, Miss Everdene, it is in this manner that your good looks may do more harm than good in the world.” The sergeant looked down the mead in critical abstraction. “Probably some one man on an average falls in love with each ordinary woman. She can marry him: he is content, and leads a useful life. Such women as you a hundred men always covet—your eyes will bewitch scores on scores into an unavailing fancy for you—you can only marry one of that many. Out of these say twenty will endeavour to drown the bitterness of despised love in drink; twenty more will mope away their lives without a wish or attempt to make a mark in the world, because they have no ambition apart from their attachment to you; twenty more—the susceptible person myself possibly among them—will be always draggling after you, getting where they may just see you, doing desperate things. Men are such constant fools! The rest may try to get over their passion with more or less success. But all these men will be saddened. And not only those ninety-nine men, but the ninety-nine women they might have married are saddened with them. There’s my tale. That’s why I say that a woman so charming as yourself, Miss Everdene, is hardly a blessing to her race.”

“Oh, it’s quite true enough. I may as well be hanged for a sheep as for a lamb—an old country saying, not worth much, but it suits a rough soldier—and so I’ll say what I think, without regard to whether it pleases you, and without expecting or meaning to win your pardon. Why, Miss Everdene, it is in this way that your beauty may do more harm than good in the world.” The sergeant gazed down the meadow in reflective disapproval. “As a rule, perhaps one man falls in love with every ordinary woman. She can marry him; he is satisfied, and goes on to lead a useful life. But women such as you are desired by a hundred men at once—your eyes will cast scores and scores into hopeless admiration for you—you can marry only one of them. Of those, let us say twenty will try to drown the pain of rejected love in drink; twenty more will waste their lives in idleness, with no desire and no effort to make any mark in the world, because they have no ambition beyond their devotion to you; twenty more—the impressionable sort, myself perhaps among them—will be forever trailing after you, going wherever they can merely catch sight of you, doing desperate things. Men are such hopeless fools! The rest may manage, with greater or less success, to get over their passion. But all these men will be made unhappy. And not only those ninety-nine men, but the ninety-nine women they might have married are made unhappy too. There’s my story. That is why I say that a woman as lovely as you, Miss Everdene, is scarcely a blessing to her kind.”

I passed the night wretchedly. Sometimes my pulse beat so quickly and hardly that I felt the palpitation of every artery; at others, I nearly sank to the ground through languor and extreme weakness. Mingled with this horror, I felt the bitterness of disappointment; dreams that had been my food and pleasant rest for so long a space were now become a hell to me; and the change was so rapid, the overthrow so complete!

I passed the night in misery. At times my pulse throbbed so fast and feebly that I could feel the beating of every artery; at others, I seemed almost to sink to the ground from languor and utter weakness. Along with this terror, I suffered the sharp sting of disappointment. Dreams that had long been my sustenance and my pleasant repose had now become a hell to me; and so swift was the transformation, so total the ruin!

A factitious reply had been again upon his lips, but it was again suspended, and he looked at her with an arrested eye. The truth was, that as she now stood—excited, wild, and honest as the day—her alluring beauty bore out so fully the epithets he had bestowed upon it that he was quite startled at his temerity in advancing them as false. He said mechanically, “Ah, why?” and continued to look at her.

Another invented answer had risen once more to his lips, but it was once more checked, and he regarded her with a look suddenly arrested. The truth was that, as she stood there now—excited, untamed, and as candid as daylight itself—her irresistible beauty justified so completely the very names he had flung at it that he was almost startled by his own audacity in calling them false. He murmured, almost involuntarily, “Ah, why?” and kept his eyes upon her.

By this time Mr. Direck was beginning to master the simple strategy of the sport. He was also beginning to master the fact that Cecily was the quickest, nimblest, most indefatigable player on the field. He scouted for her and passed to her. He developed tacit understandings with her. Ideas of protecting her had gone to the four winds of Heaven. Against them Teddy and a sidecar girl with Raeburn in support made a memorable struggle. Teddy was as quick as a cat. "Four-Three" looked like winning, but then Teddy and the tall Indian and Mrs. Teddy pulled square. They almost repeated this feat and won, but Mr. Manning saved the situation with an immense oblique hit that sent the ball to Mr. Direck. He ran with the ball up to Raeburn and then dodged and passed to Cecily. There was a lively struggle to the left; the ball was hit out by Mr. Raeburn and thrown in by a young Britling; lost by the forwards and rescued by the padded lady. Forward again! This time will do it!

At this point, Mr. Thorne was starting to grasp the fundamental maneuvers of the game. He was also recognizing that Beatrice was the most agile, swift, and tireless participant on the pitch. He served as her scout and distributor, developing a silent rapport with her. Any impulse to shield her had vanished completely. Facing them, Arthur and the girl from the sidecar, bolstered by Sterling, put up a formidable fight. Arthur was as lithe as a leopard. A "Four-Three" result seemed imminent, but then Arthur, the lanky Sikh, and Mrs. Arthur leveled the score. They nearly achieved the same feat to win, but Mr. Fletcher saved the day with a massive diagonal strike that found Mr. Thorne. He sprinted toward Sterling, feinted, and centered the ball to Beatrice. A frantic skirmish broke out on the left; Sterling knocked the ball out of bounds, and a young Pemberton threw it back in; the front line fumbled, but the cushioned matron recovered it. Onward once more! This final push will surely be the one!

In pursuance of this determination, little Oliver, to his excessive astonishment, was released from bondage, and ordered to put himself into a clean shirt. He had hardly achieved this very unusual gymnastic performance, when Mr. Bumble brought him, with his own hands, a basin of gruel, and the holiday allowance of two ounces and a quarter of bread. At this tremendous sight, Oliver began to cry very piteously: thinking, not unnaturally, that the board must have determined to kill him for some useful purpose, or they never would have begun to fatten him up in that way.

Following through on this decision, little Oliver, to his utter amazement, was freed from his confinement and told to change into a fresh shirt. He had barely completed this remarkably unfamiliar physical feat when Mr. Bumble personally delivered a bowl of gruel and the special holiday portion of two and a quarter ounces of bread. Confronted by this overwhelming sight, Oliver began to weep most sorrowfully, convinced, not unreasonably, that the board must have resolved to dispatch him for some practical end—otherwise they would never have started to fatten him up in such a manner.

“‘I have done— Put by the lute. Song and singing soon are over As the airy shades that hover In among the purple clover. I have done— Put by the lute. Once I sang as early thrushes Sing among the dewy bushes; Now I’m mute. I am like a weary linnet, For my throat has no song in it; I have had my singing minute. I have done. Put by the lute.’”

“I have finished—set the lute aside. Song and singing pass away quickly, like the airy shadows that drift among the purple clover. I have finished—set the lute aside. Once I sang like the early thrushes singing in the dewy bushes; now I am silent. I am like an exhausted linnet, for there is no song left in my throat; my moment for singing has come and gone. I have finished. Set the lute aside.”

“It’s clever all right,” was the retort, “but it’s fair, too. The only men who know their Latin are the apothecaries, the lawyers, and the Latin professors. And if Martin wants to be one of them, I miss my guess. But what’s all that got to do with Herbert Spencer anyway? Martin’s just discovered Spencer, and he’s wild over him. Why? Because Spencer is taking him somewhere. Spencer couldn’t take me anywhere, nor you. We haven’t got anywhere to go. You’ll get married some day, and I’ll have nothing to do but keep track of the lawyers and business agents who will take care of the money my father’s going to leave me.”

“Smart enough, I’ll give you that,” came the sharp retort, “but it’s perfectly fair, too. The only people who actually know their Latin are pharmacists, barristers, and classics department lecturers. And if Elias wants to end up as any of those, I’ve badly misjudged him. But what on earth does any of that have to do with Herbert Spencer, anyway? Elias just stumbled across Spencer’s work a little while back, and he’s utterly wild for it. Why? Because Spencer gives him a path to follow, somewhere to go. Spencer would never lead me anywhere, and he wouldn’t do a thing for you either. We don’t have anyplace we need to reach in the first place. You’ll get married and settle down eventually, and all I’ll ever have to occupy myself with is checking in on the solicitors and financial managers who handle the fortune my father’s set to leave me.”

The third draught that I craved from the fount of knowledge was enlightenment concerning the character known as A Man About Town. He was more vague in my mind than a type should be. We must have a concrete idea of anything, even if it be an imaginary idea, before we can comprehend it. Now, I have a mental picture of John Doe that is as clear as a steel engraving. His eyes are weak blue; he wears a brown vest and a shiny black serge coat. He stands always in the sunshine chewing something; and he keeps half-shutting his pocket knife and opening it again with his thumb. And, if the Man Higher Up is ever found, take my assurance for it, he will be a large, pale man with blue wristlets showing under his cuffs, and he will be sitting to have his shoes polished within sound of a bowling alley, and there will be somewhere about him turquoises.

The third sip I desired from the wellspring of understanding was illumination regarding the figure called A Man About Town. He lingered in my thoughts more indistinctly than any archetype should. We must possess a tangible notion of a thing, even an invented one, before we can truly grasp it. For instance, I carry a mental image of John Doe as crisp as an etched copperplate. His gaze is a watery blue; he sports a tan waistcoat and a glossy black serge jacket. He is forever poised in sunlight, chewing on something, his pocket knife half-closed and flicked open again with his thumb. And should the Man Higher Up ever be discovered, you may rely on my word—he will be a tall, pallid gentleman with blue wristbands peeking beneath his sleeves, seated to have his boots buffed within earshot of a skittle alley, and somewhere near him there will be turquoises.

The ultimate and saddest singularity of woman’s effort and invention was reached when, with a quickened breathing, she rose to a stooping posture, and, resting her two little arms upon the shoulders of the dog, leant firmly thereon, and murmured stimulating words. Whilst she sorrowed in her heart she cheered with her voice, and what was stranger than that the strong should need encouragement from the weak was that cheerfulness should be so well stimulated by such utter dejection. Her friend moved forward slowly, and she with small mincing steps moved forward beside him, half her weight being thrown upon the animal. Sometimes she sank as she had sunk from walking erect, from the crutches, from the rails. The dog, who now thoroughly understood her desire and her incapacity, was frantic in his distress on these occasions; he would tug at her dress and run forward. She always called him back, and it was now to be observed that the woman listened for human sounds only to avoid them. It was evident that she had an object in keeping her presence on the road and her forlorn state unknown.

The most grievous, singular apex of Elara’s desperate ingenuity arrived when, chest rising and falling fast, she pushed herself up into a hunched stance, rested her two slight forearms on the hound’s broad shoulders, leaned her weight steadily against him, and murmured soft, urging words under her breath. Though her chest ached with unspoken grief, her tone stayed bright and buoyant, and there was no stranger paradox than that a creature so sturdy should draw courage from one so frail, or that warm hope could be kindled by a spirit so crushed by despair. Her companion paced forward at a crawl, and she picked her way beside him in tiny, shuffling steps, half her mass bearing down on his broad frame. Every now and then her knees buckled, just as they had when she’d tried walking unassisted, when she’d relied on crutches, when she’d clung to roadside fence posts for support. The hound, who now fully grasped what she needed and how little she could manage on her own, grew nearly frantic with worry when she stumbled; he would yank at the hem of her skirt and dart a few paces ahead, as if trying to pull her onward. She always called him back to her side, and anyone watching might have noticed she now strained to catch the sound of other people only to veer out of their way. It was clear she was determined to keep her presence on the lonely lane and her wretched condition hidden from all passersby.

It was Mr. Britling's case for Hugh that he was something exceptional, something exceptionally good, and that the peculiar need there was to take care of him was due to a delicacy of nerve and fibre that was ultimately a virtue. The boy was quick, quick to hear, quick to move, very accurate in his swift way, he talked unusually soon, he began to sketch at an early age with an incurable roughness and a remarkable expressiveness. That he was sometimes ungainly, often untidy, that he would become so mentally preoccupied as to be uncivil to people about him, that he caught any malaise that was going, was all a part of that. The sense of Mrs. Britling's unexpressed criticisms, the implied contrasts with the very jolly, very uninspired younger family, kept up a nervous desire in Mr. Britling for evidences and manifestations of Hugh's quality. Not always with happy results; it caused much mutual irritation, but not enough to prevent the growth of a real response on Hugh's part to his father's solicitude. The youngster knew and felt that his father was his father just as certainly as he felt that Mrs. Britling was not his mother. To his father he brought his successes and to his father he appealed.

Mr. Hale’s conviction about Elias was that the boy was no ordinary child, marked by an uncommon goodness, and that the extra care he required stemmed from a fragility of nerve and temperament that was, at its core, a rare strength. Elias was sharp: quick to catch a stray sound, quick to dart into motion, startlingly precise in all his fast, impulsive actions. He spoke far earlier than most children, and took up drawing before he was even school-aged, his work marked by a stubborn, unpolished roughness and a striking, unteachable knack for conveying feeling. That he was occasionally clumsy, frequently unkempt, so lost in his own thoughts he could come off as rude to those around him, and prone to catching every passing ailment, all fit into this same pattern of delicate, overactive inner life. The quiet, unvoiced criticism from Mrs. Hale, the unspoken comparisons she drew between Elias and her own much cheerier, thoroughly unremarkable younger children, kept Mr. Hale constantly hungry for small signs and demonstrations of Elias’s unique gifts. This eagerness did not always turn out well; it sparked a great deal of mutual irritation, but never enough to snuff out the genuine, growing response Elias held for his father in return for his steady solicitude. The boy knew with unshakable certainty that his father was his father, just as he knew without question that Mrs. Hale would never be his mother. He brought every small success to his father, and it was his father he turned to with every appeal for help or understanding.

“Well, now, you’d hardly believe it, but that man—our Miss Everdene’s father—was one of the ficklest husbands alive, after a while. Understand? ’a didn’t want to be fickle, but he couldn’t help it. The pore feller were faithful and true enough to her in his wish, but his heart would rove, do what he would. He spoke to me in real tribulation about it once. ‘Coggan,’ he said, ‘I could never wish for a handsomer woman than I’ve got, but feeling she’s ticketed as my lawful wife, I can’t help my wicked heart wandering, do what I will.’ But at last I believe he cured it by making her take off her wedding-ring and calling her by her maiden name as they sat together after the shop was shut, and so ’a would get to fancy she was only his sweetheart, and not married to him at all. And as soon as he could thoroughly fancy he was doing wrong and committing the seventh, ’a got to like her as well as ever, and they lived on a perfect picture of mutel love.”

Oh, you’d scarce believe it, but that man—our Miss Everdene’s own father—turned out to be one of the most fickle husbands walking the earth, after a while. Mark this: he never meant to be unsteady, he just couldn’t help himself. Poor fellow, every part of him wanted to be loyal and true to his wife, but his heart would wander no matter how hard he tried to rein it in. He poured out his trouble to me once, proper wretched over the whole mess. “Coggan,” he said, “I could never wish for a handsomer woman than the one I’ve got, but the second I remember she’s my lawfully wedded wife, my wicked heart drifts off, no matter what I do to stop it.” But I think he cured himself of it in the end, you see: he had her take off her wedding ring, and he’d call her by her maiden name every night once the shop was shut and they were sitting together alone. That way, he could fool himself into thinking she was only his sweetheart, not his spouse at all. The minute he could fully believe he was doing wrong and breaking the seventh commandment, he grew as fond of her as he’d ever been, and they went on to live as perfect a picture of quiet, devoted love as you could ever find.

With the next morning, there came a rumour, that two men and a boy were in the cage at Kingston, who had been apprehended over night under suspicious circumstances; and to Kingston Messrs. Blathers and Duff journeyed accordingly. The suspicious circumstances, however, resolving themselves, on investigation, into the one fact, that they had been discovered sleeping under a haystack; which, although a great crime, is only punishable by imprisonment, and is, in the merciful eye of the English law, and its comprehensive love of all the King’s subjects, held to be no satisfactory proof, in the absence of all other evidence, that the sleeper, or sleepers, have committed burglary accompanied with violence, and have therefore rendered themselves liable to the punishment of death; Messrs. Blathers and Duff came back again, as wise as they went.

At daybreak, word spread that three strangers—two grown men and a lad—had been locked in the Kingston jail, seized the previous evening on questionable grounds. Accordingly, Messrs. Crane and Tully set off for Kingston. Yet the questionable grounds, upon closer scrutiny, dwindled to a single detail: they had been found asleep beneath a hayrick. Now, though this is indeed a serious offense, it warrants only confinement, and in the charitable view of English law, with its ample affection for every subject of the Crown, mere slumber under straw, absent any further proof, cannot be taken as certain evidence that the slumberer or slumberers have perpetrated a violent burglary and thus made themselves answerable to the gallows. Crane and Tully, therefore, returned no wiser than when they departed.

Once they were seated in the living-room, he began to get on easily—more easily by far than he had expected. She made it easy for him; and the gracious spirit with which she did it made him love her more madly than ever. They talked first of the borrowed books, of the Swinburne he was devoted to, and of the Browning he did not understand; and she led the conversation on from subject to subject, while she pondered the problem of how she could be of help to him. She had thought of this often since their first meeting. She wanted to help him. He made a call upon her pity and tenderness that no one had ever made before, and the pity was not so much derogatory of him as maternal in her. Her pity could not be of the common sort, when the man who drew it was so much man as to shock her with maidenly fears and set her mind and pulse thrilling with strange thoughts and feelings. The old fascination of his neck was there, and there was sweetness in the thought of laying her hands upon it. It seemed still a wanton impulse, but she had grown more used to it. She did not dream that in such guise new-born love would epitomize itself. Nor did she dream that the feeling he excited in her was love. She thought she was merely interested in him as an unusual type possessing various potential excellencies, and she even felt philanthropic about it.

Once they’d settled into the faded velvet armchairs of her front parlor, the conversation unfolded far more easily than he’d dared to anticipate. She met him halfway at every turn, the unforced warmth of her manner stoking his adoration to a giddy, dizzying pitch he’d never thought possible. They first chatted about the stack of books he’d borrowed the week prior: the wild, throbbing verse of Dowson he’d memorized half of by heart, and the layered, allusive poems of Rossetti he still couldn’t parse. She steered the talk gently from topic to topic, all while turning over the question of how she might lift him up, a problem she’d mulled constantly since their first meeting by the town’s frozen mill pond. She ached to help him. He tugged at a vein of pity and tenderness no other person had ever reached in her, and that softness was not belittling to him so much as it was gently maternal, rooted in care, not condescension. It could never be the ordinary sort of pity, not when the man who inspired it was so unapologetically vividly male that he roused fluttering, girlish alarms in her, sent her thoughts spinning and her pulse thrumming with odd, unrecognizable feelings. She was still drawn to the strong, sun-browned slope of his neck, and the thought of pressing her palms flat to it hummed with quiet sweetness. It still felt like a bold, unruly impulse, but she’d grown far more comfortable with the thought of it lately. She never guessed that new, fledgling love so often dressed itself in exactly these small, unnameable, half-wanton urges. She never guessed that the warm, thrumming feeling he roused in her was love at all, instead convincing herself she was merely curious about him, an unusual, rough-edged figure brimming with untapped potential, and she even framed the whole interest as a sort of quiet, philanthropic project.

As soon as they were alone, and the door closed, Thomasin said, turning up her pale, tearful face to him, “It is killing me, this, Damon! I did not mean to part from you in anger at Anglebury this morning; but I was frightened and hardly knew what I said. I’ve not let Aunt know how much I suffered today; and it is so hard to command my face and voice, and to smile as if it were a slight thing to me; but I try to do so, that she may not be still more indignant with you. I know you could not help it, dear, whatever Aunt may think.”

The second the door clicked shut behind them and they were fully alone, Thomasin tilted her pale, tear-streaked face up to Damon. “This whole mess is eating me alive, Damon! I never wanted to leave you on bad terms when we were at Anglebury this morning. I was just so shaken I barely knew what was coming out of my mouth. I haven’t told Aunt a thing about how much I’ve suffered today, either. It’s so hard to keep my expression and voice steady, to smile like this is just some trivial annoyance that doesn’t bother me at all, but I’m forcing myself to do it so she doesn’t get even angrier with you. I know you couldn’t help what happened, dear, no matter what Aunt thinks.”

“Very well, then let me see. I tell you how we’ll manage,” said the other, who perhaps felt it would be extremely awkward to offend his leading man just at this time. “I won’t tell ’em anything about your keeping silence; go on with the piece and say nothing, doing what you can by a judicious wink now and then, and a few indomitable nods in the heroic places, you know. They’ll never find out that the speeches are omitted.”

“Alright then, let me think. I’ve got the perfect way we can make this work,” the other man said, likely well aware that crossing his leading actor right now would be deeply awkward. “I won’t say a single thing to anyone about you staying silent. Just carry on with the performance as usual, don’t utter a line, toss in a well-timed wink every now and again, and a few resolute, grand nods during the heroic beats, you know what I mean. When the boy had vanished, every last glimmer of visible life drained from the surrounding landscape, though the rough, stuttering chirps of male grasshoppers rising from every furze tuft made clear that even as all larger creatures lay listless and drained, a hidden world of insects hummed with unflagging, full-bodied vitality.

Why did I not die? More miserable than man ever was before, why did I not sink into forgetfulness and rest? Death snatches away many blooming children, the only hopes of their doting parents; how many brides and youthful lovers have been one day in the bloom of health and hope, and the next a prey for worms and the decay of the tomb! Of what materials was I made that I could thus resist so many shocks, which, like the turning of the wheel, continually renewed the torture?

Why haven’t I died? More wretched than any person who has ever walked this earth, why didn’t I slip into total forgetfulness and finally rest? Death snatches countless thriving children from the world, the only hope their doting parents live for; how many brides and young lovers are bursting with health and bright possibility one day, only to become fodder for worms and the slow rot of the grave the next? What even am I made of, that I can withstand so many blows, each one spinning back around like a turning wheel to heap fresh torture on me again and again?

This obscure, obsolete, superseded country figures in Domesday. Its condition is recorded therein as that of heathy, furzy, briary wilderness—“Bruaria.” Then follows the length and breadth in leagues; and, though some uncertainty exists as to the exact extent of this ancient lineal measure, it appears from the figures that the area of Egdon down to the present day has but little diminished. “Turbaria Bruaria”—the right of cutting heath-turf—occurs in charters relating to the district. “Overgrown with heth and mosse,” says Leland of the same dark sweep of country.

This forgotten, outworn, bypassed tract of earth is entered in the Great Survey. Its character is preserved there as a scrubby, thorn-choked, desolate wasteland—“Erica.” Then follow its dimensions in archaic leagues; and, though the true scale of those ancient units remains a matter of some debate, the figures indicate that the reach of Blackwood Moor has scarcely contracted since that distant era. “Privilegium Fodiendi”—the entitlement to harvest peat—is cited in old scrolls pertaining to the region. “Clothed in bracken and gray lichen,” noted Camden of this same brooding expanse of land.

It was more than a month’s hard-earned wages, and it reduced his stock of money amazingly; but when he added the hundred dollars he was to receive from the _Examiner_ to the four hundred and twenty dollars that was the least _The Youth’s Companion_ could pay him, he felt that he had reduced the perplexity the unwonted amount of money had caused him. That sum was more than he earned in a full month of backbreaking work, and it cut his available cash sharply, but when he tallied the hundred dollars coming to him from the _Herald_ alongside the minimum four hundred twenty he would get from _Young Folks' Quarterly_, he was relieved to whittle down the awkward surplus of money that had been weighing on him as of late. He didn’t even care that he tore and stained his good suit terribly while fumbling to learn to ride the bike the rest of the way home. That same night, he called his tailor from the pay phone at Mr. Hollister’s general store and put in an order for a new suit. As he walked along, engaging with every word both thoughtfully and skeptically, he couldn’t help but notice how seamlessly the professor’s expertise spanned every topic they touched on. For his own part, the discussion kept turning up gaps in his own learning, vast blank swathes of entire subject areas he’d never even dabbled in. Even so, his deep dive into Spencer’s work had given him a clear sketch of the full landscape of all there was to know, he realized. Filling in every empty spot on that sketch was just a matter of putting in the time. When that day came, everyone better watch their step, he smirked to himself—shallow waters ahead for anyone who thought they could outmatch him. For a while he felt like he could kneel right there at the professor’s feet, eager to soak up every last word, reverent of the older man’s smarts. But the longer he listened, the more he picked up on a faint flaw in the professor’s reasoning, a quirk so scattered and hard to pin down he never would have spotted it if it didn’t pop up again and again. The second he pinned that flaw down, he felt himself standing on even ground with the man immediately.

Love may, as Shakespeare said, feed on itself: but Sarah could not bring herself to eat the dandelions that had graced, as ornaments, the first spiritual banquet of her heart’s true affection.

Just as these once-vibrant blossoms, symbols of romance, had been reduced to a lowly vegetable, the hopes she had nurtured all summer had similarly withered and died. Though poets claim that love can sustain itself, Sarah could not bring herself to consume the very flowers that had decorated the first sacred feast of her heart’s true devotion.

In great families, when an advantageous place cannot be obtained, either in possession, reversion, remainder, or expectancy, for the young man who is growing up, it is a very general custom to send him to sea. The board, in imitation of so wise and salutary an example, took counsel together on the expediency of shipping off Oliver Twist, in some small trading vessel bound to a good unhealthy port. This suggested itself as the very best thing that could possibly be done with him: the probability being, that the skipper would flog him to death, in a playful mood, some day after dinner, or would knock his brains out with an iron bar; both pastimes being, as is pretty generally known, very favourite and common recreations among gentleman of that class. The more the case presented itself to the board, in this point of view, the more manifold the advantages of the step appeared; so, they came to the conclusion that the only way of providing for Oliver effectually, was to send him to sea without delay.

In prominent households, when no suitable position—whether immediate, future, contingent, or anticipated—can be secured for a boy coming of age, it is quite customary to pack him off to sea. The board, following this prudent and time-honored precedent, deliberated on the wisdom of dispatching Oliver Twist aboard some modest merchant vessel bound for a reliably insalubrious port. The idea presented itself as the finest possible solution for the boy: the odds being that the captain, in a fit of whimsy after dinner one day, would either whip him to death or stove in his skull with an iron rod—both diversions being, as is widely acknowledged, especially cherished and habitual among gentlemen of that profession. The more the board turned the matter over from this angle, the more numerous the benefits of the measure appeared; thus they resolved that the sole effective means of settling Oliver's future was to send him to sea forthwith.

It was a small tube or trochar, with a lance passing down the inside; and Gabriel began to use it with a dexterity that would have graced a hospital surgeon. Passing his hand over the sheep’s left flank, and selecting the proper point, he punctured the skin and rumen with the lance as it stood in the tube; then he suddenly withdrew the lance, retaining the tube in its place. A current of air rushed up the tube, forcible enough to have extinguished a candle held at the orifice.

Gabriel was already moving among the swollen, prostrate sheep. He’d flung his coat aside, rolled his shirtsleeves up to his elbows, and drawn the life-saving implement from his pocket. It was a small trocar: a hollow tube fitted with a sharp lance running through its center, and Gabriel wielded it with the precise dexterity that would do a seasoned hospital surgeon proud. He ran his hand over the sheep’s left flank to find the correct spot, then drove the lance, still housed in the tube, through the skin and distended rumen. He withdrew the lance in a single sharp movement, leaving the tube fixed in place. A forceful stream of air rushed up through the tube, powerful enough to snuff out a candle held directly at its opening.

Such a perfect, delicate, and necessary finish did the figure give to the dark pile of hills that it seemed to be the only obvious justification of their outline. Without it, there was the dome without the lantern; with it the architectural demands of the mass were satisfied. The scene was strangely homogeneous, in that the vale, the upland, the barrow, and the figure above it amounted only to unity. Looking at this or that member of the group was not observing a complete thing, but a fraction of a thing.

So perfect, so subtle, and so essential was the figure’s effect on the dark cluster of hills that it seemed the only plain reason for their shape. Without it, there was a dome lacking its lantern; with it, the whole architectural need of the mass was fulfilled. The scene was remarkably unified, for the valley, the upland, the barrow, and the figure standing above it together made only a single whole. To look at one part or another of the grouping was not to see something complete, but merely a fragment of one.

I sat reflecting upon my evident cosmopolite and wondering how the poet had managed to miss him. He was my discovery and I believed in him. How was it? “The men that breed from them they traffic up and down, but cling to their cities’ hem as a child to the mother’s gown.”

I sat mulling over the unfeigned, far-roaming cosmopolitan I’d crossed paths with, wondering how generations of poets had never stumbled on his type to immortalize. He was my own unshared discovery, and I held full belief in his singular character. How had he slipped through the cracks? “The men born of that stock drift back and forth trading across borders, yet cling to the frayed edge of their home city’s skirt as a small child clutches at the fabric of its mother’s gown.”

You get your livelihood from the masters of society, and whoever feeds a man is that man’s master. Yes, you are a henchman. You are interested in advancing the interests of the aggregations of capital you serve.”

“Oh, of course. Your practice is entirely corporate. You don’t take on working-class clients or criminal cases, so you never rely on wife-beaters or small-time pickpockets for your income. Your entire livelihood comes straight from the ruling masters of society, and everyone knows the hand that feeds a man is the hand that commands him. That makes you a hired henchman, plain and simple. Your only priority is advancing the interests of the massive capital conglomerates you work for.”

"It's good to work in the night, and it's good to wake in the morning," said Mr. Britling, rubbing his hands together. "I suppose I wrote nearly two thousand words. So quiet one is, so concentrated. And as soon as I have had my breakfast I shall go on with it again."

“There’s such a pleasure to working through the hush of night, and just as much to rousing with the first pale wash of morning,” Mr. Hale said, chafing his palms briskly against one another. “I reckon I put down nearly two thousand words last night. No noise to pull you off course, so sharp, so fully anchored to the work. And the moment I finish my breakfast, I’m going straight back to it.”

“There she died,” said Monks, “after a lingering illness; and, on her death-bed, she bequeathed these secrets to me, together with her unquenchable and deadly hatred of all whom they involved—though she need not have left me that, for I had inherited it long before. She would not believe that the girl had destroyed herself, and the child too, but was filled with the impression that a male child had been born, and was alive. I swore to her, if ever it crossed my path, to hunt it down; never to let it rest; to pursue it with the bitterest and most unrelenting animosity; to vent upon it the hatred that I deeply felt, and to spit upon the empty vaunt of that insulting will by dragging it, if I could, to the very gallows-foot. She was right. He came in my way at last. I began well; and, but for babbling drabs, I would have finished as I began!”

“That’s where she died,” Monks said, “wasting away after a long, drawn-out illness. On her deathbed, she passed all those secrets down to me, along with her ravenous, poisonous hatred for everyone tangled up in them—though she didn’t even need to leave that part to me. I’d inherited that same hatred deep in my bones years before she was even dying. She refused to believe the girl had killed herself and taken the baby with her, not for a second. She was completely convinced a baby boy had been born, and that he was still alive out there somewhere. I swore to her if that boy ever crossed my path, I’d hunt him down without mercy. I’d never let him rest, hound him with the most bitter, unforgiving malice I possessed. I’d pour every drop of my deep-seated hatred out on him, and tear down that empty, arrogant brag in that offensive will by dragging him straight to the gallows steps if I could manage it. She was right. He finally turned up in my life. I got off to a perfect start, too, and if it wasn’t for those chattering, loose-lipped whores running their mouths, I’d have finished the job just as clean as I started it!”

He wore his tie drawn through a topaz ring instead of fastened with a stick pin; and once he had written to the editor of a magazine that “Junie’s Love Test” by Miss Libbey, had been the book that had most influenced his life.

He threaded his necktie through a topaz ring rather than securing it with a pin, and he had once sent a letter to a magazine editor confessing that Miss Libbey’s “Junie’s Love Test” was the book that had most shaped his life.

“I’ll tell you,” said Sydney. “I lighted on you, Mr. Barsad, coming out of the prison of the Conciergerie while I was contemplating the walls, an hour or more ago. You have a face to be remembered, and I remember faces well. Made curious by seeing you in that connection, and having a reason, to which you are no stranger, for associating you with the misfortunes of a friend now very unfortunate, I walked in your direction. I walked into the wine-shop here, close after you, and sat near you. I had no difficulty in deducing from your unreserved conversation, and the rumour openly going about among your admirers, the nature of your calling. And gradually, what I had done at random, seemed to shape itself into a purpose, Mr. Barsad.”

“Let me enlighten you,” Julian said softly. “My gaze chanced upon you, Mr. Vane, as you departed the shadows of the Châtelet while I stood observing its heavy walls some time ago. Your features possess a distinct quality, and I have a keen faculty for recollection. Spurred by the sight of you at that particular threshold, and mindful of your past link to the calamities of a friend who now languishes in sorrow, I took it upon myself to trail you. I followed you into this very cellar, keeping close, and found a place beside you. From your own reckless speech and the common talk of the ruffians who surround you, your true profession was easily unmasked. And so, what started as a wandering impulse has begun to take the form of a design, Mr. Vane.”

Mr. Oak carried about him, by way of watch, what may be called a small silver clock; in other words, it was a watch as to shape and intention, and a small clock as to size. This instrument being several years older than Oak’s grandfather, had the peculiarity of going either too fast or not at all. The smaller of its hands, too, occasionally slipped round on the pivot, and thus, though the minutes were told with precision, nobody could be quite certain of the hour they belonged to. The stopping peculiarity of his watch Oak remedied by thumps and shakes, and he escaped any evil consequences from the other two defects by constant comparisons with and observations of the sun and stars, and by pressing his face close to the glass of his neighbours’ windows, till he could discern the hour marked by the green-faced timekeepers within. It may be mentioned that Oak’s fob being difficult of access, by reason of its somewhat high situation in the waistband of his trousers (which also lay at a remote height under his waistcoat), the watch was as a necessity pulled out by throwing the body to one side, compressing the mouth and face to a mere mass of ruddy flesh on account of the exertion required, and drawing up the watch by its chain, like a bucket from a well.

Mr. Vale wore, for a watch, what might more properly have been called a little silver clock; that is to say, it was a watch in shape and purpose, and a clock only in its smallness. This piece of mechanism was older than his grandfather by several years, and possessed the singular habit of either running far too quickly or else refusing to run at all. The lesser hand, moreover, had a trick of sometimes slipping upon its pivot; so that while the minutes were shown with exactness, no one could be altogether sure to what hour they belonged. Vale cured the stopping by means of knocks and jolts, and escaped the inconvenience of the other two imperfections by perpetual reference to the sun and stars, together with such observations as he could make by pressing his face close against his neighbours’ panes, until he could distinguish the hour displayed in the green faces of the clocks within. It should be added that, his fob being hard of access by reason of its rather lofty position in the waistband of his trousers, which itself lay at a yet more remote elevation beneath his waistcoat, the watch had to be produced, of necessity, by throwing the body over to one side, compressing mouth and countenance into a mere crimson lump under the strain, and hauling the watch up by its chain as one might draw a bucket from a well.

All the air round was so thick and dark, the people were so passionately revengeful and fitful, the innocent were so constantly put to death on vague suspicion and black malice, it was so impossible to forget that many as blameless as her husband and as dear to others as he was to her, every day shared the fate from which he had been clutched, that her heart could not be as lightened of its load as she felt it ought to be. The shadows of the wintry afternoon were beginning to fall, and even now the dreadful carts were rolling through the streets. Her mind pursued them, looking for him among the Condemned; and then she clung closer to his real presence and trembled more.

The atmosphere was so dense and oppressive, the crowds so savagely vindictive and erratic, the blameless so frequently executed on mere suspicion and dark spite, and it was so impossible to dismiss the thought that many as guiltless as her husband and as cherished by others as he was by her daily met the doom he had narrowly escaped, that her spirit could not shake off its burden as she knew it should. The gloom of the winter dusk was creeping in, and even at that hour the grim tumbrels were rumbling through the thoroughfares. Her thoughts tracked them, seeking his face among the doomed; then she pressed nearer to his living form and shivered more violently.

It was curious to observe some faces which stood out prominently from among the group. There was the chairman himself, (the landlord of the house,) a coarse, rough, heavy built fellow, who, while the songs were proceeding, rolled his eyes hither and thither, and, seeming to give himself up to joviality, had an eye for everything that was done, and an ear for everything that was said—and sharp ones, too. Near him were the singers: receiving, with professional indifference, the compliments of the company, and applying themselves, in turn, to a dozen proffered glasses of spirits and water, tendered by their more boisterous admirers; whose countenances, expressive of almost every vice in almost every grade, irresistibly attracted the attention, by their very repulsiveness. Cunning, ferocity, and drunkeness in all its stages, were there, in their strongest aspect; and women: some with the last lingering tinge of their early freshness almost fading as you looked: others with every mark and stamp of their sex utterly beaten out, and presenting but one loathsome blank of profligacy and crime; some mere girls, others but young women, and none past the prime of life; formed the darkest and saddest portion of this dreary picture.

It was strange to pick out certain visages that seemed to rise distinctly from the crowd. At the center sat the host himself—the tavern’s proprietor—a thick-set, vulgar, heavy-handed man, who, while the ditties rang on, let his gaze wander freely around, and, though he feigned full surrender to merriment, missed no gesture enacted nor whisper uttered—and those senses were keen. Close beside him clustered the performers: accepting, with the practiced indifference of their trade, the crowd’s flattery, and drinking one by one from a dozen offered tumblers of gin and water, thrust forward by their louder, rowdier partisans—faces so stamped with nearly every shade and species of vice that their very ugliness seized the eye. Guile, savagery, and intoxication in every degree were on display in their most vivid forms; and women: some with the final ghost of youthful bloom fading even as you watched; others with every trace and sign of womanhood so thoroughly hammered out that nothing remained but a sickening vacancy of debauchery and guilt; a few mere girls, others scarcely grown, none beyond the height of their years—these composed the gloomiest, most pitiable fragment of the whole bleak scene.
