Ruth had only a small thing to ask—just that Norman had planned to escort her to a lecture that evening, but he’d come down with a headache, and she felt so let down, and she still held the tickets, and if he happened to be free, might he be kind enough to accompany her instead?

“There, you mistake,” he had rushed on. “All persons in society, all cliques in society—or, rather, nearly all persons and cliques—ape their betters. Now, who are the best betters? The idlers, the wealthy idlers. They do not know, as a rule, the things known by the persons who are doing something in the world. To listen to conversation about such things would mean to be bored, wherefore the idlers decree that such things are shop and must not be talked about. Likewise they decree the things that are not shop and which may be talked about, and those things are the latest operas, latest novels, cards, billiards, cocktails, automobiles, horse shows, trout fishing, tuna-fishing, big-game shooting, yacht sailing, and so forth—and mark you, these are the things the idlers know. In all truth, they constitute the shop-talk of the idlers. And the funniest part of it is that many of the clever people, and all the would-be clever people, allow the idlers so to impose upon them. As for me, I want the best a man’s got in him, call it shop vulgarity or anything you please.”

“There you err,” he had plunged ahead. “Every circle in society, every coterie—or, rather, nearly every person and set—mimics its superiors. And who, pray, are the foremost superiors? The leisured, the moneyed idlers. They remain ignorant, by and large, of the matters familiar to those who truly accomplish something in the world. To endure talk of such matters would be tedium, so the idlers pronounce those subjects shop and forbid their discussion. Equally they ordain what is not shop and may be spoken of, and those topics are the freshest operas, freshest novels, cards, billiards, cocktails, motorcars, horse shows, trout angling, tuna angling, big-game stalking, yacht cruising, and the like—and observe, these are the very things the idlers know. In sober fact, they constitute the shop-talk of the idlers. And the most amusing stroke is that many clever people, and all the would-be clever people, permit the idlers to foist this upon them. For my part, I demand the best a man possesses, call it shop coarseness or what you will.”

Nothing could be more complete than the alteration that had taken place in my feelings since the night of the appearance of the dæmon. I had before regarded my promise with a gloomy despair as a thing that, with whatever consequences, must be fulfilled; but I now felt as if a film had been taken from before my eyes and that I for the first time saw clearly. The idea of renewing my labours did not for one instant occur to me; the threat I had heard weighed on my thoughts, but I did not reflect that a voluntary act of mine could avert it. I had resolved in my own mind that to create another like the fiend I had first made would be an act of the basest and most atrocious selfishness, and I banished from my mind every thought that could lead to a different conclusion.

The transformation of my inner state since the night the creature appeared was absolute. I had previously regarded my promise with a sense of hopeless resignation, believing it a burden that must be endured regardless of the cost; now, however, it felt as though a shroud had been lifted from my sight, granting me a sudden, piercing clarity. Not for a single moment did I consider returning to my work. Though the monster’s threats haunted my thoughts, I refused to believe that any voluntary action of mine should be taken to avert his wrath. I had resolved that to fashion another being like the first would be an act of the most profound and wicked selfishness, and I cast out every impulse that might have swayed me from this conviction.

Oak, upon hearing these remarks, became more curious to observe her features, but this prospect being denied him by the hooding effect of the cloak, and by his aërial position, he felt himself drawing upon his fancy for their details. In making even horizontal and clear inspections we colour and mould according to the wants within us whatever our eyes bring in. Had Gabriel been able from the first to get a distinct view of her countenance, his estimate of it as very handsome or slightly so would have been as his soul required a divinity at the moment or was ready supplied with one. Having for some time known the want of a satisfactory form to fill an increasing void within him, his position moreover affording the widest scope for his fancy, he painted her a beauty.

When Ash heard these words, his desire to study her features grew keener; yet the hooded fall of the cloak denied him that sight, and his lofty vantage did no more to help him, so that he found himself compelled to invent their particulars out of his own imagination. Even in the plainest, most level act of looking, we shape and tint what our eyes present to us according to some inward need. Had Raphael, from the first, been granted a clear view of her face, his judgment of it, whether as truly beautiful or only passably so, would have answered to the divinity his soul then demanded, or was prepared at once to accept. Since for some time he had been conscious of a need for some fitting image to occupy a swelling emptiness within him, and since his situation gave his fancy the fullest license, he endowed her with beauty.

He felt a shock himself, and a blush of embarrassment shone faintly on his sunburned cheeks, though to him it burned as hotly as when his cheeks had been exposed to the open furnace-door in the fire-room. Such sordid things as stabbing affrays were evidently not fit subjects for conversation with a lady. People in the books, in her walk of life, did not talk about such things—perhaps they did not know about them, either.

A tremor of shock hit him, and a faint flush of shame colored his weather-beaten face, though in his own mind it burned with the intensity of an open furnace door in the engine room. Crude matters like knife fights were clearly unsuitable topics for a lady’s ears. The refined people in novels, those from her station in life, did not discuss such brutality—perhaps they were entirely unaware that such things even existed.

"But I suppose all sorts of people could write an Irish pamphlet. Nobody but you could write 'The Silent Places.' Oh, _why_ don't you finish that great beautiful thing, and leave all this world of reality and newspapers, all these Crude, Vulgar, Quarrelsome, Jarring things to other people? You have the magic gift, you might be a poet, you can take us out of all these horrid things that are, away to Beautyland, and you are just content to be a critic and a disputer. It's your surroundings. It's your sordid realities. It's that Practicality at your elbow. You ought never to see a newspaper. You ought never to have an American come within ten miles of you. You ought to live on bowls of milk drunk in valleys of asphodel."

“But I daresay any number of people could contrive an Irish pamphlet. Only you could have written ‘The Still Places.’ Oh, why don’t you bring that great, lovely thing to its end, and leave all this world of fact and journals, all these coarse, noisy, contentious, discordant affairs, to other people? You have the enchanted faculty; you might be a poet. You can carry us away from all these dreadful realities to Beauty-land, and instead you are content to be merely a critic and a quarreller. It is your company about you. It is your wretched realities. It is that Practicality always at your elbow. You ought never to catch sight of a newspaper. You ought never to let an American come within ten miles of you. You ought to live on dishes of milk sipped in valleys of asphodel.”

After shouting again, several times, it became manifest that he had attracted the attention of two willing but deliberate labouring men. They emerged slowly, first as attentive heads, from the landscape. With their assistance the car was restored to the road again. Mr. Direck assisted manfully, and noted the respect that was given to Mr. Britling and the shillings that fell to the men, with an intelligent detachment. They touched their hats, they called Mr. Britling "Sir." They examined the car distantly but kindly. "Ain't 'urt 'e, not a bit 'e ain't, not really," said one encouragingly. And indeed except for a slight crumpling of the mud-guard and the detachment of the wire of one of the headlights the automobile was uninjured. Mr. Britling resumed his seat; Mr. Direck gravely and in silence got up beside him. They started with the usual convulsion, as though something had pricked the vehicle unexpectedly and shamefully behind. And from this point Mr. Britling, driving with meticulous care, got home without further mishap, excepting only that he scraped off some of the metal edge of his footboard against the gate-post of his very agreeable garden.

After calling out repeatedly, it grew clear that he had caught the notice of two obliging yet unhurried farmhands. They rose gradually, at first just watchful crowns, from the fields. With their help the motor was guided back onto the lane. Mr. Direck lent his strength stoutly, and observed with a cool perceptiveness the deference shown to Mr. Britling and the coins that passed into the men’s palms. They tugged their caps, they addressed Mr. Britling as “Sir.” They studied the machine from a polite distance but with goodwill. “She ain’t ’armed, not a scratch she ain’t, not truly,” one offered cheerfully. And in truth, save for a slight buckling of the wing and the loosening of a headlamp’s wiring, the vehicle was unharmed. Mr. Britling settled back behind the wheel; Mr. Direck, solemn and wordless, climbed in beside him. They set off with the customary shudder, as if something had jabbed the car unexpectedly and indecently from the rear. And from that moment Mr. Britling, steering with scrupulous caution, reached home without further trouble, apart from grazing the metal rim of his running board against the gatepost of his most pleasant garden.

She came indoors with her face flushed, and her eyes still showing traces of her recent excitement. Yeobright looked up astonished; he had never seen her in any way approaching to that state before. She passed him by, and would have gone upstairs unnoticed, but Clym was so concerned that he immediately followed her.

She stepped over the threshold indoors, her cheeks flushed bright, and faint traces of her recent flurry of excitement still glinting in her eyes. Clym Yeobright looked up, astonished; he had never caught even a hint of this kind of unguarded, heightened state in her before. She slipped past him, ready to climb the stairs without drawing notice, but Clym was so thoroughly concerned that he rose immediately and followed close at her heels.

He fled to his stateroom, where he lurked until the steamer was clear of the dock. In the dining saloon, at luncheon, he found himself in the place of honor, at the captain’s right; and he was not long in discovering that he was the great man on board. But no more unsatisfactory great man ever sailed on a ship. He spent the afternoon in a deck-chair, with closed eyes, dozing brokenly most of the time, and in the evening went early to bed.

He retreated to his cabin and stayed hidden there until the steamer had pulled away from the pier. When he entered the dining saloon for lunch, he was shown to the seat of honor, directly at the captain’s right hand, and it didn’t take him long to realize he was regarded as the most important person aboard. Yet no more discontented celebrity had ever traveled on that vessel. He passed the afternoon slumped in a deck-chair, eyes shut, slipping in and out of a fitful doze, and retired to his berth early that evening.

Yeobright did not interrupt the preparations, and went home again. The next morning, when Thomasin withdrew the curtains of her bedroom window, there stood the Maypole in the middle of the green, its top cutting into the sky. It had sprung up in the night, or rather early morning, like Jack’s bean-stalk. She opened the casement to get a better view of the garlands and posies that adorned it. The sweet perfume of the flowers had already spread into the surrounding air, which, being free from every taint, conducted to her lips a full measure of the fragrance received from the spire of blossom in its midst. At the top of the pole were crossed hoops decked with small flowers; beneath these came a milk-white zone of Maybloom; then a zone of bluebells, then of cowslips, then of lilacs, then of ragged-robins, daffodils, and so on, till the lowest stage was reached. Thomasin noticed all these, and was delighted that the May revel was to be so near.

Clym did not pause the bustle, and returned to his dwelling. At dawn, when Tamsin drew back the linen from her chamber casement, the Maypole already pierced the common’s heart, its crown needling the heavens. It had risen in the darkness—or earliest morning—like a stalk from a fairy tale. She pushed the lattice wide to study the wreaths and nosegays that clothed it. The honeyed breath of the blossoms had already seeped into the unstained air, which bore to her mouth an undiluted draught of the scent cascading from that floral steeple. At the summit, crossed hoops wore a fretwork of tiny blooms; below them hung a creamy band of hawthorn; then a girdle of harebells, then cowslips, then lilac, then ragged-robin, narcissus, and onward, until the lowest tier was met. Tamsin marked each ring, and rejoiced that the Maying would unfold so close at hand.

Not all Hugh's letters were concerned with these grim technicalities. It was not always that news and gossip came along; it was rare that a young man with a commission would condescend to talk shop to two young men without one; there were few newspapers and fewer maps, and even in France and within sound of guns, Hugh could presently find warfare almost as much a bore as it had been at times in England. But his criticism of military methods died away. "Things are done better out here," he remarked, and "We're nearer reality here. I begin to respect my Captain. Who is developing a sense of locality. Happily for our prospects." And in another place he speculated in an oddly characteristic manner whether he was getting used to the army way, whether he was beginning to see the sense of the army way, or whether it really was that the army way braced up nearer and nearer to efficiency as it got nearer to the enemy. "And here one hasn't the haunting feeling that war is after all an hallucination. It's already common sense and the business of life....

Not all of Hugh’s letters were taken up with those grim technical matters. News and gossip did not always come his way; it was uncommon for a young man with a commission to descend to shop talk for the benefit of two young men who had none; newspapers were scarce and maps scarcer still, and even in France, with the guns audible, Hugh could for the time being find war almost as dull as it had sometimes seemed in England. Yet his complaints about military methods gradually fell silent. “Things are done better out here,” he observed, and, “We’re nearer reality here. I’m beginning to respect my Captain, who is acquiring a sense of locality. Happily for our prospects.” And elsewhere he wondered, in a peculiarly his own way, whether he was simply growing accustomed to army ways, whether he was beginning to perceive their reason, or whether it was really that army ways drew closer and closer to efficiency as they drew nearer the enemy. “And here one doesn’t have the haunting feeling that war is after all an hallucination. It’s already common sense and the business of life....”

The messenger rode back at an easy trot, stopping pretty often at ale-houses by the way to drink, but evincing a tendency to keep his own counsel, and to keep his hat cocked over his eyes. He had eyes that assorted very well with that decoration, being of a surface black, with no depth in the colour or form, and much too near together--as if they were afraid of being found out in something, singly, if they kept too far apart. They had a sinister expression, under an old cocked-hat like a three-cornered spittoon, and over a great muffler for the chin and throat, which descended nearly to the wearer’s knees. When he stopped for drink, he moved this muffler with his left hand, only while he poured his liquor in with his right; as soon as that was done, he muffled again.

The messenger rode back at an easy trot, pausing often at alehouses along the way to drink, yet seeming determined to keep his own counsel and to wear his hat pulled low over his eyes. Those eyes suited that fashion well: black on the surface, with no depth of color or expression, and set much too close together, as if they feared being detected in some misdeed if they kept too far apart. They gave him a sinister look beneath an old cocked hat like a three-cornered spittoon, and above a huge muffler for his chin and throat that fell almost to his knees. Whenever he stopped to drink, he lifted the muffler with his left hand only long enough to pour the liquor in with his right; once he had done so, he drew it up again.

We stepped into the shadowed cell and found Celeste at its far edge, seated upon a heap of straw; iron cuffs bound her wrists, and her brow lay heavy upon her knees. At the sight of our entrance she stood, and the moment the guards withdrew and left us three alone, she cast herself at Helena’s feet in a storm of tears. My cousin wept with her.

He dared not go near Ruth’s neighborhood in the daytime, but night found him lurking like a thief around the Morse home, stealing glimpses at the windows and loving the very walls that sheltered her. Several times he barely escaped being caught by her brothers, and once he trailed Mr. Morse down town and studied his face in the lighted streets, longing all the while for some quick danger of death to threaten so that he might spring in and save her father. On another night, his vigil was rewarded by a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, and her arms raised as she fixed her hair before a mirror. It was only for a moment, but it was a long moment to him, during which his blood turned to wine and sang through his veins. Then she pulled down the shade. But it was her room—he had learned that; and thereafter he strayed there often, hiding under a dark tree on the opposite side of the street and smoking countless cigarettes. One afternoon he saw her mother coming out of a bank, and received another proof of the enormous distance that separated Ruth from him. She was of the class that dealt with banks. He had never been inside a bank in his life, and he had an idea that such institutions were frequented only by the very rich and the very powerful.

He did not venture near Ruth’s neighborhood during the day, but at night he lingered around the Morse house like a prowler, watching the windows and feeling a sense of reverence for the very walls that protected her. He narrowly avoided being spotted by her brothers on several occasions, and once he even followed Mr. Morse into the city, studying the man’s face beneath the streetlamps while secretly hoping for some fatal accident to occur so that he might rush in and save her father’s life. One night, his persistence was rewarded when he caught a glimpse of Ruth through a second-story window. He saw only her head and shoulders, her arms lifted as she styled her hair in front of a mirror. It lasted only a moment, yet for him, that moment stretched on as his blood turned to wine and raced through his heart. Then she lowered the shade. Now that he knew which room was hers, he returned often, hiding beneath a dark tree on the far side of the street and smoking one cigarette after another. One afternoon, he happened to see her mother leaving a bank, which served as another reminder of the vast social gulf between them. She belonged to the class of people who used banks; he had never once entered such a building, believing they were reserved solely for the most affluent and powerful members of society.

They went out, leaving Lucie, and her husband, her father, and the child, by a bright fire. Mr. Lorry was expected back presently from the Banking House. Miss Pross had lighted the lamp, but had put it aside in a corner, that they might enjoy the fire-light undisturbed. Little Lucie sat by her grandfather with her hands clasped through his arm: and he, in a tone not rising much above a whisper, began to tell her a story of a great and powerful Fairy who had opened a prison-wall and let out a captive who had once done the Fairy a service. All was subdued and quiet, and Lucie was more at ease than she had been.

They stepped outside, leaving Lucie alone with her husband, her father, and the child beside a glowing hearth. Mr. Lorry was due back shortly from the bank. Miss Pross had lit the lamp but pushed it into a shadowy corner so they could savor the firelight without interruption. Young Lucie nestled next to her grandfather, her small fingers laced around his arm, while he, in a voice barely above a murmur, spun her a tale about a mighty Fairy who had shattered a prison wall to free a prisoner who had once helped the Fairy. Everything felt hushed and serene, and Lucie felt more at peace than she had in some time.

The cloth was laid for supper; the table was covered with bread and butter, plates and glasses; a porter-pot and a wine-bottle. At the upper end of the table, Mr. Noah Claypole lolled negligently in an easy-chair, with his legs thrown over one of the arms: an open clasp-knife in one hand, and a mass of buttered bread in the other. Close beside him stood Charlotte, opening oysters from a barrel: which Mr. Claypole condescended to swallow, with remarkable avidity. A more than ordinary redness in the region of the young gentleman’s nose, and a kind of fixed wink in his right eye, denoted that he was in a slight degree intoxicated; these symptoms were confirmed by the intense relish with which he took his oysters, for which nothing but a strong appreciation of their cooling properties, in cases of internal fever, could have sufficiently accounted.

The supper cloth was spread ready for the meal, the table loaded with bread and butter, plates and glasses, a stout porter pot and a bottle of wine. At the head of the table, Mr. Noah Claypole slouched lazily in an easy chair, his legs slung carelessly over one armrest: an open pocket knife in one hand, a thick chunk of buttered bread in the other. Standing just beside him was Charlotte, prying oysters open from a barrel: morsels Mr. Claypole deigned to swallow with sharp, greedy enthusiasm. An unusual bright redness across the young gentleman’s nose, and a sort of fixed, half-winking twitch in his right eye, marked that he was slightly intoxicated; these symptoms were confirmed by the fierce relish with which he ate his oysters, a preference that could only be adequately explained by a strong awareness of their cooling qualities, perfect for soothing the internal fever brought on by drink.

Her nerves extended into those tresses, and her temper could always be softened by stroking them down. When her hair was brushed she would instantly sink into stillness and look like the Sphinx. If, in passing under one of the Egdon banks, any of its thick skeins were caught, as they sometimes were, by a prickly tuft of the large _Ulex Europæus_—which will act as a sort of hairbrush—she would go back a few steps, and pass against it a second time.

Her moods flowed into those locks, and the gentle drawing of a comb through them could always soothe her spirit. While her hair was being smoothed she would lapse at once into quietude and resemble a stone effigy. If, while walking beneath one of the heath’s overhanging thickets, a stray strand happened to snag on a bristling spray of the great _Ulex Europæus_—which serves as a natural brush—she would retrace her steps and brush deliberately against it once more.

“This is a common case--the enchanting of a thing to one eye and leaving it in its proper form to another. You have heard of it before, Sandy, though you haven't happened to experience it. But no harm is done. In fact, it is lucky the way it is. If these ladies were hogs to everybody and to themselves, it would be necessary to break the enchantment, and that might be impossible if one failed to find out the particular process of the enchantment. And hazardous, too; for in attempting a disenchantment without the true key, you are liable to err, and turn your hogs into dogs, and the dogs into cats, the cats into rats, and so on, and end by reducing your materials to nothing finally, or to an odorless gas which you can't follow--which, of course, amounts to the same thing. But here, by good luck, no one's eyes but mine are under the enchantment, and so it is of no consequence to dissolve it. These ladies remain ladies to you, and to themselves, and to everybody else; and at the same time they will suffer in no way from my delusion, for when I know that an ostensible hog is a lady, that is enough for me, I know how to treat her.”

This is a frequent occurrence—the way a spell might alter something in one man's sight while leaving it in its true form for another. You’ve heard of such things before, Sandy, even if you haven't witnessed it personally. But there is no real damage here. In truth, it’s a stroke of luck. If these ladies looked like swine to themselves and the rest of the world, we would be forced to break the enchantment, which might be impossible without knowing the exact ritual involved. It’s a dangerous business, too; if you try to reverse a spell without the correct formula, you're liable to make a mistake and turn your hogs into dogs, then cats, then rats, until you eventually reduce your subjects to nothing at all, or perhaps a scentless vapor that vanishes—which amounts to the same thing. Fortunately, only my vision is clouded, so there is no reason to undo the magic. These women remain noble ladies to you and everyone else; they won't suffer from my misunderstanding, for once I realize that a supposed pig is actually a lady, that is enough for me to treat her with the proper respect.

“I heard your dog howling and scratching at the door of the hut when I came to the milking (it was so lucky, Daisy’s milking is almost over for the season, and I shall not come here after this week or the next). The dog saw me, and jumped over to me, and laid hold of my skirt. I came across and looked round the hut the very first thing to see if the slides were closed. My uncle has a hut like this one, and I have heard him tell his shepherd not to go to sleep without leaving a slide open. I opened the door, and there you were like dead. I threw the milk over you, as there was no water, forgetting it was warm, and no use.”

“I heard your hound wailing and clawing at the cabin door when I arrived for the evening milking—such a mercy that Daisy’s flow is nearly done for the year, and I shan’t need to come this way beyond a week or two more. The beast caught sight of me, bounded over, and seized the hem of my dress. I crossed the yard at once and circled the hut, my first thought being to check whether the vents were shut. My own uncle keeps a cabin much like this one, and I’ve heard him warn his shepherd never to sleep without a vent cracked open. I drew back the door, and there you lay as still as a corpse. I dashed the milk over you, there being no water to hand, forgetting in my haste that it was warm and would do nothing.”

Martin talked for fifteen minutes with him, nor could Ruth have wished better behavior on her lover’s part. Not once did his eyes flash nor his cheeks flush, while the calmness and poise with which he talked surprised her. But in Martin’s estimation the whole tribe of bank cashiers fell a few hundred per cent, and for the rest of the evening he labored under the impression that bank cashiers and talkers of platitudes were synonymous phrases. The army officer he found good-natured and simple, a healthy, wholesome young fellow, content to occupy the place in life into which birth and luck had flung him. On learning that he had completed two years in the university, Martin was puzzled to know where he had stored it away. Nevertheless Martin liked him better than the platitudinous bank cashier.

Martin chatted with him for a full fifteen minutes, and Ruth couldn’t have asked for better behavior from her boyfriend. He never once let his eyes flash with irritation or his cheeks flush with frustration, and the steady, unflappable poise he carried through the whole conversation caught her completely off guard. But as far as Martin was concerned, the entire profession of bank cashiers plummeted a good few hundred points in his esteem, and for the rest of the evening he couldn’t shake the belief that “bank cashier” was just another term for someone who spouts empty, overused platitudes. The army officer, by contrast, struck him as good-natured and unpretentious, a healthy, well-adjusted young man who was perfectly content occupying the spot in life that birth and luck had tossed him into. When he learned the officer had completed two years of university, Martin was puzzled to figure out where he’d stored all that education, since he gave no obvious sign of it in casual interaction. Even so, Martin still liked him far better than the trite, cliché-ridden bank cashier.

It struck him motionless. The gaoler standing at his side, and the other gaolers moving about, who would have been well enough as to appearance in the ordinary exercise of their functions, looked so extravagantly coarse contrasted with sorrowing mothers and blooming daughters who were there--with the apparitions of the coquette, the young beauty, and the mature woman delicately bred--that the inversion of all experience and likelihood which the scene of shadows presented, was heightened to its utmost. Surely, ghosts all. Surely, the long unreal ride some progress of disease that had brought him to these gloomy shades!

It froze him where he stood. The jailer beside him, and the others drifting through their duties—men whose looks would have passed without remark in the common run of their work—now seemed grotesquely rough set against the grieving mothers and fresh-faced daughters gathered there, against the spectres of the flirt, the youthful beauty, the gently reared matron. The shadowy scene, already a reversal of all that was natural and probable, sharpened into something absolute. Surely, every one a phantom. Surely, the long hallucinatory journey some fevered advance of illness that had delivered him into these sombre depths.

Ikey Schoenstein was the night clerk of the Blue Light and the friend of his customers. Thus it is on the East Side, where the heart of pharmacy is not glacé. There, as it should be, the druggist is a counsellor, a confessor, an adviser, an able and willing missionary and mentor whose learning is respected, whose occult wisdom is venerated and whose medicine is often poured, untasted, into the gutter. Therefore Ikey’s corniform, be-spectacled nose and narrow, knowledge-bowed figure was well known in the vicinity of the Blue Light, and his advice and notice were much desired.

Ikey Schoenstein served as the night clerk at the Blue Light pharmacy and was a confidant to his patrons. This was the way of the East Side, where the drugstore’s heart hasn’t frozen over. There, as it ought to be, the pharmacist acts as counselor, confessor, guide—a devoted and capable missionary and sage whose learning earns respect, whose mysterious knowledge is revered, and whose remedies are often tipped, unsipped, into the gutter. So Ikey’s horn-rimmed, spectacled nose and his slender, studiously stooped frame were familiar sights around the Blue Light, and both his counsel and his attention were eagerly sought.

It was late that night and after an eternity of resolutions and doubts and indecisions that Mrs. Britling went to her husband. He was sitting close up to the fire with his chin upon his hands, waiting for her; he felt that she would come to him, and he was thinking meanwhile of Hugh with a slow unprogressive movement of the mind. He showed by a movement that he heard her enter the room, but he did not turn to look at her. He shrank a little from her approach.

It was deep into the night when Mrs. Britling finally went to her husband, after what had felt like an eternity of weighing resolutions, nursing doubts, and wrestling with indecision. He was huddled close to the fire, chin resting on his hands, waiting for her; he had known she would come, and in the interim his thoughts had moved slowly, circling nowhere, fixed entirely on Hugh. A small, subtle shift of his posture signaled he’d heard her enter the room, but he did not turn to look at her. He shrank back just slightly as she approached.

That was all the writing. It was so much, however, to her who received it, that she turned from Defarge to his wife, and kissed one of the hands that knitted. It was a passionate, loving, thankful, womanly action, but the hand made no response--dropped cold and heavy, and took to its knitting again.

The writing ended there. Yet for the woman who received it, the words meant so much that she turned from Defarge toward his wife and kissed one of the hands that held the needles. It was a gesture of intense, heartfelt gratitude, but the hand offered no reaction—it simply fell, cold and heavy, and returned to its knitting.

When he had gone, the craving for a personal share in the nation's physical exertions became overpowering in Mr. Britling. He wanted, he felt, to "get his skin into it." He had decided that the volunteer movement was a hopeless one. The War Office, after a stout resistance to any volunteer movement at all, decided to recognise it in such a manner as to make it ridiculous. The volunteers were to have no officers and no uniforms that could be remotely mistaken for those of the regulars, so that in the event of an invasion the Germans would be able to tell what they had to deal with miles away. Wilkins found his conception of a whole nation, all enrolled, all listed and badged according to capacity, his dream of every one falling into place in one great voluntary national effort, treated as the childish dreaming of that most ignorant of all human types, a "novelist." _Punch_ was delicately funny about him; he was represented as wearing a preposterous cocked hat of his own design, designing cocked hats for every one. Wilkins was told to "shut up" in a multitude of anonymous letters, and publicly and privately to "leave things to Kitchener." To bellow in loud clear tones "leave things to Kitchener," and to depart for the theatre or the river or an automobile tour, was felt very generally at that time to be the proper conduct for a patriot. There was a very general persuasion that to become a volunteer when one ought to be just modestly doing nothing at all, was in some obscure way a form of disloyalty....

When he was gone, Mr. Britling felt an overwhelming urge to take a personal part in the country’s physical efforts. He wanted, as he put it, to “get his skin into it.” He had already come to think the volunteer movement a hopeless affair. After resisting any volunteer movement at all for a good while, the War Office had decided to acknowledge it in a way that made it absurd. The volunteers were to have no officers and no uniforms that could even faintly be mistaken for those of the regular army, so that if the Germans ever invaded, they would be able to see exactly what they were facing from miles off. Wilkins saw his idea of an entire nation enrolled, registered, and badged according to ability, his vision of everyone falling into place in one great voluntary national effort, dismissed as the foolish dream of that most ignorant of human creatures, a “novelist.” _Punch_ made gentle fun of him; he was shown wearing a ridiculous cocked hat of his own invention, and designing cocked hats for everyone else. Wilkins was told to “shut up” in a flood of anonymous letters, and both publicly and privately told to “leave things to Kitchener.” To shout in clear, loud tones, “leave things to Kitchener,” and then go off to the theatre or the river or an automobile excursion, was widely felt at the time to be the proper behavior for a patriot. There was a very widespread belief that to become a volunteer when one should be quietly doing nothing at all was, in some obscure way, a form of disloyalty....

Only the most superficial mind would assert nowadays that man is a reasonable creature. Man is an unreasonable creature, and it was entirely unreasonable and human for Mr. Britling during his nocturnal self-reproaches to mix up his secret resentment at his infatuation for Mrs. Harrowdean with his ill-advised attack upon the wall of Brandismead Park. He ought never to have bought that car; he ought never to have been so ready to meet Mrs. Harrowdean more than halfway.

Only the most shallow-minded person would now insist that man is a rational being. Man is an irrational being, and it was wholly irrational, and entirely human, for Mr. Britling, in the course of his nightly self-reproaches, to confuse his private resentment over his infatuation for Mrs. Harrowdean with his ill-judged assault on the wall of Brandismead Park. He should never have bought that car; he should never have been so eager to meet Mrs. Harrowdean more than halfway.

Thus situated, employed in the most detestable occupation, immersed in a solitude where nothing could for an instant call my attention from the actual scene in which I was engaged, my spirits became unequal; I grew restless and nervous. Every moment I feared to meet my persecutor. Sometimes I sat with my eyes fixed on the ground, fearing to raise them lest they should encounter the object which I so much dreaded to behold. I feared to wander from the sight of my fellow creatures lest when alone he should come to claim his companion.

So placed, trapped in the most loathsome of tasks, sunk in a loneliness that offered not a single moment’s reprieve from the scene before me, my mood grew fractured; I turned edgy and unsettled. At every instant I dreaded encountering my tormentor. At times I sat staring rigidly at the ground, afraid to lift my gaze lest it meet the very thing I most feared to see. I grew afraid to stray from the presence of other people, lest in solitude he should appear to reclaim his companion.

They were happy days, those of Oliver’s recovery. Everything was so quiet, and neat, and orderly; everybody so kind and gentle; that after the noise and turbulence in the midst of which he had always lived, it seemed like Heaven itself. He was no sooner strong enough to put his clothes on, properly, than Mr. Brownlow caused a complete new suit, and a new cap, and a new pair of shoes, to be provided for him. As Oliver was told that he might do what he liked with the old clothes, he gave them to a servant who had been very kind to him, and asked her to sell them to a Jew, and keep the money for herself. This she very readily did; and, as Oliver looked out of the parlour window, and saw the Jew roll them up in his bag and walk away, he felt quite delighted to think that they were safely gone, and that there was now no possible danger of his ever being able to wear them again. They were sad rags, to tell the truth; and Oliver had never had a new suit before.

The days of Oliver’s recovery were unreservedly happy. Every part of the house was quiet, neat, and perfectly ordered; everyone around him was so kind and gentle that, after a lifetime spent amid constant noise and turmoil, the place felt like nothing short of heaven. As soon as he was strong enough to dress himself properly, Mr. Brownlow arranged for a full new suit, a new cap, and a new pair of shoes to be made for him. When Oliver was told he could do whatever he liked with his old clothes, he gave them to a servant who had been particularly good to him, and asked her to sell them to the Jewish peddler who frequented the area and keep the money for herself. She did so gladly, and as Oliver watched from the parlor window while the peddler rolled the clothes into his bag and walked off, he felt thoroughly delighted to know they were gone for good, and there was no possible chance he would ever have to wear them again. To tell the truth, those clothes were little more than tattered rags, and Oliver had never owned a new suit in his entire life.

Maryann, who had been afraid to shout in the robber’s presence, having seen him depart had no fear. She hastily slipped on her clothes, stumped down the disjointed staircase with its hundred creaks, ran to Coggan’s, the nearest house, and raised an alarm. Coggan called Gabriel, who now again lodged in his house as at first, and together they went to the paddock. Beyond all doubt the horse was gone.

Maryann, who had been too afraid to cry out while the robber stood there, now that she had seen him go, felt no fear at all. She hurriedly dressed, clattered down the broken staircase, which gave a hundred creaks beneath her feet, ran to Coggan’s, the nearest house, and sounded the alarm. Coggan called Gabriel, who was once more lodging in his house as he had at first, and the two of them went together to the paddock. There could be no question of it: the horse had been taken.

The strong tide, so swift, so deep, and certain, was like a congenial friend, in the morning stillness. He walked by the stream, far from the houses, and in the light and warmth of the sun fell asleep on the bank. When he awoke and was afoot again, he lingered there yet a little longer, watching an eddy that turned and turned purposeless, until the stream absorbed it, and carried it on to the sea.--“Like me.”

The powerful current, so rapid, so deep, and so sure, seemed in the morning hush like a kindly companion. He wandered beside the water, far from the dwellings, and in the sun’s light and warmth he fell asleep upon the bank. When he woke and rose again, he stayed there still a while longer, gazing at a whirlpool that spun and spun without purpose, until the stream took it into itself and bore it onward to the sea.—“Like me.”

When I came to again, I was sitting under an oak tree, on the grass, with a whole beautiful and broad country landscape all to myself--nearly. Not entirely; for there was a fellow on a horse, looking down at me--a fellow fresh out of a picture-book. He was in old-time iron armor from head to heel, with a helmet on his head the shape of a nail-keg with slits in it; and he had a shield, and a sword, and a prodigious spear; and his horse had armor on, too, and a steel horn projecting from his forehead, and gorgeous red and green silk trappings that hung down all around him like a bedquilt, nearly to the ground.

When I came to myself again, I was seated on the grass beneath an oak tree, with a whole broad, lovely stretch of country spread out before me—almost to myself. Not quite, for there was a man on horseback looking down at me, a man who seemed to have stepped straight out of a storybook. He was clad from head to foot in ancient iron armor, with a helmet upon his head shaped like a nail keg and slit for sight; he carried a shield, a sword, and a monstrous spear, and even his horse wore armor, with a steel horn jutting from its forehead and splendid red-and-green silk trappings hanging all about it like a bedspread, almost to the ground.

“This is the room,” the housekeeper announced, her voice burred deep in her throat. “A lovely room. Doesn’t come free often. Last summer I had some truly refined guests—no trouble whatsoever, and their payment arrived right on the dot. The water closet’s down the hall. Sprowls and Mooney had it for three months. They performed a vaudeville act. “But the upshot of it all—of my thinking and reading and loving—is that I am going to move to Grub Street. I shall leave masterpieces alone and do hack-work—jokes, paragraphs, feature articles, humorous verse, and society verse—all the rot for which there seems so much demand. Then there are the newspaper syndicates, and the newspaper short-story syndicates, and the syndicates for the Sunday supplements. I can go ahead and hammer out the stuff they want, and earn the equivalent of a good salary by it. There are free-lances, you know, who earn as much as four or five hundred a month. I don’t care to become as they; but I’ll earn a good living, and have plenty of time to myself, which I wouldn’t have in any position.”

The end result of all my reflections, my studies, and my passions is this: I am bound for Grub Street. I’ll abandon any thought of masterpieces and turn to hack-work—jokes, squibs, feature pieces, light verse, society rhymes—all the fluff that seems to be in such endless demand. Then there are the newspaper syndicates, the short-story syndicates, and the syndicates that feed the Sunday supplements. I can churn out exactly the stuff they want and pull in the equivalent of a decent salary. There are free-lances, as you know, who make four or five hundred a month. I have no wish to become one of them; but I’ll earn a comfortable living and keep ample time for myself, which no fixed position would ever allow me.

In the room from which the conversation proceeded Bathsheba and her servant-companion, Liddy Smallbury, were to be discovered sitting upon the floor, and sorting a complication of papers, books, bottles, and rubbish spread out thereon—remnants from the household stores of the late occupier. Liddy, the maltster’s great-granddaughter, was about Bathsheba’s equal in age, and her face was a prominent advertisement of the light-hearted English country girl. The beauty her features might have lacked in form was amply made up for by perfection of hue, which at this winter-time was the softened ruddiness on a surface of high rotundity that we meet with in a Terburg or a Gerard Douw; and, like the presentations of those great colourists, it was a face which kept well back from the boundary between comeliness and the ideal. Though elastic in nature she was less daring than Bathsheba, and occasionally showed some earnestness, which consisted half of genuine feeling, and half of mannerliness superadded by way of duty.

Inside the room where the voices originated, Bathsheba and her companion-servant, Liddy Smallbury, could be found seated on the floor. They were sorting through a chaotic jumble of documents, books, vials, and discarded items—the leftover belongings of the former resident. Liddy, the great-granddaughter of the maltster, was nearly the same age as Bathsheba, her face a striking portrait of a spirited English country girl. Any lack of refinement in her features was easily overlooked because of her magnificent complexion; in the winter air, her full, round face held a gentle flush similar to those seen in the works of Terburg or Gerard Douw. Like the figures in those old Dutch paintings, her beauty was grounded and pleasant, stopping well short of the sublime. Though she was resilient, she wasn't as adventurous as Bathsheba, and she occasionally exhibited a seriousness that was equal parts genuine sentiment and a dutiful sense of decorum.

The piece was the well-known play of Saint George, and all who were behind the scenes assisted in the preparations, including the women of each household. The girls could never be brought to respect tradition in designing and decorating the armour; they insisted on attaching loops and bows of silk and velvet in any situation pleasing to their taste. Gorget, gusset, basinet, cuirass, gauntlet, sleeve, all alike in the view of these feminine eyes were practicable spaces whereon to sew scraps of fluttering colour.

The production in question was the beloved, widely known staging of Saint George, and everyone working backstage pitched in with the preparations, including the women from every local household. If sisters and sweethearts hadn’t lent their support, the costumes would almost certainly have turned out poorly; that said, this particular brand of assistance was not without its downsides. No one could ever get these young women to honor longstanding tradition when designing and decorating the armor; they insisted on sewing silk and velvet loops and bows anywhere that suited their personal taste. Gorget, gusset, basinet, cuirass, gauntlet, sleeve—every last one of these components, in these women’s eyes, was a perfectly usable surface to stitch on scraps of bright, fluttering color.

“Now, let me recommend you,” pursued Stryver, “to look it in the face. I have looked it in the face, in my different way; look it in the face, you, in your different way. Marry. Provide somebody to take care of you. Never mind your having no enjoyment of women’s society, nor understanding of it, nor tact for it. Find out somebody. Find out some respectable woman with a little property--somebody in the landlady way, or lodging-letting way--and marry her, against a rainy day. That’s the kind of thing for _you_. Now think of it, Sydney.”

“Now, I advise you,” persisted Thorne, “to confront the matter directly. I have faced it after my own fashion; you must face it after yours. Take a wife. Secure someone to oversee your comfort. Pay no heed to your lack of relish for feminine company, nor your ignorance of their nature, nor your want of social grace. Seek someone out. Find a reputable woman with a modest inheritance—someone of the domestic or house-managing sort—and wed her as a hedge against hard times. That is precisely the arrangement for a man like you. Reflect upon it, Elias.”

How Mr. Brownlow went on, from day to day, filling the mind of his adopted child with stores of knowledge, and becoming attached to him, more and more, as his nature developed itself, and showed the thriving seeds of all he wished him to become—how he traced in him new traits of his early friend, that awakened in his own bosom old remembrances, melancholy and yet sweet and soothing—how the two orphans, tried by adversity, remembered its lessons in mercy to others, and mutual love, and fervent thanks to Him who had protected and preserved them—these are all matters which need not to be told. I have said that they were truly happy; and without strong affection and humanity of heart, and gratitude to that Being whose code is Mercy, and whose great attribute is Benevolence to all things that breathe, happiness can never be attained.

How Mr. Harcourt passed from day to day, storing the mind of his adopted boy with knowledge, and growing more and more fond of him as his character unfolded itself, and revealed the flourishing seeds of all he would fain have him become; how he discerned in him fresh lineaments of his earliest friend, and these recalled to his own breast memories long past, sad indeed, yet gentle and consoling; how the two children of misfortune, taught by suffering, remembered its lesson in kindness to others, in tenderness for one another, and in grateful praise to Him who had watched over and preserved them—these are matters there is no need to tell. I have said that they were truly happy; and without deep affection and a humane heart, and thankfulness to that Being whose law is Mercy, and whose chief attribute is goodness toward all that lives and breathes, happiness can never be won.

I’ll take my oath I didn’t say ‘Joseph Poorgrass o’ Weatherbury, sir.’ No, no; what’s right is right, and I never said sir to the bird, knowing very well that no man of a gentleman’s rank would be hollering there at that time o’ night. ‘Joseph Poorgrass of Weatherbury,’—that’s every word I said, and I shouldn’t ha’ said that if ’t hadn’t been for Keeper Day’s metheglin.... There, ’twas a merciful thing it ended where it did.”

“No, no, now—that’s going too far!” cried the shrinking fellow, who had all at once grown as bold as brass. “I never said _sir_. I’ll swear I never called out, ‘Abel Mugford o’ Nethercombe, sir.’ No, no; fairness is fairness, and I never put the title on the bird, being well aware that no gentleman of standing would be bawling there at that hour o’ the night. ‘Abel Mugford of Nethercombe’—that was the whole of it, every syllable; and I’d never ha’ spoken so much, if it hadn’t been for old Gaffin’s barley-wine.... There now, it was a merciful thing the matter stopped where it did.”

About half a dozen men came forward; and, one being selected by the magistrate, he deposed that he had been out fishing the night before with his son and brother-in-law, Daniel Nugent, when, about ten o’clock, they observed a strong northerly blast rising, and they accordingly put in for port. It was a very dark night, as the moon had not yet risen; they did not land at the harbour, but, as they had been accustomed, at a creek about two miles below. He walked on first, carrying a part of the fishing tackle, and his companions followed him at some distance. As he was proceeding along the sands, he struck his foot against something and fell at his length on the ground. His companions came up to assist him, and by the light of their lantern they found that he had fallen on the body of a man, who was to all appearance dead. Their first supposition was that it was the corpse of some person who had been drowned and was thrown on shore by the waves, but on examination they found that the clothes were not wet and even that the body was not then cold. They instantly carried it to the cottage of an old woman near the spot and endeavoured, but in vain, to restore it to life. It appeared to be a handsome young man, about five and twenty years of age. He had apparently been strangled, for there was no sign of any violence except the black mark of fingers on his neck.

About half a dozen men stepped forward, and when one was chosen by the magistrate, he stated under oath that he had been out fishing the previous night with his son and his brother-in-law, Daniel Nugent, when, about ten o’clock, they noticed a hard northerly wind beginning to rise, and so turned in for shore. It was a very dark night, the moon not yet having risen; they did not go in at the harbour, but, as they were accustomed to do, at a creek some two miles below it. He went on ahead, carrying part of the fishing gear, while the others followed at some distance behind. As he was making his way along the sands, his foot struck against something and he fell full length to the ground. His companions hurried up to help him, and by the light of their lantern they discovered that he had fallen upon the body of a man, who seemed to all appearances dead. At first they supposed it to be the corpse of someone drowned and washed ashore by the waves, but on closer inspection they found that the clothing was not wet, and that the body was not even cold. They immediately carried it to the cottage of an old woman nearby and tried, though without success, to bring it back to life. It seemed to be that of a handsome young man, about twenty-five years old. He had evidently been strangled, for there was no mark of injury save the black imprint of fingers around his neck.

“Oh, I cannot say—please, let me go!” she cried, her breath quickening as her chest began to heave. “I am terrified of what I should do! The king was in a flaming fury, and launched out his challenge and epithets with a most royal vigor. The knights were some little distance by now. They halted, greatly surprised, and turned in their saddles and looked back, as if wondering if it might be worth while to bother with such scum as we. Then they wheeled and started for us. Not a moment must be lost. I started for _them_. I passed them at a rattling gait, and as I went by I flung out a hair-lifting soul-scorching thirteen-jointed insult which made the king's effort poor and cheap by comparison. I got it out of the nineteenth century where they know how. They had such headway that they were nearly to the king before they could check up; then, frantic with rage, they stood up their horses on their hind hoofs and whirled them around, and the next moment here they came, breast to breast. I was seventy yards off, then, and scrambling up a great bowlder at the roadside. When they were within thirty yards of me they let their long lances droop to a level, depressed their mailed heads, and so, with their horse-hair plumes streaming straight out behind, most gallant to see, this lightning express came tearing for me! When they were within fifteen yards, I sent that bomb with a sure aim, and it struck the ground just under the horses' noses.

The king seethed with white-hot rage, hurling insults and challenges with all the blustering vigor of his royal station. The knights had already ridden a fair distance away when they stopped, thoroughly taken aback. They twisted in their saddles to glance back, plainly debating if miscreants like us were even worth the trouble of engaging. Then they spun their mounts around and charged straight for our position. There was not a second to spare. I ran headlong toward them instead. I darted past their galloping line at a breakneck clip, and as I went I loosed a blistering, blood-boiling, thirteen-part insult so vicious it made the king’s earlier outburst look tame and amateurish by comparison. I’d picked it up back in the nineteenth century, where folks knew how to craft a proper jeer. They had so much forward momentum that they nearly barrelled straight into the king before they could rein their steeds in. Mad with fury, they hauled their horses up onto their hind legs, spun them sharp around, and came thundering after me, their armored shoulders nearly brushing as they galloped side by side. By that point I was seventy yards away, scrambling up the side of a huge boulder perched at the edge of the road. When they drew within thirty yards of me, they lowered their long lances to a horizontal position, ducked their helmeted heads, and with their horsehair plumes streaming straight back in the wind—looking every bit the picture of daring knightly valor—this ironclad lightning bolt came tearing straight for my position! The second they were fifteen yards out, I hurled the bomb with perfect aim, and it struck the dirt just inches in front of their horses’ snorting nostrils.

He could hold fast to his conscientious objection to the United States, and still choose a course that would satisfy Marnie entirely. He took that path without a second thought.

Another part of the room soon riveted her gaze. At the other side of the chimney stood the settle, which is the necessary supplement to a fire so open that nothing less than a strong breeze will carry up the smoke. It is, to the hearths of old-fashioned cavernous fireplaces, what the east belt of trees is to the exposed country estate, or the north wall to the garden. Outside the settle candles gutter, locks of hair wave, young women shiver, and old men sneeze. Inside is Paradise. Not a symptom of a draught disturbs the air; the sitters’ backs are as warm as their faces, and songs and old tales are drawn from the occupants by the comfortable heat, like fruit from melon plants in a frame.

Her attention was soon seized by another corner of the room. Opposite the chimney stood the high-backed settle, that indispensable companion to a hearth so wide and open that only a stiff wind can draw the smoke upward. It is to the fireplaces of old, cavernous rooms what a sheltering belt of trees is to an exposed estate, or a north wall to a garden. Beyond the settle, candles flicker, strands of hair lift and stir, young women tremble, and old men catch cold. Within it lies Paradise. Not the faintest whisper of a draught troubles the air; the sitters’ backs bask as warmly as their faces, and the snug heat coaxes songs and old stories from them as gently as a frame coaxes melons from the vine.

He and his ledgers were frequently called upon regarding assets that had been seized and declared national property. Whatever he could manage to preserve for the rightful owners, he saved. There was no man alive better suited to guard the interests of Tellson’s or to maintain a discreet silence.

The girl on the summit of the load sat motionless, surrounded by tables and chairs with their legs upwards, backed by an oak settle, and ornamented in front by pots of geraniums, myrtles, and cactuses, together with a caged canary—all probably from the windows of the house just vacated. There was also a cat in a willow basket, from the partly-opened lid of which she gazed with half-closed eyes, and affectionately surveyed the small birds around.

Perched right on the very top of the stacked moving load, the girl sat perfectly still. Upside-down tables and chairs ringed her on all sides, with a heavy oak settle propped up behind her back. Lined up in front of her were potted geraniums, myrtles, and cacti, alongside a canary in a small cage—every last one of these items had almost certainly been pulled straight from the windows of the house they’d just vacated. There was a cat tucked into a woven willow basket too, peeking out through its half-opened lid with half-closed, drowsy eyes, as it gazed fondly at the small birds gathered nearby.

He went downstairs and out into the street, breathing great breaths of air. He had been suffocating in that atmosphere, while the apprentice’s chatter had driven him frantic. There had been times when it was all he could do to refrain from reaching over and mopping Jim’s face in the mush-plate. The more he had chattered, the more remote had Ruth seemed to him. How could he, herding with such cattle, ever become worthy of her? He was appalled at the problem confronting him, weighted down by the incubus of his working-class station. Everything reached out to hold him down—his sister, his sister’s house and family, Jim the apprentice, everybody he knew, every tie of life. Existence did not taste good in his mouth. Up to then he had accepted existence, as he had lived it with all about him, as a good thing. He had never questioned it, except when he read books; but then, they were only books, fairy stories of a fairer and impossible world. But now he had seen that world, possible and real, with a flower of a woman called Ruth in the midmost centre of it; and thenceforth he must know bitter tastes, and longings sharp as pain, and hopelessness that tantalized because it fed on hope.

He descended the narrow stairwell and stepped into the open air, drawing deep, desperate lungfuls of the evening. The stagnant atmosphere inside had begun to choke him, and Arthur’s relentless, vapid prattle had pushed his nerves to a jagged edge. More than once, he had felt a violent impulse to seize the boy by the scruff and bury his grinning face in the bowl of cold porridge. Every mindless syllable Arthur uttered seemed to widen the gulf between himself and Elena. How could a man mired in such common company ever hope to stand beside her?

The sheer scale of his predicament struck him with a heavy dread, the leaden weight of his birthright pinning him to the earth. It was as if every thread of his life—his sister’s cramped parlor, her noisy brood, the apprentice’s idiocy, and every familiar face—conspired to drag him back into the mud. The very flavor of his life had turned to ash. Until this moment, he had moved through his days with a blunt acceptance, viewing his lot as natural and sufficient. Doubts had only ever flickered when he retreated into novels, but those had been mere fables—glimpses of a porcelain reality that could never truly exist. Now, however, he had glimpsed that world in the flesh, a tangible paradise with Elena at its radiant heart. From this night on, his bread would taste of bile, his desires would cut like glass, and he would be haunted by a despair that was all the more cruel for its refusal to let go of hope.

Soapy had confidence in himself from the lowest button of his vest upward. He was shaven, and his coat was decent and his neat black, ready-tied four-in-hand had been presented to him by a lady missionary on Thanksgiving Day. If he could reach a table in the restaurant unsuspected success would be his. The portion of him that would show above the table would raise no doubt in the waiter’s mind. A roasted mallard duck, thought Soapy, would be about the thing—with a bottle of Chablis, and then Camembert, a demi-tasse and a cigar. One dollar for the cigar would be enough. The total would not be so high as to call forth any supreme manifestation of revenge from the café management; and yet the meat would leave him filled and happy for the journey to his winter refuge.

Soapy had unshakable confidence in every part of himself from the lowest button of his vest upward. He was clean-shaven, his coat was presentable, and his crisp pre-tied black four-in-hand tie had been a gift from a lady missionary on Thanksgiving Day. If he could make his way to a table in the restaurant without raising suspicion, his gambit would be a success. Every inch of him visible above the table would give the waitstaff no cause for doubt. A roasted mallard duck, Soapy mused, would be just the thing—paired with a bottle of Chablis, followed by Camembert cheese, a demi-tasse, and a cigar. One dollar would be more than enough for the cigar. The total bill would not be steep enough to provoke any excessive retaliation from the café management, yet the rich meat would leave him sated and happy for the journey to his winter refuge.

Mr. Direck had learnt at the main-line junction that he had to tell the guard to stop the train for Matching's Easy; it only stopped "by request"; the thing was getting better and better; and when Mr. Direck seized his grip and got out of the train there was just one little old Essex station-master and porter and signalman and everything, holding a red flag in his hand and talking to Mr. Britling about the cultivation of the sweet peas which glorified the station. And there was the Mr. Britling who was the only item of business and the greatest expectation in Mr. Direck's European journey, and he was quite unlike the portraits Mr. Direck had seen and quite unmistakably Mr. Britling all the same, since there was nobody else upon the platform, and he was advancing with a gesture of welcome.

At the trunk-line interchange, Mr. Direck discovered he must ask the conductor to halt the train at Matching's Easy; it was a request-only stop; the whole affair grew more charming by the minute; and when he snatched his valise and alighted, there stood a single elderly Essex stationmaster—who was also porter, signalman, and everything else—clutching a red pennant and chatting with Mr. Britling about the sweet-pea borders that adorned the platform. And there was the very Mr. Britling who constituted the sole purpose and supreme hope of Mr. Direck's European tour, and he looked nothing like the photographs Mr. Direck had studied yet was unmistakably Mr. Britling nonetheless, since no other soul occupied the platform, and he was stepping forward with a welcoming hand.

Bathsheba loved Troy in the way that only self-reliant women love when they abandon their self-reliance. When a strong woman recklessly throws away her strength she is worse than a weak woman who has never had any strength to throw away. One source of her inadequacy is the novelty of the occasion. She has never had practice in making the best of such a condition. Weakness is doubly weak by being new.

Elspeth adored Torin with the fierce, unmoored devotion that only fiercely independent women know when they cast aside their own hard-won self-sufficiency. When a woman accustomed to holding her own carelessly discards that well-earned fortitude, she fares far worse than the soft, untested woman who never possessed such strength to squander in the first place. A core root of her helplessness lies in how entirely unacquainted she is with this state of being. She has never had cause to practice navigating, let alone making the most of, such vulnerable disarray. Fragility is doubly feeble when it is still new, unrooted in long habit.

And then out I came. But I didn't get any shout. There was a wondering and eloquent silence for a moment, then a great wave of laughter began to sweep along that human sea, but a warning bugle-blast cut its career short. I was in the simplest and comfortablest of gymnast costumes--flesh-colored tights from neck to heel, with blue silk puffings about my loins, and bareheaded. My horse was not above medium size, but he was alert, slender-limbed, muscled with watchsprings, and just a greyhound to go. He was a beauty, glossy as silk, and naked as he was when he was born, except for bridle and ranger-saddle.

I stepped out then. But no roar of welcome met me. Instead, a stunned and expressive silence lingered for a beat, followed by a massive wave of laughter that began to roll through the sea of people, only to be cut dead by a sharp bugle blast. I was dressed in the most basic and practical of athletic outfits—flesh-toned tights from my throat to my heels, with blue silk ruffles around my waist, and nothing on my head. My horse was only average-sized, but he was sharp and lean, his muscles like coiled springs, and he was a pure racer. He was a gorgeous creature, his coat as glossy as satin, and he was as unadorned as the day he was born, except for his bridle and a ranger-saddle.
