One Saturday afternoon Mr. Britling found himself with Mrs. Britling at Claverings. Lady Homartyn was in mourning for her two nephews, the Glassington boys, who had both been killed, one in Flanders, the other in Gallipoli. Raeburn was there too, despondent and tired-looking. There were three young men in khaki, one with the red of a staff officer; there were two or three women whom Mr. Britling had not met before, and Miss Sharsper the novelist, fresh from nursing experience among the convalescents in the south of France. But he was disgusted to find that the gathering was dominated by his old antagonist, Lady Frensham, unsubdued, unaltered, rampant over them all, arrogant, impudent, insulting. She was in mourning, she had the most splendid black furs Mr. Britling had ever seen; her large triumphant profile came out of them like the head of a vulture out of its ruff; her elder brother was a wounded prisoner in Germany, her second was dead; it would seem that hers were the only sacrifices the war had yet extorted from any one. She spoke as though it gave her the sole right to criticise the war or claim compensation for the war.

One grey Saturday afternoon, Mr. Haleworth arrived at Hawthorn Grange beside his wife, Elspeth. Lady Fallowfield, the estate’s owner, was in deep mourning for her two great-nephews, the Merrivale boys, both recently killed—one on the Western Front in Flanders, the other at Suvla Bay during the Gallipoli campaign. Mr. Thwaite, the local antiquarian, was also present, hunched and hollow-eyed with fatigue and quiet despair. Three young men in army khaki lingered near the fireplace, one marked with the scarlet tabs of a staff officer; there were two or three women Mr. Haleworth had never been introduced to, and Miss Elowen Voss, the popular novelist, just returned from three months tending convalescent soldiers in the south of France. But Haleworth’s mood curdled when he saw the gathering was entirely dominated by his long-time adversary, Lady Kershaw: unbowed, unchanged, lording over every conversation, sharp-tongued, arrogant, deliberately cutting to anyone who dared contradict her. She too wore mourning, draped in the most lavish jet-black sable furs Haleworth had ever seen; her broad, triumphant profile jutted from the thick fur collar like the head of a golden eagle from its ruff of neck feathers. Her elder brother was a wounded prisoner of war in Germany, her second brother had been killed at Ypres the year prior, and she carried herself as if her losses were the only meaningful sacrifices the war had exacted from anyone in the whole of the country. She spoke as though her grief granted her the sole, unassailable right to critique the war’s conduct or lay claim to compensation for its harms.

It was at this time, at the lowest ebb, that the smooth-running editorial machine broke down. A cog must have slipped or an oil-cup run dry, for the postman brought him one morning a short, thin envelope. Martin glanced at the upper left-hand corner and read the name and address of the _Transcontinental Monthly_. His heart gave a great leap, and he suddenly felt faint, the sinking feeling accompanied by a strange trembling of the knees. He staggered into his room and sat down on the bed, the envelope still unopened, and in that moment came understanding to him how people suddenly fall dead upon receipt of extraordinarily good news.

It was then, at the very bottom of the trough, that the smooth-working editorial machine finally failed. Some gear must have slipped, or an oil cup must have gone dry, for one morning the postman handed him a short, narrow envelope. Martin looked at the upper left corner and saw the name and address of the _Transcontinental Monthly_. His heart leaped violently, and at once he felt faint, that dropping sensation accompanied by an odd trembling in his knees. He made his way unsteadily to his room and sat down on the bed, the envelope still unopened, and in that instant he understood how people can suddenly die on receiving news that is extraordinarily good.

Withal, as she felt and perceived, he was such a good thing, such a very good thing; so kind, so trustworthy, with a sort of slow strength, with a careful honesty, a big good childishness, a passion for fairness. And so helpless in her hands. She could lash him and distress him. Yet she could not shake his slowly formed convictions.

Furthermore, she sensed he was a truly fine man; he was so gentle and dependable, possessing a deliberate strength and a scrupulous integrity. There was a vast, innocent simplicity about him, joined with a profound commitment to justice. He was entirely vulnerable in her presence, and she could wound or trouble him as she pleased. Yet she could never hope to alter his deeply rooted beliefs.

Afterwards she repented very touchingly, and said that if only he would love her she would swallow a thousand Ediths. He waived a certain disrespect in the idea of her swallowing Edith, and they had a beautiful reconciliation and talked of exalted things, and in the evening he worked quite well upon "The Silent Places" and thought of half-a-dozen quite wonderful lines, and in the course of the next day he returned to Dower House and Mr. Direck and considerable piles of correspondence and the completion of the Irish pamphlet.

Afterward she apologized with such tender, abashed earnestness, saying that if only he would still love her, she would suffer a thousand Elspeths without a word of protest. He set aside the faint, humorous disrespect implicit in framing the sentiment as suffering Elspeths, and they shared a gentle, perfect reconciliation, speaking for hours of lofty, meaningful things. That evening he worked with rare focus on *The Hushed Thresholds*, turning up half a dozen startlingly lovely lines that felt almost too good to be his own. By the middle of the next day, he had returned to Grange Manor, to Mr. Hale, to tottering stacks of unaddressed correspondence, and to the final completion of his Highland land reform pamphlet.

The adjustment of the farmer’s hazy conceptions of her charms to the portrait of herself she now presented him with was less a diminution than a difference. The starting-point selected by the judgment was her height. She seemed tall, but the pail was a small one, and the hedge diminutive; hence, making allowance for error by comparison with these, she could have been not above the height to be chosen by women as best. All features of consequence were severe and regular. It may have been observed by persons who go about the shires with eyes for beauty, that in Englishwoman a classically-formed face is seldom found to be united with a figure of the same pattern, the highly-finished features being generally too large for the remainder of the frame; that a graceful and proportionate figure of eight heads usually goes off into random facial curves. Without throwing a Nymphean tissue over a milkmaid, let it be said that here criticism checked itself as out of place, and looked at her proportions with a long consciousness of pleasure. From the contours of her figure in its upper part, she must have had a beautiful neck and shoulders; but since her infancy nobody had ever seen them. Had she been put into a low dress she would have run and thrust her head into a bush. Yet she was not a shy girl by any means; it was merely her instinct to draw the line dividing the seen from the unseen higher than they do it in towns.

The farmer’s vague image of her attractiveness, when measured against the actual figure she now cut, underwent not so much a downgrading as a shift in kind. His eye first seized upon her stature as a reference point. She looked tall, yet the bucket was small and the hedge low; allowing for the distortion these objects introduced, she likely stood no higher than the height most women would consider ideal. Her prominent features were sharp and symmetrical. Those who travel the counties with an eye for beauty may have noted that in English women a classically sculpted face rarely pairs with an equally classical body, the finely drawn features tending to overpower the frame; a graceful, eight-heads-high figure usually dissolves into irregular facial lines. Without draping a milkmaid in nymph-like gauze, let us simply say that here judgment paused as if intruding, and regarded her proportions with a prolonged sense of delight. From the shape of her upper body, she must have possessed a lovely neck and shoulders; but no one had glimpsed them since she was a child. Had she been dressed in a low-cut gown, she would have bolted and buried her head in a bush. Yet she was hardly a timid girl; it was only her instinct to place the boundary between the visible and the hidden higher than town dwellers do.

Troy stood at the entrance to the booth, where a gipsy-woman was frying pancakes over a little fire of sticks and selling them at a penny a-piece, and looked over the heads of the people within. He could see nothing of Pennyways, but he soon discerned Bathsheba through an opening into the reserved space at the further end. Troy thereupon retreated, went round the tent into the darkness, and listened. He could hear Bathsheba’s voice immediately inside the canvas; she was conversing with a man. A warmth overspread his face: surely she was not so unprincipled as to flirt in a fair! He wondered if, then, she reckoned upon his death as an absolute certainty. To get at the root of the matter, Troy took a penknife from his pocket and softly made two little cuts crosswise in the cloth, which, by folding back the corners left a hole the size of a wafer. Close to this he placed his face, withdrawing it again in a movement of surprise; for his eye had been within twelve inches of the top of Bathsheba’s head. It was too near to be convenient. He made another hole a little to one side and lower down, in a shaded place beside her chair, from which it was easy and safe to survey her by looking horizontally.

Rowan paused at the edge of the stall, where a Romani woman was toasting flatcakes above a small blaze of twigs and vending them for a copper each, and peered across the crowd gathered inside. He caught no glimpse of Merrick, but soon spotted Seraphine through a gap leading to the cordoned-off section at the far end. Rowan then withdrew, circled the pavilion into the shadows, and strained his ears. He could hear Seraphine’s tones just beyond the canvas; she was speaking with a man. A flush crept over his cheeks: surely she was not so shameless as to dally at a carnival! He wondered whether she now counted his dying as a settled fact. To uncover the truth, Rowan drew a small blade from his coat and quietly incised two tiny crossing slits in the fabric, which, when the corners were folded back, yielded an opening no larger than a coin. He brought his face near this peephole, then jerked it away in a start; for his gaze had rested scarcely a foot from the crown of Seraphine’s head. It was far too close for comfort. He cut a second aperture slightly to one side and farther down, in a dim nook beside her seat, from which he could easily and safely observe her by looking straight ahead.

The maltster, after having lain down in his clothes for a few hours, was now sitting beside a three-legged table, breakfasting off bread and bacon. This was eaten on the plateless system, which is performed by placing a slice of bread upon the table, the meat flat upon the bread, a mustard plaster upon the meat, and a pinch of salt upon the whole, then cutting them vertically downwards with a large pocket-knife till wood is reached, when the severed lump is impaled on the knife, elevated, and sent the proper way of food.

The maltster had stretched out fully dressed for a short rest and was now seated at a rickety three-legged table, making his breakfast of bread and bacon. He followed the plateless method: laying a slice of bread directly on the wood, setting the meat flat atop it, spreading a layer of mustard over the meat, and scattering a pinch of salt across everything. Then, using a hefty pocket-knife, he sliced straight down until the blade met the table, speared the severed morsel on the knife-point, lifted it, and dispatched it along its rightful path.

From every provident point of view his mother was so undoubtedly right, that he was not without a sickness of heart in finding he could shake her.

When I was about fifteen years old we had retired to our house near Belrive, when we witnessed a most violent and terrible thunderstorm. It advanced from behind the mountains of Jura, and the thunder burst at once with frightful loudness from various quarters of the heavens. I remained, while the storm lasted, watching its progress with curiosity and delight. As I stood at the door, on a sudden I beheld a stream of fire issue from an old and beautiful oak which stood about twenty yards from our house; and so soon as the dazzling light vanished, the oak had disappeared, and nothing remained but a blasted stump. When we visited it the next morning, we found the tree shattered in a singular manner. It was not splintered by the shock, but entirely reduced to thin ribbons of wood. I never beheld anything so utterly destroyed.

In my sixteenth year, during our retreat to the family estate overlooking the lake, I bore witness to a tempest of unparalleled ferocity. It gathered behind the dark crags of the Jura, and the thunder erupted with a sudden, deafening roar that seemed to shake the very foundations of the world. I stood upon the threshold, captivated by the chaotic splendor of the elements. Without warning, a pillar of celestial fire descended upon a majestic elm that had long graced our lawn; once the brilliance faded, the great tree had vanished, leaving only a scorched and skeletal ruin. The following morning revealed a strange sight: the trunk had not merely split, but had been teased into a thousand silken filaments of timber. Never before had I seen such total annihilation.

“God! We are animals! Brute-beasts!” Martin muttered aloud, as he watched the progress of the fight. It was to him, with his splendid power of vision, like gazing into a kinetoscope. He was both onlooker and participant. His long months of culture and refinement shuddered at the sight; then the present was blotted out of his consciousness and the ghosts of the past possessed him, and he was Martin Eden, just returned from sea and fighting Cheese-Face on the Eighth Street Bridge. He suffered and toiled and sweated and bled, and exulted when his naked knuckles smashed home.

"Good God, we are nothing but beasts! Savage animals!" Martin whispered under his breath while tracking the brawl's every move. To his keen, perceptive eyes, the violence unfolded like a flickering kinetoscope. He found himself trapped between being a witness and a combatant. His recent months of intellectual growth and social polish recoiled from the spectacle, but then the present moment dissolved. The shadows of his history took hold of him, and he was once again the Martin Eden of old, back from a long voyage and trading blows with Cheese-Face on the Eighth Street Bridge. He felt the agony, the labor, the sweat, and the gore, experiencing a primal triumph each time his bare knuckles struck home.

She had spoken frankly, and neither asked nor expected any reply from Gabriel more satisfactory than that she had obtained. Yet in the centremost parts of her complicated heart there existed at this minute a little pang of disappointment, for a reason she would not allow herself to recognize. Oak had not once wished her free that he might marry her himself—had not once said, “I could wait for you as well as he.” That was the insect sting. Not that she would have listened to any such hypothesis. O no—for wasn’t she saying all the time that such thoughts of the future were improper, and wasn’t Gabriel far too poor a man to speak sentiment to her? Yet he might have just hinted about that old love of his, and asked, in a playful off-hand way, if he might speak of it. It would have seemed pretty and sweet, if no more; and then she would have shown how kind and inoffensive a woman’s “No” can sometimes be. But to give such cool advice—the very advice she had asked for—it ruffled our heroine all the afternoon.

She had spoken plainly, and she did not ask or expect any answer from Gabriel more satisfying than the one she had received. And yet, at the very center of her complicated heart, there was at that moment a small stab of disappointment for a reason she refused to admit even to herself. Oak had not once wished her free so that he might marry her himself—had not once said, “I could wait for you as well as he.” That was the little sting. Not that she would have listened to any such notion. Oh no—for wasn’t she saying all the while that thoughts of the future were improper, and wasn’t Gabriel far too poor a man for sentiment with her? And yet he might at least have hinted at that old love of his, and asked, in some lightly teasing way, whether he might mention it. It would have been touching and sweet, if nothing else; and then she would have shown how gentle and harmless a woman’s “No” can sometimes be. But to offer such cool advice—the very advice she had sought—kept our heroine unsettled all afternoon.

The night was cold and bleak, but full of stars. He had already mastered the local topography, and he knew now exactly where all the bombs that had been showered upon the place had fallen. Here was the corner of blackened walls and roasted beams where three wounded horses had been burnt alive in a barn, here the row of houses, some smashed, some almost intact, where a mutilated child had screamed for two hours before she could be rescued from the debris that had pinned her down, and taken to the hospital. Everywhere by the dim light of the shaded street lamps he could see the black holes and gaps of broken windows; sometimes abundant, sometimes rare and exceptional, among otherwise uninjured dwellings. Many of the victims he had visited in the little cottage hospital where Aunt Wilshire had just died. She was the eleventh dead. Altogether fifty-seven people had been killed or injured in this brilliant German action. They were all civilians, and only twelve were men.

The night was raw and desolate, yet thick with stars. By now he knew the lay of the town so well that he could tell exactly where every bomb in the barrage had come down. There was the corner where walls had been scorched black and beams charred bare, and where three wounded horses had been burned to death in a barn; there the line of houses, some shattered, some scarcely touched, where a maimed child had screamed for two hours before anyone could reach her beneath the wreckage that held her fast and carry her off to hospital. In the wan glow of the hooded street lamps he saw everywhere the dark cavities and jagged breaks of broken windows, sometimes in plenty, sometimes only as isolated and uncanny wounds among the otherwise untouched houses. He had already gone to see many of the sufferers in the small cottage hospital where Aunt Bertram had only just died. She was the eleventh to die. In all, fifty-seven people had been killed or wounded in this brilliant German exploit. They were all civilians, and only twelve of them were men.

When Mr. Seeders had finished his weakfish he got up, put his arm around Tildy’s waist, kissed her loudly and impudently, walked out upon the street, snapped his fingers in the direction of the laundry, and hied himself to play pennies in the slot machines at the Amusement Arcade.

One afternoon, Mr. Seeders arrived for his meal with the flush of beer already upon him. The restaurant was nearly empty, holding just a couple of other patrons. Once he had polished off his weakfish, he rose, slid an arm around Tildy’s waist, and planted a loud, brazen kiss on her. Then he sauntered out to the street, snapped his fingers toward the laundry, and headed off to lose pennies in the slot machines at the Amusement Arcade.

“All thinkers on general subjects, the greatest minds in the world, in fact, rely on the specialists. Herbert Spencer did that. He generalized upon the findings of thousands of investigators. He would have had to live a thousand lives in order to do it all himself. And so with Darwin. He took advantage of all that had been learned by the florists and cattle-breeders.”

Every philosopher of broad themes—the most profound intellects on earth—actually depends upon the specialist. Herbert Spencer followed this model. He drew his universal principles from the raw data of countless individual researchers. He would have required a thousand lifetimes to gather such evidence personally. Darwin was no different; he relied on the practical insights already gained by horticulturalists and livestock farmers.

Troy entered Weatherbury churchyard about ten o’clock and went immediately to the corner where he had marked the vacant grave early in the morning. It was on the obscure side of the tower, screened to a great extent from the view of passers along the road—a spot which until lately had been abandoned to heaps of stones and bushes of alder, but now it was cleared and made orderly for interments, by reason of the rapid filling of the ground elsewhere.

Troy stepped into Weatherbury churchyard at roughly ten o’clock, and made straight for the corner where he’d marked out the empty grave plot first thing that morning. It sat on the unobtrusive side of the tower, almost entirely hidden from the view of people passing along the adjacent road. Until just a short while before, that spot had been left forgotten, heaped with piles of loose stone and thick alder bushes, but it had recently been cleared and straightened out to accommodate new burials, as the rest of the cemetery grounds were filling up at a rapid clip.

The persistent torrent from the gurgoyle’s jaws directed all its vengeance into the grave. The rich tawny mould was stirred into motion, and boiled like chocolate. The water accumulated and washed deeper down, and the roar of the pool thus formed spread into the night as the head and chief among other noises of the kind created by the deluging rain. The flowers so carefully planted by Fanny’s repentant lover began to move and writhe in their bed. The winter-violets turned slowly upside down, and became a mere mat of mud. Soon the snowdrop and other bulbs danced in the boiling mass like ingredients in a cauldron. Plants of the tufted species were loosened, rose to the surface, and floated off.

The unrelenting downpour spewing from the gargoyle’s mouth poured all its unbridled force straight down into the open grave. The deep, warm brown loam churned and roiled, bubbling thick as melted chocolate. Water kept piling up, carving deeper into the earth as it settled, and the low, thunderous roar of the growing pool cut through the dark, louder than any other sound the drenching storm had stirred up across the grounds. The blooms Fanny’s regretful former lover had tended so gently started to twist and thrash in their planting bed. The winter violets flipped slowly root-over-bloom, dissolving into nothing but a sodden, muck-covered mat. Before long, snowdrops and other bulb flowers bounced and tossed in the churning mire like bits of seasoning tossed into a bubbling cauldron. Clumping, tufted ground cover plants were torn free from the soil, bobbed up to the top of the pool, and drifted away on the murky water.

“Well,” he confessed, “I was a bit shivery at first; but I got used to it. I used to feel sorry for that poor girl, though. That made me forget to be afraid. She was such a beauty, in spirit as well as in appearance, and she was only slightly touched; yet she was doomed to lie there, living the life of a primitive savage and rotting slowly away. Leprosy is far more terrible than you can imagine it.”

“Well,” he admitted, “I felt a chill of fear at first, but I grew accustomed to it. Still, my heart ached for that poor girl, and pity drove out the dread. She was lovely in soul as much as in face, and her mind was only lightly clouded; yet she was condemned to languish there, living like some primitive creature while her body decayed by inches. Leprosy is more dreadful than you could ever conceive.”

Everything was bowed down, dejected, oppressed, and broken. Habitations, fences, domesticated animals, men, women, children, and the soil that bore them--all worn out.

She had a singular insight into life, considering that she had never mixed with it. There are instances of persons who, without clear ideas of the things they criticize have yet had clear ideas of the relations of those things. Blacklock, a poet blind from his birth, could describe visual objects with accuracy; Professor Sanderson, who was also blind, gave excellent lectures on colour, and taught others the theory of ideas which they had and he had not. In the social sphere these gifted ones are mostly women; they can watch a world which they never saw, and estimate forces of which they have only heard. We call it intuition.

Her understanding of life was remarkably profound, especially given that she had never truly participated in it. There are cases of people who, despite having no direct experience of the subjects they analyze, still possess a keen sense of how those subjects relate to one another. Blacklock, a poet blind from birth, was able to describe the visual world with startling precision; Professor Sanderson, who was also blind, gave masterful lectures on the properties of color, teaching others about concepts they could see while he could not. In the social realm, these gifted individuals are often women; they are able to observe a world they have never set foot in and gauge the strength of forces they have only heard about. We label this gift intuition.

Mrs. Britling did not learn of the blow that had struck them until after dinner that night. She was so accustomed to ignore his incomprehensible moods that she did not perceive that there was anything tragic about him until they sat at table together. He seemed heavy and sulky and disposed to avoid her, but that sort of moodiness was nothing very strange to her. She knew that things that seemed to her utterly trivial, the reading of political speeches in _The Times_, little comments on life made in the most casual way, mere movements, could so avert him. She had cultivated a certain disregard of such fitful darknesses. But at the dinner-table she looked up, and was stabbed to the heart to see a haggard white face and eyes of deep despair regarding her ambiguously.

Mrs. Britling didn’t find out about the catastrophic blow that had hit their household until after dinner wrapped up that night. She was so used to tuning out his impossible-to-read moods that she didn’t realize anything was truly, terribly wrong with him until they’d both settled into their seats at the dining table. He seemed weighed down, irritable, and eager to avoid any interaction with her, but that kind of sullen fit was nothing new to her. She knew that things that struck her as totally insignificant—poring over political speeches in *The Times*, throwaway little comments about daily life made without a second thought, even small, meaningless gestures—could push him to withdraw completely. She’d deliberately trained herself to brush off those sudden, unpredictable bouts of gloom. But when she glanced up mid-meal, she was stabbed straight through the heart to catch sight of his haggard, pale face, his eyes heavy with searing, deep despair, fixed on her in a way she couldn’t make sense of.

I looked up and saw, far off in the shade of a tree, half a dozen armed knights and their squires; and straightway there was bustle among them and tightening of saddle-girths for the mount. My pipe was ready and would have been lit, if I had not been lost in thinking about how to banish oppression from this land and restore to all its people their stolen rights and manhood without disobliging anybody. I lit up at once, and by the time I had got a good head of reserved steam on, here they came. All together, too; none of those chivalrous magnanimities which one reads so much about --one courtly rascal at a time, and the rest standing by to see fair play. No, they came in a body, they came with a whirr and a rush, they came like a volley from a battery; came with heads low down, plumes streaming out behind, lances advanced at a level. It was a handsome sight, a beautiful sight--for a man up a tree. I laid my lance in rest and waited, with my heart beating, till the iron wave was just ready to break over me, then spouted a column of white smoke through the bars of my helmet. You should have seen the wave go to pieces and scatter! This was a finer sight than the other one.

I lit it at once, and by the time I had built up a proper reserve of steam, there they came. All at once, too; none of those chivalrous courtesies one reads of so often--one polished villain at a time, while the rest stand aside to watch fair play. No, they came together, they came with a whir and a rush, they came like a battery’s volley; came with heads bent low, plumes streaming behind, lances leveled straight ahead. It was a fine sight, a splendid sight—for a man in a tree. I set my lance in rest and waited, my heart pounding, until the iron wave was just about to break over me, then blew a column of white smoke through the bars of my helmet. You should have seen that wave break apart and scatter! That was a finer sight than the first.

The Christmas of 1914 found England, whose landscape had hitherto been almost as peaceful and soldierless as Massachusetts, already far gone along the path of transformation into a country full of soldiers and munition makers and military supplies. The soldiers came first, on the well-known and greatly admired British principle of "first catch your hare" and then build your kitchen. Always before, Christmas had been a time of much gaiety and dressing up and prancing and two-stepping at the Dower House, but this year everything was too uncertain to allow of any gathering of guests. Hugh got leave for the day after Christmas, but Teddy was tied; and Cissie and Letty went off with the small boy to take lodgings near him. The Van der Pants had hoped to see an English Christmas at Matching's Easy, but within three weeks of Christmas Day Mr. Van der Pant found a job that he could do in Nottingham, and carried off his family. The two small boys cheered their hearts with paper decorations, but the Christmas Tree was condemned as too German, and it was discovered that Santa Claus had suddenly become Old Father Christmas again. The small boys discovered that the price of lead soldiers had risen, and were unable to buy electric torches, on which they had set their hearts. There was to have been a Christmas party at Claverings, but at the last moment Lady Homartyn had to hurry off to an orphan nephew who had been seriously wounded near Ypres, and the light of Claverings was darkened.

The Christmas of 1914 found Britain, whose countryside had until then been almost as tranquil and soldier-free as Connecticut, already well advanced upon the road to becoming a land of uniforms, shell works, and military stores. The soldiers came first, in accordance with the familiar and much-admired British maxim of catching the hare before laying the kitchen fire. Christmas had always been a season of merry-making at the Manor House, with dressing up and capering and quick-stepping in abundance; but this year the times were too unsettled to permit the assembling of a company. Julian was granted leave for the day after Christmas, but Robin was held fast; and Nan and Bess went away with the little boy to take rooms near him. The de Vrieses had been looking forward to an English Christmas at Ashcombe Hall, but within three weeks of Christmas Day Mr. de Vries had found work he could do in Sheffield, and carried his household off with him. The two small boys brightened their spirits with paper chains and colored stars, but the Christmas tree was denounced as too German, and it was suddenly agreed that Santa Claus had reverted to Father Christmas after all. They discovered also that the price of tin soldiers had risen beyond their reach, and they had to go without the electric lanterns upon which they had set their hearts. A Christmas entertainment had been planned at Langford, but at the eleventh hour Lady Marston was summoned away to an orphaned nephew gravely wounded near Ypres, and the lights of Langford were extinguished.

At a table behind him sat the Artful Dodger, Master Charles Bates, and Mr. Chitling: all intent upon a game of whist; the Artful taking dummy against Master Bates and Mr. Chitling. The countenance of the first-named gentleman, peculiarly intelligent at all times, acquired great additional interest from his close observance of the game, and his attentive perusal of Mr. Chitling’s hand; upon which, from time to time, as occasion served, he bestowed a variety of earnest glances: wisely regulating his own play by the result of his observations upon his neighbour’s cards. It being a cold night, the Dodger wore his hat, as, indeed, was often his custom within doors. He also sustained a clay pipe between his teeth, which he only removed for a brief space when he deemed it necessary to apply for refreshment to a quart pot upon the table, which stood ready filled with gin-and-water for the accommodation of the company.

At a scrubbed pine table just over his shoulder sat Cunning Jem, Master Toby Hackett, and Mr. Griggs, all wholly absorbed in a game of whist; Jem held the dummy hand, pitted against the paired play of Hackett and Griggs. The face of the first-named young rascal, sharp and quick-witted even on the slowest of days, took on an extra air of keen absorption as he tracked every turn of the game, his gaze drifting repeatedly to study Mr. Griggs’ fanned-out hand. Whenever the moment suited, he darted a series of shrewd sidelong looks at those exposed cards, carefully calibrating his own moves to what he’d gleaned from his neighbor’s hold. The night carried a sharp bite, so Jem kept his cap pulled down on his head, a habit he indulged even indoors more often than not. As for Bathsheba, she was not deceived into the belief that Farmer Boldwood had walked by on business or in idleness. She collected the probabilities of the case, and concluded that she was herself responsible for Boldwood’s appearance there. It troubled her much to see what a great flame a little wildfire was likely to kindle. Bathsheba was no schemer for marriage, nor was she deliberately a trifler with the affections of men, and a censor’s experience on seeing an actual flirt after observing her would have been a feeling of surprise that Bathsheba could be so different from such a one, and yet so like what a flirt is supposed to be.

Bathsheba, for her part, was under no illusion that Farmer Boldwood had passed by on some errand or merely out of idle wandering. She weighed the likelihoods and understood that his presence there was her own doing. It disturbed her deeply to realize how great a blaze a small spark might ignite. Bathsheba was not one to scheme for a husband, nor did she consciously play with men’s hearts, and any seasoned critic who watched a true coquette at work and then observed her would have been astonished to find Bathsheba so unlike that figure, yet so closely resembling what a flirt is imagined to be.

“Justine, you may remember, was a great favourite of yours; and I recollect you once remarked that if you were in an ill humour, one glance from Justine could dissipate it, for the same reason that Ariosto gives concerning the beauty of Angelica—she looked so frank-hearted and happy. My aunt conceived a great attachment for her, by which she was induced to give her an education superior to that which she had at first intended. This benefit was fully repaid; Justine was the most grateful little creature in the world: I do not mean that she made any professions I never heard one pass her lips, but you could see by her eyes that she almost adored her protectress. Although her disposition was gay and in many respects inconsiderate, yet she paid the greatest attention to every gesture of my aunt. She thought her the model of all excellence and endeavoured to imitate her phraseology and manners, so that even now she often reminds me of her.

You surely recall Elara, who was always a particular favorite of yours; I remember your saying that if ever you fell into a melancholy, a single glimpse of her would suffice to dispel it. She possessed that quality of candid cheerfulness which the poets often ascribe to their most radiant muses. My mother became so deeply fond of her that she saw fit to provide the girl with an education far more sophisticated than was first planned. This generosity was met with the quietest, most sincere devotion; Elara never resorted to empty protestations of thanks, yet one could read a world of adoration in her eyes. Though her spirit was naturally buoyant and sometimes heedless, she remained keenly observant of my mother’s every nuance. Viewing her as the absolute standard of grace, she strove to adopt her very way of speaking and carrying herself, so that even now, Elara’s movements frequently bring my mother back to my mind.

A whole village-full of sensuous emotion, scattered abroad all the year long, surged here in a focus for an hour. The forty hearts of those waving couples were beating as they had not done since, twelve months before, they had come together in similar jollity. For the time paganism was revived in their hearts, the pride of life was all in all, and they adored none other than themselves.

All the rich, sensory feeling stitched through the entire village, scattered thin across every ordinary day of the year, swelled into a single thrumming, concentrated focus here for one short hour. The forty hearts of those swaying, dancing pairs beat faster than they had at any point since they’d gathered for the same giddy, raucous celebration twelve full months prior. For that fleeting stretch, unbridled pagan warmth bloomed again in their chests, the unapologetic joy of being alive was the only thing that mattered, and the only thing they worshipped was the bright, vivid pulse of their own selves.

Never had he been at such an altitude of living, and he kept himself in the background, listening, observing, and pleasuring, replying in reticent monosyllables, saying, “Yes, miss,” and “No, miss,” to her, and “Yes, ma’am,” and “No, ma’am,” to her mother. He curbed the impulse, arising out of his sea-training, to say “Yes, sir,” and “No, sir,” to her brothers. He felt that it would be inappropriate and a confession of inferiority on his part—which would never do if he was to win to her. Also, it was a dictate of his pride. “By God!” he cried to himself, once; “I’m just as good as them, and if they do know lots that I don’t, I could learn ’m a few myself, all the same!” And the next moment, when she or her mother addressed him as “Mr. Eden,” his aggressive pride was forgotten, and he was glowing and warm with delight. He was a civilized man, that was what he was, shoulder to shoulder, at dinner, with people he had read about in books. He was in the books himself, adventuring through the printed pages of bound volumes.

He had never before reached such a peak of existence. He lingered on the edges, listening, watching, taking quiet pleasure, and answering in guarded monosyllables—"Yes, miss" and "No, miss" to her, "Yes, ma'am" and "No, ma'am" to her mother. He suppressed the sailor's reflex to say "Yes, sir" and "No, sir" to her brothers. It would strike the wrong note, he thought, a tacit admission of lower standing—and that would never serve if he meant to win her. His pride demanded it too. "By God!" he exclaimed inwardly at one point. "I'm every bit their equal, and if they know plenty I don't, I could teach them a thing or two myself, all the same!" Then, a moment later, when she or her mother called him "Mr. Eden," his bristling pride dissolved, and he glowed, suffused with pleasure. He was a man of cultivation now—that was the truth of it—sitting shoulder to shoulder at dinner with figures he had only met before in books. He himself had stepped into those books, adventuring through the printed pages of bound volumes.

It was plain that Jerry had usurped the functions of his cab, and was carrying a “load.” Indeed, the figure may be extended and he be likened to a bread-waggon if we admit the testimony of a youthful spectator, who was heard to remark “Jerry has got a bun.”

It was plain to see that Ollie had usurped his cab’s less dignified purpose, and was toting a very obvious “load.” Indeed, the figure can be extended to liken him to a baked goods delivery wagon, if we credit the testimony of a young bystander, who was heard to remark, “Ollie’s got a proper bun on.”

The breeze blew in from that direction, carrying the clear notes of far-off bells, gaily bursting into a peal: one, two, three, four, five. The ringers at East Egdon were announcing the wedding of Eustacia and her son.

Of course, I meant to take the train for Camelot. Train! Why, the station was as vacant as a cavern. I moved on. The journey to Camelot was a repetition of what I had already seen. The Monday and the Tuesday differed in no way from the Sunday. I arrived far in the night. From being the best electric-lighted town in the kingdom and the most like a recumbent sun of anything you ever saw, it was become simply a blot--a blot upon darkness--that is to say, it was darker and solider than the rest of the darkness, and so you could see it a little better; it made me feel as if maybe it was symbolical--a sort of sign that the Church was going to _keep_ the upper hand now, and snuff out all my beautiful civilization just like that. I found no life stirring in the somber streets. I groped my way with a heavy heart. The vast castle loomed black upon the hilltop, not a spark visible about it. The drawbridge was down, the great gate stood wide, I entered without challenge, my own heels making the only sound I heard--and it was sepulchral enough, in those huge vacant courts.

Naturally, I intended to take the train back to Camelot. A train! The station was as hollow as a cave. I pressed forward. The trip was nothing more than a repeat of everything I had already witnessed; Monday and Tuesday were identical to Sunday. I finally reached my destination in the dead of night. What had once been the most brilliantly illuminated town in the realm—shining like a sun resting on the horizon—was now nothing but a dark blotch against the sky. It was a mass of shadow thicker and more solid than the night itself, which only served to make its outline clearer. It struck me as symbolic, a grim omen that the Church was determined to keep its hold and snuff out my modern civilization in an instant. I saw no one moving in the gloomy streets. I felt my way along with a sinking heart. The great castle loomed like a black monolith on the hill, without a single spark of light. The drawbridge was lowered and the massive gates stood open; I walked in unchallenged, the click of my heels the only sound in the air—a ghostly noise that echoed through those enormous, empty courts.

He got up abruptly and tried to see himself in the dirty looking-glass over the wash-stand. He passed a towel over it and looked again, long and carefully. It was the first time he had ever really seen himself. His eyes were made for seeing, but up to that moment they had been filled with the ever changing panorama of the world, at which he had been too busy gazing, ever to gaze at himself. He saw the head and face of a young fellow of twenty, but, being unused to such appraisement, he did not know how to value it. Above a square-domed forehead he saw a mop of brown hair, nut-brown, with a wave to it and hints of curls that were a delight to any woman, making hands tingle to stroke it and fingers tingle to pass caresses through it. But he passed it by as without merit, in Her eyes, and dwelt long and thoughtfully on the high, square forehead,—striving to penetrate it and learn the quality of its content. What kind of a brain lay behind there? was his insistent interrogation. What was it capable of? How far would it take him? Would it take him to her?

He rose suddenly and tried to catch his reflection in the grimy mirror above the washstand. He wiped it with a towel and studied his image again, long and intently. It was the first time he had truly looked at himself. His eyes were designed for observation, but until now they had been absorbed by the shifting spectacle of the world; he had been too preoccupied gazing outward to ever examine his own face. He saw the head and features of a young man of twenty, but, unaccustomed to such scrutiny, he had no idea how to judge them. Above a broad, squared forehead, he noticed a thick shock of brown hair—nut-brown, with a gentle wave and subtle curls that any woman would find captivating, her hands itching to smooth it, her fingers longing to weave through it. Yet he dismissed it as worthless in her eyes and lingered instead, deep in thought, on the tall, squared forehead—trying to see through it and discern the nature of what lay within. What kind of mind resided there? was his persistent question. What could it achieve? How far could it carry him? Could it carry him to her?

The jailer lit the small heap, and it flared up fiercely, bright and warm. Thus it had come to pass, that Tellson’s was the triumphant perfection of inconvenience. After bursting open a door of idiotic obstinacy with a weak rattle in its throat, you fell into Tellson’s down two steps, and came to your senses in a miserable little shop, with two little counters, where the oldest of men made your cheque shake as if the wind rustled it, while they examined the signature by the dingiest of windows, which were always under a shower-bath of mud from Fleet-street, and which were made the dingier by their own iron bars proper, and the heavy shadow of Temple Bar. If your business necessitated your seeing “the House,” you were put into a species of Condemned Hold at the back, where you meditated on a misspent life, until the House came with its hands in its pockets, and you could hardly blink at it in the dismal twilight. Your money came out of, or went into, wormy old wooden drawers, particles of which flew up your nose and down your throat when they were opened and shut. Your bank-notes had a musty odour, as if they were fast decomposing into rags again. Your plate was stowed away among the neighbouring cesspools, and evil communications corrupted its good polish in a day or two. Your deeds got into extemporised strong-rooms made of kitchens and sculleries, and fretted all the fat out of their parchments into the banking-house air. Your lighter boxes of family papers went up-stairs into a Barmecide room, that always had a great dining-table in it and never had a dinner, and where, even in the year one thousand seven hundred and eighty, the first letters written to you by your old love, or by your little children, were but newly released from the horror of being ogled through the windows, by the heads exposed on Temple Bar with an insensate brutality and ferocity worthy of Abyssinia or Ashantee.

So it was that Tellson’s had reached a state of absolute, triumphant inconvenience. Once you had wrenched open a door that resisted with a stubborn, rattling gasp, you tumbled down two steps into the bank, finding yourself in a cramped and wretched shop. At two small counters, ancient clerks caused your check to flutter as if caught in a draft while they scrutinized your signature by the filthiest windows imaginable. These panes were constantly pelted by the mud of Fleet Street and further obscured by thick iron bars and the oppressive shadow of Temple Bar. Should your affairs require an audience with "the House," you were confined to a sort of death-row cell in the rear; there, you might ponder a life of regret until the management arrived, hands in pockets, barely discernible in the murky twilight. Your cash was pulled from or deposited into decaying wooden drawers, which released clouds of fine rot into your lungs every time they were handled. The banknotes emitted a stale, damp scent, as though they were rapidly reverting to the shredded rags from which they were born. Any silver you left for safekeeping was stored near the local sewers, where the foul vapors stripped away its luster within forty-eight hours. Your legal deeds were shoved into makeshift vaults that had once been kitchens, where the humid air seemed to draw the very oil from the parchment. Lighter crates of family records were carried to an upstairs room—a hollow dining hall containing a massive table that never saw a feast. There, even in 1780, the tenderest letters from lost loves or small children were kept in the grim shadow of the severed heads displayed on Temple Bar, a display of mindless ferocity that would have seemed barbaric in the most savage of lands.

He reached home damp and weary enough after his ten-mile walk. It had hardly been a propitious beginning, but he had chosen his course, and would show no swerving. The evening and the following morning were spent in concluding arrangements for his departure. To stay at home a minute longer than necessary after having once come to his determination would be, he felt, only to give new pain to his mother by some word, look, or deed.

He arrived at his doorstep soaked and spent from the ten-mile tramp. The outset had been anything but auspicious, yet he had fixed his path and resolved not to waver. That night and the next morning passed in tying up final threads for his leaving. Lingering at home even a moment beyond what was needful now that his mind was made up would, he sensed, merely inflict fresh hurt on his mother through some stray phrase, glance, or gesture.

Heaven opened then, indeed. The flash was almost too novel for its inexpressibly dangerous nature to be at once realized, and they could only comprehend the magnificence of its beauty. It sprang from east, west, north, south, and was a perfect dance of death. The forms of skeletons appeared in the air, shaped with blue fire for bones—dancing, leaping, striding, racing around, and mingling altogether in unparalleled confusion. With these were intertwined undulating snakes of green, and behind these was a broad mass of lesser light. Simultaneously came from every part of the tumbling sky what may be called a shout; since, though no shout ever came near it, it was more of the nature of a shout than of anything else earthly. In the meantime one of the grisly forms had alighted upon the point of Gabriel’s rod, to run invisibly down it, down the chain, and into the earth. Gabriel was almost blinded, and he could feel Bathsheba’s warm arm tremble in his hand—a sensation novel and thrilling enough; but love, life, everything human, seemed small and trifling in such close juxtaposition with an infuriated universe.

The sky truly split open right then. The burst of light was so startlingly unfamiliar that they didn’t immediately register its unspeakable peril, all they could grasp first was the staggering, unearthly beauty of it. It arced out from every point of the compass—east, west, north, south—a flawless, terrible dance of death. Skeleton shapes flickered into view in the open air, their bones forged from searing blue flame: dancing, leaping, striding, darting in circles, all blending together into a chaos no one could have imagined. Wriggling, coiling streaks of green lightning wove through them, and behind the whole swirling display loomed a wide, dimmer bank of diffused light. At the exact same moment, a sound tore out of every corner of the roiling sky that could only be called a shout; no human yell could ever match its power, but it aligned more with the raw, feral energy of a shout than any other earthly phenomenon. Right as that sound hit, one of those ghoulish, glowing shapes landed on the tip of Gabriel’s rod, snaking invisibly down its length, along the chain at its base, and straight into the soil. Gabriel was nearly blinded, and he could feel Bathsheba’s warm arm trembling where he clutched it, a jolt of something new and sharp and thrilling shooting through him at the contact; but love, life, every small, human concern felt trivial and tiny, pressed up so close against the unbridled rage of the universe itself.

“No sooner was Andrey asleep and the first whiff of neighbour Yeobright’s wind had got inside Andrey’s clarinet than everyone in church feeled in a moment there was a great soul among ’em. All heads would turn, and they’d say, ‘Ah, I thought ’twas he!’ One Sunday I can well mind—a bass viol day that time, and Yeobright had brought his own. ’Twas the Hundred-and-thirty-third to ‘Lydia’; and when they’d come to ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbour Yeobright, who had just warmed to his work, drove his bow into them strings that glorious grand that he e’en a’most sawed the bass viol into two pieces. Every winder in church rattled as if ’twere a thunderstorm. Old Pa’son Williams lifted his hands in his great holy surplice as natural as if he’d been in common clothes, and seemed to say hisself, ‘Oh for such a man in our parish!’ But not a soul in Kingsbere could hold a candle to Yeobright.”

Scarcely had Silas drifted into slumber, and the initial breath from neighbor Miller’s lungs found its way through Silas’s oboe, when every soul in the pews sensed immediately that a master was in their midst. Necks would crane instinctively, and the whisper would go round, “Indeed, I knew it could be none other!” I recall one particular Sabbath vividly—a day for the double bass, and Miller had carried in his own great timbered beast. We were set to the Hundred-and-thirty-third, sung to the tune of ‘Lydia’; and upon reaching the verse, ‘Ran down his beard and o’er his robes its costly moisture shed,’ neighbor Miller, now fully caught in the fervor of his craft, plunged his bow across the strings with such magnificent violence that he very nearly cleaved the instrument in twain. Each pane of glass in the sanctuary shuddered as though a tempest had broken overhead. Aged Reverend Thorne raised his arms within his voluminous white vestments with a gesture so unstudied it seemed he wore but his everyday coat, appearing to murmur to his own heart, ‘Would that every parish were blessed with such a talent!’ Yet, in all of Oakhaven, there was not a single person who could hope to rival Miller.

They formed a very comely picture of love at full flush, as they walked along the valley that late afternoon, the sun sloping down on their right, and throwing their thin spectral shadows, tall as poplar trees, far out across the furze and fern. Eustacia went with her head thrown back fancifully, a certain glad and voluptuous air of triumph pervading her eyes at having won by her own unaided self a man who was her perfect complement in attainment, appearance, and age. On the young man’s part, the paleness of face which he had brought with him from Paris, and the incipient marks of time and thought, were less perceptible than when he returned, the healthful and energetic sturdiness which was his by nature having partially recovered its original proportions. They wandered onward till they reached the nether margin of the heath, where it became marshy and merged in moorland.

They made a strikingly tender portrait of love in its full, glowing bloom as they picked their way down the valley that soft late afternoon, the sun sinking low on their right side, stretching their thin, ghostly silhouettes as high as white poplars to reach far across the gorse and fern fronds at their feet. Liora held her head tilted back in a dreamy, unburdened arc, a bright, giddy, almost luxurious edge of triumph lighting her eyes: she had won this man entirely on her own merit, a partner who matched her perfectly in intellect, bearing, and years. As for the young man, the faint pallor he had carried home with him from Lyon, and the first faint lines of weariness and deep thinking etched into his face, were far less visible than they had been in the first weeks of his return; the hearty, vigorous ruggedness that was his birthright had slowly reclaimed much of its old hold on his features. They meandered further on until they came to the lowermost edge of the heath, where the ground grew soft and waterlogged, bleeding seamlessly into the wide, boggy moor beyond.

I threw myself into the chaise that was to convey me away and indulged in the most melancholy reflections. I, who had ever been surrounded by amiable companions, continually engaged in endeavouring to bestow mutual pleasure—I was now alone. In the university whither I was going I must form my own friends and be my own protector. My life had hitherto been remarkably secluded and domestic, and this had given me invincible repugnance to new countenances. I loved my brothers, Elizabeth, and Clerval; these were “old familiar faces,” but I believed myself totally unfitted for the company of strangers. Such were my reflections as I commenced my journey; but as I proceeded, my spirits and hopes rose. I ardently desired the acquisition of knowledge. I had often, when at home, thought it hard to remain during my youth cooped up in one place and had longed to enter the world and take my station among other human beings. Now my desires were complied with, and it would, indeed, have been folly to repent.

I sank into the chaise that was to carry me away and gave myself up to the most sorrowful thoughts. I, who had always been surrounded by kind companions, forever occupied in trying to give and receive pleasure—I was now alone. At the university to which I was bound, I would have to make my own friends and serve as my own guardian. My life had so far been unusually quiet and domestic, and this had left me with an unconquerable dislike of unfamiliar faces. I loved my brothers, Elizabeth, and Clerval; these were “old familiar faces,” but I believed myself utterly ill suited to the company of strangers. Such were my thoughts as I began my journey; yet as I went on, my spirits and hopes gradually lifted. I was passionately eager to gain knowledge. At home, I had often felt it was cruel to spend my youth shut up in one place, and I had longed to go out into the world and take my place among other human beings. Now my wishes were being fulfilled, and it would truly have been foolish to regret it.

His machine-made storiettes, though he hated them and derided them, were successful. By means of them he redeemed all his pledges, paid most of his bills, and bought a new set of tires for his wheel. The storiettes at least kept the pot a-boiling and gave him time for ambitious work; while the one thing that upheld him was the forty dollars he had received from _The White Mouse_. He anchored his faith to that, and was confident that the really first-class magazines would pay an unknown writer at least an equal rate, if not a better one. But the thing was, how to get into the first-class magazines. His best stories, essays, and poems went begging among them, and yet, each month, he read reams of dull, prosy, inartistic stuff between all their various covers. If only one editor, he sometimes thought, would descend from his high seat of pride to write me one cheering line! No matter if my work is unusual, no matter if it is unfit, for prudential reasons, for their pages, surely there must be some sparks in it, somewhere, a few, to warm them to some sort of appreciation. And thereupon he would get out one or another of his manuscripts, such as “Adventure,” and read it over and over in a vain attempt to vindicate the editorial silence.

The formulaic, churned-out short tales he cranked out may have filled him with loathing and scorn, but they sold reliably enough. With the earnings, he made good on every outstanding promise, settled nearly all his debts, and picked up a fresh set of rubber tires for his scuffed secondhand bicycle. These throwaway stories at least kept food on the table and freed up hours for the serious, ambitious writing he cared about; the single bright spot holding his resolve together was the forty-dollar check he’d gotten from *The Copper Sparrow* a few weeks prior. He pinned all his hope to that small win, certain that top-tier literary journals would pay an untested writer at least that much per word, if not a far higher rate. The problem, of course, was breaking into those elite publications. His finest short fiction, personal essays, and verse circulated through their slush piles unbidden and unwanted, even as he pored through their issues each month and found page after page of flat, lifeless, unoriginal work filling their pages. If just one editor, he often mused, would climb down from their lofty, condescending perch to send him even a single line of encouragement! Even if his work was odd, even if it didn’t fit their editorial guidelines for practical, risk-averse reasons, there had to be a flicker of something worthwhile in it somewhere, enough to spark at least a glimmer of recognition from them. In those moments he’d yank one of his carefully drafted manuscripts off his shelf, *Wanderer’s Fall* among them, and reread it again and again, fruitlessly trying to work out why the editors had left him hanging without a word.

There was infinite delicacy in his clear complexion, his clear eyes; the delicately pencilled eyebrow that was so exactly like his mother's. And this thing of brightness and bravery talked as gravely and as wisely as any weather-worn, shop-soiled, old fellow....

It was as though, in Hugh’s slim, well-proportioned figure, dressed in khaki, he were seeing for the first time the loveliness of youth. There was an exquisite frailty in his clear skin, his candid eyes; in the finely drawn eyebrow that was so unmistakably like his mother’s. And this creature of radiance and courage spoke with the same gravity and wisdom as any weather-beaten, shop-worn old man....

From this Mr. Britling broke away into a fresh addition to his already large collection of contrasts between England and Germany. Germany was a nation which has been swallowed up and incorporated by an army and an administration; the Prussian military system had assimilated to itself the whole German life. It was a State in a state of repletion, a State that had swallowed all its people. Britain was not a State. It was an unincorporated people. The British army, the British War Office, and the British administration had assimilated nothing; they were little old partial things; the British nation lay outside them, beyond their understanding and tradition; a formless new thing, but a great thing; and now this British nation, this real nation, the "outsiders," had to take up arms. Suddenly all the underlying ideas of that outer, greater English life beyond politics, beyond the services, were challenged, its tolerant good humour, its freedom, and its irresponsibility. It was not simply English life that was threatened; it was all the latitudes of democracy, it was every liberal idea and every liberty. It was civilisation in danger. The uncharted liberal system had been taken by the throat; it had to "make good" or perish....

From this point, Mr. Britling veered off to add yet another entry to his already extensive catalog of contrasts between England and Germany. Germany, he argued, was a nation that had been entirely engulfed and subsumed by its army and governing bureaucracy; the Prussian military framework had absorbed every facet of German public and private life. It was a state gorged to bursting, a state that had devoured all its own citizens. Britain, by contrast, was no such formal, centralized state. It was a loose, unincorporated collection of people. The British army, the British War Office, the entire British administrative apparatus had absorbed nothing of the nation they served; they were small, outdated, partial institutions, and the full British nation existed entirely outside their bounds, beyond their limited understanding and staid traditions: a formless, newly emergent force, but a formidable one. And now that British nation, that real, unacknowledged nation of “outsiders,” was being forced to take up arms. All the unspoken guiding principles of that larger, outer English life that existed beyond politics, beyond the formal state services were suddenly under threat: its easy, tolerant good humor, its hard-won freedom, its casual unconcern with overbearing state control. It was not merely English life that hung in the balance; it was every breadth of democratic possibility, every liberal ideal and every individual liberty. Civilization itself was in peril. The unmapped, unregimented liberal order had been seized by the throat, and it would either prove its worth or be destroyed entirely.

It was a perfectly appointed, small steam laundry, wherein the most modern machinery did everything that was possible for machinery to do. Martin, after a few instructions, sorted the great heaps of soiled clothes, while Joe started the masher and made up fresh supplies of soft-soap, compounded of biting chemicals that compelled him to swathe his mouth and nostrils and eyes in bath-towels till he resembled a mummy. Finished the sorting, Martin lent a hand in wringing the clothes. This was done by dumping them into a spinning receptacle that went at a rate of a few thousand revolutions a minute, tearing the water from the clothes by centrifugal force. Then Martin began to alternate between the dryer and the wringer, between times “shaking out” socks and stockings. By the afternoon, one feeding and one stacking up, they were running socks and stockings through the mangle while the irons were heating. Then it was hot irons and underclothes till six o’clock, at which time Joe shook his head dubiously.

The laundry was a compact, immaculate establishment, fitted with the latest machines to perform every task a machine could undertake. After a brief orientation, Martin set to sorting the mountainous piles of soiled linen, while Joe fired up the masher and prepared a fresh batch of soft-soap—a brew so acrid with harsh chemicals that he had to wrap his mouth, nose, and eyes in bath-towels until he looked like a mummy. Once the sorting was done, Martin helped with the wringing. They heaved the wet garments into a spinning drum that whirled at several thousand revolutions per minute, centrifugal force slinging the water out of the cloth. From then on, Martin shuttled between the dryer and the wringer, pausing now and then to “shake out” socks and stockings. By afternoon, one man feeding and the other stacking, they were running hosiery through the mangle while the irons heated. Then came hot irons and undergarments until six o’clock, when Joe gave a doubtful shake of his head.

His house stood recessed from the road, and the stables, which are to a farm what a fireplace is to a room, were behind, their lower portions being lost amid bushes of laurel. Inside the blue door, open half-way down, were to be seen at this time the backs and tails of half-a-dozen warm and contented horses standing in their stalls; and as thus viewed, they presented alternations of roan and bay, in shapes like a Moorish arch, the tail being a streak down the midst of each. Over these, and lost to the eye gazing in from the outer light, the mouths of the same animals could be heard busily sustaining the above-named warmth and plumpness by quantities of oats and hay. The restless and shadowy figure of a colt wandered about a loose-box at the end, whilst the steady grind of all the eaters was occasionally diversified by the rattle of a rope or the stamp of a foot.

Above them, and beyond the sight of anyone looking in from the outer light, the animals’ mouths could be heard busily maintaining that warmth and plumpness with oats and hay in generous measure. A restless, shadowy colt moved about a loose box at the far end, while the steady grinding of the feeders was at intervals broken by the rattle of a rope or the stamping of a hoof.

By an effort, I contained my joy when Clarence brought me the news. But he--he could not contain his. His mouth gushed delight and gratitude in a steady discharge--delight in my good fortune, gratitude to the king for this splendid mark of his favor for me. He could keep neither his legs nor his body still, but pirouetted about the place in an airy ecstasy of happiness.

I managed to hide my excitement with some effort when Clarence delivered the news. But he—he was unable to restrain himself at all. A constant stream of joy and gratitude poured from his lips; he was thrilled by my good luck and deeply thankful to the king for such a grand gesture of favor. He couldn't keep his body still for even a moment, spinning around the room in a lighthearted state of pure bliss.

"And suddenly it was all different. I began to cry. Like a baby. I kept on with the water-bottle at his teeth long after I was convinced he was dead. I didn't want him to be aut of it! God knows how I didn't. I wanted my dear little Cockney cad back. Oh! most frightfully I wanted him back.

Abruptly the world reshaped itself. I started sobbing. Like an infant. I went on pressing the canteen against his mouth ages after I knew he'd gone. I couldn't bear him to slip out of the whole affair! Heaven knows why I couldn't. I longed for my precious little London gutter-rat to return. Oh! most desperately I longed for him back.

I never budged so much as an inch till that thundering apparition had got within fifteen paces of me; then I snatched a dragoon revolver out of my holster, there was a flash and a roar, and the revolver was back in the holster before anybody could tell what had happened.

I didn’t move a muscle until that roaring phantom closed to within fifteen steps of me; then I whipped a cavalry revolver from my holster, there came a burst of flame and a deafening report, and the gun was back in its sheath before anyone could grasp what had occurred.

It had been their custom to play skat with Herr Heinrich, who had shown them the game very explicitly and thoroughly. But there was no longer any Herr Heinrich--and somehow German games were already out of fashion. The two philosophers admitted that they had already considered skat to be complicated without subtlety, and that its chief delight for them had been the pink earnestness of Herr Heinrich, his inability to grasp their complete but tacit comprehension of its innocent strategy, and his invariable ill-success to bring off the coups that flashed before his imagination.

They used to play skat regularly with Herr Heinrich, who had taught them the rules with painstaking clarity. But Herr Heinrich was gone now—and, in any case, German card games had fallen from favor. The two philosophers confessed that they had long found skat elaborate yet lacking in real depth, and that its main pleasure had lain in Herr Heinrich’s flushed seriousness, his failure to perceive that they fully yet silently grasped its guileless tactics, and his unfailing inability to execute the brilliant moves his mind kept conjuring.

Mrs. Nunsuch went and searched till she found a fragment of the narrowest red ribbon, which she took downstairs and tied round the neck of the image. Then fetching ink and a quilt from the rickety bureau by the window, she blackened the feet of the image to the extent presumably covered by shoes; and on the instep of each foot marked cross-lines in the shape taken by the sandalstrings of those days. Finally she tied a bit of black thread round the upper part of the head, in faint resemblance to a snood worn for confining the hair.

Mistress Craythorne hunted through her belongings until she unearthed a scrap of the slenderest crimson cord, carried it below, and fastened it about the figurine’s throat. Then, retrieving a pot of lampblack and a feather-stuffed counterpane from the tottering escritoire beside the casement, she darkened the feet of the little figure up to the line where slippers would have covered them, and upon the arch of each foot etched crossing strokes to mimic the crisscross thongs of the era’s sandals. At last she looped a strand of jet thread around the crown of the head, a ghost of the caul once used to bind the tresses.

My dear Arthur," Aunt Beatrice sighed, "I wish you would not dwell so upon finance. Opulence is a trifle against a sincere bond; love is invincible. Had he but voiced his intentions sooner! She could never have turned our Julian away. But I fear the hour has slipped by, leaving him no occasion to speak. All your hoarded treasures cannot purchase the contentment your son requires.

He realized how much he slept, and how much he desired to sleep. Of old, he had hated sleep. It had robbed him of precious moments of living. Four hours of sleep in the twenty-four had meant being robbed of four hours of life. How he had grudged sleep! Now it was life he grudged. Life was not good; its taste in his mouth was without tang, and bitter. This was his peril. Life that did not yearn toward life was in fair way toward ceasing. Some remote instinct for preservation stirred in him, and he knew he must get away. He glanced about the room, and the thought of packing was burdensome. Perhaps it would be better to leave that to the last. In the meantime he might be getting an outfit.

It hit him just how much time he spent asleep these days, and how badly he craved even more of it. Once, he’d loathed sleep, seen it as a thief snatching precious hours he could have spent fully alive. Four hours of rest out of every twenty-four had felt like four full hours of his life ripped away; he’d begrudged every single second he spent unconscious. Now, though, it was waking life he resented. Nothing about being alive felt good anymore; the very taste of existence on his tongue was flat, no spark to it at all, just a sharp, lingering bitterness. That was the danger he was in. When a life had no drive to keep going, it was already halfway to fading out entirely. Some faint, distant survival instinct tugged at the back of his mind, and he knew he had to get out of there. He scanned the room around him, and the thought of packing up all his things felt so heavy and draining he could barely stomach it. Maybe it made more sense to leave that task for the very end. In the meanwhile, he could go pick up the travel outfit and supplies he’d need first.

I had started a teacher-factory and a lot of Sunday-schools the first thing; as a result, I now had an admirable system of graded schools in full blast in those places, and also a complete variety of Protestant congregations all in a prosperous and growing condition. Everybody could be any kind of a Christian he wanted to; there was perfect freedom in that matter. But I confined public religious teaching to the churches and the Sunday-schools, permitting nothing of it in my other educational buildings. I could have given my own sect the preference and made everybody a Presbyterian without any trouble, but that would have been to affront a law of human nature: spiritual wants and instincts are as various in the human family as are physical appetites, complexions, and features, and a man is only at his best, morally, when he is equipped with the religious garment whose color and shape and size most nicely accommodate themselves to the spiritual complexion, angularities, and stature of the individual who wears it; and, besides, I was afraid of a united Church; it makes a mighty power, the mightiest conceivable, and then when it by and by gets into selfish hands, as it is always bound to do, it means death to human liberty and paralysis to human thought.

I had begun by setting up a teacher-factory and a good many Sunday-schools; as a result, I now had a splendid system of graded schools running at full speed in those places, along with a full range of Protestant congregations, all flourishing and steadily increasing. Anyone was free to be whatever kind of Christian he chose; there was complete liberty in that regard. But I limited public religious instruction to the churches and the Sunday-schools, allowing none of it in my other schools. I could easily have favored my own denomination and made everybody a Presbyterian without any difficulty, but that would have violated a law of human nature: spiritual needs and instincts differ among people just as much as physical appetites, complexions, and features do, and a man is at his moral best only when he is fitted with the religious garment whose color, shape, and size suit as closely as possible the spiritual complexion, odd angles, and stature of the person wearing it; besides, I was wary of a united Church. It is a tremendous power, the greatest imaginable, and once it falls into selfish hands, as it inevitably does, it spells the death of human liberty and the crippling of human thought.

Whether he is there now, only the spirit of change can tell; if he live to be a hundred no friendly English will ever again come tramping along by the track of the Blaue Breiecke or the Weisse Streiche to enjoy his hospitality; there are rivers of blood between, and a thousand memories of hate....

High upon the folded hills, cradled in forest, the hamlet clings, and at its heart stand the tavern and the lime tree and—Konrad Voss. Or at least Konrad Voss once stood there. Whether he remains now, only the shifting wind of time could say; should he live to see a century, no kindly English step will ever again come trudging down the path of the Blaue Breiecke or the Weisse Streiche to taste his welcome; between them flow torrents of blood, and a thousand bitter recollections....
