“And so,” he concluded, in a swift résumé, “no state composed of the slave-types can endure. The old law of development still holds. In the struggle for existence, as I have shown, the strong and the progeny of the strong tend to survive, while the weak and the progeny of the weak are crushed and tend to perish. The result is that the strong and the progeny of the strong survive, and, so long as the struggle obtains, the strength of each generation increases. That is development. But you slaves—it is too bad to be slaves, I grant—but you slaves dream of a society where the law of development will be annulled, where no weaklings and inefficients will perish, where every inefficient will have as much as he wants to eat as many times a day as he desires, and where all will marry and have progeny—the weak as well as the strong. What will be the result? No longer will the strength and life-value of each generation increase. On the contrary, it will diminish. There is the Nemesis of your slave philosophy. Your society of slaves—of, by, and for, slaves—must inevitably weaken and go to pieces as the life which composes it weakens and goes to pieces.

“And so,” he concluded, in a sharp, rapid summary, “no state built of people with a slave’s mindset can ever endure. The ancient law of development still holds unshaken. In the struggle for existence, as I have demonstrated, the strong and their descendants are disposed to survive, while the weak and their children are crushed and fated to perish. The outcome is that the strong and their line persist, and as long as that struggle remains in place, each generation grows more capable than the last. That is the very essence of development. But you slaves— I will grant that it is a miserable thing to be a slave— but you cling to a fantasy of a society where the law of development is struck down, where no weak, unfit person ever perishes, where every incompetent person gets all the food they crave whenever they want it, and where everyone may marry and have children—the weak just as freely as the strong. What will come of this? The strength and inherent vitality of each generation will no longer rise. On the contrary, it will dwindle. That is the nemesis of your slave philosophy. Your society of slaves, by slaves, and for slaves, will inevitably weaken and crumble just as the lives that form it weaken and are snuffed out.”

It was a hot, wild night, and they were all three reminded of the old Sunday night when they had looked at the lightning from the same place.

It was a hot, restless night, and the three of them were all reminded of that old Sunday evening when they had watched the lightning from the same spot.

“Well, your want of love seems to me the one thing that takes away harm from such an agreement with him. If wild heat had to do wi’ it, making ye long to over-come the awkwardness about your husband’s vanishing, it mid be wrong; but a cold-hearted agreement to oblige a man seems different, somehow. The real sin, ma’am in my mind, lies in thinking of ever wedding wi’ a man you don’t love honest and true.”

“It seems to me that your lack of affection is the very thing that strips this arrangement of its guilt. Had you been driven by some feverish impulse to ignore the shadow of your husband’s disappearance, it might well be a sin; yet a dispassionate pact made merely to assist him feels altogether different. To my mind, ma’am, the true transgression lies in the prospect of wedlock with a man you do not honestly and deeply love.”

The afternoon drew on apace, and, looking to the right towards the sea as he walked beside the horse, Poorgrass saw strange clouds and scrolls of mist rolling over the long ridges which girt the landscape in that quarter. They came in yet greater volumes, and indolently crept across the intervening valleys, and around the withered papery flags of the moor and river brinks. Then their dank spongy forms closed in upon the sky. It was a sudden overgrowth of atmospheric fungi which had their roots in the neighbouring sea, and by the time that horse, man, and corpse entered Yalbury Great Wood, these silent workings of an invisible hand had reached them, and they were completely enveloped, this being the first arrival of the autumn fogs, and the first fog of the series.

The afternoon waned swiftly, and as Poorgrass trudged beside the horse, he glanced seaward to the right. There he saw queer clouds and trailing ribbons of mist unfurling over the long ridges that hemmed the land in that direction. They swelled into thicker volumes, drifting lazily across the hollows between and curling about the dry, paper-like reeds along the moor and riverbanks. Soon their clammy, porous masses sealed off the sky. It was a swift efflorescence of vaporous growths rooted in the nearby sea, and by the time horse, man, and corpse reached Yalbury Great Wood, these mute machinations of an unseen agency had overtaken them, wrapping them entirely—the first arrival of the autumn fogs, and the first fog of the season.

This man, whom he had seen upon the Jury, stood drinking at the little counter, in conversation with the Defarges, man and wife. The Vengeance took her part in the exchange as freely as though she were one of the house itself.

Yeobright gave him some particulars of her illness and death, and continued: “After this no kind of pain will ever seem more than an indisposition to me. I began saying that I wanted to ask you something, but I stray from subjects like a drunken man. I am anxious to know what my mother said to you when she last saw you. You talked with her a long time, I think?”

Yeobright passed along specific details of her illness and eventual death to him, then continued speaking: “After all this, no sort of pain will ever register as anything worse than a minor ailment to me. I started out meaning to ask you a question, didn’t I? I wander off subject as easily as a man addled by drink. What I’m eager to know is what my mother said to you the last time you saw each other. You spent a good long while talking with her, if I recall correctly?”

Meanwhile, Gabriel, gripping a long beech branch in one hand and his shepherd’s crook in the other, went on beating the stack, knocking away every burning ember he could reach.

Don’t forget that, for his eyes are sunk in his head so much deeper than any other man’s, that you might almost tell him by that alone. His face is dark, like his hair and eyes; and, although he can’t be more than six or eight and twenty, withered and haggard. His lips are often discoloured and disfigured with the marks of teeth; for he has desperate fits, and sometimes even bites his hands and covers them with wounds—why did you start?” said the girl, stopping suddenly.

“He’s tall,” the girl said, “solidly built, but not heavy. He walks like he’s trying not to draw attention, always glancing over his shoulder as he moves, first one side, then the other. He’s dark-skinned, same as his hair and eyes, and even though he can’t be more than twenty-six or twenty-eight, his face looks worn and gaunt. "Writing is recording, not living. But now I feel suddenly that we are living intensely. It is as if the whole quality of life was changing. There are such times. There are times when the spirit of life changes altogether. The old world knew that better than we do. It made a distinction between weekdays and Sabbaths, and between feasts and fasts and days of devotion. That is just what has happened now. Week-day rules must be put aside. Before--oh! three days ago, competition was fair, it was fair and tolerable to get the best food one could and hold on to one's own. But that isn't right now. War makes a Sabbath, and we shut the shops. The banks are shut, and the world still feels as though Sunday was keeping on...."

Writing is about capturing life, not truly living it. But right now I'm struck by the sensation that we're living with extraordinary intensity. It feels as though the very texture of existence is transforming. Such moments do come. There are moments when the soul of life shifts entirely. The ancient world understood this far better than we do. They drew lines between ordinary days and holy days, between celebrations and periods of fasting and sacred observance. That's precisely what's unfolding now. The rules of everyday life must be suspended. Just a few days ago—oh, barely three days past—rivalry seemed fair, it seemed right and acceptable to scramble for the best provisions and guard what was yours. But that no longer fits. War creates a Sabbath, and we close the shops. The banks are closed, and the world still carries the feeling of an endless Sunday....

The coach rattled away, over nearly the same ground as that which Oliver had traversed when he first entered London in company with the Dodger; and, turning a different way when it reached the Angel at Islington, stopped at length before a neat house, in a quiet shady street near Pentonville. Here, a bed was prepared, without loss of time, in which Mr. Brownlow saw his young charge carefully and comfortably deposited; and here, he was tended with a kindness and solicitude that knew no bounds.

The coach rattled off, covering nearly the exact stretch of road Oliver had traveled when he first arrived in London in the Dodger’s company; upon reaching the Angel at Islington, it veered down an alternate route, finally pulling to a stop outside a neat house on a quiet, leaf-shaded street close to Pentonville. A bed was readied without a moment’s delay here, and Mr. Brownlow watched as his young charge was settled carefully and comfortably into it; he was tended to with a warmth and thoughtfulness that knew no limits.

I passed the bridge of Pélissier, where the ravine, which the river forms, opened before me, and I began to ascend the mountain that overhangs it. Soon after, I entered the valley of Chamounix. This valley is more wonderful and sublime, but not so beautiful and picturesque as that of Servox, through which I had just passed. The high and snowy mountains were its immediate boundaries, but I saw no more ruined castles and fertile fields. Immense glaciers approached the road; I heard the rumbling thunder of the falling avalanche and marked the smoke of its passage. Mont Blanc, the supreme and magnificent Mont Blanc, raised itself from the surrounding _aiguilles_, and its tremendous _dôme_ overlooked the valley.

I crossed the span of Pélissier, where the chasm carved by the river yawned before me, and I started to climb the slope that loomed above it. Not long after, I came into the vale of Chamounix. This vale was more awe-inspiring and majestic, yet lacked the loveliness and charm of Servox, which I had lately traversed. Towering, snow-clad peaks formed its nearest walls, but I beheld no further remnants of fortresses or verdant meadows. Vast glaciers crept close to the path; I caught the distant roar of a tumbling avalanche and glimpsed the haze of its descent. Mont Blanc, the sovereign and resplendent Mont Blanc, soared above the encircling _aiguilles_, its immense _dôme_ commanding the entire valley.

We embraced him as a specimen, and in three minutes we had all but died for one another as friends.

We took him up as a curiosity, and within moments had nearly perished of devotion, each for the other, like sworn companions.

Mrs. Morse drove by Martin on the street one day, and smiled and nodded. He smiled back and lifted his hat. The episode did not affect him. A month before it might have disgusted him, or made him curious and set him to speculating about her state of consciousness at that moment. But now it was not provocative of a second thought. He forgot about it the next moment. He forgot about it as he would have forgotten the Central Bank Building or the City Hall after having walked past them. Yet his mind was preternaturally active. His thoughts went ever around and around in a circle. The centre of that circle was “work performed”; it ate at his brain like a deathless maggot. He awoke to it in the morning. It tormented his dreams at night. Every affair of life around him that penetrated through his senses immediately related itself to “work performed.” He drove along the path of relentless logic to the conclusion that he was nobody, nothing. Mart Eden, the hoodlum, and Mart Eden, the sailor, had been real, had been he; but Martin Eden! the famous writer, did not exist. Martin Eden, the famous writer, was a vapor that had arisen in the mob-mind and by the mob-mind had been thrust into the corporeal being of Mart Eden, the hoodlum and sailor. But it couldn’t fool him. He was not that sun-myth that the mob was worshipping and sacrificing dinners to. He knew better.

Mrs. Leland passed Julian one afternoon in the street, smiled, and dipped her head. He answered with a smile and a lift of his hat. It meant nothing to him. A month earlier it might have repelled him, or at least stirred his curiosity and sent him wondering what went on behind her eyes at that instant. Now it provoked no second reflection. The next moment it was gone from his mind. He forgot it as he might have forgotten the granite post office or the courthouse after walking by them. And yet his mind was in unnatural motion. His thoughts wheeled and circled without rest. At the center of that circle was “work accomplished”; it gnawed at his brain like an immortal worm. He woke to it in the morning. It haunted his sleep at night. Everything in life that came to him through the senses was instantly referred to “work accomplished.” He followed the pitiless chain of logic to its end and found himself reduced to no one, to nothing. Jules Eden, the rough, and Jules Eden, the seaman, had been real, had been himself; but Julian Eden, the celebrated author, did not exist. Julian Eden, the celebrated author, was a mist rising in the public mind and by that public mind imposed upon the flesh of Jules Eden, rough and sailor. But it could not deceive him. He was not that sun-born idol to whom the crowd prayed and for whom it sacrificed its dinners. He knew better.

“Just look at it!” he cried. “An’ it’s all mine! It’s free. I can lie down under them trees an’ sleep for a thousan’ years if I want to. Aw, come on, Mart, let’s chuck it. What’s the good of waitin’ another moment. That’s the land of nothin’ to do out there, an’ I got a ticket for it—an’ it ain’t no return ticket, b’gosh!”

“Look at that!” he shouted. “And it’s all mine! It’s wide open. I could lie down under those trees and sleep for a thousand years if I felt like it. Come on, Mart, let's just leave it all behind. What’s the point in waiting another minute? That’s the land of leisure out there, and I’ve got my ticket in hand—and believe me, I’m never coming back!”

Venn was loth to depart, for all on earth that interested him lay under this roof. As nobody in the house had any more sleep that night, except the two who slept for ever, there was no reason why he should not remain. So he retired into the niche of the fireplace where he had used to sit, and there he continued, watching the steam from the double row of banknotes as they waved backwards and forwards in the draught of the chimney till their flaccidity was changed to dry crispness throughout. Then the woman came and unpinned them, and, folding them together, carried the handful upstairs. Presently the doctor appeared from above with the look of a man who could do no more, and, pulling on his gloves, went out of the house, the trotting of his horse soon dying away upon the road.

Venn was reluctant to go, for everything on earth that stirred his interest was under that roof. Since no one in the house would sleep again that night, save the two who slept forever, there was no reason he should not stay. So he withdrew to the niche in the fireplace where he had once sat, and remained there, watching the steam rise from the double row of banknotes as they swayed to and fro in the chimney draft, until their limp softness had given way entirely to a dry, crisp stiffness. Then the woman came to unpin them, and, gathering them together, carried the little stack upstairs. Soon after, the doctor came down from above with the air of a man who had done all he could, and, drawing on his gloves, left the house, the quick sound of his horse’s trotting soon fading along the road.

As the resting man looked at the barrow he became aware that its summit, hitherto the highest object in the whole prospect round, was surmounted by something higher. It rose from the semiglobular mound like a spike from a helmet. The first instinct of an imaginative stranger might have been to suppose it the person of one of the Celts who built the barrow, so far had all of modern date withdrawn from the scene. It seemed a sort of last man among them, musing for a moment before dropping into eternal night with the rest of his race.

Watching the ancient mound, the reclining man saw that its summit, formerly the highest mark on the horizon, was crowned by something taller. It projected from the rounded earth like a crest upon a casque. A dreamer might have mistaken the silhouette for one of the prehistoric builders, so far had the modern world retreated from the view. He seemed a final sentinel of his people, lingering a moment in thought before falling into the same eternal shadow as the rest of his vanished kin.

Mr. Bumble emerged at early morning from the workhouse-gate, and walked with portly carriage and commanding steps, up the High Street. He was in the full bloom and pride of beadlehood; his cocked hat and coat were dazzling in the morning sun; he clutched his cane with the vigorous tenacity of health and power. Mr. Bumble always carried his head high; but this morning it was higher than usual. There was an abstraction in his eye, an elevation in his air, which might have warned an observant stranger that thoughts were passing in the beadle’s mind, too great for utterance.

Mr. Bumble stepped out through the workhouse gate at dawn and strode up the High Street with a stately bearing and authoritative tread. He stood at the very peak of his beadle’s glory; his tricorne hat and coat gleamed brilliantly in the early sunlight, and he gripped his walking stick with the firm vigor of robust vitality. Mr. Bumble habitually held his chin aloft, but this morning it rose even higher than normal. A faraway look in his gaze and a loftiness in his demeanor might have signaled to any perceptive passerby that ideas were stirring in the beadle’s head—thoughts far too momentous to be spoken aloud.

As if endowed with the spirit of prophecy, Bathsheba gasped out, “No, it is not true; it cannot be true!” Then she said and heard no more. The ice of self-command which had latterly gathered over her was broken, and the currents burst forth again, and overwhelmed her. A darkness came into her eyes, and she fell.

As though seized by a flash of foresight, Bathsheba choked out, “No, it isn’t true; it can’t be true!” After that, she neither spoke nor perceived anything more. The frozen composure that had lately formed around her shattered, and a flood of emotion broke loose and swept her away. A blackness clouded her vision, and she dropped to the ground.

It cannot be expected that this system of farming would produce any very extraordinary or luxuriant crop. Oliver Twist’s ninth birthday found him a pale thin child, somewhat diminutive in stature, and decidedly small in circumference. But nature or inheritance had implanted a good sturdy spirit in Oliver’s breast. It had had plenty of room to expand, thanks to the spare diet of the establishment; and perhaps to this circumstance may be attributed his having any ninth birth-day at all. Be this as it may, however, it _was_ his ninth birthday; and he was keeping it in the coal-cellar with a select party of two other young gentleman, who, after participating with him in a sound thrashing, had been locked up for atrociously presuming to be hungry, when Mrs. Mann, the good lady of the house, was unexpectedly startled by the apparition of Mr. Bumble, the beadle, striving to undo the wicket of the garden-gate.

No one could have imagined that such a mode of cultivation would yield a thriving or abundant harvest. On the morning of his ninth birthday, Oliver Twist was a pallid, slender boy, short for his age and alarmingly narrow around the middle. Still, either through birth or some hidden bequest, a tenacious spirit had taken root inside him. It had been granted ample space to grow, owing to the meager rations of the workhouse, and it may well have been this very fact that allowed him to reach a ninth birthday at all. Whatever the truth of it, that day was indeed his ninth birthday, and he was spending it in the coal-cellar alongside a chosen company of two other boys, who, after sharing a vigorous beating with him, had been shut away for the outrageous crime of daring to feel hunger. Just then, Mrs. Mann, the household’s kindly matron, was suddenly alarmed by the sight of Mr. Bumble the beadle, struggling to unfasten the latch of the garden gate.

She had dressed the hive with herbs and honey, fetched a ladder, brush, and crook, made herself impregnable with armour of leather gloves, straw hat, and large gauze veil—once green but now faded to snuff colour—and ascended a dozen rungs of the ladder. At once she heard, not ten yards off, a voice that was beginning to have a strange power in agitating her.

Every man and woman on the property was fully occupied with the urgent work of saving the hay crop—even Liddy had stepped out of the house to pitch in—so Bathsheba decided she would hive the bees herself, if she could pull it off. She’d coated the inside of the empty hive with herbs and honey to draw the swarm in, fetched a ladder, soft bristle brush, and beekeeping crook, then armored herself against stings with thick leather gloves, a wide-brimmed straw hat, and an oversized gauze veil that had once been a vibrant green, now faded to a murky snuff shade. She’d just climbed a dozen rungs up the ladder when she heard a voice no more than ten yards away, one that was starting to hold a strange, unsettling power to rattle her completely.

As he drifted down a small rise through a huddled hamlet, he caught the jumbled clatter and tinkle of moving traffic up ahead, and spotted the fluorescent danger triangle posted to warn of an upcoming crossroads. He eased off the accelerator, then pulled to a sharp, sudden stop.

Prince Michael’s shoes were wrecked far beyond the skill of the carefullest cobbler. The ragman would have declined any negotiations concerning his clothes. The two weeks’ stubble on his face was grey and brown and red and greenish yellow—as if it had been made up from individual contributions from the chorus of a musical comedy. No man existed who had money enough to wear so bad a hat as his.

Prince Adrian’s shoes were ruined beyond the repair of the most meticulous cobbler. The ragpicker would have refused all discussion of his garments. The fortnight’s stubble on his face was grey and brown and rusty red and a sickly yellow, as though it had been assembled from separate offerings by the chorus of a comic opera. No man alive possessed sufficient means to wear a hat as dreadful as his.

It is scarcely necessary to state that Gabriel could not avoid noting the farmer’s bearing to-night towards their entertainer. Yet there was nothing exceptional in his actions beyond what appertained to his time of performing them. It was when the rest were all looking away that Boldwood observed her; when they regarded her he turned aside; when they thanked or praised he was silent; when they were inattentive he murmured his thanks. The meaning lay in the difference between actions, none of which had any meaning of itself; and the necessity of being jealous, which lovers are troubled with, did not lead Oak to underestimate these signs.

Gabriel could hardly fail to mark, without need of saying so, the farmer’s conduct that evening toward their hostess. Still, there was nothing singular in his gestures save the moment he chose for them. While the others averted their gaze, Boldwood watched her; when they looked her way, he glanced off; if they offered thanks or compliments, he kept quiet; if their attention strayed, he whispered his gratitude. The import resided in the contrast among acts that, taken singly, signified nothing; and the jealous watchfulness that afflicts lovers did not prevent Oak from reading these tokens truly.

In all weathers, in the snow and frost of winter, in the bitter winds of spring, in the hot sunshine of summer, in the rains of autumn, and again in the snow and frost of winter, Lucie passed two hours of every day at this place; and every day on leaving it, she kissed the prison wall. Her husband saw her (so she learned from her father) it might be once in five or six times: it might be twice or thrice running: it might be, not for a week or a fortnight together. It was enough that he could and did see her when the chances served, and on that possibility she would have waited out the day, seven days a week.

Through biting winter frosts and drifting snows, through the raw gales of spring, under the blazing summer sun, amid the autumn downpours, and once more through the biting frosts and snows of winter, Elara kept her daily vigil of two hours at that spot; and each day, as she departed, she pressed her lips to the stone wall. Her beloved saw her (so her father told her) perhaps once in five or six visits, perhaps two or three times in succession, perhaps not at all for a week or even a fortnight together. It sufficed that he could and did behold her whenever fortune permitted, and on that slender chance she would have lingered the whole day long, every day of the week.

“Frighten him!” echoed Sikes. “It’ll be no sham frightening, mind you. If there’s anything queer about him when we once get into the work; in for a penny, in for a pound. You won’t see him alive again, Fagin. Think of that, before you send him. Mark my words!” said the robber, poising a crowbar, which he had drawn from under the bedstead.

“Scare him?” Sikes echoed. “This won’t be some harmless act, believe me. If he shows any sign of trouble once the job begins, I’m going all the way. You won’t see him alive again, Fagin. Think long and hard about that before you send him out. Remember what I’ve told you!” the thief said, balancing a crowbar he had pulled from beneath the bed frame.

Leaving her instructions with Gabriel and Maryann, that they were to see everything carefully locked up for the night, she went out of the house just at the close of a timely thunder-shower, which had refined the air, and daintily bathed the coat of the land, though all beneath was dry as ever. Freshness was exhaled in an essence from the varied contours of bank and hollow, as if the earth breathed maiden breath; and the pleased birds were hymning to the scene. Before her, among the clouds, there was a contrast in the shape of lairs of fierce light which showed themselves in the neighbourhood of a hidden sun, lingering on to the farthest north-west corner of the heavens that this midsummer season allowed.

After charging Gabriel and Maryann with the task of securing the house for the night, she stepped outdoors just as a brief thunderstorm concluded. The rain had cleansed the atmosphere and lightly dampened the earth's surface, though the ground underneath remained entirely dry. A crisp vitality radiated from every ridge and valley, as if the soil itself were exhaling a pure, sweet breath, and the joyful birds offered a chorus to the landscape. In front of her, the sky displayed a striking contrast: patches of violent light flickered around a veiled sun, which drifted toward the furthest northwestern edge of the horizon that the midsummer solstice would permit.

Lunch was an open-air feast again. Three visitors had dropped in; they had motored down from London piled up on a motor-cycle and a side-car; a brother and two sisters they seemed to be, and they had apparently reduced hilariousness to a principle. The rumours of coming hockey that had been floating on the outskirts of Mr. Direck's consciousness ever since his arrival, thickened and multiplied.... It crept into his mind that he was expected to play....

Three visitors had come in; they had driven down from London, packed together on a motor-cycle and side-car, a brother and two sisters, apparently, and they seemed to have made hilarity into a creed. The rumours of the hockey match to come, which had been drifting at the edge of Mr. Direck’s awareness ever since his arrival, grew denser and more numerous.... It dawned on him that he was expected to play....

Henry wished to dissuade me, but seeing me bent on this plan, ceased to remonstrate. He entreated me to write often. “I had rather be with you,” he said, “in your solitary rambles, than with these Scotch people, whom I do not know; hasten, then, my dear friend, to return, that I may again feel myself somewhat at home, which I cannot do in your absence.”

Julian endeavored to divert me from my path, but finding my resolve firm, he ceased his objections. He urged me to correspond with him often. “I would choose to be your companion,” he said, “in those bleak wanderings, rather than stay among these unfamiliar folk; return quickly, then, my trusted friend, so that I may again find my bearings, which I can never truly possess while we are apart.”

He told one grim story of an invalid woman unable to move, lying in bed in her _appartement_, and of how her husband went out on the balcony to look at the Zeppelin. There was a great noise of shooting. Ever and again he would put his head back into the room and tell her things, and then after a time he was silent and looked in no more. She called to him, and called again. Becoming frightened, she raised herself by a great effort and peered through the glass. At first she was too puzzled to understand what had happened. He was hanging over the front of the balcony, with his head twisted oddly. Twisted and shattered. He had been killed by shrapnel fired from the outer fortifications....

He recounted a harrowing tale of a woman tethered to her bed by infirmity, and of her husband, who had stepped onto the terrace to watch the silver airship. The sky groaned with the thunder of the batteries. At intervals, he would lean back into the room to describe the spectacle, but eventually, his voice failed and he looked in no more. She called his name, then called again, her heart quickening. With a desperate, agonizing effort, she hoisted herself up to peer through the window. At first, the image refused to make sense. He was slumped over the railing, his head canted at a horrific, impossible angle. It was shattered and unrecognizable. He had been struck by a jagged shard of metal launched from the city’s own defenses.

“Not till you let me go—not till you let me go—Never—never!” screamed the girl. Sikes looked on, for a minute, watching his opportunity, and suddenly pinioning her hands dragged her, struggling and wrestling with him by the way, into a small room adjoining, where he sat himself on a bench, and thrusting her into a chair, held her down by force. She struggled and implored by turns until twelve o’clock had struck, and then, wearied and exhausted, ceased to contest the point any further. With a caution, backed by many oaths, to make no more efforts to go out that night, Sikes left her to recover at leisure and rejoined Fagin.

“Not until you let me go—not until you let me go—Never—never!” the girl shrieked. Sikes watched her for a moment, waiting for his chance, and then, with a sudden grip that trapped her arms, hauled her, fighting and twisting against him, into a little room next door. There he settled himself on a bench, and, forcing her into a chair, held her there by main strength. She struggled and pleaded by turns until twelve o’clock struck, and then, tired out and spent, gave over the struggle at last. After warning her, with many oaths, not to make any further attempt to leave that night, Sikes left her to come round as she could and went back to Fagin.

About this time we retired to our house at Belrive. This change was particularly agreeable to me. The shutting of the gates regularly at ten o’clock and the impossibility of remaining on the lake after that hour had rendered our residence within the walls of Geneva very irksome to me. I was now free. Often, after the rest of the family had retired for the night, I took the boat and passed many hours upon the water. Sometimes, with my sails set, I was carried by the wind; and sometimes, after rowing into the middle of the lake, I left the boat to pursue its own course and gave way to my own miserable reflections. I was often tempted, when all was at peace around me, and I the only unquiet thing that wandered restless in a scene so beautiful and heavenly—if I except some bat, or the frogs, whose harsh and interrupted croaking was heard only when I approached the shore—often, I say, I was tempted to plunge into the silent lake, that the waters might close over me and my calamities for ever. But I was restrained, when I thought of the heroic and suffering Elizabeth, whom I tenderly loved, and whose existence was bound up in mine. I thought also of my father and surviving brother; should I by my base desertion leave them exposed and unprotected to the malice of the fiend whom I had let loose among them?

It was during this season that we sought refuge at our villa in Clairmont. This transition brought a profound relief to my spirit; the rigid locking of the town gates at the stroke of ten and the prohibition against lingering upon the waves past twilight had made our urban dwelling feel like a stifling cage. Now, liberty was mine. Frequently, once my kin had surrendered to sleep, I would unmoor the skiff and spend the dark watches upon the great expanse of the water. On certain nights, I let the canvas catch the breeze to carry me where it would; on others, having pulled the oars until I reached the center of the lake, I allowed the vessel to drift aimlessly while I surrendered to the agony of my own dark reflections. 

Amidst the profound stillness of the world, I felt myself the sole discordant note in an otherwise celestial landscape—save perhaps for the erratic flight of a bat or the coarse, rhythmic pulse of the marsh-dwellers that fell silent as I neared the bank. In those moments, I was frequently seized by the urge to cast myself into the obsidian depths, seeking a final shroud beneath the waves to bury my griefs and my crimes eternally. Yet, the memory of the noble and enduring Isabella stayed my hand; I loved her with a desperate devotion, and her very life was intertwined with my own. I considered, too, my father and my remaining brother. Could I, through a coward's exit, abandon them to the cruel whims of the demon I had unleashed upon their world?

“I stand aside, messieurs, by my heap of stones, to see the soldiers and their prisoner pass (for it is a solitary road, that, where any spectacle is well worth looking at), and at first, as they approach, I see no more than that they are six soldiers with a tall man bound, and that they are almost black to my sight--except on the side of the sun going to bed, where they have a red edge, messieurs. Also, I see that their long shadows are on the hollow ridge on the opposite side of the road, and are on the hill above it, and are like the shadows of giants. Also, I see that they are covered with dust, and that the dust moves with them as they come, tramp, tramp! But when they advance quite near to me, I recognise the tall man, and he recognises me. Ah, but he would be well content to precipitate himself over the hill-side once again, as on the evening when he and I first encountered, close to the same spot!”

I step aside, gentlemen, beside my pile of stones, to watch the soldiers and their captive go by (for this is a lonely road, where any sight is worth observing), and at first, as they draw near, I see only that they are six soldiers escorting a tall man in restraints, and they appear nearly black to my eyes—except along the edge where the sun is setting, where they glow with a crimson rim, gentlemen. I also notice their stretched shadows falling across the sunken ridge on the far side of the road, and climbing the slope beyond it, resembling the shadows of giants. I notice too that they are coated in dust, and the dust travels with them as they advance, tramp, tramp! But when they come quite close to me, I know the tall man, and he knows me. Ah, how gladly he would fling himself down the hillside once more, just as on that evening when he and I first met, near this very place!

In another part of the hill an altogether different scene began to force itself upon the eye towards midday. A circular tent, of exceptional newness and size, was in course of erection here. As the day drew on, the flocks began to change hands, lightening the shepherd’s responsibilities; and they turned their attention to this tent and inquired of a man at work there, whose soul seemed concentrated on tying a bothering knot in no time, what was going on.

Elsewhere upon the slope, as the sun climbed toward noon, a wholly separate spectacle commenced to impress itself upon the sight. A round marquee, remarkable for its freshness and uncommon breadth, was being raised upon that spot. With the advancing hours, the sheep passed into other care, easing the herder’s burden; so the men shifted their gaze toward this pavilion and asked a fellow labouring there—whose spirit appeared wholly bent on subduing a vexing tangle without delay—what affair was afoot.

I pray you let the drama halt while Chorus stalks to the footlights and drops an epicedian tear upon the fatness of Mr. Hoover. Tune the pipes to the tragedy of tallow, the bane of bulk, the calamity of corpulence. Tried out, Falstaff might have rendered more romance to the ton than would have Romeo’s rickety ribs to the ounce. A lover may sigh, but he must not puff. To the train of Momus are the fat men remanded. In vain beats the faithfullest heart above a 52-inch belt. Avaunt, Hoover! Hoover, forty-five, flush and foolish, might carry off Helen herself; Hoover, forty-five, flush, foolish and fat is meat for perdition. There was never a chance for you, Hoover.

I beg you, let the action pause while Chorus steps down to the footlights and sheds a dirge-like tear over the ample girth of Mr. Hoover. Let the pipes be tuned to the tragedy of tallow, to the curse of heaviness, the disaster of corpulence. Trimmed down, Falstaff might have lent more romance to the ton than Romeo’s frail ribs could ever yield to the pound. A lover may sigh, but he must not wheeze. The fat men are consigned to the retinue of Momus. In vain does the most faithful heart beat above a fifty-two-inch belt. Begone, Hoover! Hoover, at forty-five, flushed and foolish, might have borne Helen herself away; Hoover, forty-five, flushed, foolish, and fat is food for damnation. You never had a chance, Hoover.

He brought his great discovery to Ruth, sharing with her all his joy and wonder in it. But she did not seem to be so enthusiastic over it. She tacitly accepted it and, in a way, seemed aware of it from her own studies. It did not stir her deeply, as it did him, and he would have been surprised had he not reasoned it out that it was not new and fresh to her as it was to him. Arthur and Norman, he found, believed in evolution and had read Spencer, though it did not seem to have made any vital impression upon them, while the young fellow with the glasses and the mop of hair, Will Olney, sneered disagreeably at Spencer and repeated the epigram, “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.”

He presented his monumental revelation to Ruth, attempting to convey the full measure of his awe and delight. However, she did not share his fervor. She accepted his words with a quiet reserve, appearing to have already encountered such concepts during her own schooling. The ideas failed to move her as intensely as they had moved him; he might have been surprised by this had he not reasoned that the material was simply familiar to her, while to him it was a startling new light. He found that Arthur and Norman also accepted evolution and had read Spencer, yet the work seemed to have made no significant impact on them. Meanwhile, Will Olney—the bespectacled youth with the unruly hair—dismissed Spencer with a caustic sneer, repeating the mocking refrain: “There is no god but the Unknowable, and Herbert Spencer is his prophet.”

I closed not my eyes that night. My internal being was in a state of insurrection and turmoil; I felt that order would thence arise, but I had no power to produce it. By degrees, after the morning’s dawn, sleep came. I awoke, and my yesternight’s thoughts were as a dream. There only remained a resolution to return to my ancient studies and to devote myself to a science for which I believed myself to possess a natural talent. On the same day I paid M. Waldman a visit. His manners in private were even more mild and attractive than in public, for there was a certain dignity in his mien during his lecture which in his own house was replaced by the greatest affability and kindness. I gave him pretty nearly the same account of my former pursuits as I had given to his fellow professor. He heard with attention the little narration concerning my studies and smiled at the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, but without the contempt that M. Krempe had exhibited. He said that “These were men to whose indefatigable zeal modern philosophers were indebted for most of the foundations of their knowledge. They had left to us, as an easier task, to give new names and arrange in connected classifications the facts which they in a great degree had been the instruments of bringing to light. The labours of men of genius, however erroneously directed, scarcely ever fail in ultimately turning to the solid advantage of mankind.” I listened to his statement, which was delivered without any presumption or affectation, and then added that his lecture had removed my prejudices against modern chemists; I expressed myself in measured terms, with the modesty and deference due from a youth to his instructor, without letting escape (inexperience in life would have made me ashamed) any of the enthusiasm which stimulated my intended labours. I requested his advice concerning the books I ought to procure.

I didn’t sleep a wink all that night. My whole mind was roiling with chaos and unrest, even as I sensed some kind of clarity would eventually settle out of it—yet I had no power to force that order to come. Slowly, once dawn had broken across the sky, I finally drifted off. When I woke, all the frantic, tangled thoughts I’d had the night before felt like a faded, half-remembered dream. All that remained was a firm resolution to return to my long-held course of study, and devote myself fully to the science I’d always believed I had a natural talent for.

I went to visit Professor Waldman that very same day. He was far gentler and more charming in private than he was in public; the quiet, stately dignity he carried during his lectures softened entirely in his own home, replaced by open, warm affability and kindness. I told him almost the exact same account of my past pursuits that I’d shared with his fellow professor. He listened closely as I walked him through my earlier studies, and smiled when I mentioned the names of Cornelius Agrippa and Paracelsus, with none of the sneering contempt Professor Krempe had shown at those very same names.

“These were men whose relentless, unflagging zeal laid most of the foundation for the knowledge modern scientists build their work on,” he said. “They did the hard work of bringing so many key facts to light; our far simpler task is to give those facts new, standardized names and sort them into connected, logical systems. Even the work of brilliant men, no matter how misdirected it might seem at the time, almost always ends up serving the lasting good of humanity in the end.”

I listened to his words, which he delivered without any trace of arrogance or performative pretension, then told him his lecture had erased all the prejudices I’d held against modern chemists. I spoke in careful, measured terms, with the modesty and deference a young student owes their instructor, careful not to let any of the fiery enthusiasm spurring my planned work slip out; my inexperience with the world would have left me mortified to show that level of unguarded eagerness. I ended by asking him for advice on which books I ought to purchase for my studies.

He was a fairly well-educated man for one of middle class—exceptionally well educated for a common soldier. He spoke fluently and unceasingly. He could in this way be one thing and seem another: for instance, he could speak of love and think of dinner; call on the husband to look at the wife; be eager to pay and intend to owe.

For a man of middle-class background, he was reasonably well educated—exceptionally learned, even, for a common rank-and-file soldier. He spoke smoothly, fluently, and almost without pause. This gift of gab let him embody one thing while appearing to be another: he could gush about the tender thrills of love while his thoughts drifted to his next meal, drop by a husband’s home for a supposed visit when his real target was the man’s wife, or act impatient to settle a tab when he fully intended to leave the debt unpaid.

“Another circumstance strengthened and confirmed these feelings. Soon after my arrival in the hovel I discovered some papers in the pocket of the dress which I had taken from your laboratory. At first I had neglected them, but now that I was able to decipher the characters in which they were written, I began to study them with diligence. It was your journal of the four months that preceded my creation. You minutely described in these papers every step you took in the progress of your work; this history was mingled with accounts of domestic occurrences. You doubtless recollect these papers. Here they are. Everything is related in them which bears reference to my accursed origin; the whole detail of that series of disgusting circumstances which produced it is set in view; the minutest description of my odious and loathsome person is given, in language which painted your own horrors and rendered mine indelible. I sickened as I read. ‘Hateful day when I received life!’ I exclaimed in agony. ‘Accursed creator! Why did you form a monster so hideous that even _you_ turned from me in disgust? God, in pity, made man beautiful and alluring, after his own image; but my form is a filthy type of yours, more horrid even from the very resemblance. Satan had his companions, fellow devils, to admire and encourage him, but I am solitary and abhorred.’

“Another event deepened and confirmed these emotions. Not long after I reached the hut, I found some papers in the pocket of the garment I had taken from your laboratory. At first I paid them no attention; but once I could read the characters in which they were written, I set myself to examine them carefully. They proved to be your journal for the four months before my creation. In these pages you recorded in the smallest detail each stage of your work; mixed with this were notes of domestic events. You surely remember these papers. They are here. In them is everything that concerns my cursed beginning; the entire account of that revolting chain of circumstances that brought it about is laid bare; and the most exact description of my vile, detestable form is given in language that reflected your own terror and made mine unchangeable. I grew faint as I read. ‘Miserable day when I was given life!’ I cried in torment. ‘Accursed maker! Why did you fashion a monster so monstrous that even you recoiled from me in horror? God, in mercy, made man fair and attractive, in his own likeness; but my shape is a foul copy of yours, made more dreadful by the very resemblance. Satan had his companions, his fellow demons, to admire him and cheer him on, but I am alone and loathed.’

When she had disappeared Charley, with misgiving in his eyes, slowly came from the stable door, and going to another point in the bank he looked over. Eustacia was leaning against it on the outside, her face covered with her hands, and her head pressing the dewy heather which bearded the bank’s outer side. She appeared to be utterly indifferent to the circumstance that her bonnet, hair, and garments were becoming wet and disarranged by the moisture of her cold, harsh pillow. Clearly something was wrong.

Once she had vanished entirely from view, Charley, his eyes heavy with unease, moved slowly away from the stable doorway, then picked a different spot along the bank to peer over its edge. Eustacia was leaning against the bank’s outer face, her face buried in her hands, her head pressed into the dewy heather that bristled along the slope’s outer edge. She seemed completely unconcerned that her bonnet, hair, and clothing were growing damp and rumpled from the moisture seeping out of that cold, scratchy makeshift pillow. It was obvious something was deeply amiss.

“Show myself a man of spirit?... Ah, well! let me take the name of drunkard humbly—let me be a man of contrite knees—let it be! I know that I always do say ‘Please God’ afore I do anything, from my getting up to my going down of the same, and I be willing to take as much disgrace as there is in that holy act. Hah, yes!... But not a man of spirit? Have I ever allowed the toe of pride to be lifted against my hinder parts without groaning manfully that I question the right to do so? I inquire that query boldly?”

“Prove myself a man of mettle?... Ah, very well! I will accept the title of drunkard meekly—let me be a man bowed on penitent knees—so be it! I am aware that I utter ‘God willing’ before every deed, from my rising to my resting, and I am ready to bear whatever shame such a sacred habit may carry. Hah, indeed!... Yet not a man of spirit? Have I ever permitted pride’s boot to be aimed at my backside without stoutly groaning that I dispute the privilege to do so? I put that question boldly?”

“He is not ungrateful for winning her,” whispered Eustacia, “and in that respect he is a good man. Many women would go far for such a husband. But do I desire unreasonably much in wanting what is called life—music, poetry, passion, war, and all the beating and pulsing that are going on in the great arteries of the world? That was the shape of my youthful dream; but I did not get it. Yet I thought I saw the way to it in my Clym.”

“He doesn’t take the blessing of winning her for granted,” Eustacia whispered, “and in that regard, he is a good man. Many women would go to great lengths for a husband like him. But am I craving something unreasonably excessive when I long for what is called true life—music, poetry, burning passion, the fire of grand struggle, all the thrumming, surging pulse that beats in the great arteries of the wider world? That was the exact shape of my youthful dream; I never got to hold it, yet I once thought I saw the path to it in my Clym.”

The Echoes rarely answered to the actual tread of Sydney Carton. Some half-dozen times a year, at most, he claimed his privilege of coming in uninvited, and would sit among them through the evening, as he had once done often. He never came there heated with wine. And one other thing regarding him was whispered in the echoes, which has been whispered by all true echoes for ages and ages.

The murmurs seldom stirred at Sydney Carton’s own footfalls. At most, on five or six occasions each year, he exercised his right to appear without summons, settling among them for the night as he had habitually done in former days. He never arrived flushed with drink. And one further secret about him was breathed through the murmurs—a secret that every faithful murmur has carried across the centuries.

Sunday he had intended to devote to studying for the high school examination, but the pearl-diving article lured him away, and he spent the day in the white-hot fever of re-creating the beauty and romance that burned in him. The fact that the _Examiner_ of that morning had failed to publish his treasure-hunting article did not dash his spirits. He was at too great a height for that, and having been deaf to a twice-repeated summons, he went without the heavy Sunday dinner with which Mr. Higginbotham invariably graced his table. To Mr. Higginbotham such a dinner was advertisement of his worldly achievement and prosperity, and he honored it by delivering platitudinous sermonettes upon American institutions and the opportunity said institutions gave to any hard-working man to rise—the rise, in his case, which he pointed out unfailingly, being from a grocer’s clerk to the ownership of Higginbotham’s Cash Store.

Sunday was meant for cramming for the high school exam, but the article on pearl diving seduced him, and he surrendered the hours to the white-hot thrill of reshaping the beauty and romance blazing inside him. That morning’s _Examiner_ hadn’t printed his treasure-hunting piece, yet his mood stayed aloft, untouched. Ignoring two calls to the table, he skipped the hefty Sunday dinner Mr. Higginbotham always laid on as a trophy of his worldly success. To Mr. Higginbotham, that meal proclaimed his prosperity, and he sanctified it with trite little sermons on American institutions and the chances they offered any industrious man to climb—his own climb, as he never failed to note, having lifted him from grocery clerk to proprietor of Higginbotham’s Cash Store.

“While I was overcome by these feelings, I left the spot where I had committed the murder, and seeking a more secluded hiding-place, I entered a barn which had appeared to me to be empty. A woman was sleeping on some straw; she was young, not indeed so beautiful as her whose portrait I held, but of an agreeable aspect and blooming in the loveliness of youth and health. Here, I thought, is one of those whose joy-imparting smiles are bestowed on all but me. And then I bent over her and whispered, ‘Awake, fairest, thy lover is near—he who would give his life but to obtain one look of affection from thine eyes; my beloved, awake!’

While I was still swayed by these emotions, I left the place where I had committed the murder, and, in search of a more hidden refuge, entered a barn that had seemed to me deserted. There, upon a bed of straw, a woman was sleeping. She was young, not indeed so lovely as the one whose portrait I held, yet pleasing in appearance and radiant with the freshness of youth and health. Here, I thought, was one of those to whom those joyful smiles are granted that are denied to me. Then I bent over her and murmured, “Awake, fairest; thy lover is at hand—he who would give his life to win but one glance of affection from thine eyes. My beloved, awake!”

Bathsheba’s was an impulsive nature under a deliberative aspect. An Elizabeth in brain and a Mary Stuart in spirit, she often performed actions of the greatest temerity with a manner of extreme discretion. Many of her thoughts were perfect syllogisms; unluckily they always remained thoughts. Only a few were irrational assumptions; but, unfortunately, they were the ones which most frequently grew into deeds.

Marwenna carried an impulsive, fiery streak beneath a cool, deliberative outward bearing. Endowed with the shrewd, strategic mind of Eleanor of Aquitaine and the reckless, romantic spirit of Isabella of France, she often pulled off acts of staggering boldness with a demeanor of quiet, unflappable discretion. Most of her ruminations were perfectly constructed, logically sound syllogisms; unluckily, they never progressed beyond the realm of idle thought. Only a tiny fraction were ungrounded, impulsive leaps of assumption; yet those, unfortunately, were the notions that most often took root and grew into tangible action.

Its owner went downstairs into the courtyard, got into his carriage, and drove away. Not many people had talked with him at the reception; he had stood in a little space apart, and Monseigneur might have been warmer in his manner. It appeared, under the circumstances, rather agreeable to him to see the common people dispersed before his horses, and often barely escaping from being run down. His man drove as if he were charging an enemy, and the furious recklessness of the man brought no check into the face, or to the lips, of the master. The complaint had sometimes made itself audible, even in that deaf city and dumb age, that, in the narrow streets without footways, the fierce patrician custom of hard driving endangered and maimed the mere vulgar in a barbarous manner. But, few cared enough for that to think of it a second time, and, in this matter, as in all others, the common wretches were left to get out of their difficulties as they could.

The Count descended to the stone quadrangle, stepped into his waiting coach, and sped off. He had found few companions at the gala; he had stood in a pocket of isolation, and the Cardinal’s reception of him had been notably frigid. In light of this, it seemed to afford him a certain grim satisfaction to see the dregs of the city flee before his galloping horses, their lives held by a thread as they scrambled from his path. His driver steered with the ferocity of a cavalryman in mid-charge, yet this lethal speed provoked neither a frown nor a word of caution from the master. A murmur of protest was sometimes heard, even in that unhearing city and muted century, suggesting that in these tight thoroughfares lacking walkways, the haughty tradition of high-speed travel brutally crippled the poor. However, no one of consequence gave the matter a second thought, and in this, as in all things, the wretched populace was left to endure their misery as best they could.

The girl who admitted him looked in his face as if she did not know what answer to return, and showed him in to where Venn was seated, saying to the reddleman, “Will you tell him, please?”

At four o’clock there came a soft tap at the door. It was from Ned, who had been sent by Captain Waye to learn whether any news had reached them of Selina. The maid who admitted him looked up into his face as though at a loss for what reply to make, and ushered him in to where Durnford was seated, saying to the younger man, “Will you tell him, please?”

"Our feeding here is typical of the whole system. It is a system invented not with any idea of getting the best results--that does not enter into the War Office philosophy--but to have a rule for everything, and avoid arguments. There is rather too generous an allowance of bread and stuff per man, and there is a very fierce but not very efficient system of weighing and checking. A rather too generous allowance is, of course, a direct incentive to waste or stealing--as any one but our silly old duffer of a War Office would know. The checking is for quantity, which any fool can understand, rather than for quality. The test for the quality of army meat is the smell. If it doesn't smell bad, it is good....

“Our provisioning here is a fair sample of the whole machine. It is a machine devised not with the least notion of securing the best outcome—that never enters the creed of the War Department—but in order that there may be a rule for every circumstance and no one need argue. The ration of bread and other matter per man is, if anything, somewhat too liberal, and over it there hangs a very savage though not very effective apparatus of weighing and inspection. An allowance a shade too ample is, naturally enough, a plain invitation to waste or pilfering—as anyone except our absurd old fool of a War Department would understand. The inspection concerns quantity, which any idiot can grasp, rather than quality. The proper test of army meat is the nose. If it does not stink, it is good....

To-day there were more changes on the bill of fare than usual. The soups were lighter; pork was eliminated from the entrées, figuring only with Russian turnips among the roasts. The gracious spirit of spring pervaded the entire menu. Lamb, that lately capered on the greening hillsides, was becoming exploited with the sauce that commemorated its gambols. The song of the oyster, though not silenced, was _dimuendo con amore_. The frying-pan seemed to be held, inactive, behind the beneficent bars of the broiler. The pie list swelled; the richer puddings had vanished; the sausage, with his drapery wrapped about him, barely lingered in a pleasant thanatopsis with the buckwheats and the sweet but doomed maple.

Today the menu had shifted more than usual. The soups were thinner; pork had vanished from the entrées, appearing only beside Russian turnips among the roasts. A gentle breath of spring ran through every dish. Lamb, which not long ago had frolicked on the greening slopes, was now being dressed in a sauce that recalled its playful leaps. The oyster’s voice, though not yet hushed, was fading in a tender decrescendo. The frying-pan seemed to rest idly behind the kindly shelter of the broiler. The list of pies grew longer; the heavier puddings had disappeared; the sausage, wrapped snugly in its own covering, lingered faintly in a quiet meditation on mortality alongside the buckwheat cakes and the sweet, soon-to-be-sacrificed maple.

He looked, wistfully, into their faces, one by one when they passed out, as though to see which way the greater number leant; but that was fruitless. The jailer touched him on the shoulder. He followed mechanically to the end of the dock, and sat down on a chair. The man pointed it out, or he would not have seen it.

He gazed longingly at their faces as they exited, one after the other, as if trying to gauge which way the consensus was tilting; but the effort was futile. The guard touched his shoulder. He moved like an automaton toward the end of the dock and sat down. He wouldn't have even seen the chair if the man hadn't gestured toward it.

Then afterwards he had escaped. In quite a little time he had escaped. He had been in a railway station somewhere in Belgium; locked in a waiting-room with three or four French prisoners, and the junction had been bombed by French and British aeroplanes. Their guard and two of the prisoners had been killed. In the confusion the others had got away into the town. There were trucks of hay on fire, and a store of petrol was in danger. "After that one was bound to escape. One would have been shot if one had been found wandering about."

His getaway followed shortly thereafter. It had taken remarkably little time for him to break free. He had been confined within a Belgian railway station, held in a waiting area alongside several French captives, when Allied aircraft launched an assault on the junction. The explosion claimed the lives of their sentry and two of the other detainees. Amidst the ensuing turmoil, the remaining men managed to flee into the nearby streets. Wagons filled with hay were ablaze, and a nearby fuel depot was on the verge of igniting. "In such circumstances, flight was the only option. To be discovered roaming the area would have meant certain execution."

And so Oliver remained all that day, and for the greater part of many subsequent days, seeing nobody, between early morning and midnight, and left during the long hours to commune with his own thoughts. Which, never failing to revert to his kind friends, and the opinion they must long ago have formed of him, were sad indeed.

And so Oliver remained there all that first day, and for the greater part of many days that came after, seeing not a single soul between the break of dawn and the deep of midnight, left to occupy the long, empty hours in conversation with his own thoughts. Those thoughts never strayed long from his kind old friends, and the poor opinion they must surely have formed of him by now, which made them bitter with sadness indeed.

“I will think of this,” she said hurriedly. “Whether I can honestly make use of you as a friend, or must close with you as a lover—that is what I must ask myself. If I wish to go and decide to accept your company I will signal to you some evening at eight o’clock punctually, and this will mean that you are to be ready with a horse and trap at twelve o’clock the same night to drive me to Budmouth harbour in time for the morning boat.”

“I’ll give this some thought,” she said quickly. “I need to ask myself whether I can honestly rely on you as a friend, or if I must commit to you as a lover. If I decide to leave and want you to come with me, I’ll signal you at exactly eight o’clock one evening. That will be your cue to be ready with a horse and carriage at midnight that same night to drive me to the Budmouth docks in time for the morning boat.”

It was not a reckless manner, the manner in which he said these words aloud under the fast-sailing clouds, nor was it more expressive of negligence than defiance. It was the settled manner of a tired man, who had wandered and struggled and got lost, but who at length struck into his road and saw its end.

It was not a wild gesture, the way he spoke those words into the racing sky, nor did it betray carelessness any more than rebellion. It was the steady bearing of a weary man, who had roamed and fought and lost his way, but who had finally found his path and glimpsed its close.

“Well, then, there 's photographing,” said Captain Abel. “It doos seem amazing, as the minister said: you set down, and square yourself, and slick your hair, and stare stiddy into a funnel, and a man ducks his head under a covering, and pop! there you be, as natural as life,--if not more so. And when Uncle Capen was a young man, there wasn't nothing but portraits and minnytures, and these black-paper-and-scissors portraits,--what do they call 'em? Yes, sir, all that come in under his observation.”

“Well, then, there’s photography,” said Captain Silas. “It does seem astonishing, as the minister said: you sit down, and square yourself up, and smooth your hair, and stare steady into a little funnel, and a man ducks his head under a cloth, and pop! there you are, as natural as life—if not more so. And when Uncle Hiram was a young man, there was nothing but portraits and miniatures, and those black-paper-and-scissors likenesses—what do they call them? Yes, sir, all that came within his observation.”
